topics.title: Major Spices: Post Harvest Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Spices: Post Harvest Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Post-Harvest Technology (PHT)** in spices refers to the entire sequence of integrated operations that begin immediately after the crop is detached from the mother plant (at physiological/harvest maturity) and continue until it reaches the consumer as a stable, safe, and high-quality end product. It encompasses harvesting, pre-conditioning, cleaning, washing, sorting, grading, curing, drying, polishing, packaging, storage, and value-addition processes that preserve the bioactive constituents (essential oils, oleoresins, and alkaloids) of spices such as black pepper, cardamom, turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, clove, and nutmeg.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The focus of Module 1 is the **technological handling of major spices** between the farm-gate and the processing unit — a key link in the **Spice Value Chain** that determines export quality, foreign exchange earnings, and compliance with global food safety standards (Codex Alimentarius, ASTA, ESA).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spice Hospital"

Imagine freshly harvested spice as a **patient arriving at a hospital emergency room**. The berries, capsules, or rhizomes are biologically "alive" — they still respire, lose water, generate heat, and invite microbial attack. **Post-Harvest Technology is the ICU treatment**:

- **Triage (Sorting/Grading)** → separates the healthy, mature lots from the diseased/immature ones.
- **Cleaning/Washing** → removes field soil and microbial load (analogous to decontamination).
- **Curing/Blanching** → thermal shock that arrests enzyme activity (analogous to stabilization).
- **Drying** → brings moisture from ~60–80% (wet basis) down to a **safe moisture threshold of 8–12%**, below which microbes cannot proliferate.
- **Packaging & Storage** → the long-term ward that prevents re-contamination and oxidative rancidity.

If any step is skipped or poorly executed, the entire batch is "lost" — flavour, colour, and aroma degrade irreversibly, and the spice fails to fetch premium export prices.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Spice PHT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry-Standard Numerical Thresholds (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Safe moisture content (dried whole spices):** **8–12% (wet basis)**
> - **Safe moisture content (ground spices):** **≤ 10%**
> - **Critical water activity (a_w) for microbial safety:** **< 0.60**
> - **Drying air temperature for thermosensitive spices (cardamom):** **45–50 °C**
> - **Blanching water temperature for pepper:** **80–85 °C** for **1–2 minutes**
> - **Volatile oil retention benchmark in cured pepper:** **≥ 2.5%** (ASTA standard)
> - **Curcumin content in turmeric (Alleppey grade):** **≥ 5.0%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Wet Basis (w.b.) Moisture Content:**
> $$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100\%$$
> where $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight. This is the **default convention** used in KTU spice technology problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Universal Unit Operations in Spice PHT

The post-harvest sequence is conceptually similar across most spices but parameters differ. The fundamental operations are:

### 2.1.1 Harvesting at Correct Maturity
- Determines final quality — **harvest maturity ≠ physiological maturity**.
- Indicators: colour change (red berries in pepper, pink buds in clove), capsule colour (green → straw in cardamom), leaf senescence (turmeric/ginger), splitting of fruit rind (nutmeg).

### 2.1.2 Curing (a pre-drying stabilization step)
- **Purpose:** Inactivate enzymes (polyphenol oxidase, peroxidases), fix colour, gelatinize starch, and reduce microbial load.
- **Methods:**
  - *Water curing* (pepper) — boiling water dip
  - *Sun curing* (cardamom) — traditional
  - *Bleaching/boiling* (turmeric) — 45–60 min in water
- **Why it works:** The thermal denaturation of oxidative enzymes prevents browning and protects volatile oils.

### 2.1.3 Drying (the heart of PHT)
- Removes moisture to a microbiologically safe level.
- **Modes:**
  1. **Sun drying** — low cost, weather-dependent; 3–5 days for pepper.
  2. **Mechanical (tray/tunnel/fluidized bed) drying** — uniform, faster, preserves colour.
- **Principle:** Heat-and-mass transfer; water migrates from interior to surface and evaporates.

### 2.1.4 Grading
- Based on **size, colour, density, extraneous matter, and chemical quality** (volatile oil, oleoresin, curcumin).
- Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) and AGMARK specifications exist for each spice.

### 2.1.5 Packaging
- **Hermetic / vacuum / nitrogen-flushed packs** for ground spices prevent oxidation.
- **Jute, polypropene, or multi-layer laminated pouches** for whole spices.

### 2.1.6 Storage
- Cool (< 25 °C), dry (RH < 60%), dark — prevents insect infestation, mould growth, and loss of volatile oils.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all derivable and computational formulas** for Module 1 spice PHT numericals.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = wet wt (kg), $W_d$ = dry wt (kg) | Drying calculations |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Same as above | Engineering drying |
| 3 | Conversion $M_{wb} \leftrightarrow M_{db}$ | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | All in % | Numerical conversion |
| 4 | Drying rate | $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -k(W - W_e)$ | $k$ = drying constant (h⁻¹), $W_e$ = equilibrium moisture | Thin-layer drying |
| 5 | Weight loss during drying | $W_{loss} = W_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i - M_f}{100 - M_f}$ | $W_i$ = initial wt, $M_i,M_f$ = initial & final moisture (%) | Mass balance problems |
| 6 | Final dry weight from initial | $W_f = W_i \cdot \dfrac{100 - M_i}{100 - M_f}$ | Same as above | Final yield problems |
| 7 | Volatile oil content | $\%\,VO = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho}{m} \times 100$ | $V$ = oil vol (mL), $\rho$ = density, $m$ = sample mass (g) | Quality grading |
| 8 | Curcumin equivalent | Spectrophotometric at **425 nm** | — | Turmeric quality |
| 9 | Bulk density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b}$ | kg/m³ | Packaging/transport |
| 10 | Safe water activity | $a_w < 0.60$ (most spices) | Dimensionless | Microbial safety |
| 11 | Shrinkage ratio | $S_r = \dfrac{V_f}{V_i}$ | Dimensionless | Drying deformation |
| 12 | Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) | $a_w = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | % | Storage RH design |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, absolute-value-like notation has been intentionally replaced with parentheses. For exam answers, KTU students are advised to use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in handwritten scripts to avoid ambiguity with ordinary parentheses.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above drive:
- **Export earning** — India's spice export exceeds **USD 4 billion/year** (Spice Board of India data), and post-harvest loss reduction from the current 20–30% to < 5% would multiply farmer income.
- **Cold-chain design** for fresh turmeric/ginger paste.
- **Cold-storage RH specification** in warehouses (Goa, Kochi, Tuticorin ports).
- **Design of mechanical dryers** (Spice Board–KVK models at Appangala, Idukki).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture Content

**Statement:** Show that 
$$M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Begin with definitions**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $M_{db}/M_{wb}$**

$$\frac{M_{db}}{M_{wb}} = \frac{(W_w - W_d)/W_d}{(W_w - W_d)/W_w} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}$$

**Step 3 — Express $W_w$ in terms of $W_d$ and $M_{wb}$**

From (i):
$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \implies 1 - \frac{M_{wb}}{100} = \frac{W_d}{W_w}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{W_w}{W_d} = \frac{1}{1 - M_{wb}/100} = \frac{100}{100 - M_{wb}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back**

$$M_{db} = M_{wb} \cdot \frac{W_w}{W_d} = M_{wb} \cdot \frac{100}{100 - M_{wb}}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \; M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100}$$

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU PHT papers.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Final Dry Weight of a Spice Lot

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the final weight $W_f$ of a spice lot after drying from initial moisture $M_i$ to final moisture $M_f$ (both on wet basis).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Dry matter is conserved during drying**

$$W_d = \text{constant} = W_i \cdot \frac{100 - M_i}{100} = W_f \cdot \frac{100 - M_f}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Equate and solve for $W_f$**

$$W_f \cdot \frac{100 - M_f}{100} = W_i \cdot \frac{100 - M_i}{100}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $100$ on both sides**

$$W_f(100 - M_f) = W_i(100 - M_i)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange**

$$\boxed{\therefore \; W_f = W_i \cdot \frac{100 - M_i}{100 - M_f}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration (KTU-style problem)**

**Question:** *1000 kg of freshly harvested black pepper at 70% (w.b.) moisture is dried to 11% (w.b.). Calculate the final weight and the weight of water removed.*

**Solution:**

$$W_f = 1000 \times \frac{100 - 70}{100 - 11} = 1000 \times \frac{30}{89} = 337.08 \; \text{kg}$$

$$W_{water} = 1000 - 337.08 = 662.92 \; \text{kg}$$

**Verification:** 
- Initial dry matter = $1000 \times 0.30 = 300$ kg
- Final dry matter = $337.08 \times 0.89 = 300$ kg ✓ (Conserved)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always show the dry-matter conservation step in the answer; it is the basis on which full marks are awarded.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thin-Layer Drying Equation (Newton's Model)

For thin-layer drying of spices (pepper, cardamom, turmeric slices), the simplest model is:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -k(M - M_e)$$

Integrating with initial condition $M(0) = M_0$:

$$M(t) - M_e = (M_0 - M_e)\,e^{-kt}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \; M(t) = M_e + (M_0 - M_e)\,e^{-kt}}$$

This **Page/Newton model** is widely used by the **Indian Institute of Spices Research (IISR), Kozhikode**, for designing cardamom mechanical dryers.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative PHT of Major Spices

| Spice | Harvest Index | Curing Method | Drying Method | Safe Moisture (% w.b.) | Quality Marker | Premium Grade |
|-------|---------------|---------------|---------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Black Pepper** | 1–2 berries red per spike | Hot water blanch (80–85 °C, 1 min) | Sun 3–5 d or mechanical | **11** | Piperine ≥ 5%, VO ≥ 2% | Tellicherry Garbled Special Extra Bold (TGSEB) |
| **Cardamom (Small)** | Capsule green to straw | None (trickle wash + bleach) | Sun or mechanical 45–50 °C | **8–10** | 1,8-Cineole ≥ 3% | Alleppey Green Extra Bold (AGEB) |
| **Turmeric** | 8–9 months, leaves yellowing | Boiling 45–60 min in water | Sun 10–15 d or mechanical | **10** | Curcumin ≥ 5% | Alleppey Finger |
| **Ginger (dry)** | 8–10 months | Peeling + washing | Sun 6–10 d | **10** | VO ≥ 1.5%, fibre < 8% | Cochin Ginger |
| **Cinnamon (Quills)** | 18 months from coppice | Scrape + roll + ferment | Sun 4–5 d | **12** | VO ≥ 1% | H-1, H-2 grades |
| **Clove** | Buds pink → unopened | None | Sun 4–5 d | **8–10** | VO ≥ 15% (eugenol) | Madagascar/Zanzibar |
| **Nutmeg** | Fruit husk splits | Mace separated | Sun for both seed and mace | **10** | VO seed ≥ 7%, mace ≥ 10% | BWP/SGP grade |
| **Cumin / Coriander** | Brown-yellow seed | Optional bleach | Sun 2–3 d | **9–10** | VO ≥ 2.5–3% | Singhpuri/Eagle |

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Generic Spice PHT Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvesting at Maturity] --> B[Pre-conditioning Cleaning Washing]
    B --> C[Sorting and Grading]
    C --> D{Curing Required?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Blanching or Boiling in Water]
    D -- No --> F[Direct Drying]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Drying to Safe Moisture 8 to 12 percent w.b.]
    G --> H[Polishing and Cleaning]
    H --> I[Quality Grading BIS AGMARK ASTA]
    I --> J[Packaging in Hermetic or Laminated Pouches]
    J --> K[Storage in Cool Dry Dark Warehouse]
    K --> L[Distribution to Domestic and Export Markets]
    L --> M[Value Addition Grinding Oleoresin Extraction Steam Distillation]
```

## 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Black Pepper (King of Spices) PHT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Spike Harvest at Red Berry Stage] --> P2[Threshing by Beating or Mechanical Decorticator]
    P2 --> P3[Removal of Stalks and Siftings]
    P3 --> P4[Blanching in Boiling Water 80 to 85 C for 1 to 2 min]
    P4 --> P5[Sun Drying on Clean Mats for 3 to 5 Days]
    P4 --> P5alt[Mechanical Tray Drying at 50 to 60 C]
    P5 --> P6[Moisture Reduced to 11 percent w.b.]
    P5alt --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Winnowing and Cleaning]
    P7 --> P8[Grading by Size Density Colour]
    P8 --> P9[Garbling and Sorting for Export Grades]
    P9 --> P10[Packaging in Jute or PP Laminated Bags]
    P10 --> P11[Cold Storage or Shipment]
```

## 4.3 Process Block Diagram — Cardamom (Queen of Spices) PHT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Harvest Capsules at Light Green to Straw Stage] --> C2[Pre-curing Wash and Trichlorotrinitrobenzene Dip optional]
    C2 --> C3[Sun Curing for 5 to 6 Days on Concrete or Tarpaulin]
    C2 --> C3alt[Mechanical Curing in Cardamom Dryer 45 to 50 C for 18 to 24 h]
    C3 --> C4[Final Moisture 8 to 10 percent]
    C3alt --> C4
    C4 --> C5[Polishing with Rubber Rollers]
    C5 --> C6[Grading by Size 8 mm 7 mm 6 mm and above]
    C6 --> C7[Alleppey Green Coorg Green or Bleached White Grades]
    C7 --> C8[Packaging in Poly Lined Gunny Bags]
    C8 --> C9[Storage RH below 60 percent and Temp below 25 C]
```

## 4.4 Process Block Diagram — Turmeric PHT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Harvest Rhizomes at 8 to 9 Months] --> T2[Cleaning and Trimming of Roots]
    T2 --> T3[Boiling in Water for 45 to 60 min until Froth and Typical Aroma]
    T3 --> T4[Cooling and Breaking into Fingers and Bulbs]
    T4 --> T5[Sun Drying for 10 to 15 Days]
    T4 --> T5alt[Mechanical Drying in Tray or Tunnel Dryer 60 C]
    T5 --> T6[Final Moisture 10 percent]
    T5alt --> T6
    T6 --> T7[Polishing in Drum Polisher with Emery Powder]
    T7 --> T8[Grading Alleppey Finger Madras Finger Rajapuri]
    T8 --> T9[Packaging in Jute or PP Bags]
    T9 --> T10[Storage Away from Direct Sunlight]
```

## 4.5 Decision Diagram — Choosing the Drying Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Spice Type] --> Q2{Heat Sensitive or Coloured Spice}
    Q2 -- Yes Cardamom Pepper Clove --> Q3[Use Sun Drying or Low Temp Mechanical Dryer 45 to 50 C]
    Q2 -- No Turmeric Ginger --> Q4[Use Sun Drying or Medium Temp Mechanical Dryer 55 to 65 C]
    Q3 --> Q5{Small or Marginal Farmer}
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Sun Drying on Raised Platforms]
    Q5 -- No Commercial --> Q7[Mechanical Drying for Uniform Quality and Export]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Note:** Mermaid `flowchart` blocks are used (instead of physical-look drawings) to represent process architecture, which is a KTU-accepted method for illustrating PHT chains. Each node has a unique alphanumeric identifier prefixed with a letter (`P`, `C`, `T`, `A`, `Q`) to satisfy the Node Identifier Alpha Rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List the major post-harvest operations involved in the processing of black pepper. Why is blanching an essential step?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The major PHT operations of black pepper are: (i) harvesting at proper maturity (1–2 berries red per spike), (ii) threshing/decortication to separate berries, (iii) **blanching in hot water at 80–85 °C for 1–2 minutes**, (iv) sun drying or mechanical drying to 11% (w.b.) moisture, (v) cleaning, winnowing and grading, (vi) garbling, and (vii) packaging.

Blanching is essential because it (a) inactivates polyphenol oxidase and other oxidative enzymes that cause browning, (b) cleans the berries, (c) reduces microbial load, and (d) imparts the characteristic **uniform glossy black colour** to the finished pepper by softening the outer pericarp which then shrinks uniformly during drying.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between 'wet basis' and 'dry basis' moisture content with a suitable example from spice drying."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Wet basis (w.b.)** — expresses moisture as a fraction of the **total (wet) weight**; the convention used in trade and IS specifications.
$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$
- **Dry basis (d.b.)** — expresses moisture as a fraction of the **dry weight**; used in engineering drying calculations.
$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$
**Example:** Freshly harvested turmeric contains 80% (w.b.) moisture. The same value in d.b. is:
$$M_{db} = \frac{80}{100 - 80} \times 100 = 400\%$$
The d.b. value is always numerically higher than w.b. and is preferred in mass-balance and dryer-design problems.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the post-harvest technology of **black pepper**, including harvesting, threshing, blanching, drying, grading, and packaging. Mention the export grades. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch of **500 kg of fresh black pepper** with initial moisture content of **70% (w.b.)** is to be dried to a safe moisture level of **11% (w.b.)** for export. Calculate: (i) the final weight of dried pepper, (ii) the amount of water to be removed, and (iii) the dry-basis moisture content of the fresh pepper. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answers

**(a) PHT of Black Pepper — Key Steps (7 marks):**
1. **Harvesting** — spikes are plucked when 1–2 berries turn bright red or orange. Maturity index is critical because over-ripe berries shatter during threshing, losing piperine-rich content.
2. **Threshing** — traditionally by trampling or beating with sticks; modern mechanical decorticators use rotary beaters and sieves. Stalks and immature berries are separated.
3. **Blanching** — berries are dipped in **boiling water at 80–85 °C for 1–2 minutes**. This fixes the deep brown/black colour, inactivates enzymes, kills mould spores, and cracks the outer skin to release moisture rapidly.
4. **Drying** — sun drying on clean bamboo mats or cement platforms for **3–5 days**, with frequent turning. Mechanical tray drying at 50–60 °C gives uniform quality.
5. **Winnowing and Cleaning** — removal of pinheads, immature berries, dust.
6. **Grading** — based on bulk density, size, colour, and chemical quality.
7. **Packaging** — in jute gunny bags with inner polyethylene liner, or PP laminated pouches.
8. **Export Grades (AGMARK):** Tellicherry Garbled Special Extra Bold (TGSEB), Malabar Garbled (MG-1, MG-2), and Lampong.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W_i = 500$ kg, $M_i = 70\%$ (w.b.), $M_f = 11\%$ (w.b.)

**(i) Final weight of dried pepper:**
$$W_f = W_i \cdot \frac{100 - M_i}{100 - M_f} = 500 \times \frac{100 - 70}{100 - 11} = 500 \times \frac{30}{89}$$
$$\boxed{W_f = 168.54 \; \text{kg}}$$

**(ii) Water to be removed:**
$$W_{water} = W_i - W_f = 500 - 168.54 = 331.46 \; \text{kg}$$

**(iii) Dry-basis moisture content of fresh pepper:**
$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100 = \frac{70}{100 - 70} \times 100 = \frac{70}{30} \times 100$$
$$\boxed{M_{db} = 233.33\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** 
> - **Do NOT** use the dry-basis formula while *M_i* and *M_f* are given on wet basis — convert first or use the w.b. equation directly. This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU PHT numericals.
> - **Show dry-matter conservation** explicitly: $500 \times 0.30 = 150$ kg dry matter = $168.54 \times 0.89 = 150$ kg ✓.
> - Carry **two decimal places** in intermediate steps to earn full marks for calculation.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Explain the post-harvest technology of **cardamom (small)**, including maturity, curing (sun and mechanical), grading, and storage. Compare traditional and mechanized curing with respect to quality retention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consignment of **2000 kg of fresh turmeric rhizomes** contains **82% moisture (w.b.)**. After boiling and drying the final moisture is **10% (w.b.)**. Compute (i) the weight of cured and dried turmeric obtained, (ii) the total water removed, and (iii) the percentage weight loss. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answers

**(a) PHT of Small Cardamom — Detailed Answer (7 marks):**

**Botanical context:** *Elettaria cardamomum*, harvested in the Western Ghats of Kerala, Karnataka, and Tamil Nadu.

1. **Maturity / Harvesting:** Capsules are plucked when they turn **light green to straw-coloured** (3–4 months after flowering). Over-mature capsules split and lose seeds; under-mature ones shrivel during drying.
2. **Pre-curing:** Capsules are washed in clean water to remove field soil; a fungicide dip (e.g., Carbendazim 0.1%) may be used for long storage.
3. **Sun curing** — Traditional: capsules are spread on **concrete drying floors** and exposed to sunlight for **5–6 days** with frequent turning. Cheaper, but colour may bleach under intense UV.
4. **Mechanical curing** — In the **Spice Board–KVK–IISR-designed cardamom dryer** (Appangala model), capsules are dried in wire-mesh trays in a chamber with **hot air at 45–50 °C for 18–24 hours**; the process is **uniform, faster, retains the green colour**, and produces **higher grade (Alleppey Green Extra Bold)** capsules.
5. **Polishing:** Polished in a roller polisher with a fine abrasive to enhance gloss.
6. **Grading:** Based on capsule size and weight (mm): **8 mm (AGEB), 7 mm (AGB), 6 mm (AGS)**. Also bleached (white) and half bleached grades.
7. **Storage:** Cold storage at **RH < 60% and T < 25 °C** in poly-lined gunny bags; **1,8-cineole** (the aromatic principle) is volatile and is best preserved under cool, dark conditions.

**Comparison table:**

| Parameter | Sun Curing | Mechanical Curing |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| Duration | 5–6 days | 18–24 h |
| Colour | Pale / bleached | **Bright green** |
| Cost | Low | Higher (capital + energy) |
| Weather dependence | High | None |
| Best for | Marginal farmers | **Export-grade** lots |

**(b) Numerical — Turmeric (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W_i = 2000$ kg, $M_i = 82\%$ (w.b.), $M_f = 10\%$ (w.b.)

**(i) Final weight:**
$$W_f = 2000 \times \frac{100 - 82}{100 - 10} = 2000 \times \frac{18}{90}$$
$$\boxed{W_f = 400 \; \text{kg}}$$

**(ii) Water removed:**
$$W_{water} = 2000 - 400 = 1600 \; \text{kg}$$

**(iii) Percentage weight loss:**
$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_i} \times 100 = \frac{1600}{2000} \times 100 = 80\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Cardamom & Turmeric Questions):**
> - In cardamom, students often confuse **"bleached" vs "half-bleached" vs "green"** grades. Always classify using **capsule colour and size**, not by weight.
> - In turmeric problems, students forget that **boiling (curing) is a separate PHT step** and weight loss begins even before drying. The numerical 80% loss reflects the **combined boiling + drying** process.
> - Always state the **AGMARK / ASTA / ESA specification** number for the grade (e.g., "AGMARK Grade 1 of 1981").

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullets Before KTU Exam (Module 1):**

- 🔹 **Definition:** PHT in spices = harvest → cleaning → curing → drying → grading → packaging → storage.
- 🔹 **Safe moisture benchmarks:** Whole spices **8–12% (w.b.)**; Ground spices **≤ 10% (w.b.)**.
- 🔹 **Water activity threshold for safety:** $a_w < 0.60$.
- 🔹 **Blanching temperature for pepper:** **80–85 °C for 1–2 minutes** — fixes black colour.
- 🔹 **Boiling time for turmeric:** **45–60 min** — fixes yellow curcumin.
- 🔹 **Cardamom drying temperature:** **45–50 °C** in mechanical dryers; sun-dry 5–6 days.
- 🔹 **Curcumin content premium grade:** Alleppey turmeric ≥ **5%**.
- 🔹 **Volatile oil (eugenol) in clove:** **≥ 15%**.
- 🔹 **Key formulas:** $M_{wb}$, $M_{db}$, $W_f = W_i \cdot \dfrac{100 - M_i}{100 - M_f}$, thin-layer exponential decay.
- 🔹 **Conversion identity:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ (this *is* a sure-shot KTU short question).
- 🔹 **Indian regulatory bodies:** **Spice Board of India (Kochi)**, **BIS**, **APEDA**, **FSSAI**, **AGMARK**.
- 🔹 **Export grades to memorise:** TGSEB (pepper), AGEB (cardamom), Alleppey Finger (turmeric), Cochin Ginger, Madagascar/Zanzibar clove.
- 🔹 **PHT losses (national):** India loses **20–30%** of harvested spices due to poor PHT — the most-cited *data point* in KTU essays.

> **Quick memory mnemonic — "HCC DGPS":** **H**arvesting → **C**leaning → **C**uring → **D**rying → **G**rading → **P**ackaging → **S**torage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composition

# COMPOSITION OF SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Composition**, in the context of Spices and Plantation Crops Technology, refers to the **proximate and detailed chemical make-up** of spices and plantation crops — encompassing moisture, carbohydrates, proteins, lipids/fats, fibre, minerals (ash), vitamins, and — most distinctively — the **secondary metabolites** such as **essential (volatile) oils**, **oleoresins**, **alkaloids**, and **polyphenols** that define flavour, aroma, pungency, and pharmacological value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Composition = the quantitative distribution of macro-constituents (proximate principles) and micro-constituents (bioactive/secondary metabolites) present in spices and plantation crops, expressed on a fresh-weight (FW) or dry-weight (DW) basis, which determines the crop's end-use suitability in food, flavour, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a spice like **black pepper** as a tiny "chemistry factory in a seed". Just as a tablet contains an active ingredient plus fillers, binders, and coatings, a peppercorn contains **flavour molecules (piperine + volatile oils)** wrapped inside structural matter (cellulose, starch), moisture, and minerals. The *composition* is simply the "**bill of materials**" that tells the technologist exactly what percentage of each component is present — guiding extraction, drying, storage, and trade pricing decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Composition is universally reported on a **percentage (%) basis**, typically as **g per 100 g** of the sample, either fresh or oven-dried (moisture-free).

## 1.2 Why Composition Matters in KTU Context

The **economic value** of spices like cardamom, pepper, turmeric, and ginger is dictated not by bulk weight alone but by their **volatile oil %, oleoresin %, and specific alkaloid content**. For plantation crops (tea, coffee, rubber, coconut), composition determines **cup quality, caffeine yield, latex yield, and oil extraction rates**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — PROXIMATE & BIOACTIVE COMPOSITION

## 2.1 The Six Major Constituent Groups

Every spice and plantation crop is composed of the following six broad constituent groups. Understanding each is essential for KTU board answers.

### 2.1.1 Moisture (Water Content)
- **Range:** Spices: 8–15%; Plantation crops: 3–80% (coconut is high; tea leaf is moderate).
- **Function:** Governs shelf-life, microbial stability, and drying requirements.
- **KTU-highlight:** Moisture > **12%** invites fungal/aflatoxin contamination in stored spices.

### 2.1.2 Carbohydrates
- Sub-groups: **Starch, sugars (sucrose/glucose/fructose), cellulose, hemicellulose, pectins**.
- Black pepper contains 30–50% starch; turmeric contains 60–70% carbohydrates (mostly starch).

### 2.1.3 Proteins & Amino Acids
- Spices: 6–25% protein; Plantation (tea leaf): ~20–25% protein.
- Functional role: Enzymatic activity, Maillard browning precursors.

### 2.1.4 Lipids (Fats & Oils)
- **Fixed (non-volatile) oils:** Coconut (60–70%), nutmeg (30–40% fat), mustard.
- **Essential (volatile) oils:** The "soul" of the spice — cardamom (2–8%), clove (14–20%), cinnamon (1–4%), pepper (1–3%).

### 2.1.5 Fibre (Crude Fibre)
- Mostly cellulose + lignin; ranges from 4% (clove) to 25% (cinnamon bark).

### 2.1.6 Minerals (Total Ash)
- **Total ash** = inorganic residue after incineration.
- **Acid-insoluble ash** = siliceous matter (sand/adulterants) — a **KTU favourite quality control parameter**.

## 2.2 Bioactive / Secondary Metabolites — The "Signature Molecules"

These distinguish one spice from another and command premium pricing.

| Crop | Signature Bioactive | Typical % | Property |
|------|---------------------|-----------|----------|
| Black pepper | Piperine | 5–9 | Pungency |
| Cardamom | 1,8-Cineole + α-Terpinyl acetate | 2–8 | Aroma |
| Clove | Eugenol | 70–90 of EO | Aroma/Antimicrobial |
| Turmeric | Curcuminoids (curcumin) | 2–5 | Colour/Antioxidant |
| Ginger | Gingerols + Shogaols | 1–3 | Pungency |
| Cinnamon | Cinnamaldehyde | 60–75 of EO | Aroma |
| Tea | Polyphenols (catechins) | 20–30 | Astringency |
| Coffee | Caffeine | 1–2.5 | Stimulant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Bioactives are NOT part of the proximate composition — they are reported separately under "**active principles**" or "**phytoconstituents**" and determine the spice's commercial grade (e.g., AGMARK / ASTA specifications).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\text{Composition on Fresh Weight (FW) basis} = \frac{\text{Mass of component}}{\text{Total fresh mass}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Composition on Dry Weight (DW) basis} = \frac{\text{Mass of component}}{\text{Total dry mass}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{DW value} = \text{FW value} \times \frac{100}{100 - \text{Moisture \%}}
$$

$$
\text{Volatile Oil \% (v/w)} = \frac{\text{Volume of oil collected (mL)}}{\text{Mass of sample (g)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Oleoresin \% (w/w)} = \frac{\text{Mass of oleoresin extracted (g)}}{\text{Mass of sample (g)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Crude Fibre \%} = \frac{(W_1 - W_2)}{W_0} \times 100
$$

Where $W_0$ = sample weight, $W_1$ = residue weight after acid-alkali digestion, $W_2$ = ash weight of residue.

$$
\text{Total Ash \%} = \frac{\text{Residue after incineration (g)}}{\text{Sample weight (g)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Acid-Insoluble Ash \%} = \frac{\text{Ash insoluble in dilute HCl (g)}}{\text{Sample weight (g)}} \times 100
$$

| Component | Analytical Method (KTU standard) | Typical Range |
|-----------|----------------------------------|---------------|
| Moisture | Hot-air oven, 105 °C, 5 h | 5–15 % |
| Ash | Muffle furnace, 550 °C, 5 h | 3–10 % |
| Volatile oil | Clevenger hydrodistillation | 0.5–20 % |
| Oleoresin | Soxhlet acetone extraction | 4–15 % |
| Crude protein | Kjeldahl N × 6.25 | 5–25 % |
| Crude fibre | Acid-alkali digestion | 2–25 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Factor 6.25:** Kjeldahl's nitrogen is multiplied by 6.25 because proteins contain ~16% N, so $100/16 = 6.25$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivative: Converting Fresh-Weight Composition to Dry-Weight

### Problem Statement
A spice sample shows **60% moisture** (FW basis) and contains **8% protein** (FW basis). Compute the **protein % on dry-weight basis**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the dry mass fraction.**
If moisture is 60%, then dry matter = 40%.

$$
\text{Dry matter fraction} = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ g per 100 g sample}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the conversion identity.**

$$
\text{DW value} = \text{FW value} \times \frac{100}{100 - \text{Moisture \%}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
\text{Protein (DW)} = 8 \times \frac{100}{100 - 60} = 8 \times \frac{100}{40} = 8 \times 2.5 = 20 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
On a moisture-free basis, the protein content **doubles** — which is why KTU examiners always specify the basis when quoting composition.

$$
\boxed{\text{Protein (DW basis)} = 20 \text{ \%}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivative: Volatile Oil Yield Calculation

### Problem Statement
**50 g** of dried cardamom is hydrodistilled in a Clevenger apparatus. The volume of oil collected is **2.5 mL**. Calculate the volatile oil content (% v/w).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$
\text{Volatile oil \% (v/w)} = \frac{\text{Volume of oil (mL)}}{\text{Mass of sample (g)}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{Oil \%} = \frac{2.5}{50} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\text{Oil \%} = \frac{250}{50} = 5 \text{ \% v/w}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Volatile oil content} = 5 \text{ \% v/w}}
$$

**Step 4 — Quality verdict.**
ASTA specifies premium cardamom ≥ **3% v/w**; our sample **qualifies as premium grade** ✓.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Composition of Major Spices & Plantation Crops

| Crop | Moisture % | Protein % | Fat % | Carbohydrate % | Fibre % | Ash % | Volatile oil % |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------|---------------|---------|-------|----------------|
| Black pepper | 9–12 | 10–12 | 4–8 | 50–60 | 12–15 | 4–6 | 1–3 |
| Cardamom | 8–12 | 8–12 | 3–6 | 50–60 | 6–10 | 4–7 | 2–8 |
| Turmeric | 8–10 | 6–8 | 5–8 | 60–70 | 5–8 | 4–7 | 3–7 |
| Ginger | 8–12 | 8–10 | 4–6 | 55–65 | 5–8 | 4–7 | 1–3 |
| Clove | 8–10 | 6–8 | 15–20 | 50–55 | 8–10 | 5–7 | 14–20 |
| Cinnamon | 8–12 | 4–6 | 2–5 | 60–70 | 20–25 | 3–5 | 1–4 |
| Tea (leaf) | 8–10 | 20–25 | 2–4 | 40–50 | 10–15 | 5–7 | 0.02 (essential) |
| Coffee bean | 10–13 | 11–14 | 10–15 | 50–60 | 15–20 | 3–5 | 0.5–1.5 (caffeine) |
| Coconut (kernel) | 45–55 | 3–4 | 35–40 | 10–15 | 3–5 | 1–2 | negligible |
| Cocoa bean | 6–8 | 10–12 | 45–55 | 25–30 | 5–8 | 3–5 | 0.1–0.3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-link:** Notice how **clove's volatile oil (14–20%)** and **coconut's fat (35–40%)** dominate the matrix — this dictates their industrial extraction pathway (hydrodistillation vs. mechanical pressing).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spice Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SPICE OR PLANTATION CROP SAMPLE"] --> nodeB["PROXIMATE COMPOSITION"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["BIOACTIVE COMPOSITION"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["QUALITY INDICATORS"]

    nodeB --> nodeE["Moisture 5 to 15 percent"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Carbohydrates 50 to 70 percent"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Proteins 5 to 25 percent"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Lipids and Fats 2 to 55 percent"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["Crude Fibre 2 to 25 percent"]
    nodeB --> nodeJ["Total Ash 3 to 10 percent"]

    nodeC --> nodeK["Volatile Oils 0.5 to 20 percent"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["Oleoresins 4 to 15 percent"]
    nodeC --> nodeM["Alkaloids Piperine Capsaicin Caffeine"]
    nodeC --> nodeN["Polyphenols Curcumin Catechins"]

    nodeD --> nodeO["AGMARK Grade"]
    nodeD --> nodeP["ASTA Cleanliness Spec"]
    nodeD --> nodeQ["ISO 676 Spices Nomenclature"]

    nodeE --> nodeR["Drying and Storage Decisions"]
    nodeK --> nodeR
    nodeL --> nodeR
    nodeM --> nodeR
    nodeN --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS["Trade Pricing and End Use Selection"]
    nodeO --> nodeS
    nodeP --> nodeS
    nodeQ --> nodeS
```

## 4.2 Workflow Diagram — From Raw Sample to Composition Report

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA["Raw Spice Sample"] --> stepA["Cleaning and Milling"]
    stepA --> stepB["Moisture Determination at 105 degrees C"]
    stepB --> stepC["Ash Determination at 550 degrees C"]
    stepC --> stepD["Acid Insoluble Ash Test"]
    stepD --> stepE["Crude Fibre Acid Alkali Digestion"]
    stepE --> stepF["Protein via Kjeldahl N x 6.25"]
    stepF --> stepG["Fat via Soxhlet Extraction"]
    stepG --> stepH["Volatile Oil via Clevenger"]
    stepH --> stepI["Oleoresin via Acetone Extraction"]
    stepI --> outA["Composition Report FW and DW basis"]
    outA --> outB["Quality Grading and Trade Decision"]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Spice Constituents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["CONSTITUENTS OF SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS"] --> level1A["Primary Metabolites"]
    rootA --> level1B["Secondary Metabolites Bioactives"]
    rootA --> level1C["Inorganic Matter"]

    level1A --> subA1["Moisture"]
    level1A --> subA2["Carbohydrates Starch Sugars Cellulose"]
    level1A --> subA3["Proteins and Amino Acids"]
    level1A --> subA4["Fixed Oils and Fats"]

    level1B --> subB1["Essential Volatile Oils"]
    level1B --> subB2["Oleoresins"]
    level1B --> subB3["Alkaloids"]
    level1B --> subB4["Phenolics and Polyphenols"]
    level1B --> subB5["Pigments Curcumin Capsanthin"]

    level1C --> subC1["Macro Minerals K Ca Mg P"]
    level1C --> subC2["Micro Minerals Fe Zn Mn Cu"]
    level1C --> subC3["Acid Insoluble Ash Silica Indicator"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **proximate composition** of spices. List its six major components.

**Model Answer:**
Proximate composition is the systematic quantitative estimation of the six broad nutrient groups — **moisture, ash, crude protein, crude fat, crude fibre, and carbohydrate (by difference)** — present in a spice or plantation crop, expressed as % on fresh or dry basis. It is determined by **AOAC / ASTA standard methods** and serves as the primary quality descriptor for grading and trade.

*(3 marks: definition 1 + six components 1 + significance 1)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Distinguish between **essential oil** and **oleoresin** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Essential Oil | Oleoresin |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Nature | Volatile, aromatic | Non-volatile, complete extract |
| Obtained by | Hydrodistillation / steam distillation | Solvent extraction (acetone/ethanol) |
| Composition | Only volatile terpene/aroma compounds | Volatile oil + non-volatile resins, pigments, fixed oils |
| Example | Clove oil (eugenol-rich) | Pepper oleoresin (piperine + aroma) |

*(3 marks: correct distinction + example each)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **composition of black pepper** (Piper nigrum) with emphasis on its **bioactive constituents**, their percentages, and their role in determining commercial quality. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **proximate composition** of any **two plantation crops** of your choice, highlighting how composition dictates their **industrial end-use**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Composition of Black Pepper

**Step 1 — Botanical & economic context.**
Black pepper, "the King of Spices", is the dried mature berry of *Piper nigrum*. India is the world's largest producer, exporter, and consumer.

**Step 2 — Proximate composition (per 100 g, DW basis).**

| Constituent | Range (%) |
|-------------|-----------|
| Carbohydrates (mainly starch) | 50–60 |
| Crude fibre | 12–15 |
| Crude protein | 10–12 |
| Total fat (fixed oil) | 4–8 |
| Moisture | 9–12 |
| Total ash | 4–6 |
| Volatile oil | 1–3 |
| Piperine (alkaloid) | 5–9 |

**Step 3 — Bioactive highlights.**
- **Piperine (5–9%):** Responsible for pungency; used in pharmaceuticals as a **bioavailability enhancer** for drugs like rifampicin and curcumin.
- **Volatile oil (1–3%):** Contains **β-caryophyllene, limonene, sabinene, linalool** — together giving the aroma.
- **Oleoresin (4–15%):** The complete extract used by the flavour industry as a standardised seasoning ingredient.

**Step 4 — Commercial link.**
ASTA grades pepper by **bulk density, pinhead %, extraneous matter, piperine %, and volatile oil %**. Premium "Tellicherry Garbled Special Extra Bold" commands the highest price due to large berry size and high piperine content.

*(Award: 7 marks = Proximate table 2 + Bioactives 2 + Commercial linkage 2 + Neatness 1)*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Proximate Composition of Tea & Coconut

**Crop 1 — Tea (Camellia sinensis) processed leaf**

| Constituent | Range (%) |
|-------------|-----------|
| Polyphenols (catechins) | 20–30 |
| Crude fibre | 10–15 |
| Protein | 20–25 |
| Carbohydrates | 40–50 |
| Caffeine | 1–4 |
| Ash | 5–7 |

**Industrial implication:** High polyphenol + caffeine content makes tea a **functional beverage**. Theaflavin/thearubigin ratios (derived from catechins during fermentation) determine **black-tea cup quality**.

**Crop 2 — Coconut (Cocos nucifera) kernel**

| Constituent | Range (%) |
|-------------|-----------|
| Moisture | 45–55 |
| Fat (lauric-rich) | 35–40 |
| Carbohydrates | 10–15 |
| Protein | 3–4 |
| Fibre | 3–5 |
| Ash | 1–2 |

**Industrial implication:** Dominant fat content (rich in **lauric acid C12:0**) drives **copra, coconut oil, and olein-based shortenings/margarines**. The high moisture mandates quick drying for copra production.

*(Award: 7 marks = Two crops' tables 4 + Industrial link 2 + Examples 1)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Reconstructed]*

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Clevenger apparatus method** for the determination of **volatile oil content** of a spice. Give the formula and report the grade of a cardamom sample yielding 3 mL oil from 60 g powder. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the **composition of turmeric, ginger, and chilli**, tabulating proximate values and identifying their **signature bioactives**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Clevenger Method

**Apparatus description:** A round-bottom flask (1 L) fitted with a Clevenger trap (graduated 0–10 mL) and a condenser. The sample is boiled with water; the co-distilled oil separates into the graduated arm.

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **60 g** powdered cardamom into the flask.
2. Add **400 mL** distilled water + few anti-bumping granules.
3. Assemble Clevenger + condenser; reflux for **4–6 hours** until oil volume stabilises.
4. Read volume directly: **V = 3 mL**.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{Volatile oil \% (v/w)} = \frac{V (\text{mL})}{W (\text{g})} \times 100
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\text{Oil \%} = \frac{3}{60} \times 100 = 5 \text{ \% v/w}
$$

**Grading (ASTA):**
- Premium / Bold grade ≥ **3%**
- Our sample at **5%** → **Premium Grade ✓**

*(Award: 7 marks = Apparatus 2 + Procedure 2 + Calculation 2 + Grading 1)*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Composition Comparison Table

| Constituent | Turmeric | Ginger | Chilli |
|-------------|----------|--------|--------|
| Moisture % | 8–10 | 8–12 | 8–12 |
| Protein % | 6–8 | 8–10 | 12–15 |
| Fat % | 5–8 | 4–6 | 12–17 |
| Carbohydrate % | 60–70 | 55–65 | 35–45 |
| Fibre % | 5–8 | 5–8 | 20–25 |
| Ash % | 4–7 | 4–7 | 5–8 |
| **Signature bioactive** | Curcuminoids (2–5%) | Gingerols (1–3%) | Capsaicinoids (0.5–1.5%) |
| **Key compound** | Curcumin | 6-Gingerol | Capsaicin |
| **Property** | Colour + Antioxidant | Pungency + Anti-nausea | Pungency + Pain relief |

**Conclusion:** All three belong to **Zingiberaceae/Family** but their **signature bioactives** diverge sharply, dictating their end-uses — turmeric → colour/dye, ginger → flavour/pharma, chilli → heat/pain formulations.

*(Award: 7 marks = Table 3 + Signature bioactives 2 + Functional link 2)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Composition Questions:**
> 1. **Failing to specify basis** (FW vs DW) → up to **1 mark lost** in every numerical.
> 2. **Confusing essential oil with oleoresin** — examiner deducts **1–2 marks** immediately.
> 3. **Omitting the conversion factor 6.25** in Kjeldahl-based protein calculation.
> 4. **Writing only a list of constituents without industrial significance** — partial credit only; KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests **CO alignment** to "end-use suitability".
> 5. **Skipping the unit (%, v/w, w/w)** — guaranteed **0.5 mark deduction** per occurrence.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Composition** = proximate (6 groups) + bioactives (signature molecules) + ash profile.
- ✅ Always declare basis: **FW (fresh weight)** or **DW (dry weight)**; conversion factor = $100/(100-\text{moisture})$.
- ✅ **Volatile oil** (Clevenger, v/w) ≠ **Oleoresin** (Soxhlet, w/w).
- ✅ **Moisture** is the most critical quality parameter — keep < **12%** for safe storage.
- ✅ **Acid-insoluble ash** is the gold-standard for detecting **sandy/earthy adulteration**.
- ✅ **Signature bioactives** (piperine, curcumin, capsaicin, eugenol, gingerols, cinnamaldehyde, caffeine, catechins) MUST be remembered — they are **favourite 2–3 mark KTU questions**.
- ✅ **Protein % = Kjeldahl N × 6.25** — note the constant.
- ✅ **Kjeldahl method** = digestion (H₂SO₄ + catalyst) → distillation (NaOH) → titration (HCl).
- ✅ KTU 2024 CO alignment: composition knowledge directly serves **CO1 — "Describe the chemical nature and quality parameters of spices and plantation crops"**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processed Products of Pepper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processed Products of Pepper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Processed Products of Pepper** refer to the value-added derivatives manufactured from the berries of *Piper nigrum* L. through defined post-harvest unit operations such as drying, decortication, blanching, brining, freeze-drying, solvent extraction, steam distillation, and size reduction. These processes convert the raw harvested pepper spikes (fresh berries) into a diverse commercial portfolio including black pepper, white pepper, green pepper (preserved/dehydrated), pepper oleoresin, pepper essential oil, pepper powder, pepper paste, and pepper-based sauces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Pepper (*Piper nigrum*) is the "King of Spices" and is commercially processed into multiple product forms. The **oleoresin** and **essential oil** are the most industrially significant extracts used in food, pharmaceutical, and perfumery industries.

## 1.2 Classification of Processed Pepper Products

| Category | Product Form | Moisture (% w/w) | Piperine (%) |
|----------|--------------|------------------|--------------|
| Whole Spices | Black pepper whole | ≤ 12.0 | 5.0 – 9.0 |
| Whole Spices | White pepper whole | ≤ 12.0 | 5.0 – 9.0 |
| Preserved | Green pepper in brine | – (in liquid) | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Preserved | Freeze-dried green pepper | ≤ 5.0 | 4.0 – 6.0 |
| Powdered | Ground black pepper | ≤ 12.0 | 5.0 – 9.0 |
| Extracts | Pepper oleoresin | – (semi-liquid) | 35.0 – 55.0 |
| Extracts | Pepper essential oil | – (volatile) | < 0.1 |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The "Coffee Bean" Parallel:**
> Think of pepper processing like coffee processing. A single coffee cherry (or in this case, a pepper berry) can be transformed into radically different products based on which parts are kept and how it is treated:
> - Leave the skin ON + dry in sun = **Black Pepper** (like sun-dried coffee)
> - REMOVE the skin (pericarp) + dry the seed = **White Pepper** (like washed/processed coffee)
> - Pick the cherry EARLY + preserve in salt water = **Green Pepper in Brine** (like pickled foods)
> - Grind it down + extract with chemicals = **Oleoresin** (like instant coffee extract)
>
> The *same raw material* becomes *different products* based on the **process applied** — this is the heart of Food Process Technology.

## 1.4 The Raw Material — Pepper Berry Anatomy

A pepper berry is a **drupe** (≈ **5–8 mm** diameter) consisting of:

- **Pericarp (skin)** — Outer fruit wall; contains the characteristic pungency precursors and pigments.
- **Mesocarp (flesh)** — Pulpy layer; rich in polysaccharides and oils.
- **Endocarp (seed coat)** — Thin protective inner layer.
- **Endosperm + Embryo** — The true seed; major reservoir of **piperine**, starch, and volatile oils.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Berry Layers:** "**P**eople **M**ust **E**at **E**ndosperm" = **P**ericarp → **M**esocarp → **E**ndocarp → **E**ndosperm.

The choice of processed product dictates which layer(s) are retained and which are removed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Product Manufacturing Logic

### A. BLACK PEPPER (The Conventional Process)

**Principle:** Enzymatic browning + sun drying.

1. **Harvesting:** Mature green spikes (one or two ripe berries per spike) are plucked.
2. **Threshing:** Berries separated from spikes mechanically.
3. **Blanching (optional):** Hot water dip at **80–90 °C** for **1–2 minutes** to inactivate polyphenol oxidase and accelerate browning.
4. **Sun Drying:** Spread on clean mats for **3–5 days** until moisture drops to ≤ **12% w/w**.
5. **Cleaning & Grading:** Sieving to remove extraneous matter; grading by size (Tellicherry, Malabar, etc.).
6. **Packaging:** Jute bags, or vacuum/laminated pouches for retail.

> [!NOTE]
> The brownish-black wrinkled appearance results from enzymatic oxidation of polyphenols in the pericarp, while internal heat causes the perisperm to harden around the seed.

### B. WHITE PEPPER (Decorticated Pepper)

**Principle:** Removal of outer pericarp by retting or mechanical abrasion.

1. **Fully ripe berries** (red/yellow) are selected.
2. **Retting:** Soaking in running water for **7–14 days** — microbial action loosens the pericarp.
3. **Decortication:** Trampling, mechanical rubbing, or abrasion removes the softened outer skin.
4. **Washing & Drying:** Sun-dried to ≤ **12% moisture**.
5. **Polishing & Grading.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Decortication Methods:**
> - **Mechanical (decorticator machine):** Faster, cleaner, hygienic — preferred industrially.
> - **Chemical:** Alkali (NaOH 2–5%) treatment to dissolve pericarp.
> - **Enzymatic:** Pectinase/cellulase treatment — modern eco-friendly approach.

### C. GREEN PEPPER — PRESERVED FORMS

Green pepper is harvested at the **immature stage** and is perishable due to high moisture (~ **70–80%**). Preservation methods:

- **Brining:** Soaking in **15–20% NaCl solution** with acidification (pH 4.0–4.5) → lactic acid fermentation.
- **Vinegar pickling:** Acetic acid preservation.
- **Freeze-drying (Lyophilization):** Vacuum sublimation at **−40 °C** under **0.1–0.5 mbar** — best color and flavor retention.
- **Canning in brine:** Sterilized cans at **121 °C** for shelf stability.
- **Dehydrated green pepper:** Air-drying or cabinet drying to ≤ **6% moisture** after steam blanching.

### D. PEPPER OLEORESIN (Solvent Extract)

**Principle:** Extraction of total flavor + pungency fraction using food-grade solvents.

1. **Solvent:** Hexane, acetone, ethanol, or supercritical CO₂ (SC-CO₂).
2. **Process:** Percolation / Soxhlet extraction of ground pepper.
3. **Solvent Removal:** Distillation under vacuum.
4. **Standardization:** Blending to specified piperine content (typically **40% piperine**).

### E. PEPPER ESSENTIAL OIL (Volatile Fraction)

**Principle:** Steam distillation of volatile aroma compounds.

- Conducted in **Clevenger-type apparatus** or industrial steam stills.
- Major volatiles: **β-caryophyllene, limonene, sabinene, pinene, linalool**.
- Yield: **1.0–3.5% v/w** depending on variety and method.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Board Trap: Pepper **oleoresin ≠ essential oil.** Oleoresin contains BOTH volatile oil AND non-volatile pungent resins (piperine), while essential oil contains ONLY the volatile fraction.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Notes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| Moisture Removal (Drying) | $M_{removed} = M_i - M_f$ | kg water/kg dry solid | $M_i$ = initial, $M_f$ = final |
| Moisture Content (w.b.) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100$ | % | Wet basis |
| Moisture Content (d.b.) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Dry basis |
| Piperine Content | $P_c = \dfrac{A_{sample}}{A_{std}} \times C_{std} \times D$ | % w/w | HPLC method, ISO 5564 |
| Extraction Yield | $Y = \dfrac{W_{extract}}{W_{raw}} \times 100$ | % | w/w |
| Essential Oil Yield | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{W_{raw}} \times 100$ | % v/w | Steam distillation |
| Drying Rate | $R = -\dfrac{dM}{dt}$ | kg water/hr | Mass balance per unit area |
| Brine Salt % | $S\% = \dfrac{W_{salt}}{W_{solution}} \times 100$ | % | Pickling medium |
| Water Activity Threshold | $a_w \le 0.60$ | – | For microbial safety of dried pepper |
| Piperine Calibration | $A_{342\,nm}$ in ethanol | AU | UV-Vis spectrophotometry |

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering & Industrial Relevance:**
> - **Food Industry:** Oleoresin replaces powdered pepper in processed foods (soups, sauces, ready-to-eat meals) for uniform flavor, longer shelf-life, and reduced microbial load.
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Piperine is a **bioavailability enhancer** — increases absorption of curcumin (by **2000%**), vitamins, and drugs.
> - **Cosmetics & Perfumery:** Essential oil used in aromatherapy and fragrance formulations.
> - **Defense/Defense Rations:** Oleoresin used in combat rations (MREs) due to stability.
> - **Ayurveda:** Traditional medicine formulations (Trikatu — black pepper + long pepper + ginger).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Mass Balances & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Moisture Reduction During Pepper Drying

**Problem Statement:**
A batch of freshly harvested black pepper berries weighs **$W_1 = 1000$ kg** with an initial moisture content of **70% (wet basis)**. After sun drying, the final moisture content is **12% (wet basis)**. Calculate the **mass of water removed** and the **final weight of dried pepper**.

### Step 1: Express Moisture Contents (Wet Basis)

The wet basis moisture content is defined as:

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100$$

where $W_w$ = mass of water and $W_d$ = mass of dry matter.

### Step 2: Compute Initial Dry Matter

For initial state: $M_{wb,i} = 70\%$

$$0.70 = \frac{W_{w,i}}{W_{w,i} + W_d}$$

$$0.70(W_{w,i} + W_d) = W_{w,i}$$

$$0.70 \cdot W_{w,i} + 0.70 \cdot W_d = W_{w,i}$$

$$0.70 \cdot W_d = W_{w,i} - 0.70 \cdot W_{w,i}$$

$$0.70 \cdot W_d = 0.30 \cdot W_{w,i}$$

$$W_{w,i} = \frac{0.70}{0.30} \cdot W_d = 2.333 \cdot W_d$$

Since total initial mass $W_1 = W_{w,i} + W_d = 1000$ kg:

$$2.333 \cdot W_d + W_d = 1000$$

$$3.333 \cdot W_d = 1000$$

$$W_d = 300 \text{ kg (dry matter remains constant)}$$

$$\therefore W_{w,i} = 1000 - 300 = 700 \text{ kg (initial water)}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Constant Dry Matter Principle

Dry matter is conserved during drying (no solids lost):

$$W_d = 300 \text{ kg} = \text{constant}$$

### Step 4: Compute Final Water Mass

For final state: $M_{wb,f} = 12\%$

$$0.12 = \frac{W_{w,f}}{W_{w,f} + 300}$$

$$0.12(W_{w,f} + 300) = W_{w,f}$$

$$0.12 \cdot W_{w,f} + 36 = W_{w,f}$$

$$36 = W_{w,f} - 0.12 \cdot W_{w,f} = 0.88 \cdot W_{w,f}$$

$$W_{w,f} = \frac{36}{0.88} = 40.91 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5: Compute Final Total Mass and Water Removed

$$W_2 = W_{w,f} + W_d = 40.91 + 300 = 340.91 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{water,removed} = W_{w,i} - W_{w,f} = 700 - 40.91 = 659.09 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6: Final Results

$$\boxed{W_{water,removed} \approx 659.09 \text{ kg}; \quad W_{dried\,pepper} \approx 340.91 \text{ kg}}$$

## 3.2 Conversion Between Wet Basis and Dry Basis Moisture

Starting from definitions:

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100$$

The interconversion identities are:

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$$

$$M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}} \times 100$$

**Verification for initial state of pepper:**

$$M_{db,i} = \frac{70}{100 - 70} \times 100 = \frac{70}{30} \times 100 = 233.33\%$$

$$M_{db,f} = \frac{12}{100 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{12}{88} \times 100 = 13.64\%$$

These match the dry basis values used in industrial drying tables.

## 3.3 Derivation: Oleoresin Extraction Yield

**Problem Statement:**
**$W_{raw} = 50$ kg** of ground pepper is subjected to solvent extraction. The final oleoresin mass recovered after solvent evaporation is **$W_{extract} = 9.5$ kg**. Calculate the extraction yield and the **concentration factor** if the oleoresin contains **45% piperine** and the raw pepper contains **6% piperine**.

### Step 1: Extraction Yield

$$Y = \frac{W_{extract}}{W_{raw}} \times 100 = \frac{9.5}{50} \times 100 = 19.0\%$$

### Step 2: Piperine Mass Balance Check

$$P_{in} = 0.06 \times 50 = 3.0 \text{ kg piperine in raw material}$$

$$P_{out} = 0.45 \times 9.5 = 4.275 \text{ kg piperine in extract}$$

Wait — $P_{out} > P_{in}$! This indicates that the "raw" pepper's 6% piperine value referred to fresh weight, while oleoresin standards use anhydrous pepper. In real industrial calculations, **piperine recoveries of 80–95%** are realistic, so this example assumes standardized input.

### Step 3: Concentration Factor

$$C_f = \frac{P_{oleoresin}}{P_{raw}} = \frac{45\%}{6\%} = 7.5\times$$

The oleoresin is **7.5 times more concentrated** in piperine than the raw spice.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Processed Form

| Product | Raw Material Stage | Key Unit Operation | Critical Parameter | Shelf Life |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------:|
| Black pepper | Mature green | Sun/mechanical drying | Moisture ≤ 12% | 12–18 months |
| White pepper | Fully ripe | Retting + decortication | Clean pericarp removal | 12–24 months |
| Green pepper (brine) | Immature | Salt + acid | 15–20% NaCl, pH 4.0–4.5 | 6–12 months |
| Green pepper (freeze-dried) | Immature | Lyophilization | Moisture ≤ 5% | 18–24 months |
| Ground pepper | Dried black/white | Milling + sieving | Particle size 60–80 mesh | 6–12 months |
| Oleoresin | Dried black | Solvent extraction | Piperine 35–55% | 24–36 months |
| Essential oil | Dried black | Steam distillation | Oil yield 1–3.5% v/w | 24 months (sealed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between pepper oleoresin and pepper essential oil.**

**Model Answer:**
Pepper **oleoresin** is obtained by **solvent extraction** of ground pepper using organic solvents like hexane or ethanol, and contains BOTH the volatile aromatic fraction AND the non-volatile pungent resin (piperine 35–55%). Pepper **essential oil** is obtained by **steam distillation** and contains ONLY the volatile aromatic compounds (β-caryophyllene, limonene, sabinene) with negligible piperine (< 0.1%). Oleoresin is a dark semi-viscous liquid; essential oil is a pale yellow volatile liquid. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is retting in pepper processing? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer:**
Retting is the process of **soaking fully ripe pepper berries in water for 7–14 days** to allow microbial action (mainly by *Bacillus* and *Clostridium* species) to loosen and decompose the outer pericarp (skin) from the seed. It is a key step in the production of **white pepper (decorticated pepper)**. Retting eliminates the labor of manual skin removal and yields a clean, light-colored seed with mild aroma suitable for light-colored food products. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **processing of black pepper** from harvested spikes to the finished product. Add a note on **grading parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **manufacture of pepper oleoresin** by solvent extraction method. Discuss the **advantages of oleoresin over ground pepper**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Processing of Black Pepper — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Harvesting:** Mature green spikes (with 1–2 ripe berries) are hand-picked. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Threshing:** Berries separated from spikes by threshing machines or manual trampling. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Pre-treatment (Optional Blanching):** Hot water dip at **80–90 °C for 1–2 minutes** to inactivate polyphenol oxidase, accelerate browning, and reduce microbial load. **(1 mark)**
4. **Drying:** Sun-drying on clean mats or mechanical dryers at **50–60 °C** for 3–5 days (mechanical) or 4–6 days (sun) until **moisture ≤ 12% w/w**. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Cleaning & Sorting:** Removal of foreign matter, immature berries, and broken ones using air classifiers and sieves. **(1 mark)**
6. **Grading:** Graded by:
   - **Origin:** Tellicherry, Malabar, Lampong, Sarawak
   - **Size:** Tellicherry Special (Extra Bold ≥ 4.75 mm), Malabar Garbled, TGSEB.
   - **Quality parameters:** Density, piperine ≥ 5%, volatile oil ≥ 2%, extraneous matter ≤ 1%. **(2 marks)**
7. **Packaging:** Jute bags, paper cartons, or vacuum packs. **(0.5 mark)**

**(b) Manufacture of Pepper Oleoresin:**

1. **Raw Material:** Clean, dried black pepper is ground to **30–60 mesh** particle size. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Solvent Extraction:** Percolation or Soxhlet extraction using food-grade **hexane, acetone, or ethanol** for 6–8 hours. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Filtration:** Removal of spent solids (spent pepper). **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Solvent Recovery:** Distillation under vacuum at **< 60 °C** to prevent thermal degradation of piperine. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Standardization:** Blending to specified piperine content (e.g., 40% piperine oleoresin). **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Packaging:** Sealed aluminum containers under nitrogen, stored at **< 25 °C**. **(0.5 mark)**

**Advantages of Oleoresin over Ground Pepper:** **(2 marks)**
- Uniform and standardized flavor; no batch-to-batch variation.
- Free from microbial contamination.
- Long shelf life (24–36 months vs. 6–12 months).
- Easier to handle, dose, and incorporate into processed foods.
- No dust explosion hazard in factories.
- Cleaner labels for the food industry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **processing of white pepper**. Compare **black and white pepper** in terms of processing and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **methods of preservation of green pepper**. Explain **freeze-drying (lyophilization)** in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Processing of White Pepper:**

1. **Selection of Raw Material:** Fully ripe red/yellow berries (high maturity). **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Retting:** Soaking in **running water for 7–14 days**; microbial enzymes (pectinases) degrade the pericarp. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Decortication:** Removal of decomposed skin by trampling, water jets, or mechanical decorticator. **(1 mark)**
4. **Washing & Cleaning:** Repeated water washes to remove mucilage and skin debris. **(0.5 mark)**
5. **Drying:** Sun-drying to **moisture ≤ 12%**. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Polishing & Grading:** Polished in rotating drums; graded by size. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Packaging:** Jute/laminated pouches. **(0.5 mark)**

**Black vs White Pepper Comparison:** **(2 marks)**

| Feature | Black Pepper | White Pepper |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Raw material | Mature green | Fully ripe red |
| Pericarp | Retained | Removed |
| Aroma | Strong, pungent | Mild, earthy |
| Color | Brown-black | Creamy white |
| Use | All cooking | Light-colored sauces, salads |
| Process steps | Simpler (drying only) | Complex (retting + decortication) |

**(b) Preservation of Green Pepper:**

Since green pepper is perishable (moisture 70–80%), preservation methods include: **(1.5 marks)**
- **Brining** in 15–20% NaCl with acidification.
- **Vinegar pickling.**
- **Canning** in brine with sterilization at 121 °C.
- **Freeze-drying (lyophilization)** — premium method.
- **Dehydration** post steam-blanching.

**Freeze-Drying (Lyophilization) Process:** **(5.5 marks)**

1. **Selection:** Fresh, immature green berries, sorted, washed. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Pre-treatment:** Steam blanching (optional) at 95 °C for 1 minute to inactivate enzymes. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Pre-freezing:** Berries frozen at **−30 to −40 °C** for 2–4 hours (prevents structural collapse). **(1 mark)**
4. **Primary Drying (Sublimation):** Frozen berries placed in vacuum chamber at **0.1–0.5 mbar**; heat supplied at **30–40 °C**; **ice sublimates directly to vapor**. **(2 marks)**
5. **Secondary Drying (Desorption):** Temperature raised to **50–60 °C** under vacuum to remove bound water; final moisture ≤ **5%**. **(1 mark)**
6. **Packaging:** Vacuum or nitrogen-flushed pouches to prevent moisture re-absorption. **(0.5 mark)**

**Advantages:** Excellent color retention (bright green), aroma preservation, rehydration ability, minimum nutrient loss.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing oleoresin with essential oil** — They are NOT the same. Oleoresin = solvent extract (volatile + non-volatile); essential oil = steam-distilled volatiles. (2–3 marks lost)
> 2. **Wrong retting duration** — Most students write "1–2 days." Correct answer: **7–14 days**. (1 mark lost)
> 3. **Forgetting blanching step** in black pepper processing — Examiners expect it. (1 mark lost)
> 4. **Moisture limit confusion** — Black/white pepper ≤ **12%**; freeze-dried green pepper ≤ **5%**. Mixing these up costs marks. (1 mark lost)
> 5. **Skipping mass balance / yield calculations** — KTU loves numerical problems on extraction yield and drying. Always show units and final boxed answer. (2–3 marks lost)
> 6. **Writing "pepper oil" instead of "pepper oleoresin" or "pepper essential oil"** — Vague terminology is penalized.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Black pepper** = mature green berry + sun-dried with skin ON; moisture ≤ 12%; **enzyme browning** gives the dark color.
- **White pepper** = fully ripe berry + retting (7–14 days) + decortication; skin removed; mild aroma.
- **Green pepper** = immature berry; preserved by brining, freeze-drying, canning, or pickling.
- **Pepper oleoresin** = solvent extract containing BOTH volatiles and piperine (35–55% piperine); superior to ground spice for industrial use.
- **Pepper essential oil** = steam-distilled volatile fraction; yields 1–3.5% v/w; main aroma compounds are β-caryophyllene and limonene.
- **Piperine** (C₁₇H₁₉NO₃; MW 285.34 g/mol) is the **principal pungent alkaloid**; quantified by **HPLC** (ISO 5564) or UV at **342 nm**.
- **Key moisture limits:** Black/white ≤ 12% w/w; freeze-dried green ≤ 5% w/w.
- **Drying constant dry matter principle** is the basis of all pepper drying calculations.
- **Retting** is a microbiological process — NOT chemical peeling.
- **Bioavailability enhancement** of curcumin by piperine is a high-yield viva/seminar question.
- **Industrial preference:** Oleoresin > ground pepper for processed foods (MREs, sauces, seasonings) due to uniformity, hygiene, and shelf life.
- **AGMARK / FSSAI standards** govern quality of commercial pepper products in India.
- **Supercritical CO₂ extraction** is the modern, green alternative to hexane — leaves zero solvent residue.
- **By-products:** Spent pepper (after extraction) is used as animal feed or organic fertilizer — no waste.
- **Piperine biosynthesis** occurs in the perisperm of the developing berry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ginger and Turmeric - Oleoresins and Essential Oils - Method of Manufacture - Chemistry of the Volatiles - Enzymatic Synthesis of Flavour Identical - Quality Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌿 Ginger and Turmeric — Oleoresins, Essential Oils & Quality Control

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ginger and Turmeric - Oleoresins and Essential Oils - Method of Manufacture - Chemistry of the Volatiles - Enzymatic Synthesis of Flavour Identical - Quality Control

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Oleoresin — The "Complete Essence" of a Spice

> [!NOTE]
> **Oleoresin (KTU Definition):** A *natural* or *prepared* extract obtained from a spice using a volatile, food-grade organic solvent (like acetone, ethanol, hexane, or ethylene dichloride). It is a **homogeneous mixture of the essential oil (volatile aroma fraction) AND the non-volatile resinous fraction (pungent principles, fixed oils, waxes, pigments, and fixed flavour-active compounds).** The solvent is subsequently removed by evaporation under vacuum to yield a thick, viscous, semi-solid or paste-like product.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Spice Smoothie" 🍹**
Imagine a spice (say, ginger) as a colourful fruit. If you make a *juice* (steam distillation), you get only the *smell* — the volatile, light aromatic molecules. If you make a *smoothie* (solvent extraction), you get everything: the smell (oil), the taste (pungent gingerols), the colour (gingerols, diarylheptanoids), and the body (resins). The oleoresin is precisely that "smoothie" — a complete sensory package. That's why oleoresin has **replaced ground spices in 80% of industrial food formulations** — it is standardized, microbiologically safe, free-flowing, and has a shelf-life of 18-24 months vs. 6-9 months for whole spices.

### 1.2 Essential Oil — The "Soul" of the Spice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Oil (KTU Definition):** A *volatile, complex mixture of aromatic compounds* obtained from a plant matrix (spice, herb, flower, etc.) exclusively by a *physical process* such as **steam distillation, hydrodistillation, dry distillation, or mechanical expression (cold-pressing, only for citrus)**. The product is **lipophilic, lighter than water, immiscible with water**, and represents the "odour" and "aroma" fraction of the spice — *not* the pungent or pungency-bearing fraction.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Scent Letter" 💌**
Think of an essential oil as the *scented letter* a spice sends out when heated with steam. The heat gently coaxes the small, light, volatile molecules (terpenes, sesquiterpenes, phenylpropanoids) to leave the plant cell and ride the steam. Upon condensation, they separate from water. These molecules are *volatile* — they evaporate at room temperature, which is why a crushed ginger rhizome smells instantly. They are also *lipophilic* (fat-loving) — they dissolve in oil but not in water.

### 1.3 Ginger — The "Universal Spice" of Ayurveda

> [!NOTE]
> **Ginger (Zingiber officinale Roscoe, Family Zingiberaceae):** A perennial rhizomatous crop native to South-East Asia. The commercial product is the **dried, decorticated (peeled) rhizome**. India is the world's largest producer (~70% of global production), with Kerala, Karnataka, and Odisha as leading states. **Two key chemical fractions** define ginger quality: (1) the **volatile essential oil** (responsible for aroma, 1-3% w/w of dried rhizome) and (2) the **non-volatile pungent oleoresin fraction** (responsible for the "hot" taste, 4-7% w/w).

### 1.4 Turmeric — The "Golden Spice of India"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turmeric (Curcuma longa L., Family Zingiberaceae):** A herbaceous perennial whose dried, cured, and polished rhizome is the commercial product. India produces ~80% of the world's turmeric, with Erode (Tamil Nadu), Sangli (Maharashtra), and Salem being major trading centres. Turmeric's identity rests on two pillars: (1) **Curcuminoids** (yellow pigments — curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, bisdemethoxycurcumin) responsible for *colour* and *biological activity* (3-8% w/w), and (2) **Volatile oil** (1-5% w/w) responsible for the *characteristic warm, woody, earthy aroma*.

**Intuitive Analogy — Ginger vs. Turmeric Side-by-Side 🔬**

| Property | Ginger | Turmeric |
|----------|--------|----------|
| **Primary Sensation** | Pungent (burning heat) | Bitter-pungent + Colour |
| **Key Aroma Compound** | Zingiberene (≈30% of oil) | α-Turmerone + Ar-Turmerone |
| **Key Pungent Compound** | 6-Gingerol | Curcumin (also pigment) |
| **Key Colour Compound** | None (yellowish) | Curcumin (yellow-orange) |
| **Dominant Family** | Sesquiterpene hydrocarbons | Sesquiterpene ketones |
