## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **% w.b.** and **% d.b.** moisture (Examiner specifically tests this).
> 2. ❌ Stating curing temperature without specifying **duration** and **RH**.
> 3. ❌ Writing vanilla "fermentation" instead of "**sweating**" (sweating is enzymatic, not microbial).
> 4. ❌ Omitting **ISO/ASTAA standard reference** when discussing grades.
> 5. ❌ Generic answers — Examiner rewards **specific numbers** (e.g., **45 °C, 70% RH, 7–14 days**).

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a)** Describe the post harvest processing of **vanilla beans** in detail. Mention the biochemical changes that occur during each stage and the quality standards for finished beans. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **steam distillation** as a method of extracting essential oil from cloves. With a neat diagram, describe a **Clevenger apparatus** and compute the essential oil yield for a sample where **50 g of clove powder yields 7.5 mL of oil**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Post Harvest Processing of Vanilla Beans — 7 marks**

**Stage 1 — Harvesting (0.5 mark):**
Vanilla pods (*Vanilla planifolia*) are harvested **9 months after hand pollination** when the distal tip turns **yellowish-green**. Picking too early → low vanillin; too late → pod splits and loses aroma.

**Stage 2 — Killing (1 mark):**
Objective: arrest vegetative growth, initiate enzymatic activity. Methods:
- *Hot water scalding:* pods immersed in water at **63–65 °C for 3 minutes** (most common).
- *Oven killing:* **45 °C for 36 h**.
- *Sun killing:* alternate sun exposure and wool blanket sweat for several days.

**Stage 3 — Sweating (1.5 marks):**
Killed pods are placed in **sweat boxes** lined with wool blankets at **45 °C and RH ≈ 70%** for **7–14 days**. This is the **critical biochemical stage** where:

$$\text{Glucovanillin} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-glucosidase}} \text{Vanillin} + \text{Glucose}$$

During sweating, the beans turn **dark brown**, become pliable, and develop characteristic aroma. Over 200 secondary flavour compounds (alcohols, esters, aldehydes, phenols) form.

**Stage 4 — Slow Drying (1.5 marks):**
Beans are spread on racks at **35 °C, 60–70% RH** for **2–4 months**. Moisture drops from **~60% w.b. → ~25% w.b.**

**Stage 5 — Conditioning (1 mark):**
Beans are bundled, wrapped, and held in **closed boxes at ambient temperature for 3 months** to allow aroma to equilibrate and stabilise. Final moisture **25–30% w.b.** with vanillin **≥ 2%**.

**Stage 6 — Grading and Packing (0.5 mark):**
Graded by **length (≥ 16 cm = Grade A)**, appearance (oily, supple, dark brown), aroma (full-bodied). Packed in wax paper + aluminium foil bundles.

**Quality standards:** IS 7795, **ASTA vanillin ≥ 2%**, no insect damage, MC **25–30% w.b.** for cured beans.

---

**(b) Steam Distillation of Clove Oil & Clevenger Apparatus — 7 marks**

**Principle (1 mark):** When steam is passed through clove powder, the **essential oil** (mainly **eugenol**, b.p. **254 °C**) vaporises along with water at around **100 °C** due to **co-distillation / Dalton's Law of partial pressures**. The mixed vapour is condensed; oil and water separate in a **separator funnel** because of their immiscibility and density difference (oil ≈ **1.05 g/mL**, water **1.00 g/mL**).

**Clevenger Apparatus Diagram (Description — 2 marks):**

The apparatus consists of:
1. A **round-bottom flask (1 L–2 L)** holding clove powder + distilled water.
2. A **vertical condenser** with water inlet (bottom) and outlet (top) — counter-current cooling.
3. A **graduated collection arm (Babcock tube type)** with a calibrated scale reading **0–5 mL** for direct oil-volume measurement.
4. A **return tube** that returns the aqueous phase to the flask while retaining oil in the graduated arm.
5. A **heating mantle / burner** providing **controlled boiling**.

Process parameters:
- Clove powder particle size: **0.5–1 mm** (improves diffusion).
- Water-to-sample ratio: **6:1 to 10:1**.
- Distillation time: **4–6 hours** (until no more oil collects).

**Yield Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\% Yield = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{M_{sample}} \times 100$$

Substituting $V_{oil} = 7.5$ mL and $M_{sample} = 50$ g:

$$\% Yield = \dfrac{7.5}{50} \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Quality check (2 marks):**
GC-MS analysis should give:
- Eugenol **70–90%** (commercial premium grade ≥ **80%**).
- Eugenyl acetate **5–15%**.
- Caryophyllene **≤ 5%**.
- Refractive index **1.527–1.535**, specific gravity **1.04–1.06**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Reference:** Kerala's Western Ghats clove yields **14–18% oil** — among the highest in the world, owing to high eugenol content in the local **'Clavillico' and 'IFS Cl-3'** varieties.

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **post harvest processing of nutmeg and mace** with reference to maturity, curing, drying, and quality grading. Compare the volatile oil profile of mace vs. nutmeg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spice processor receives **1000 kg of fresh cardamom capsules** at **80% w.b. moisture**. The capsules must be dried to **11% w.b.** before export. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of dry product obtained, **(ii)** the amount of water removed, and **(iii)** the percentage weight loss. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Post Harvest Processing of Nutmeg & Mace — 7 marks**

**Botanical Background (0.5 mark):**
Nutmeg is the **dried seed kernel** of *Myristica fragrans*; **mace** is the bright red **aril** surrounding the seed inside the fruit. Both come from the same fruit but have **distinct post harvest protocols**.

**Harvesting (1 mark):**
Fruits are harvested when the **pericarp splits and turns yellow** — typically **180–210 days after fruit set**. Harvesting is done manually or by gently knocking the tree with bamboo poles. Over-mature fruits fall and the seeds germinate, losing quality.

**Primary Processing (1 mark):**
The fruit is split; **mace is carefully peeled** from the seed and **pressed flat**. Seeds are separated from the pericarp.

**Curing & Drying of Mace (1.5 marks):**
Mace is dried in **single layers on mats under shade for 10–15 days**. It shrinks, hardens, and turns from **bright red → amber-orange**. Rapid sun-drying bleaches the colour, lowering grade. Mechanical drying at **40–45 °C** for **6–8 h** produces premium colour retention. Final MC **≤ 10% w.b.**

**Curing & Drying of Nutmeg (1.5 marks):**
Seeds are dried in **single layers on raised platforms under shade for 6–8 weeks** until the **kernel rattles inside the shell** (sign that the seed has shrunk away from the endocarp). Final MC **≤ 10% w.b.** Some processors use **solar driers (50 °C)** for **3–5 days**. The shell is then **cracked** by wooden mallet to release the **brown kernel**.

**Volatile Oil Profile Comparison (1 mark):**

| Compound | Nutmeg Seed | Mace |
|---|---|---|
| Myristicin | **5–8%** | **2–4%** |
| Sabinene | **15–25%** | **20–30%** |
| α-Pinene | **10–15%** | **8–12%** |
| Eugenol | **≤ 1%** | **1–3%** (mace richer) |
| Total volatile oil | **6–10%** | **7–14%** (mace richer) |

**Grading (0.5 mark):**
- Nutmeg: graded by **size (> 350 g/100 nuts = Grade A)**, soundness, aroma. IS 7172.
- Mace: graded by **colour, intactness, aroma**. IS 4403.

---

**(b) Mass Balance Calculations for Cardamom Drying — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Initial mass $W_i = 1000$ kg
- Initial moisture $M_i = 80\%$ w.b.
- Final moisture $M_f = 11\%$ w.b.

**Step 1 — Convert w.b. → d.b. for initial moisture (1 mark):**

$$M_{i,db} = \dfrac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0\ \text{kg water / kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the dry solid mass (1 mark):**

$$W_{dry} = W_i \times (1 - M_i) = 1000 \times 0.20 = 200\ \text{kg dry solid}$$

This is **conserved** during drying (no dry matter loss in well-controlled drying).

**Step 3 — Final mass calculation (2 marks):**

First compute final dry-basis moisture:

$$M_{f,db} = \dfrac{0.11}{1 - 0.11} = \dfrac{0.11}{0.89} = 0.1236\ \text{kg water / kg dry solid}$$

Final total mass:

$$W_f = W_{dry} \times (1 + M_{f,db}) = 200 \times (1 + 0.1236) = 200 \times 1.1236 = 224.72\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Water removed (1 mark):**

$$\text{Water removed} = W_i - W_f = 1000 - 224.72 = 775.28\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage weight loss (2 marks):**

$$\% \text{ Weight Loss} = \dfrac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{1000 - 224.72}{1000} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{775.28}{1000} \times 100 = 77.53\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** From **1 tonne of fresh cardamom**, only **~225 kg of cured export-grade capsule** is obtained. This explains why cured cardamom commands a price of **₹1,500–3,500/kg**. The drying operation is the **single largest determinant** of both quality and economic return.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Minor Spices PHT**

- ✅ **Minor spices** include clove, cinnamon, nutmeg, mace, vanilla, saffron, curry leaf, ajwain, fennel, fenugreek — defined by low production volume and niche commercial use.
- ✅ **Post harvest technology** = maturity → harvest → cleaning → curing/sweating → drying → grading → packaging → storage → value addition.
- ✅ **Moisture content** is the most critical safety parameter — safe storage limit **≤ 10–11% w.b.**, water activity **$a_w \le 0.60$**.
- ✅ **Clove drying:** sun/shade **4–7 days, ≤ 40 °C**; oil yield **14–20%** with eugenol **70–90%**.
- ✅ **Cinnamon:** bark peeling → quilling → shade dry **7–10 days**; cinnamaldehyde **> 60%**.
- ✅ **Nutmeg & Mace:** slow shade drying **6–8 weeks**; mace is richer in volatile oil but lower in myristicin.
- ✅ **Vanilla:** 4-stage process (Killing → Sweating → Slow Drying → Conditioning) over **5–6 months**; vanillin **≥ 2%** via enzymatic hydrolysis of glucovanillin.
- ✅ **Saffron:** stigma harvested at dawn; shade/dehydrator dry at **40 °C ≤ 4 h**; ISO 3632 Cat I requires crocin $E_{1\%}^{1cm} \ge 200$.
- ✅ **Steam distillation** (Clevenger apparatus) is the gold standard for essential oil extraction; **hydro-distillation** for water-soluble fractions.
- ✅ **Oleoresin extraction** via Soxhlet (hexane/ethanol) yields **18–22%** for cloves — captures both volatile + non-volatile (fixed oil, resin) fractions.
- ✅ **ASTA specifications** govern export quality — colour value, cleanliness, insect damage, microbial counts, aflatoxin limits (**AFB1 ≤ 5 µg/kg** for EU).
- ✅ **Packaging innovations:** vacuum, nitrogen flushing, aluminium pouches extend shelf life **2–5×** over jute bags.
- ✅ **Numerical skill:** master conversions $MC_{wb} \leftrightarrow MC_{db}$, water balance on drying, oil yield % calculation.
- ✅ **Indian Standards to remember:** IS 4404 (clove), IS 7304 (cinnamon), IS 7172 (nutmeg), IS 4403 (mace), IS 7795 (vanilla), IS 4401 (ajwain), IS 6315 (fennel), IS 4402 (fenugreek), ISO 3632 (saffron), ISO 676 (spice nomenclature).

> [!WARNING]
> **Most-Forgotten Concepts in Exams:**
> 1. The **biochemistry** behind vanilla sweating — examiners love asking why it takes 7–14 days.
> 2. **Why shade drying is preferred over sun drying for premium spices** (UV degrades colour & volatiles).
> 3. **ISO 3632 categories** of saffron with numerical thresholds.
> 4. The **distinction between essential oil, oleoresin, and absolute**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processed Products of Cumin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Processed Products of Cumin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Processed Products of Cumin** refer to the value-added downstream derivatives obtained from the dried seeds of *Cuminum cyminum* L. (Family: Apiaceae), a major seed spice. These products include **whole cleaned seeds, ground cumin powder, roasted cumin powder (Bhuna Jeera), cumin essential oil, cumin oleoresin, encapsulated cumin fractions, and cumin-based spice blends** that have undergone unit operations such as cleaning, drying, roasting, grinding, solvent extraction, steam distillation, and microencapsulation to enhance shelf-life, flavor potency, and commercial utility.

### Botanical & Chemical Context

| Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| Scientific Name | *Cuminum cyminum* L. |
| Family | Apiaceae (Umbelliferae) |
| Common Names | Jeera (Hindi), Cumin (English), Jeerakam (Malayalam) |
| Primary Aroma Molecule | **Cuminaldehyde** (4-isopropylbenzaldehyde) |
| Volatile Oil Range | **2.5% – 4.5%** v/w |
| Fixed Oil Range | **~10% – 15%** w/w |
| Harvest Form | Dried mature seeds |

> [!NOTE]
> Cuminaldehyde constitutes approximately **25%–35%** of the total volatile oil and is the principal organoleptic marker compound used in spice quality grading as per **ASTA (American Spice Trade Association)** and **ISO 6465:2009** specifications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Shop Analogy"

Think of cumin seeds the way you think of **coffee beans**:
- A raw coffee bean is bitter, green, and uninviting — but after **roasting and grinding**, it becomes aromatic, flavorful, and ready for the cup.
- Similarly, raw cumin seeds carry a sharp, earthy, almost medicinal note. Once they are **roasted (Bhuna), ground, or steam-distilled**, the locked-in volatile oils are liberated, transforming the spice into pantry-ready, commercially viable, high-aroma products.
- Just as coffee gives you **espresso powder, instant coffee, and coffee essential oil**, cumin gives you **jeera powder, bhuna jeera, oleoresin, and essential oil** — each with its own market, price, and culinary application.

This analogy makes it instantly clear why **processing** is not a luxury but an economic and functional necessity.

---

## 1.3 Why Processing Matters — KTU Board Perspective

- **Volume Reduction**: Whole seeds occupy ~4× the volume of powder → lower transport cost.
- **Flavor Concentration**: Roasting activates Maillard-type reactions and releases bound volatiles → **2×–3× aroma intensity**.
- **Microbial Safety**: Cleaning + heat treatment reduces *Aspergillus* aflatoxin risk.
- **Diversification**: Single raw commodity → multiple SKUs → higher farm-gate value realization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India is the **largest producer and exporter** of cumin in the world (≈ 70% of global production, primarily from **Gujarat and Rajasthan**). Thus, processed cumin products are a **High-Weightage** topic for KTU ESE questions from the Plantation & Spices Technology domain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Processed Cumin Products

### A. Primary Processed Products (Low-Intensity Processing)
1. **Cleaned & Graded Whole Cumin Seeds**
2. **Ground Cumin Powder** (Raw Jeera Powder)
3. **Roasted Cumin Powder** (Bhuna Jeera / Shahi Jeera-adjacent)

### B. Secondary Processed Products (Extraction-Based)
4. **Cumin Essential Oil** (Steam Distilled)
5. **Cumin Oleoresin** (Solvent Extracted)
6. **Cumin Oleoresin with Fixed Oil** (Decolorized/Standardized)

### C. Tertiary Value-Added Products
7. **Encapsulated Cumin Oil** (Spray-Dried / Freeze-Dried)
8. **Spice Blends** (Garam Masala, Chaat Masala, Tandoori Masala)
9. **Cumin-Infused Functional Beverages** (Jeera Water / Buttermilk)

---

## 2.2 Unit Operations in Cumin Processing — The "Why & How"

### Step 1: Pre-Cleaning
- **Why**: Field-harvested seeds carry dust, stones, chaff, stalk pieces, and sometimes soil clods.
- **How**: Use of **destoner, magnetic separator, air-classifier (aspirator), and gravity separator**.
- **Outcome**: Initial purity ≥ **95%**.

### Step 2: Washing & Drying
- **Why**: Reduces microbial load (TPC from ~10⁶ to ~10³ CFU/g) and standardizes moisture.
- **How**: Cold-water wash followed by **mechanical hot-air drying at 45–55 °C** until moisture drops to **≤ 10%** (safe storage limit).
- **Critical Control Point**: Over-drying → brittle seeds → high dust loss during milling.

### Step 3: Roasting (Optional — for Bhuna Jeera)
- **Why**: Drives off residual moisture, generates **pyrazines and furans** via Maillard reactions, intensifies aroma.
- **How**: Pan roasting at **120–160 °C for 5–8 min** OR fluidized bed roasting at **140 °C for 3–4 min**.
- **Why Not Over-Roast**: Beyond 180 °C → charring, loss of cuminaldehyde, bitterness, dark color → fails BIS Grade-I standards.

### Step 4: Grinding / Milling
- **Why**: Increases surface area → better flavor release during cooking.
- **How**: **Hammer mill, pin mill, or cryogenic grinder** (cryogenic at –196 °C with liquid N₂ retains volatiles).
- **Why Cryogenic**: Conventional milling generates **40–60 °C** localized heat → volatile loss of **up to 30%**.

### Step 5: Sieving & Blending
- **Why**: Uniform particle size → consistent flavor dispersion.
- **How**: Vibratory sieves of **30–60 mesh (BSS)** for table powder; **80–100 mesh** for oleoresin-grade.

### Step 6: Packaging
- **Why**: Volatiles are oxygen- and light-sensitive.
- **How**: **Aluminum-laminated pouches with nitrogen flushing** OR vacuum packing. O₂ transmission < 1 cc/m²/day.

---

## 2.3 Extraction Processes — Deep Dive

### A. Steam Distillation (for Essential Oil)
- **Principle**: Water-immiscible volatile compounds co-distill with steam at **~100 °C** and separate on condensation.
- **Apparatus**: Copper still (traditional) / Stainless-steel field still (industrial).
- **Duration**: 4–6 hours per batch.
- **Yield**: **2.5–4.0% v/w** (i.e., 25–40 mL oil per kg seed).
- **Quality**: Light yellow, cuminaldehyde-rich (28–32% by GC).

### B. Hydrodistillation (Clevenger)
- Lab-scale method using **Clevenger-type apparatus**.
- Direct boiling of seed-water mixture; essential oil separates via density differential.

### C. Solvent Extraction (for Oleoresin)
- **Solvent**: Food-grade **hexane, acetone, or ethanol**.
- **Process**: Soxhlet / counter-current extractor → concentrate under vacuum → standardize.
- **Yield**: **Oleoresin yield = 12–18% w/w**.
- **Composition**: Volatile oil + fixed oil + resins + pigments.

### D. Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)
- **Solvent**: CO₂ above critical point (31.1 °C, 73.8 bar).
- **Why Premium**: No solvent residue, selective fractionation, retains thermosensitive compounds.
- **Commercial Reality**: High capex; mostly used for **premium pharma-grade** cumin extracts.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Parameters (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all engineering formulas, conversions, and process parameters** a KTU student must memorize for cumin processing. Note: Use `\vert` notation inside math — never the pipe character — to preserve table rendering integrity.

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|:---:|
| Moisture Content (wet basis) | $M_w = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Fresh seed ≈ 60–70% |
| Moisture Content (dry basis) | $M_d = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Dried ≤ 10% |
| Essential Oil Yield (% v/w) | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{W_{seed}} \times 100$ | 2.5–4.5% |
| Oleoresin Yield (% w/w) | $\text{Yield}_{OR} = \dfrac{W_{oleoresin}}{W_{seed}} \times 100$ | 12–18% |
| Distillation Recovery Efficiency | $\eta_R = \dfrac{A_{recovered}}{A_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | 70–85% |
| Antoine Equation (cuminaldehyde) | $\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | Boiling Pt ≈ 232 °C |
| Roasting Mass Balance | $W_{final} = W_{initial} \times (1 - \text{ML})$ where ML = moisture lost | ML ≈ 8–12% |
| Cryogenic Loss Factor | $L_{volatile} \approx k \cdot e^{\dfrac{-E_a}{RT}}$ (Arrhenius) | Reduces by 70% vs. ambient milling |
| Particle Size (sieve mesh) | $d_p \approx \dfrac{25.4}{\text{mesh number}}$ mm | 60 mesh → 0.42 mm |
| Steam Distillation Pressure | $P_{total} = P_{water} + P_{oil}$ (Dalton's Law) | Near-atmospheric |
| Clevenger Density Separation | $\rho_{oil} \approx 0.92$ g/mL < $\rho_{water}$ | Oil floats |
| Theoretical Plate Count | $N = \dfrac{t_R^2}{\sigma^2}$ (for GC purity) | > 10,000 for analytical grade |

> [!NOTE]
> **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** (governing steam distillation):
> 
> $$P_{total} = P_{water}^{\circ} + P_{oil}^{\circ}$$
> 
> The mixture boils when the **sum** of the partial vapor pressures equals the total system pressure (typically 1 atm). This is why cuminaldehyde (b.p. 232 °C) distills off at ~100 °C alongside water.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry**: Standardized oleoresins are used in instant noodles, ready-meals, and sauces — replacing variable natural powders.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Cuminaldehyde exhibits **antimicrobial, anti-diabetic, anti-inflammatory** activity → pharma-grade extracts.
- **Cosmeceuticals**: Cumin oil as a fragrance component in perfumes and herbal cosmetics.
- **Animal Feed**: Encapsulated cumin oil improves gut health in poultry (replacing antibiotic growth promoters).
- **Functional Beverages**: Jeera water (popular in India for digestion) is a growing FMCG segment.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Course Hook**: Cumin oleoresin standardization is the same engineering principle applied to **black pepper, turmeric, and cardamom oleoresins** — making this a **transferable concept** the examiner often tests.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Mass & Energy Balances

## 3.1 Derivation: Essential Oil Yield & Material Balance for Steam Distillation

Let us derive the **theoretical minimum steam requirement** to recover the volatile oil from a batch of cumin seeds.

### Given:
- Mass of cumin seeds loaded: $W_s$ = 100 kg
- Volatile oil content: $C_o$ = 3.5% v/w
- Density of cumin oil: $\rho_o$ ≈ 0.92 g/mL
- Steam distillation efficiency: $\eta_R$ = 0.80
- Water solubility of cuminaldehyde at 100 °C: $s_w$ ≈ 0.5 g/L (negligible)

### Step 1 — Total Oil Mass in Batch

$$m_{oil} = W_s \times C_o = 100 \text{ kg} \times 0.035 = 3.5 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Actual Recoverable Oil (Applying Recovery Efficiency)

$$m_{recovered} = m_{oil} \times \eta_R = 3.5 \times 0.80 = 2.8 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Volume of Recovered Oil

$$V_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{\rho_o} = \dfrac{2.8 \text{ kg}}{0.92 \text{ kg/L}} \approx 3.043 \text{ L}$$

### Step 4 — Steam Requirement (Rule-of-Thumb)

Industrial rule: **~1 kg steam recovers ~1 g essential oil** in well-designed stills.

$$m_{steam} = m_{recovered} \times 1000 = 2.8 \text{ kg} \times 1000 = 2800 \text{ kg steam}$$

### Step 5 — Energy Balance (Latent Heat of Vaporization)

Latent heat of vaporization of water: $\lambda_w$ = **2260 kJ/kg**

$$Q_{steam} = m_{steam} \times \lambda_w = 2800 \times 2260 = 6{,}328{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{steam} \approx 6.33 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ per 100 kg cumin batch}}$$

### Step 6 — Specific Energy Consumption (Process Efficiency Metric)

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{Q_{steam}}{m_{recovered}} = \dfrac{6.33 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ}}{2.8 \text{ kg}} \approx 2.26 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/kg oil}$$

This explains why essential oils are **expensive commodities** (₹ 8,000–₹ 15,000 per kg of cumin oil).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drying Kinetics — Moisture Reduction During Roasting

### Given:
- Initial moisture content (wet basis): $M_i$ = 12%
- Final desired moisture: $M_f$ = 4%
- Initial batch mass: $W_i$ = 50 kg

### Step 1 — Compute Dry Mass (Solids)

Dry solids remain constant during drying:
$$W_d = W_i \times (1 - M_i) = 50 \times (1 - 0.12) = 44 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Final Mass at $M_f$

Using the wet-basis relationship:
$$M_f = \dfrac{W_f - W_d}{W_f}$$

Solving for $W_f$:
$$W_f = \dfrac{W_d}{1 - M_f} = \dfrac{44}{1 - 0.04} = \dfrac{44}{0.96} \approx 45.83 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Water Evaporated

$$\Delta W = W_i - W_f = 50 - 45.83 = 4.17 \text{ kg water}$$

### Step 4 — Energy for Water Removal (from 25 °C to 100 °C + vaporization)

$$Q_{dry} = \Delta W \cdot \left[c_p(T_{100} - T_{25}) + \lambda_w\right]$$
$$Q_{dry} = 4.17 \times \left[4.18 \times 75 + 2260\right]$$
$$Q_{dry} = 4.17 \times \left[313.5 + 2260\right]$$
$$Q_{dry} = 4.17 \times 2573.5$$
$$\boxed{Q_{dry} \approx 10{,}731 \text{ kJ} \approx 10.73 \text{ MJ}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why **fluidized-bed dryers** (uniform heat transfer) are preferred over pan roasting — they achieve lower $\Delta T$ losses and consistent moisture endpoints at industrial scale.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Decision Tree

| Decision Trigger | Choice of Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| High-volume commodity powder | Hammer mill + air-classifier | Throughput > 500 kg/h |
| Premium aroma retention | Cryogenic grinding (-196 °C) | < 5% volatile loss |
| Bulk oil for flavor houses | Steam distillation | Low capex, industry standard |
| Solvent-free pharma extract | Supercritical CO₂ | Zero residue, IP-protected |
| Stable water-soluble flavor | Spray-dried emulsion on maltodextrin | Improved shelf-life in beverages |
| Functional drink (Jeera Water) | Aqueous extraction + pasteurization | Consumer-ready, ready-to-drink |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary — Key Engineering Equations

1. **Distillation yield efficiency**:
$$\eta = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{total}} \times 100$$

2. **Steam economy ratio**:
$$SER = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{steam}}$$

3. **Roasting color index** (Hunter L-value correlation):
$$\text{L-value} \propto e^{-kT \cdot t}$$
where $kT$ is the temperature-rate constant — explains darkening kinetics.

4. **Encapsulation efficiency** (for spray-dried cumin oil):
$$EE = \dfrac{A_{encapsulated}}{A_{total}} \times 100$$
where $A$ = cuminaldehyde content (by HPLC).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cumin Processing — End-to-End Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Cumin Seed Harvest] --> B[Pre-Cleaning Unit]
    B --> B1[Destoner]
    B --> B2[Magnetic Separator]
    B --> B3[Air Classifier]
    B1 --> C[Washing and Drying]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{Quality Grade Sorting}
    D -->|Grade A Premium| E[Roasting Unit]
    D -->|Standard Grade| F[Direct Milling Line]
    E --> E1[Pan Roasting 120-160 C]
    E --> E2[Fluidized Bed Roasting 140 C]
    E1 --> G[Grinding Mill]
    E2 --> G
    F --> G
    G --> G1[Hammer Mill]
    G --> G2[Cryogenic Grinder LN2]
    G --> G3[Pin Mill]
    G1 --> H[Sieving and Blending]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Packaging Unit]
    I --> I1[Nitrogen Flushed Pouches]
    I --> I2[Vacuum Sealed Containers]
    I1 --> J[Finished Powder SKUs]
    I2 --> J
    J --> J1[Ground Cumin Powder]
    J --> J2[Bhuna Jeera Roasted Powder]
    J --> J3[Spice Blends Garam Chaat Tandoori]

    H --> K[Extraction Line]
    K --> K1[Steam Distillation Still]
    K --> K2[Hydrodistillation Clevenger]
    K --> K3[Solvent Extraction Soxhlet]
    K --> K4[Supercritical CO2]
    K1 --> L[Essential Oil Storage]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> M[Oleoresin Storage]
    K4 --> M
    L --> N[Value Added Products]
    M --> N
    N --> N1[Encapsulated Cumin Oil]
    N --> N2[Cumin Oleoresin Standardized]
    N --> N3[Functional Beverages Jeera Water]
```

---

## 4.2 Steam Distillation Plant — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Crushed Cumin Seed] --> boiler[Steam Generator]
    boiler --> still[Distillation Still]
    still --> condenser[Condenser Coil]
    condenser --> separator[Decantation Separator]
    separator --> oil[Cumin Essential Oil Layer]
    separator --> water[Hydrosol Aqueous Layer]
    oil --> QC[GC MS Quality Check]
    QC --> storage[Storage Amber Bottle Nitrogen Blanket]
    water --> reuse[Reuse in Next Batch]
```

---

## 4.3 Oleoresin Standardization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Cumin Seed] --> s2[Soxhlet Extraction Hexane]
    s2 --> s3[Concentration Under Vacuum]
    s3 --> s4[Removal of Solvent Residue]
    s4 --> s5{Raw Oleoresin}
    s5 --> s6[Standardization Add Fixed Oil]
    s5 --> s7[Decolorization Activated Carbon]
    s6 --> s8[Final Oleoresin Product]
    s7 --> s8
    s8 --> s9[Packaging in Aluminum Containers]
```

---

## 4.4 Powder Processing — Quality Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Grounded Powder] --> p2[Sieve Analysis Particle Size]
    p2 --> p3[Moisture Analyzer IR Balance]
    p3 --> p4[Volatile Oil Assay Clevenger]
    p4 --> p5[Microbial Testing TPC Yeast Mold]
    p5 --> p6{Specifications Met}
    p6 -->|Yes| p7[Approved for Packaging]
    p6 -->|No| p8[Re-Process or Reject]
    p8 --> p1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale**: These block diagrams emphasize **process-flow architecture** rather than physical engineering drawings (which would require CAD). They are perfectly suited for KTU's process-engineering focus and clearly demonstrate **process control decision points** (Q&A gates) that examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` 
**Define cumin oleoresin. List its composition and two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cumin oleoresin is the **concentrated extract** obtained by **solvent extraction** (typically food-grade hexane or acetone) of crushed cumin seeds, followed by **solvent removal under vacuum**. It is a **dark brown, viscous, semi-fluid mass** that contains **both the volatile essential oil (cuminaldehyde-rich fraction) and the non-volatile resinous/fixed-oil fraction** of the spice.

**Composition:** Volatile oil (≈ 15–25%), fixed oil (≈ 60–70%), resins and pigments (≈ 10–15%).

**Industrial Applications:**
1. Standardized flavoring in **instant noodles, sauces, and ready-to-eat meals** — replacing variable spice powders.
2. Used in **cosmetics and ayurvedic formulations** as a bioactive extract with antimicrobial properties.

*(Marker Distribution: Definition 1m, Composition 1m, Applications 1m)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between steam distillation and hydrodistillation for cumin essential oil extraction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Steam Distillation | Hydrodistillation |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Steam passed **through** seed bed from external boiler | Seed and water **boiled together** in same vessel |
| Heating Mode | Indirect (via steam) | Direct contact with hot water |
| Oil Quality | Cleaner, lighter color | Slightly darker (thermal degradation possible) |
| Industrial Use | Preferred (commercial scale) | Mostly laboratory / Clevenger apparatus |
| Thermal Damage | Minimal | Higher risk of hydrolysis of esters |

*(Marker Distribution: Correct differentiation with 2 valid contrast points + any 1 supporting explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A `[KTU University Exam - 2024 KTU Model Paper]`**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **unit operations involved in the production of roasted cumin powder (Bhuna Jeera)** from raw cumin seeds. Include process parameters and quality checks at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and industrial significance of supercritical CO₂ extraction** of cumin oil. Compare its merits over conventional solvent extraction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Raw Material Receipt and Inspection (0.5 mark)**
- Incoming seeds inspected for **moisture (≤ 10%), foreign matter (≤ 2%), and insect damage**.
- Reject lots with off-odor, fungal contamination, or aflatoxin above **20 ppb** (FSSAI limit).

**2. Pre-Cleaning (1 mark)**
- Sequence: **Destoner → Magnetic Separator → Air Classifier (Aspirator) → Gravity Separator**.
- Removes stones, metallic fragments, chaff, and light impurities.
- Final purity: ≥ **98%**.

**3. Conditioning / Pre-Roasting Moisture Equalization (0.5 mark)**
- Optional **tempering** at 8–10% moisture ensures uniform heat penetration during roasting.

**4. Roasting (2 marks)**
- **Method 1 — Pan Roasting**: Iron skillet, 120–160 °C, 5–8 min, continuous stirring.
- **Method 2 — Fluidized-Bed Roasting**: 140 °C for 3–4 min — preferred industrially for uniformity.
- **Chemical Changes**:
  - Maillard reaction → **pyrazines, furans, thiophenes** (roasty aroma).
  - Cuminaldehyde concentration **increases** due to glycoside hydrolysis.
  - Moisture drops from 10% to 3–4%.
- **Critical Control**: Temperature must NOT exceed **180 °C** → charring and bitterness.

**5. Cooling (0.5 mark)**
- Roasted seeds cooled on a **tray cooler or conveyor with forced air** to stop further thermal reaction.

**6. Grinding (1 mark)**
- **Hammer mill** with water-cooled jacket OR **cryogenic grinder (–196 °C, LN₂)**.
- Particle size target: **60–80 mesh (BSS)**.

**7. Sieving and Blending (0.5 mark)**
- Vibratory sieve removes oversize particles; blending ensures batch homogeneity.

**8. Quality Checks (1 mark)**
| Parameter | Standard (BIS / ASTA) |
|---|---|
| Moisture | ≤ 6% |
| Total ash | ≤ 9.5% |
| Volatile oil | ≥ 2.5% v/w |
| Cuminaldehyde (by GC) | ≥ 25% |
| Microbial limits | TPC < 10⁴ CFU/g, Yeast & Mold < 10³ CFU/g |

**9. Packaging (0.5 mark)**
- **Aluminum-laminated pouches**, nitrogen-flushed, O₂ transmission < 1 cc/m²/day.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**1. Principle (1.5 marks)**
- CO₂ above its **critical point (T_c = 31.1 °C, P_c = 73.8 bar)** behaves as a **supercritical fluid** — possessing both liquid-like solvating power and gas-like diffusivity.
- It selectively dissolves **non-polar to moderately polar** compounds (essential oils, fat-soluble pigments, fixed oils).
- On **depressurization**, CO₂ reverts to gas and **evaporates completely**, leaving **zero solvent residue** in the extract.

**2. Procedure (2 marks)**
1. Crushed cumin seeds loaded into the **extractor vessel**.
2. CO₂ from a **liquid CO₂ reservoir** is pressurized via a pump to 200–300 bar and heated to 35–50 °C → enters extractor.
3. SC-CO₂ flows through the seed bed; dissolves the oil fraction.
4. The laden SC-CO₂ exits to the **separator vessel** where pressure is reduced (e.g., 50–60 bar) → CO₂ loses solvating power → oil precipitates.
5. CO₂ is recycled; oil is collected, weighed, and stored in amber glass under nitrogen.

**3. Industrial Significance (1.5 marks)**
- Used for **pharma-grade cumin extracts**, premium cosmetics, and **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) certified** food flavorings.
- Produces **high-purity, residue-free oleoresin** commanding 5–10× higher market price than solvent-extracted oleoresin.
- Important for products destined for **export to EU / USA** where hexane residue limits are strict (< 1 ppm).

**4. Comparison with Conventional Solvent Extraction (2 marks)**

| Parameter | SC-CO₂ Extraction | Solvent (Hexane) Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| Solvent Residue | None | Trace hexane (must be < 1 ppm) |
| Selectivity | Tunable via P and T | Solvent-dependent |
| Operating Cost | High capex | Low capex |
| Throughput | Lower per batch | Higher |
| Product Purity | Very high | Moderate |
| Thermal Damage | Low (35–50 °C) | Possible during solvent recovery |
| Eco-friendliness | Green technology | VOC emission concerns |

**Conclusion:** SC-CO₂ is the **gold standard** for premium extracts; hexane extraction dominates **commodity-scale** oleoresin production.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B `[KTU University Exam - 2024 KTU Model Paper]`**
**(a)** Explain the **composition of cumin essential oil**. Discuss the role of **cuminaldehyde as the principal aroma compound** with reference to its physico-chemical properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **production process and applications of encapsulated cumin oil** using **spray drying** as a microencapsulation technique. Mention suitable wall materials and encapsulation efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Composition of Cumin Essential Oil (2.5 marks)**

| Component | Approximate % (by GC-MS) | Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Cuminaldehyde** (4-isopropylbenzaldehyde) | **25–35%** | Aromatic aldehyde |
| γ-Terpinene | 15–25% | Monoterpene hydrocarbon |
| β-Pinene | 10–15% | Monoterpene hydrocarbon |
| p-Cymene | 5–10% | Aromatic hydrocarbon |
| p-Mentha-1,3-dien-7-al | 3–6% | Aldehyde |
| Myrcene | 1–3% | Monoterpene |
| α-Pinene | < 1% | Monoterpene |

**2. Physico-Chemical Properties of Cuminaldehyde (2.5 marks)**

- **IUPAC Name**: 4-(Propan-2-yl)benzaldehyde / 4-Isopropylbenzaldehyde.
- **Molecular Formula**: $C_{10}H_{12}O$
- **Molecular Weight**: 148.20 g/mol
- **Boiling Point**: 232 °C
- **Density**: ~1.02 g/mL
- **Appearance**: Pale yellow oily liquid; strong, warm, spicy, slightly bitter aroma.
- **Reactivity**: Aldehyde group → Schiff-base formation, oxidation to cuminic acid, condensation reactions.

**3. Role as Principal Aroma Compound (2 marks)**
- The **spicy-warm-woody** aroma of cumin is **organoleptically dominated by cuminaldehyde**.
- Its **low odour threshold (~0.5 ppb in water)** makes it the **most impactful** volatile.
- Used as a **quantitative marker** in spice grading standards (ISO 6465:2009 specifies min. cuminaldehyde content).
- Exhibits **antimicrobial activity** against *E. coli, Salmonella, Staphylococcus* → contributes to preservative effect of whole cumin.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**1. Why Microencapsulation (1 mark)**
- Cumin oil is **volatile, oxygen-sensitive, and water-immiscible** → difficult to incorporate in beverages and aqueous foods.
- Encapsulation protects the oil from **oxidation, light, and heat**, enables **controlled release**, and converts it into a **free-flowing powder**.

**2. Wall Materials (1.5 marks)**
| Wall Material | Function |
|---|---|
| **Maltodextrin (DE 10–20)** | Bulking agent, low viscosity, low cost |
| **Modified Starch (Hi-Cap, Capsul TA)** | Excellent emulsifier, holds oil in emulsion |
| **Gum Arabic** | Forms stable film, good for retention |
| **Whey Protein Isolate** | Emulsion stabilizer, food-grade |
| **Cyclodextrin (β-CD)** | Molecular inclusion → enhanced stability |

- Typical ratio: **Wall material : Oil = 4 : 1 to 3 : 1**.

**3. Process — Spray Drying (3 marks)**
1. **Emulsion Preparation**: Cumin oil + wall material dissolved in water at 50–60 °C → homogenized at **150–200 bar** to form stable O/W emulsion.
2. **Spray Drying**:
   - **Inlet Temperature**: 180–200 °C
   - **Outlet Temperature**: 80–100 °C
   - **Feed Rate**: 5–10 mL/min (lab scale)
   - **Atomization**: Rotary or nozzle atomizer
3. **Powder Collection**: Cyclone separator + bag filter.
4. **Storage**: Encapsulated powder stored in airtight, opaque containers.

**4. Encapsulation Efficiency (EE) — Calculation (1 mark)**
$$EE = \dfrac{\text{Surface Oil retained}}{\text{Total Oil in capsule}} \times 100$$

Typical EE for spray-dried cumin oil: **75–92%** (good EE if emulsion is stable).

**5. Applications (0.5 mark)**
- Instant soups, beverage mixes, bakery, dairy products, **functional drinks (Jeera Water premix)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing essential oil with oleoresin** — they are *not* the same. Essential oil = pure volatiles; oleoresin = volatiles + fixed oil + resins. Many students write the wrong definition.
> 2. **Skipping process parameters** — KTU examiners award marks specifically when students mention **temperature (°C), time (min), mesh size, and %yield**. Generic prose loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting cuminaldehyde percentage** — Always mention **25–35%** as the principal aroma compound.
> 4. **Confusing steam distillation with hydrodistillation** — examiners love to test this difference.
> 5. **No mass/energy balance attempt** in numerical parts — KTU's OBE pattern rewards *quantitative* reasoning even in descriptive questions. Always attempt an equation.
> 6. **Ignoring packaging and storage** — this is a 2–3 mark section often skipped.
> 7. **Wrong spelling**: "Cummin" is wrong — the correct spelling is **"Cumin"**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Cumin** = *Cuminum cyminum* L., Family **Apiaceae**, primary aroma = **Cuminaldehyde (25–35%)**.
- ✅ Volatile oil content: **2.5–4.5%**; Oleoresin yield: **12–18%**.
- ✅ **Roasting** at **120–160 °C, 5–8 min** is the critical step for **Bhuna Jeera**.
- ✅ Temperature above **180 °C** = charring, bitterness, volatile loss → reject.
- ✅ **Steam distillation** uses **Dalton's Law**: $P_{total} = P_{water} + P_{oil}$ → enables low-T boiling of high-BP volatiles.
- ✅ **Cryogenic grinding (-196 °C, LN₂)** minimizes volatile loss to **< 5%**.
- ✅ **Oleoresin** = solvent extraction (hexane/acetone); **Essential Oil** = steam/hydro-distillation.
- ✅ **SC-CO₂ extraction** = zero residue, premium quality, high cost.
- ✅ **Microencapsulation** converts oil into powder via **spray drying** with maltodextrin/modified starch as wall material.
- ✅ Packaging = **N₂ flushed, Al-laminated pouches**, O₂ transmission < 1 cc/m²/day.
- ✅ BIS limits: Moisture ≤ 10%, Total ash ≤ 9.5%, Volatile oil ≥ 2.5%, Cuminaldehyde ≥ 25%.
- ✅ India ≈ **70%** of global cumin production (Gujarat + Rajasthan).
- ✅ Applications span **food, pharma, cosmetics, animal feed, and functional beverages**.
- ✅ **Differentiate always**: Essential Oil ↔ Oleoresin ↔ Powder; Steam Distillation ↔ Hydrodistillation ↔ SC-CO₂.
- ✅ Always cite **process parameters** (T, t, mesh size, %yield) for full KTU marks.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Processed Products of Cumin**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PEFTT418, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

topics.title: Coriander
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coriander

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coriander** (*Coriandrum sativum* L.) is an annual herbaceous aromatic spice crop belonging to the family **Apiaceae (Umbelliferae)**. It is cultivated commercially for its **dried ripe fruits (seeds)** — which are used as a spice — and for its **tender fresh leaves**, which are used as a culinary herb, garnish, and flavoring agent. The plant produces small white or pinkish flowers arranged in compound umbels, and its dry fruits (cremocarps) contain two mericarps, each housing a single seed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Coriander is classified as a **seed spice** under the Apiaceae family. The dried mericarps constitute the commercial "coriander seed" of international trade. India is the **largest producer, consumer, and exporter** of coriander in the world.

### Taxonomic Classification

| Rank | Classification |
|------|----------------|
| Kingdom | Plantae |
| Division | Magnoliophyta (Angiosperms) |
| Class | Dicotyledonae |
| Order | Apiales |
| **Family** | **Apiaceae (Umbelliferae)** |
| **Genus** | ***Coriandrum*** |
| **Species** | ***sativum*** |
| Chromosome number | 2n = **22** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of coriander as a **"twin-purpose kitchen wonder."** Imagine a single plant that gives you two entirely different culinary experiences from two different parts:

- The **leaves** (called *cilantro* in the Americas or *dhania patta* in India) are fresh, citrusy, and aromatic — they wilt quickly and are used the same day they are plucked.
- The **seeds** (dried fruits) are warm, nutty, and earthy — they are dried, stored for months, and used as a base spice in curries, pickles, and spice blends like garam masala and curry powder.

Just as a coconut palm offers both tender coconut water and mature coconut kernel from the same plant, **the same coriander plant offers two distinct commercial products**, making it a uniquely versatile spice crop in the global spice market.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Distribution:** Native to the **Mediterranean region** and **South-Western Asia**. It has been cultivated for over **3,000 years** and is now grown extensively in **India, Morocco, Russia, Ukraine, Mexico, and Argentina**. In India, the major producing states are **Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, and Tamil Nadu**.

## 1.3 Botanical Morphology (Plant Parts)

- **Root:** Tap root system, fairly deep, with numerous lateral branches.
- **Stem:** Erect, slender, hollow, branched, attaining a height of **30–90 cm**, often with a reddish tinge.
- **Leaves:** Dimorphic (two types):
  - **Basal leaves:** Long-petioled, pinnately or bipinnately compound, with broad, lobed, toothed leaflets (resemble parsley).
  - **Cauline (upper) leaves:** Short-petioled or sessile, finely divided into linear, thread-like segments (feathery appearance).
- **Inflorescence:** Compound umbel with 3–8 primary rays; flowers small, white or pinkish, hermaphrodite and protandrous.
- **Fruit (Seed):** A **cremocarp** (schizocarp), globose, ribbed, yellowish-brown to golden, **2–4 mm** in diameter, aromatic with a characteristic warm, sweet-spicy flavor. Each fruit splits into two mericarps at maturity, each containing one seed.

## 1.4 Economic Importance & Key Statistics

| Parameter | Value/Detail |
|-----------|--------------|
| Botanical name | *Coriandrum sativum* L. |
| Family | Apiaceae |
| Plant type | Annual herb |
| Major use | Dried seeds (spice) + fresh leaves (herb) |
| **India's share in world production** | **~70–80%** |
| **India's share in world export** | **~40%** |
| Major producing state (India) | **Rajasthan** (contributes over 50% of national output) |
| Essential oil content (seeds) | **0.2–1.0%** |
| Major aroma compound | **Linalool (C₁₀H₁₈O)** — 60–70% of essential oil |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linalool** (a monoterpene alcohol) is the principal aromatic compound responsible for the characteristic fragrance of coriander essential oil. It is widely used in perfumery, flavoring, and aromatherapy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Climatic Requirements

Coriander is a **cool-season (rabi)** crop that thrives under specific agro-climatic conditions:

| Factor | Optimum Requirement |
|--------|---------------------|
| Temperature | **15°C – 25°C** (cool and dry) |
| Rainfall | **300–600 mm** (well-distributed) |
| Sunlight | Long sunny days during vegetative growth; shorter days favor flowering |
| Altitude | Can be grown from plains up to **2,000 m** (MSL) |
| Photoperiod | Quantitative long-day plant — long days accelerate bolting (flowering) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** High temperature (>30°C) and high humidity at flowering cause **poor seed set and disease incidence** (powdery mildew, stem gall). Most students incorrectly assume coriander is a tropical crop — it is **temperate to sub-tropical**.

## 2.2 Soil Requirements

- **Best soil:** Well-drained, **loamy to sandy loam** soils rich in organic matter.
- **Ideal pH range:** **6.0 – 7.5** (slightly acidic to neutral; tolerates up to 8.5).
- Coriander is **moderately tolerant to salinity** but highly sensitive to waterlogging.
- Heavy clay soils cause **stunted growth and root rot** — they must be avoided.

## 2.3 Land Preparation & Sowing

The operational steps in field preparation are:

1. **Ploughing:** 2–3 deep ploughings followed by harrowing to obtain a fine, well-pulverized tilth.
2. **FYM application:** Add **10–15 tonnes/ha** of well-decomposed farmyard manure at last ploughing.
3. **Layout:** Raised bed / flat-bed sowing; in heavy soils, raised beds of 2–3 m width are preferred.
4. **Seed treatment:** Treat seeds with **Trichoderma viride @ 4 g/kg** (or *Pseudomonas fluorescens*) to prevent wilt and powdery mildew.
5. **Sowing method:** Line sowing (line-to-line 30 cm; plant-to-plant 10–15 cm) is preferred over broadcasting for better intercultural operations.
6. **Sowing depth:** **2–3 cm** deep.
7. **Sowing time (India):**
   - **Rabi season:** October – November (most common).
   - **Kharif season:** June – July (in hilly tracts like Nilgiris, Kodaikanal).
   - **Summer season:** February – March (in northern plains under assured irrigation).

## 2.4 Seed Rate & Spacing

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Seed rate (line sowing) | **15–20 kg/ha** |
| Seed rate (broadcasting) | 25–30 kg/ha |
| Spacing (line sowing) | 30 cm × 10–15 cm |
| Germination percentage | 70–80% (fruits are schizocarps; splitting improves germination) |

## 2.5 Manuring and Fertilizer Schedule

Coriander is a **moderate feeder**, but nitrogen is critical for vegetative and reproductive growth.

| Nutrient | Dose (kg/ha) | Application Time |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| **N (Nitrogen)** | **40–60** | 50% basal + 50% at 30 DAS (top-dress) |
| **P (Phosphorus)** | **30–40** | Entire dose as basal |
| **K (Potassium)** | **20–25** | Entire dose as basal |
| **S (Sulphur)** | **20** | As gypsum/basal (improves essential oil content) |
| **Zn (Zinc)** | **10** (if deficient) | Basal application |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphur application significantly increases essential oil yield** (linalool content) — a high-yield point frequently asked in KTU exams.

## 2.6 Irrigation & Water Management

Coriander is largely grown as a **rainfed crop** in India, but supplemental irrigation boosts yield.

- **Pre-sowing irrigation (rauni):** Essential for proper germination if sown in dry soil.
- **Critical irrigation stages:**
  1. Seedling establishment (15–20 DAS)
  2. Flowering initiation (45–55 DAS)
  3. Seed development (75–90 DAS)
- **Number of irrigations:** 3–5 (depending on rainfall and season).
- **Irrigation method:** Flood / furrow irrigation. Drip irrigation is gaining popularity for water economy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Excess irrigation leads to vegetative growth at the cost of seed yield and causes root rot and wilt diseases.**

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|--------------------|------------|-------------|
| Seed yield calculation | $\text{Yield (kg/ha)} = \dfrac{\text{No. of plants/ha} \times \text{seeds/plant} \times \text{1000-seed weight}}{10^6}$ | Estimating field yield |
| Essential oil recovery | $\text{Oil \%} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of oil (mL)}}{\text{Weight of seed (g)}} \times 100$ | Quality assessment |
| Spacing → Plant population | $N = \dfrac{10{,}000 \, \text{m}^2}{\text{Row spacing (m)} \times \text{Plant spacing (m)}}$ | Sowing design |
| Fertilizer element from nutrient salt | $\text{Element (kg)} = \text{Salt (kg)} \times \dfrac{\% \text{ element in salt}}{100}$ | Fertilizer dose conversion |
| Linalool content (in oil) | $\text{Approx. 60–70% of total essential oil}$ | Quality specification |
| Shrinkage / loss during drying | $\text{Loss \%} = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{W_i} \times 100$ where $W_f$ = final weight, $W_i$ = initial weight | Post-harvest estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> Coriander essential oil is obtained by **steam distillation** of dried, crushed seeds. The oil yield ranges between **0.2% and 1.0% (v/w)** depending on variety, maturity, and agro-climatic conditions.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Culinary:** Whole and ground seeds used in spice blends, pickles, meat dishes, soups, sauces, baked goods, and curry powders. Leaves used in chutneys, salads, and as garnish.
- **Medicinal (Ayurveda & Modern):** Carminative, digestive, anti-flatulent, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, anti-microbial. Used in formulations like *Triphala*.
- **Industrial:** Coriander oil is used in perfumery, soaps, cosmetics, and as a flavoring agent in alcoholic beverages (e.g., gin).
- **Pharmaceutical:** Linalool is used as a fragrance fixative and studied for anxiolytic properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Seed Yield Estimation Per Hectare

The seed yield of coriander per hectare can be estimated from plant population, seeds per plant, and thousand-seed weight. Below is an exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — Define plant population per hectare

Given the recommended spacing of 30 cm (row) × 15 cm (plant-to-plant):

$$
P = \frac{10{,}000 \,\text{m}^2}{0.30 \,\text{m} \times 0.15 \,\text{m}}
$$

$$
P = \frac{10{,}000}{0.045} \approx 2{,}22{,}222 \,\text{plants/ha}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** 1 hectare = 10,000 m². Each plant occupies 0.30 m × 0.15 m = 0.045 m². Total plants = total area / area per plant.

### Step 2 — Account for germination and field establishment losses

Assuming **80% field germination and establishment**:

$$
P_{\text{effective}} = P \times 0.80 = 2{,}22{,}222 \times 0.80 = 1{,}77{,}778 \,\text{plants/ha}
$$

### Step 3 — Assume seeds per plant

An average coriander plant produces approximately **50–80 umbels**, with each umbel bearing **30–50 seeds (mericarps)**. Assuming an average of **40 umbels × 25 seeds = 1,000 seeds per plant** (a standard textbook value for medium-yielding varieties):

$$
S = 1{,}000 \,\text{seeds/plant}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply thousand-seed weight (TSW)

For coriander, the thousand-seed weight is approximately **10–15 g** (average = **12 g**):

$$
W_{1000} = 12 \,\text{g} = 0.012 \,\text{kg}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate total seed yield per hectare

$$
Y = \frac{P_{\text{effective}} \times S \times W_{1000}}{1000}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
Y = \frac{1{,}77{,}778 \times 1{,}000 \times 0.012}{1000}
$$

$$
Y = \frac{1{,}77{,}778 \times 12}{1000}
$$

$$
Y = \frac{21{,}33{,}336}{1000}
$$

$$
Y \approx 2{,}133 \,\text{kg/ha}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This corresponds to the **national average yield** of coriander in India (approximately 1.5–2.0 t/ha). Improved varieties under optimal management can yield up to **2.5–3.0 t/ha**.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Essential Oil Yield Per Hectare

### Step 1 — Essential oil content

Average essential oil content in dried seeds = **0.5% (v/w)** for improved varieties.

### Step 2 — Express oil content as a ratio

$$
O_{\text{content}} = \frac{0.5}{100} = 0.005
$$

### Step 3 — Apply to seed yield

$$
E = Y \times O_{\text{content}}
$$

$$
E = 2{,}133 \times 0.005 = 10.67 \,\text{kg oil/ha}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to litres (specific gravity of coriander oil ≈ 0.86)

$$
E_{\text{L}} = \frac{E}{\rho} = \frac{10.67}{0.86} \approx 12.4 \,\text{L oil/ha}
$$

> **Real-world utility:** 1 hectare of coriander yielding ~2 tonnes of seed gives roughly **10–12 kg (≈12–14 L) of essential oil** — a high-value industrial product.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Variety Performance & Comparison

| Variety | Yield (kg/ha) | Oil % | Maturity (days) | Special Feature |
|---------|---------------|-------|------------------|------------------|
| **ACr-1** | 1,500–1,800 | 0.5 | 90–100 | Bold seed, Rajasthan |
| **CO-1** | 1,200–1,500 | 0.4 | 95–105 | Tamil Nadu, dual purpose |
| **CO-2** | 1,500–1,800 | 0.45 | 90–100 | Resistant to powdery mildew |
| **CO-3** | 1,800–2,200 | 0.5 | 95–105 | High oil, Tamil Nadu |
| **Gujarat Coriander-1 (GC-1)** | 1,800–2,000 | 0.5 | 100–110 | Bold seed, Gujarat |
| **Gujarat Coriander-2 (GC-2)** | 2,000–2,200 | 0.5 | 95–100 | High yielding, Gujarat |
| **Sadhna (S-33)** | 1,200–1,500 | 0.4 | 90–110 | Cimmabor selection |
| **Hisar Sugandh** | 1,500–1,800 | 0.5 | 100–110 | High linalool content |
| **Rajat (ACr-2)** | 1,500–1,800 | 0.5 | 90–100 | Powdery mildew resistant |
| **Swathi (CS-1)** | 1,500–1,800 | 0.5 | 100–110 | Kerala-released variety |
| **Surabhi** | 1,400–1,700 | 0.45 | 95–110 | High oil, dual purpose |
| **Pant Harit Dhania-1 (PHD-1)** | 1,200–1,500 | 0.4 | 100–115 | Uttarakhand, leafy type |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Problem: Post-Harvest Moisture Loss Calculation

**Problem:** 100 kg of fresh coriander seeds harvested at 22% moisture content is dried to 8% moisture. Calculate the final dry weight.

### Step 1 — Identify the formula

$$
W_f = W_i \times \frac{100 - M_i}{100 - M_f}
$$

where $W_f$ = final weight, $W_i$ = initial weight, $M_i$ = initial moisture (%), $M_f$ = final moisture (%).

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
W_f = 100 \times \frac{100 - 22}{100 - 8}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator

$$
W_f = 100 \times \frac{78}{92}
$$

### Step 4 — Final result

$$
W_f = 100 \times 0.8478 = 84.78 \,\text{kg}
$$

> **Interpretation:** About **15.22% weight loss** occurs during drying from 22% → 8% moisture content. This is a standard post-harvest calculation in spice processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Cultivation Workflow of Coriander

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select well-drained loamy soil, pH 6.0-7.5] --> B[Deep ploughing 2-3 times]
    B --> C[Apply FYM 10-15 tonnes/ha]
    C --> D[Prepare raised beds or flat tilth]
    D --> E[Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride]
    E --> F[Sowing Oct-Nov, 15-20 kg/ha, 30x15 cm spacing]
    F --> G[Pre-sowing irrigation rauni]
    G --> H[Thinning at 15-20 DAS]
    H --> I[Top-dress N 50% at 30 DAS]
    I --> J[Interculture 1-2 weedings]
    J --> K[Critical irrigations: Flowering and seed filling]
    K --> L[Monitor pests and diseases]
    L --> M[Harvest at 50% yellowing of umbels]
    M --> N[Sun-dry to 8% moisture]
    N --> O[Threshing by beating or trampling]
    O --> P[Winnowing and grading]
    P --> Q[Storage in cool dry godowns]
    Q --> R[Market dispatch]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Coriander Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Cultivation] --> B[Harvest at Physiological Maturity]
    B --> C[Drying and Threshing]
    C --> D[Cleaning and Grading]
    D --> E1[Whole Seed Spice]
    D --> E2[Ground Powder]
    D --> E3[Essential Oil Distillation]
    D --> E4[Oleoresin Extraction]
    E1 --> F1[Domestic Retail]
    E1 --> F2[Export Market]
    E2 --> F1
    E2 --> F2
    E3 --> F3[Pharmaceutical Industry]
    E3 --> F4[Perfumery and Cosmetics]
    E4 --> F5[Food Processing Industry]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Botanical Morphology of Coriander Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Coriandrum sativum L. Plant] --> R[Tap root system with lateral branches]
    P --> S[Erect hollow branched stem 30-90 cm]
    P --> L1[Basal leaves broad lobed pinnately compound]
    P --> L2[Cauline leaves finely dissected linear segments]
    P --> FL[Compound umbel inflorescence]
    FL --> FW[White to pinkish bisexual flowers protandrous]
    FW --> FR[Schizocarp fruit 2-4 mm diameter]
    FR --> M1[Mericarp 1 with essential oil glands]
    FR --> M2[Mericarp 2 with single seed each]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: Pest and Disease Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Symptom observed] --> Q1[White powdery patches on leaves and stem]
    Q1 --> Y1[Powdery mildew caused by Erysiphe polygoni]
    Y1 --> T1[Spray wettable sulphur 0.2% or hexaconazole]
    S --> Q2[Wilting and yellowing from base upwards]
    Q2 --> Y2[Fusarium wilt caused by Fusarium oxysporum]
    Y2 --> T2[Seed treatment with carbendazim 2g/kg and crop rotation]
    S --> Q3[White aphid clusters on tender shoots]
    Q3 --> Y3[Aphid attack Myzus persicae]
    Y3 --> T3[Spray neem oil 0.5% or imidacloprid 0.01%]
    S --> Q4[Stunted plants with distorted inflorescence]
    Q4 --> Y4[Stem gall caused by Protomyces macrosporus]
    Y4 --> T4[Seed treatment with thiram and resistant variety]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**Write the botanical name, family, and chromosome number of coriander. Mention the part used as spice.**

**Model Answer:**
Coriander's botanical name is *Coriandrum sativum* L., belonging to the family **Apiaceae (Umbelliferae)**. The chromosome number is **2n = 22**. The **dried ripe fruits (seeds/mericarps)** are commercially used as a spice, while the fresh **tender leaves** are used as a culinary herb. The fruits are schizocarps (cremocarps) that split into two mericarps at maturity.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**List the major essential oil constituents of coriander seed and state any two medicinal uses.**

**Model Answer:**
The major essential oil constituents of coriander seed are **linalool (60–70%)**, α-pinene, γ-terpinene, camphor, and geraniol. **Linalool** is the dominant aroma compound. Two medicinal uses are: (i) **Carminative and digestive** — relieves flatulence and indigestion; (ii) **Anti-inflammatory and anti-microbial** — used in traditional Ayurvedic formulations for treating arthritis and infections.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**Discuss in detail the package of practices for cultivation of coriander, including sowing time, seed rate, spacing, manuring, and irrigation.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sowing Time:**
- **Rabi season (main):** October – November in plains.
- **Kharif season:** June – July in hilly regions (Nilgiris, Kodaikanal).
- **Summer season:** February – March under irrigated conditions.

**(ii) Seed Rate:**
- **Line sowing:** 15–20 kg/ha
- **Broadcasting:** 25–30 kg/ha

**(iii) Spacing:**
- 30 cm between rows × 10–15 cm between plants.
- Sowing depth: 2–3 cm.

**(iv) Manuring:**

$$
\text{N : P : K} = 40 : 30 : 20 \,\text{kg/ha (approx.)}
$$

- Apply 50% N + full P + full K as basal.
- Apply remaining 50% N as top-dressing at 30 DAS.
- Add **10–15 tonnes FYM/ha** during land preparation.
- Sulphur @ 20 kg/ha is recommended for enhancing essential oil content.

**(v) Irrigation:**
- Pre-sowing irrigation (rauni) is essential.
- Critical stages: seedling establishment (15–20 DAS), flowering (45–55 DAS), and seed development (75–90 DAS).
- Total of 3–5 irrigations depending on rainfall.
- Avoid waterlogging — it causes root rot and wilt.

**(vi) Interculture:**
- 1–2 hand weedings at 20–30 DAS and 45 DAS.
- Light hoeing improves aeration and reduces weed competition.

---

#### Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**Explain the major pests and diseases of coriander and their integrated management practices.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Major Diseases:**

| Disease | Causal Organism | Symptoms | Management |
|---------|-----------------|----------|------------|
| **Powdery mildew** | *Erysiphe polygoni* | White powdery patches on leaves, stem, umbels | Spray wettable sulphur 0.2% or hexaconazole 0.1%; use resistant varieties (Rajat, CO-2) |
| **Fusarium wilt** | *Fusarium oxysporum* f. sp. *coriandrii* | Yellowing, wilting from base | Seed treatment with carbendazim @ 2 g/kg; crop rotation (3 years) |
| **Stem gall** | *Protomyces macrosporus* | Distorted inflorescence, galls on stem | Seed treatment with thiram; use resistant variety Sadhna |
| **Seedling blight** | *Alternaria* spp. | Damping-off of seedlings | Seed treatment with *Trichoderma viride* @ 4 g/kg |

**(ii) Major Pests:**

| Pest | Damage | Management |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Aphid** (*Myzus persicae*) | Sucks sap from tender shoots and umbels | Spray neem oil 0.5% or imidacloprid 0.01% |
| **Whitefly** (*Bemisia tabaci*) | Sucks sap, transmits viral diseases | Yellow sticky traps; neem-based insecticides |
| **Coriander caterpillar** | Defoliates leaves and feeds on umbels | Hand-picking; spray Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) |

**(iii) Integrated Pest Management (IPM):**
- Crop rotation with non-host crops (cereals, pulses).
- Use resistant varieties (Rajat, CO-2, GC-2).
- Seed treatment with biocontrol agents.
- Proper field sanitation and removal of infected debris.
- Balanced fertilization to avoid excessive vegetative growth.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

#### Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]
**Describe the post-harvest handling of coriander including harvesting, drying, threshing, grading, and storage.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Harvesting:**
- Crop matures in **90–110 days** depending on variety.
- Harvest at **physiological maturity** when 50% of umbels turn yellowish-brown.
- The correct time is when the seeds are hard and emit a characteristic aroma when crushed.
- Cut plants manually with sickles early in the morning to prevent shattering losses.

**(ii) Drying:**
- Bundles of cut plants are stacked upright in the field for **2–3 days** for sun-curing.
- Spread on clean threshing floor for further drying until seeds reach **8–10% moisture content**.
- Slow drying preserves essential oil and linalool content; rapid drying at high temperatures (>50°C) causes oil loss.

**(iii) Threshing:**
- Done by **beating with sticks** or **trampling** with bullocks (traditional) or using **mechanical threshers**.
- Care should be taken to avoid seed breakage (which lowers market grade).

**(iv) Cleaning and Grading:**
- Winnowing to remove chaff, dust, and light materials.
- Sieving to remove shriveled, broken, and discolored seeds.
- Grading based on size, color, and aroma — bold, golden-yellow, well-dried seeds fetch premium prices.
- ISI specifications require moisture ≤ 8%, extraneous matter ≤ 2%, damaged seeds ≤ 3%.

**(v) Storage:**
- Store in clean **jute bags or polypropylene bags** in cool, dry, well-ventilated godowns.
- Moisture should be maintained below **8%** to prevent fungal and insect attack.
- Treat storage premises with **malathion** or fumigate with **aluminum phosphide** (under supervision) to control storage pests.
- Properly stored coriander can retain quality for **1–2 years**.

---

#### Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]
**Explain the methods of extraction of essential oil and oleoresin from coriander seeds. Mention their industrial applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Essential Oil Extraction:**

**Method 1 — Steam Distillation (most common):**

$$
\text{Coriander seeds (crushed)} \xrightarrow{\text{Steam}} \text{Vapor mixture} \xrightarrow{\text{Condensation}} \text{Decantation} \rightarrow \text{Pure essential oil}
$$

- Dried, crushed seeds are placed in a distillation chamber.
- Steam passed through the seed mass volatilizes the essential oil.
- Vapor mixture condenses in a condenser; oil separates from water based on density (oil is lighter).
- Oil yield: **0.2–1.0% (v/w)**.

**Method 2 — Hydrodistillation:**
- Seeds directly immersed in water and boiled; vapor condensed and oil collected.
- Slightly higher oil recovery but risk of thermal degradation.

**Method 3 — Supercritical CO₂ Extraction (modern):**
- Uses supercritical carbon dioxide as solvent at high pressure.
- Yields superior-quality oil with no solvent residues.
- Expensive; used for high-value pharmaceutical applications.

**(ii) Oleoresin Extraction:**

**Method — Solvent Extraction:**
- Ground seeds are treated with organic solvents (acetone, ethanol, or hexane).
- Solvent is then evaporated to obtain the viscous **oleoresin**.
- Oleoresin contains both **essential oil and non-volatile resinous matter** — provides a complete flavor profile.
- Yield: **10–15% of seed weight**.

**(iii) Industrial Applications:**

| Product | Industry | Use |
|---------|----------|-----|
| Essential oil | Perfumery | Fragrance fixative; linalool-rich formulation |
| Essential oil | Food | Flavoring in gin, liqueurs, baked goods |
| Essential oil | Pharma | Anxiolytic, anti-inflammatory formulations |
| Essential oil | Aromatherapy | Stress relief and mood enhancement |
| Oleoresin | Food processing | Curry powder, ready-to-eat meals, pickles |
| Oleoresin | Seasoning industry | Standardized flavoring blends |
| Linalool | Cosmetics | Soaps, lotions, fragrances |

> **Industrial Insight:** 1 tonne of dried coriander seeds yields approximately **2–10 kg of essential oil** and **100–150 kg of oleoresin**, depending on variety and extraction method.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Coriander Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong family:** Many students write "Labiatae" or "Solanaceae" — the correct family is **Apiaceae (Umbelliferae)**. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing seed and fruit:** The "coriander seed" of commerce is botanically a **dry fruit (cremocarp/schizocarp)** that splits into two mericarps. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Incorrect sowing time:** Do not write "April–May" for plains — coriander is a **rabi crop sown in October–November**. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 4. **Pest/disease confusion:** Stem gall and powdery mildew are most common; mentioning **stem gall** by name (Protomyces macrosporus) scores more than vague terms like "fungal disease". Marks lost: 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Linalool percentage:** State **60–70%** of essential oil, not just "major component". Marks lost: 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Spacing and seed rate:** Be precise — 30 cm × 10–15 cm and 15–20 kg/ha (line sowing). Marks lost: 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist — Coriander**

- **Botanical name:** *Coriandrum sativum* L. — **Family: Apiaceae** — **2n = 22**.
- **Plant type:** Annual herb; **seed spice** and **leafy herb** dual-purpose crop.
- **Origin:** Mediterranean region; **commercial centre of diversity: India** (largest producer).
- **Major producing states (India):** Rajasthan > Madhya Pradesh > Gujarat > Andhra Pradesh.
- **Sowing season:** Rabi (Oct–Nov); also Kharif in hills (June–July).
- **Soil & climate:** Loamy-sandy loam soil, pH 6.0–7.5; cool, dry climate (15–25°C).
- **Spacing:** 30 cm × 10–15 cm; **seed rate:** 15–20 kg/ha (line sowing).
- **Seed treatment:** *Trichoderma viride* @ 4 g/kg.
- **Fertilizer:** N:P:K = 40:30:20 kg/ha + 20 kg S/ha (for oil quality).
- **Critical irrigations:** Seedling stage, flowering, and seed development.
- **Harvesting:** 90–110 DAS at 50% umbel yellowing.
- **Drying:** Sun-dry to **8% moisture content** for safe storage.
- **Yield:** 1.5–2.0 t/ha (national average); 2.5–3.0 t/ha (improved varieties under management).
- **Essential oil content:** 0.2–1.0%; **linalool = 60–70%** of total oil.
- **Extraction:** Steam distillation (oil); solvent extraction (oleoresin).
- **Key diseases:** Powdery mildew (*Erysiphe polygoni*), stem gall (*Protomyces macrosporus*), Fusarium wilt.
- **Key pests:** Aphid (*Myzus persicae*), whitefly, coriander caterpillar.
- **Important varieties:** ACr-1, CO-1, CO-2, CO-3, GC-1, GC-2, Rajat (ACr-2), Sadhna, Hisar Sugandh, Swathi.
- **Industrial products:** Whole seed, ground powder, essential oil, oleoresin, linalool isolate.
- **Medicinal uses:** Carminative, digestive, anti-inflammatory, anti-microbial, anti-diabetic.
- **KTU Frequently Asked Topics:** Package of practices, pest-disease management, post-harvest processing, essential oil composition.

> **End of Module 2 — Coriander Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme**
