topics.title: Cinnamon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cinnamon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Cinnamon

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Cinnamon** is the dried, inner bark obtained from the shoots (coppiced stems) of the evergreen tropical tree *Cinnamomum verum* J. Presl (syn. *Cinnamomum zeylanicum* Blume), belonging to the family **Lauraceae**. It is classified as a **bark spice** and is among the world's oldest known spices, valued for its characteristic aroma, warm sweet flavour, and pharmacological properties attributable to its principal aromatic compound, **cinnamaldehyde**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — *Cinnamomum* Species of Trade Importance**
> - *Cinnamomum verum* (Syn. *C. zeylanicum*) — **Ceylon Cinnamon** ("True Cinnamon") — Sri Lanka origin.
> - *Cinnamomum cassia* (Syn. *C. aromaticum*) — **Cassia / Chinese Cinnamon** — China, Indonesia, Vietnam.
> - *Cinnamomum burmannii* — **Indonesian Korintje Cassia**.
> - *Cinnamomum loureiroi* — **Vietnamese Cassia (Saigon Cinnamon)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **cinnamon** as the **"rolled-up aromatic newspaper" of a tree**:
- A tree is grown, and when its stems are pencil-thick, they are cut.
- The outer rough bark is **scraped off** (like peeling dead skin off a stick).
- The thin, fragrant inner bark is **pried loose**, dried, and **rolled by hand into a cigar-shaped tube** (the famous "quill").
- Inside this rolled "scroll" is locked the volatile aromatic oil that gives cinnamon its identity.

Just as coffee beans are the roasted seeds of a fruit, **the cinnamon stick is not the whole tree — it is the peeled and rolled lining of a young stem**, carefully dried to lock in its aroma.

## 1.3 Standard Botanical & Trade Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Botanical name | *Cinnamomum verum* J. Presl |
| Family | **Lauraceae** |
| Chromosome number (2n) | **24** |
| Plant type | Evergreen tree, 10–15 m (coppiced to 2–3 m) |
| Edible part | **Inner bark (secondary phloem)** |
| Time to first harvest | **2–3 years** after planting |
| Economic life | **30–40 years** (ratoon/coppice crop) |
| Bark oil yield | **0.5 – 1.0 %** (dry weight basis) |
| Leaf oil yield | **0.5 – 1.8 %** (fresh weight basis) |
| Bark oleoresin yield | **8 – 12 %** (solvent extractable) |
| ISO Standard (Ceylon) | **ISO 6539 : 2014** |
| ISO Standard (Cassia) | **ISO 6538 : 2014** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** Ceylon cinnamon contains very low coumarin (< **0.01 %**), whereas Cassia contains high coumarin (**0.45 – 1.2 %**), making Ceylon cinnamon the safer "True Cinnamon" for regular dietary use.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Botany & Morphology

- **Habit:** Small evergreen tree; in commercial cultivation, the main stem is **coppiced** (cut back) to induce a bushy growth of straight, vertical shoots (called **"stools"**).
- **Leaves:** Opposite or sub-opposite, coriaceous, **trinerved** (three prominent veins from base) — a diagnostic feature of the genus *Cinnamomum*.
- **Inflorescence:** Axillary or terminal **panicles** of small, yellowish, hermaphrodite flowers.
- **Fruit:** A **drupe**, oblong, 1.5–2 cm long, black when ripe, partly enclosed by the persistent, cup-shaped receptacle.
- **Bark:** On young coppice shoots, the bark separates easily into **outer bark (corky)** and **inner bark (the commercial product)**.

## 2.2 Origin, Distribution & Global Production

- **Centre of Origin:** Sri Lanka (Ceylon) — *Cinnamomum verum* is endemic to the island.
- **Major Producers (2023 FAO data):**
  - **Indonesia** — World's #1 producer (mostly Cassia: *C. burmannii*).
  - **China** — Cassia (mainly *C. cassia* / *C. loureiroi*).
  - **Vietnam** — Saigon cassia.
  - **Sri Lanka** — True Ceylon cinnamon.
  - **India** — Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu (Idukki, Kottayam, Kannur zones).
- **Major Exporters to India:** Sri Lanka (Ceylon), Indonesia, China.

## 2.3 Climate & Soil Requirements

- **Climate:** Tropical, humid; altitude up to **1000 m** (Ceylon type prefers cooler hills; Cassia tolerates hotter plains).
- **Rainfall:** **1500 – 2500 mm** well distributed.
- **Temperature:** **20 – 30 °C**; sensitive to frost.
- **Soil:** Deep, well-drained, sandy loam rich in organic matter; tolerates pH **4.5 – 6.5** (acidic); **laterite and red loams** are ideal.
- **Drainage:** Stagnant water is lethal — cinnamon is highly susceptible to **"cinnamon root rot"** (*Phytophthora cinnamomi*).

## 2.4 Propagation & Planting

| Method | Details | KTU Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Seeds** | Most common; seeds recalcitrant, viability < 7 days | Collect from selected mother trees |
| **Vegetative** | Semi-hardwood cuttings, air-layering, approach grafting | Used for true-to-type planting |
| Nursery | Seeds in poly-bags, shade 50 % for 3 months | 6–8 month old seedlings transplanted |
| Spacing | **2 m × 2 m** (high-density) or **3 m × 3 m** (normal) | ~ 2500 plants / ha (HD) |
| Planting season | Onset of monsoon (June–July) | Pit size 60 cm³ |

## 2.5 Manuring & Irrigation

- **NPK Recommendation (India, KAU):** 100 : 50 : 100 g NPK per tree/year, split-applied.
- **Organic:** **10–15 kg FYM/tree/year** + green mulching.
- **Irrigation:** Essential during summer (Dec–Mar in Kerala); drip @ **8–10 L/tree/day** recommended.

## 2.6 KTU Formula & Standard Cheat Sheet (Cinnamon)

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bark oil recovery | $Oil\,\% = \dfrac{V_{oil}\,\,(mL)}{M_{bark}\,\,(g)} \times 100$ | % v/w |
| Cinnamaldehyde content | $C_{ald}\,\% = \dfrac{A_{ald}}{A_{std}} \times C_{std} \times \dfrac{V}{m}$ | % w/w |
| Bark yield (quills) | $Y_{quill} = N_{shoots} \times L_{quill} \times T_{bark}$ | kg dry/ha/yr |
| Quill diameter grading | Compactor $\phi \le 6$ mm (H1) to $\ge 22$ mm (H6) | mm |
| AGMARK grades (India) | **Special, Standard, Fair, Good, Medium, Ordinary** | 6 grades |
| ASTA Cleanliness spec. | Extraneous matter $\le 1$ % | % |
| Total ash (Ceylon) | $\le 5$ % (max) | % w/w |
| Acid insoluble ash | $\le 1$ % (max) | % w/w |
| Volatile oil (Ceylon) | $\ge 1$ % v/w (min) | % |
| Moisture (whole) | $\le 12$ % (max) | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of the volatile-oil formula:** Plant breeders and processing engineers use the % oil recovery formula to determine **optimum harvesting time** — when cinnamaldehyde content is at its peak, maximizing both flavour and pharmacological value.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Processing, Chemistry & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Biochemical Pathway of Cinnamaldehyde Biosynthesis (Overview)

Cinnamon's signature aroma arises from **cinnamaldehyde (C₉H₈O, MW = 132.16 g/mol)**, synthesised via the **shikimate → phenylpropanoid pathway** in the phloem parenchyma cells.

$$\text{Phenylalanine} \xrightarrow{\text{PAL}} \text{trans-Cinnamic acid} \xrightarrow{\text{4CL}} \text{Cinnamoyl-CoA} \xrightarrow{\text{HCT}} \text{Cinnamaldehyde} \xrightarrow{\text{CCR}} \text{Cinnamyl alcohol}$$

Where:
- **PAL** = Phenylalanine Ammonia-Lyase (key rate-limiting enzyme).
- **4CL** = 4-Coumarate:CoA Ligase.
- **HCT** = Hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA Transferase.
- **CCR** = Cinnamoyl-CoA Reductase.

## 3.2 Chemical Composition of True Cinnamon Bark (Dry Weight Basis)

| Component | Composition | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cinnamaldehyde (trans-) | **60 – 75 %** | Principal aroma compound |
| Eugenol | 5 – 10 % | Spicy, clove-like note |
| Linalool | 1 – 5 % | Floral, sweet note |
| β-Caryophyllene | 1 – 4 % | Woody note |
| Cinnamyl acetate | 1 – 5 % | Sweet, fruity note |
| Coumarin | $\le 0.01$ % | Bitter, hepatotoxic (regulated) |
| Tannins, mucilage, resins | 5 – 10 % | Astringency, body |
| Moisture | 8 – 12 % | — |
| Ash | 3 – 5 % | Mineral residue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cassia vs Ceylon — Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**
> Cassia bark oil contains **75 – 90 % cinnamaldehyde** (higher than Ceylon) but **0.45 – 1.2 % coumarin**. Ceylon contains only **0.01 % coumarin** but **55 – 75 % cinnamaldehyde**. This is the basis of premium pricing of Ceylon cinnamon in the EU and US "health-food" markets.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Processing of True Cinnamon (Ceylon Method)

The processing of Ceylon cinnamon is highly **labour-intensive and artisanal**, with the "**peeler (quill-maker)**" being the most skilled worker.

### Stage 1 — Harvesting (Coppicing)

The bush is harvested when shoots are **1.5 – 2.0 m tall** and **pencil-thick (1.2 – 2.5 cm diameter)**, usually at the **green / immature bark stage** when the bark peels easily.

### Stage 2 — Peeling (Bark Removal)

1. **Stem is cut at the base** with a sharp curved knife (*ketta*).
2. **Scrape off outer cork (epidermis)** using a specially shaped brass scraping knife (*koomaram*).
3. **Tap gently along the stem** to loosen the inner bark from the wood.
4. **Slit longitudinally** and **peel off the inner bark** in long strips (*"quill bark"*).

### Stage 3 — Piping / Quilling (Ceylon speciality)

1. Peeled bark strips are **nested inside one another** (smaller inside larger).
2. The nested bundle is **rolled by hand on a wooden rail** to form a single compound "**quill**".
3. **Sun-dry** on a raised platform for **4 – 7 days** until moisture < 10 %.
4. Quills are **trimmed, polished**, and sorted.

### Stage 4 — Grading

Cinnamon quills are graded by **diameter (compactor size)** and **appearance**:

| Grade | Quill Diameter | Quills / kg |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **H 1 (Fine)** | $\le 6$ mm | 75 – 100 |
| H 2 | 7 – 10 mm | 60 – 80 |
| H 3 | 11 – 14 mm | 50 – 65 |
| H 4 | 15 – 18 mm | 40 – 50 |
| H 5 | 19 – 22 mm | 30 – 40 |
| **H 6 (Crude)** | $\ge 22$ mm | 20 – 30 |

Other commercial forms:
- **Quillings** (broken pieces, smaller grade).
- **Featherings** (inner bark from twigs).
- **Chips** (bark trimmings, used for oil distillation).
- **Cinnamon Powder** (ground quills; should be $\le 1$ % ash, $\le 0.5$ % extraneous matter by ASTA).

### Stage 5 — Oil Distillation

- **Method:** Steam distillation or hydro-distillation in **Clevenger-type apparatus** (laboratory) or industrial copper stills.
- **Raw material:** Bark chips/featherings (higher yield) OR leaves (yield **eugenol-rich oil**, used differently).
- **Distillation time:** **4 – 8 hours**.
- **Recovery yield:**

$$R_{oil}\,\,(mL) = \dfrac{\text{Oil collected}}{\text{Weight of dry bark}} \times 100$$

- **Bark oil:** pale yellow, cinnamaldehyde-rich (used in flavouring).
- **Leaf oil:** dark brown, eugenol-rich (70–85 %), used in **perfumery and dentistry** (eugenol = dental analgesic).

### Stage 6 — Oleoresin Extraction

- **Method:** Solvent extraction (acetone / ethanol / hexane) of ground bark.
- **Yield:** **8 – 12 %** w/w.
- **Composition:** Volatile oil + resins + fixed oils + pigments; represents the **complete flavour profile** of cinnamon.
- **Application:** Standardized seasoning in processed foods (sauces, pickles, baked goods, meat products).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Question:** A batch of **5.0 kg** of dried cinnamon bark is subjected to steam distillation. The volume of oil recovered is **42 mL**. Calculate the **% oil recovery (v/w)**, and state if it meets the **ISO 6539 standard** for Ceylon cinnamon.

**Step 1 — Convert all to consistent units.**

$$m_{bark} = 5.0 \text{ kg} = 5000 \text{ g}, \quad V_{oil} = 42 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the % oil recovery formula.**

$$\%\,Oil = \dfrac{V_{oil}\,(mL)}{m_{bark}\,(g)} \times 100 = \dfrac{42}{5000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%\,Oil = 0.84\,\%\,v/w}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standard.**

> ISO 6539 : 2014 (Ceylon cinnamon, whole bark) specifies a **minimum of 1.0 % v/w volatile oil**.
>
> **Result:** The sample is **sub-standard** and does **not** meet ISO 6539. Possible causes: over-drying, old/aged bark, or immature harvest. The processor should investigate harvesting stage and drying protocol.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Botanical / Morphology Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef trunk fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef flower fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fruit fill:#4B0082,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bark fill:#D2691E,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef root fill:#654321,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px

    rootA[Root system — tap and lateral]:::root
    trunkA[Main stem — coppiced to 2 to 3 m]:::trunk
    shootA[Coppice shoots — 1.5 to 2 m tall]:::trunk
    leafA[Leaves — opposite, coriaceous, trinerved]:::leaf
    flowerA[Panicle inflorescence — yellowish hermaphrodite]:::flower
    fruitA[Drupe — black when ripe, partly enclosed in cupula]:::fruit
    barkA[Inner bark — peeled and quilled]:::bark

    rootA --> trunkA
    trunkA --> shootA
    shootA --> leafA
    shootA --> flowerA
    flowerA --> fruitA
    shootA --> barkA
```

## 4.2 Processing Flow of True Cinnamon (Ceylon Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#DEB887,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef final fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef discard fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:1px

    A[Coppiced shoots 1.5 to 2 m]:::step
    B[Cut at base]:::step
    C[Strip outer cork]:::step
    D[Slit and peel inner bark]:::step
    E[Nest and roll into quills]:::step
    F[Sun dry 4 to 7 days]:::step
    G[Trim and grade by diameter]:::step
    H1[Quills grade H1 to H6]:::final
    H2[Cinnamon powder]:::final
    H3[Quillings and featherings]:::step
    I[Steam distillation]:::step
    J[Bark oil — cinnamaldehyde rich]:::final
    K[Leaves — eugenol rich oil]:::final
    L[Oleoresin — solvent extract]:::final
    M[Bark chips — by product for fuel]:::discard

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G --> H1
    G --> H2
    G --> H3
    H3 --> I
    I --> J
    A --> K
    D --> L
    F --> M
```

## 4.3 Cinnamaldehyde Biosynthesis Pathway (Phenylpropanoid Route)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef enzyme fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef metabolite fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef target fill:#B22222,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px

    M1[Phenylalanine]:::metabolite
    M2[trans Cinnamic acid]:::metabolite
    M3[Cinnamoyl CoA]:::metabolite
    M4[Cinnamaldehyde]:::target
    M5[Cinnamyl alcohol]:::metabolite

    E1[PAL enzyme]:::enzyme
    E2[4CL enzyme]:::enzyme
    E3[HCT enzyme]:::enzyme
    E4[CCR enzyme]:::enzyme

    M1 -->|deamination| M2
    E1 -.->|catalyzes| M1
    M2 -->|CoA activation| M3
    E2 -.->|catalyzes| M2
    M3 -->|reduction| M4
    E3 -.->|catalyzes| M3
    M4 -->|reduction| M5
    E4 -.->|catalyzes| M4
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Ceylon cinnamon and Cassia with respect to botanical name, coumarin content, and bark appearance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ceylon Cinnamon | Cassia |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Botanical name | *Cinnamomum verum* J. Presl | *Cinnamomum cassia* (Blume) |
| Coumarin content | $\le 0.01$ % (very low) | $0.45$ to $1.2$ % (high) |
| Bark appearance | Thin, multiple soft layers rolled into compound quills; smooth tan-brown | Thick, hard, single-layer curled "cigar" quill; rough reddish-brown |
| Cinnamaldehyde | 55 – 75 % | 75 – 90 % |
| Origin / major producer | Sri Lanka | China, Indonesia, Vietnam |

> **Valuation Tip:** A complete answer must list **at least 3 contrasting points**; missing coumarin content = loss of 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**What are the commercial grades of cinnamon quills? List them in increasing order of diameter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Commercial quill grades of Ceylon cinnamon, in increasing order of diameter (compactor size):
- **H 1 (Fine)** — $\phi \le 6$ mm
- **H 2** — $7$ to $10$ mm
- **H 3** — $11$ to $14$ mm
- **H 4** — $15$ to $18$ mm
- **H 5** — $19$ to $22$ mm
- **H 6 (Crude)** — $\phi \ge 22$ mm

The smaller the diameter, the more skilled the peeling work and the **higher the price** per kg.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **origin, botany, climate and soil requirements** of *Cinnamomum verum* in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **complete post-harvest processing of Ceylon cinnamon** including harvesting, peeling, quilling, drying, grading, and the chemistry of the volatile oil. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q A (a)

**Origin & Distribution (2 marks):**
- True cinnamon (*Cinnamomum verum*) is **endemic to Sri Lanka**, the original centre of origin.
- It is now cultivated in **India (Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu), Seychelles, Madagascar, Brazil, and the West Indies**.
- India ranks among the **top 5 producers** in the world.

**Botany (3 marks):**
- **Family:** Lauraceae; **Chromosome number:** 2n = 24.
- **Habit:** Evergreen tree, naturally 10–15 m; commercially maintained as a low bush (2–3 m) through **coppicing**.
- **Leaves:** Opposite, leathery, **trinerved** (3 main veins from base) — diagnostic feature.
- **Flowers:** Small, yellowish, in **axillary/terminal panicles**, bisexual, insect-pollinated.
- **Fruit:** A small **drupe** (1.5–2 cm), black when ripe, seated on an enlarged fleshy receptacle (cupula).
- **Bark:** On young coppice shoots, the bark is easily separated into **outer corky bark (discarded)** and **inner bark (the spice)**.

**Climate & Soil (2 marks):**
- **Altitude:** Sea level up to 1000 m.
- **Temperature:** 20 – 30 °C; no frost.
- **Rainfall:** 1500 – 2500 mm, well distributed.
- **Soil:** Deep, well-drained, **sandy loam to laterite**, rich in organic matter.
- **pH:** 4.5 – 6.5 (acid-loving).
- **Drainage:** Excellent drainage mandatory — waterlogging causes **Phytophthora root rot**.

---

#### Model Answer — Q A (b)

**Harvesting (2 marks):**
- First harvest at **2 – 3 years** after planting.
- Bush is **coppiced** to ground level during the **rainy season** (June–Sept) when bark peels easily.
- Subsequent **ratoon crops** at **1.5 – 2 year intervals**.
- **Ideal shoot diameter:** 1.2 – 2.5 cm; **length:** 1.5 – 2.0 m.

**Peeling (2 marks):**
- Stems cut at base, then **scraped clean** of outer cork with a brass scraper.
- Bark is **beaten gently** with a wooden mallet to loosen it from the wood.
- A longitudinal slit is made and the **inner bark is peeled off** in long strips (≈ 1 m).

**Quilling / Piping (2 marks):**
- Bark strips are **nested inside each other** (smaller inside larger) and **rolled on a wooden rail** by hand to form a single compound "**quill**".
- This is the **unique Ceylon process** that distinguishes true cinnamon from cassia.

**Drying & Grading (1 mark):**
- Quills sun-dried for **4 – 7 days** on raised platforms until moisture < 10 %.
- Graded by **diameter (H1 to H6)**, sorted, and polished.

**Volatile Oil Chemistry (bonus / 1 mark cross-over):**
- Bark oil: **55 – 75 % trans-cinnamaldehyde**, plus eugenol (5–10 %), linalool, β-caryophyllene.
- Leaf oil: **70 – 85 % eugenol** (used in dentistry as a topical analgesic).

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **chemical composition of cinnamon bark** with emphasis on cinnamaldehyde. What are the differences in chemistry between **Ceylon cinnamon and Cassia**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write in detail the **methods of extraction of cinnamon oil and oleoresin**, their yield, composition, and **industrial applications**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q B (a)

**Composition of cinnamon bark (4 marks):**

$$\text{Volatile oil: 1 to 2 \%} \quad\quad \text{Non-volatile extractives: 6 to 10 \%}$$

Major constituents of **bark essential oil** (analysed by GC-MS):

| Compound | % in Ceylon | % in Cassia | Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **trans-Cinnamaldehyde** | 55 – 75 | 75 – 90 | Phenylpropanoid — main aroma |
| **Eugenol** | 5 – 10 | trace – 5 | Phenylpropanoid — clove note |
| **Coumarin** | $\le 0.01$ | 0.45 – 1.2 | Coumarin — hepatotoxic |
| Linalool | 1 – 5 | trace | Monoterpene alcohol |
| β-Caryophyllene | 1 – 4 | 1 – 3 | Sesquiterpene |
| Cinnamyl acetate | 1 – 5 | 0.5 – 2 | Ester — sweet-fruity |

**Ceylon vs Cassia Chemistry (3 marks):**
- **Cinnamaldehyde:** Cassia has *higher* % cinnamaldehyde → stronger aroma, harsher.
- **Coumarin:** Cassia has **100× more coumarin** → banned/restricted in the EU for daily-consumption foods; Ceylon has virtually none.
- **Eugenol:** Ceylon leaf oil is **eugenol-dominant (70–85 %)**; bark is cinnamaldehyde-dominant.
- **Toxicity:** Cassia's coumarin (LD₅₀ in rats ~ 0.7 g/kg oral) makes long-term dietary use of cassia a hepatotoxic risk; Ceylon is GRAS for daily use.

**Structural formula of cinnamaldehyde (trans-):**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH}=\text{CH–CHO} \quad\quad (M.W. = 132.16 \text{ g/mol})$$

---

#### Model Answer — Q B (b)

**Extraction of Cinnamon Bark Oil (3 marks):**
- **Method:** **Steam distillation** (industrial) or **hydro-distillation** in Clevenger apparatus (lab).
- **Raw material:** Bark chips/featherings (by-products) — preferred because they don't compromise quill yield.
- **Conditions:** 100 °C, 4 – 8 hours, atmospheric pressure.
- **Yield:** **0.5 – 1.0 %** v/w (Ceylon) / **1.0 – 2.0 %** v/w (Cassia).
- **Composition:** Cinnamaldehyde-rich, pale yellow liquid, density ~ 1.03 g/mL.

**Extraction of Cinnamon Leaf Oil (2 marks):**
- **Method:** Same — steam distillation of fresh or partly-wilted leaves.
- **Yield:** **0.5 – 1.8 %** v/w (fresh weight).
- **Composition:** **Eugenol-dominant (70 – 85 %)**, dark brown, used as a cheaper substitute for clove oil.

**Extraction of Oleoresin (2 marks):**
- **Method:** **Solvent extraction** (acetone, ethanol, or hexane) of ground bark in Soxhlet apparatus.
- **Solvent recovery:** Distilled off at 40 – 60 °C under vacuum.
- **Yield:** **8 – 12 %** w/w.
- **Composition:** Volatile oil + resins + fixed oils + pigments → represents the **complete sensory profile** of the spice.

**Industrial Applications:**

| Product | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Bark oil (cinnamaldehyde) | Flavouring baked goods, confectionery, beverages, chewing gum |
| Leaf oil (eugenol) | Dental cement, antiseptic, perfumery (clove base note) |
| Oleoresin | Seasoning blends, sauces, meat products, pickles |
| Powder | Spice rack, Ayurvedic medicine, cinnamon tea (anti-diabetic) |
| Cinnamaldehyde | Insect repellent, anti-fungal agrochemical, fragrance fixative |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Confusing **bark oil** with **leaf oil** — they are chemically *opposite* (cinnamaldehyde vs eugenol dominant). 2 marks lost easily.
> 2. Quoting **oleoresin yield (8–12 %)** as if it were **oil yield (0.5–1 %)** — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **coumarin toxicity** when comparing Cassia vs Ceylon — costs at least 1 mark.
> 4. Writing "oil is extracted by solvent extraction" — **wrong**; oil is by distillation; oleoresin is by solvent extraction. This conflation costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Botanical name:** *Cinnamomum verum* (Ceylon) / *Cinnamomum cassia* (Cassia); **Family:** Lauraceae; **2n = 24**.
- **Edible part:** **Inner bark (secondary phloem)** of coppiced shoots.
- **Diagnostic leaf feature:** **Trinerved** (3 veins from base).
- **Origin:** Sri Lanka (endemic).
- **India's main cinnamon zones:** Idukki, Kottayam, Kannur (Kerala); parts of Karnataka & Tamil Nadu.
- **Climate:** Tropical, 20 – 30 °C, 1500 – 2500 mm rainfall, acidic (pH 4.5 – 6.5), well-drained laterite/loam.
- **Propagation:** Seed (recalcitrant, viability < 7 days); semi-hardwood cuttings, air-layering for true-to-type.
- **Spacing:** 2 m × 2 m (HD) or 3 m × 3 m.
- **Manuring:** NPK 100:50:100 g/tree/yr + 10–15 kg FYM.
- **First harvest:** 2 – 3 years after planting; ratoon every 1.5 – 2 years.
- **Indicator for harvest:** Shoots pencil-thick (1.2 – 2.5 cm diameter).
- **Unique Ceylon process:** "**Quilling**" — nesting bark strips inside each other and hand-rolling.
- **Grades:** **H1 to H6** by quill diameter (6 mm → 22 mm).
- **Drying:** Sun-dry 4 – 7 days to moisture $\le 10$ %.
- **Cinnamaldehyde formula:** $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH}=\text{CH–CHO}$; **MW = 132.16 g/mol**.
- **Bark oil:** 0.5 – 1.0 % yield; cinnamaldehyde 55 – 90 %.
- **Leaf oil:** 0.5 – 1.8 % yield; **eugenol 70 – 85 %** (used in dentistry).
- **Oleoresin:** Solvent extraction, 8 – 12 % yield.
- **Coumarin:** Ceylon $\le 0.01$ % vs Cassia 0.45 – 1.2 % → **Ceylon safer for daily use**.
- **ISO standards:** **ISO 6539 (Ceylon)**, **ISO 6538 (Cassia)**.
- **Biosynthesis:** Phenylalanine → Cinnamic acid → Cinnamoyl-CoA → **Cinnamaldehyde** (PAL, 4CL, HCT enzymes).
- **AGMARK / ASTA specs:** Total ash $\le 5$ %, acid-insoluble ash $\le 1$ %, volatile oil $\ge 1$ %, moisture $\le 12$ %, extraneous matter $\le 1$ %.
- **Key industrial product distinction:** Bark oil ≠ Leaf oil ≠ Oleoresin (chemistry, method, use all differ).
- **Pharmacology:** Anti-diabetic (mimics insulin), anti-microbial (cinnamaldehyde), anti-inflammatory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fenugreek
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fenugreek

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fenugreek** (*Trigonella foenum-graecum* L.) is an annual herbaceous spice and condiment crop belonging to the family **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)**, sub-family **Papilionoideae**. The plant produces aromatic seeds and leaves that are widely used as a culinary spice, medicinal herb, and forage crop. Botanically, the generic name *Trigonella* is derived from the Latin word *"trigona"* (three-angled), referring to the **triangular shape of the corolla**, while the species epithet *foenum-graecum* literally means **"Greek hay,"** indicating its historical use as animal fodder in the Mediterranean basin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Note:** Fenugreek is classified under **seed spices** in the KTU module framework, alongside coriander, cumin, fennel, and dill. The complete botanical trinomial *Trigonella foenum-graecum* (2n = 2x = **16 chromosomes**) must be memorized verbatim for Part A short-answer questions.

### Taxonomic Classification

| Rank | Classification |
|------|----------------|
| Kingdom | Plantae |
| Division | Magnoliophyta (Angiosperms) |
| Class | Dicotyledonae |
| Order | Fabales |
| Family | Fabaceae (Leguminosae) |
| Sub-family | Papilionoideae |
| Genus | *Trigonella* |
| Species | *T. foenum-graecum* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of fenugreek as the **"Swiss Army Knife"** of the spice world. Just as a Swiss Army Knife has multiple blades serving different purposes (cutter, screwdriver, scissors), the fenugreek plant provides **three distinct economic products** from a single plant:

1. **Fresh leaves** (called *methi* in India) — used as a leafy vegetable and garnish (like coriander leaves).
2. **Dried seeds** — used as a whole spice, ground spice, and flavoring agent (like cumin or mustard).
3. **Galactomannan gum** — extracted industrially as a thickening agent (analogous to guar gum from guar bean).

The seeds, when sprouted (fenugreek sprouts), develop a **maple-syrup-like aroma** due to a compound called **sotolone (3-hydroxy-4,5-dimethyl-2(5H)-furanone)**. This same aroma is what gives curry powders and imitation maple syrup their characteristic warm, sweet, slightly burnt-sugar fragrance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world application cue:** The bottled "maple-flavored" syrup sold in supermarkets often contains fenugreek extract as the primary flavor source — a cheaper substitute for true maple syrup. This makes fenugreek a key ingredient in the **global flavor industry**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Chromosome number (2n):** **16**
- **Ploidy level:** Diploid (2x)
- **Seed oil content:** **6–10%**
- **Protein content of seeds:** **20–30%**
- **Galactomannan (gum) content:** **15–20%** of seed husk
- **Trigonelline content:** **0.35–1.0%** in seeds
- **Diosgenin content:** **0.8–2.2%** in seeds
- **Ideal seed color (mature):** **Yellowish-brown to golden-brown**
- **1000-seed weight (test weight):** **10–16 grams**

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "list the economic products of fenugreek" in a 3-mark question, always mention: (i) Leafy vegetable, (ii) Condiment/spice, (iii) Galactomannan gum, (iv) Medicinal (galactagogue, antidiabetic), and (v) Forage/fodder.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin, Distribution, and Economic Botany

### 2.1.1 Centre of Origin

Fenugreek is **indigenous to the Mediterranean region**, with secondary centres of diversity in **Central Asia, Ethiopia, and Northern India**. Archaeological evidence suggests its cultivation dates back to **4000 BCE** in the Fertile Crescent.

### 2.1.2 Global and Indian Scenario

**India is the world's largest producer and exporter** of fenugreek, contributing nearly **70–80% of global production**. Within India, the major producing states are:

| State | Share of National Production |
|-------|------------------------------|
| Rajasthan | ~35–40% |
| Gujarat | ~20–25% |
| Madhya Pradesh | ~10–15% |
| Uttar Pradesh | ~8–10% |
| Maharashtra & Punjab | ~5–8% |

## 2.2 Botany of the Plant

### 2.2.1 Morphological Features

- **Root:** Tap root system with abundant **rhizobial nodules** (nitrogen-fixing). Forms symbiosis with *Rhizobium meliloti*.
- **Stem:** Erect, hollow, branched, **30–80 cm tall**, herbaceous, slightly pubescent.
- **Leaves:** Pinnately trifoliate compound, resembling clover leaves. Leaflets are obovate, with toothed margins.
- **Flowers:** Papilionaceous (butterfly-shaped), pale yellow to white, **axillary, sessile or shortly pedicellate**, solitary or in pairs.
- **Fruit (Pod):** A **linear, sickle-shaped (falcate) legume**, 5–10 cm long, containing 10–20 seeds.
- **Seeds:** **Rhomboidal**, 3–5 mm long, with a deep groove dividing the seed into two unequal lobes. Color: golden-yellow to brown.

### 2.2.2 Floral Formula

The floral formula of fenugreek is a classic **papilionaceous (zygomorphic) flower**:

$$ \text{Br} \, \% \, \oplus \, \text{K}_{(5)} \, \text{C}_{1+2+(2)} \, \text{A}_{(9)+1} \, \text{G}_{\underline{1}} $$

Where:
- **Br** = Bracteate
- **%** = Zygomorphic (bilaterally symmetric)
- **⊕** = Bisexual
- **K(5)** = 5 fused sepals (gamosepalous calyx)
- **C1+2+(2)** = 5 petals arranged as standard (1) + wings (2) + fused keel (2)
- **A(9)+1** = 10 stamens, 9 fused into a tube + 1 free (diadelphous)
- **G̲1** = Monocarpellary, superior ovary with marginal placentation

## 2.3 Climate and Soil Requirements

### 2.3.1 Climate

Fenugreek is a **cool-season (rabi) crop**. The optimal requirements are:

| Parameter | Optimal Range |
|-----------|---------------|
| Temperature | 10–25 °C (germination: 15–20 °C) |
| Rainfall | 400–600 mm (annually) |
| Photoperiod | Day-neutral to slightly long-day |
| Frost tolerance | Moderate (young plants can tolerate -2 °C) |

### 2.3.2 Soil

- **Soil type:** Well-drained **loam to sandy loam** soils are ideal.
- **Soil pH:** **6.0–7.5** (slightly acidic to neutral).
- **Drainage:** Sensitive to waterlogging; **water stagnation causes root rot**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse fenugreek with cumin. Remember: **Fenugreek is a legume (Fabaceae)** that fixes nitrogen, whereas cumin is an Apiaceae member. This distinction matters in crop rotation planning.

## 2.4 Improved Varieties

| Variety | Source Institute | Maturity (days) | Special Feature |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Pusa Early Bunching | IARI, New Delhi | 80–90 | Early-maturing, high leaf yield |
| Pusa Kasuri | IARI | 110–120 | Large leaves, Kasuri-type |
| Kasuri Methi | PAU, Ludhiana | 100–110 | High aroma, slow bolting |
| CO 1 | TNAU, Coimbatore | 90–100 | Dual-purpose (leaf + seed) |
| CO 2 | TNAU | 100–110 | Bold seeds, high yield |
| RMt 1 | SKN Agriculture University, Jobner | 95–105 | Rajasthan-specific |
| RMt 143 | SKNAU | 100–110 | Drought tolerant |
| Lam Selection 1 | LAM, Gujarat | 100–110 | High diosgenin |
| Hisar Sonali | HAU, Hisar | 100–110 | Yellow seeds, high protein |
| Rajendra Kanti | RAU, Bihar | 90–100 | High seed yield |
| Pant Ragini | GBPUAT, Pantnagar | 110–120 | Cold tolerant |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit / Notes |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Seed rate (leaf type) | $S_r = 25 \text{ kg/ha}$ | Broadcast method |
| Seed rate (seed type) | $S_r = 15 \text{ kg/ha}$ | Line sowing |
| Row-to-row spacing (leaf) | $d_r = 20 \text{ cm}$ | Drill sowing |
| Plant-to-plant spacing (seed) | $d_p = 10 \text{ cm}$ | Thinning at 15 DAS |
| N fertilizer | $N = 20 \text{ kg/ha}$ | Basal application |
| P fertilizer | $P_2O_5 = 40 \text{ kg/ha}$ | Basal application |
| K fertilizer | $K_2O = 20 \text{ kg/ha}$ | Basal application |
| Sowing depth | $d_s = 2$ to $3 \text{ cm}$ | Avoid deeper sowing |
| Seed yield (rainfed) | $Y_s = 800$ to $1200 \text{ kg/ha}$ | |
| Seed yield (irrigated) | $Y_s = 1500$ to $2500 \text{ kg/ha}$ | |
| Leaf yield | $Y_L = 8000$ to $12000 \text{ kg/ha}$ | Multiple cuttings |
| Oil content | $O = 6$ to $10\%$ | Solvent extractable |
| Protein content | $P_p = 20$ to $30\%$ | Dry weight basis |
| Test weight | $W_{1000} = 10$ to $16 \text{ g}$ | Per 1000 seeds |

### Key Equations for Yield Economics

The **cost-benefit ratio (CBR)** is a common KTU 14-mark question for fenugreek cultivation:

$$ \text{CBR} = \frac{\text{Gross Return (Rs./ha)}}{\text{Total Cost of Cultivation (Rs./ha)}} $$

The **Net Return** is given by:

$$ \text{NR} = \text{GR} - \text{TC} $$

Where **GR = Y × P** (yield × price). A CBR > **1.5** is considered economically viable for fenugreek.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Fenugreek's industrial applications span **pharmaceutical, nutraceutical, food-processing, and cosmetic industries**:

- **Galactomannan gum** → Used as a stabilizer in ice creams, sauces, and bakery products.
- **Diosgenin** → A steroid precursor used in the synthesis of **progesterone, cortisone, and oral contraceptives**.
- **Trigonelline** → Studied for **antidiabetic and neuroprotective properties**.
- **Sotolone** → Used in flavoring industry for maple, caramel, and curry flavor profiles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Agronomic Package of Practices — Step-by-Step Derivation

### 3.1.1 Land Preparation

The field must be ploughed **2–3 times** to a fine tilth, followed by planking. The seedbed must be **level and weed-free**. Farmyard manure (FYM) is applied at the rate of:

$$ \text{FYM} = 10 \text{ to } 15 \text{ tonnes/ha} $$

**Conversion logic:** This rate corresponds to approximately **25–35 kg N/ha** equivalent through slow mineralization over the crop growth period.

### 3.1.2 Sowing Time

The sowing time depends on the cropping system and purpose:

| Purpose | Optimal Sowing Time |
|---------|---------------------|
| Leaf production | October to November (early rabi) |
| Seed production | November to December (main rabi) |

### 3.1.3 Seed Treatment

Seeds are treated with **Thiram or Captan at 2.5 g/kg seed** to protect against seed-borne diseases. For nitrogen-fixing benefits, **Rhizobium inoculation** is recommended:

$$ \text{Inoculum dose} = 200 \text{ g Rhizobium culture per } 10 \text{ kg seed} $$

### 3.1.4 Method of Sowing

Fenugreek is sown by:
- **Broadcasting** (traditional, for leaf purpose)
- **Line sowing** (recommended, in rows 20–30 cm apart)
- **Dibbling** (in rows, for seed production)

### 3.1.5 Seed Rate Derivation

For line sowing with a row spacing of **30 cm** and a target plant population of **40 plants/m²**:

$$ \text{Plant population per ha} = 40 \times 10000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ plants} $$

The seed rate (in kg/ha) is then derived as:

$$ S_r = \frac{\text{Total seeds required}}{\text{Seeds per kg} \times \text{Germination \%}} $$

Given that fenugreek has **~50,000–60,000 seeds per kg** and **germination % = 75–80%**:

$$ S_r = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{55{,}000 \times 0.78} \approx 9.3 \text{ kg/ha} $$

With a safety factor of ~1.5× (accounting for seedling mortality, bird damage, and uneven germination), the recommended seed rate becomes:

$$ S_r^{\text{recommended}} = 9.3 \times 1.5 \approx 14 \text{ kg/ha} \approx 15 \text{ kg/ha} $$

This matches the standard KTU recommendation of **15 kg/ha for line sowing**.

### 3.1.6 Manure and Fertilizer Derivation

The fertilizer recommendation is based on the **soil test crop response (STCR)** equation. For an average fertile soil with N-availability of 200 kg/ha:

$$ N_{\text{recommended}} = N_{\text{target}} - N_{\text{soil}} \times C_f $$

Where $C_f$ = 0.15 (correction factor for legume N credit + soil contribution). With $N_{\text{target}} = 30$ kg/ha:

$$ N_{\text{recommended}} = 30 - 200 \times 0.15 = 30 - 30 = 0 \text{ kg N/ha} $$

However, **starter N at 20 kg/ha** is universally recommended for initial establishment before nodulation begins (~15–20 days after sowing).

### 3.1.7 Irrigation Schedule

The **cumulative pan evaporation (CPE) method** is used:

| Growth Stage | Irrigation Depth (mm) | CPE Ratio |
|--------------|-----------------------|-----------|
| Germination (0–15 DAS) | 30–40 mm | - |
| Vegetative (15–45 DAS) | 40 mm | 1.0 |
| Flowering (45–75 DAS) | 40 mm | 0.75 |
| Pod development (75–100 DAS) | 40 mm | 0.5 |
| Maturity (100+ DAS) | Light / nil | - |

**Total water requirement:** **250–400 mm** (3–5 irrigations).

### 3.1.8 Interculture and Weed Control

- **First weeding:** 25–30 DAS
- **Second weeding:** 45–50 DAS
- **Pre-emergence herbicide:** Pendimethalin at 1.0 kg a.i./ha within 2 DAS
- **Post-emergence:** One hand hoeing at 30 DAS

## 3.2 Harvesting and Post-Harvest Technology

### 3.2.1 Harvesting Criteria

**For leaves:**
- First cutting: **30–40 DAS** (when plants have 4–6 leaves)
- Subsequent cuttings: Every 15–20 days
- Total cuttings: **4–6** per season

**For seeds:**
- Harvest when **75–80% of pods turn yellow-brown**
- Plants are cut at ground level in the morning to avoid shattering
- Bundles are sun-dried for **3–5 days**, then threshed

### 3.2.2 Threshing and Cleaning

The threshing can be done by:
- **Manual beating** with sticks
- **Mechanical threshers** (axial-flow type)

Clean seeds are obtained by **winnowing and sieving** through a **2 mm mesh**.

### 3.2.3 Seed Yield Calculation

$$ Y_s = N_p \times P_p \times W_s \times 10^{-4} \text{ (kg/ha)} $$

Where:
- $N_p$ = Number of plants per ha
- $P_p$ = Pods per plant (typically 15–25)
- $W_s$ = Seed weight per pod (typically 0.15–0.25 g)

For a typical field with $N_p = 4 \times 10^5$, $P_p = 20$, $W_s = 0.2$ g:

$$ Y_s = 4 \times 10^5 \times 20 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-4} = 1600 \text{ kg/ha} $$

## 3.3 Biochemical Composition — Analytical Matrix

The **chemical profile of mature fenugreek seeds** is essential for KTU questions on value-added products:

| Constituent | Percentage (% dry weight) | Industrial / Medicinal Use |
|-------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Carbohydrates (total) | 45–60% | Energy source |
| Galactomannan (gum) | 15–20% | Thickener, stabilizer |
| Proteins | 20–30% | Nutritional supplement |
| Lipids (fixed oil) | 6–10% | Cooking, cosmetic |
| Fiber (crude) | 7–10% | Digestive health |
| Minerals (ash) | 3–4% | Ca, P, Fe, Zn |
| Trigonelline (alkaloid) | 0.35–1.0% | Antidiabetic, neuroprotective |
| Diosgenin (saponin) | 0.8–2.2% | Steroid synthesis |
| Sotolone (lactone) | Trace (ppm) | Maple aroma |
| 4-Hydroxyisoleucine | 0.09% | Insulin secretagogue |

### Galactomannan Structure

The galactomannan gum from fenugreek is a **heteropolysaccharide** with a specific mannose:galactose (M:G) ratio:

$$ \text{M:G ratio (fenugreek)} = 1 : 1 $$

This is **distinct from guar gum (M:G = 1.6 : 1)** and **locust bean gum (M:G = 3.5 : 1)**. The structure is:

$$ [\text{Man}-\beta-(1 \rightarrow 4)-\text{Man}]_n \text{ with single Gal}-\alpha-(1 \rightarrow 6) \text{ branches} $$

The molecular weight of fenugreek galactomannan is approximately:

$$ M_w = 1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ Da} $$

This high molecular weight and the **1:1 M:G ratio** give fenugreek gum a **higher viscosity** and **better cold-water solubility** than guar gum, making it commercially valuable.

## 3.4 Extraction Process — Step-by-Step Derivation of Galactomannan

**Step 1 — Seed cleaning:** Fenugreek seeds are cleaned to remove foreign matter. Clean seeds are then **dehusked** by mild acid or thermal treatment.

**Step 2 — Gum extraction:** The dehusked endosperm is ground and treated with **hot water (80–90 °C)** at a seed-to-water ratio of:

$$ R_{s:w} = 1 : 20 $$

**Step 3 — Precipitation:** The aqueous extract is filtered and the gum is precipitated using **95% ethanol (1:1 v/v)** or **isopropanol**.

**Step 4 — Drying:** The precipitated gum is vacuum-dried at **40–50 °C** to a moisture content below **10%**.

**Step 5 — Milling:** The dried gum flakes are milled to **80–100 mesh** size.

**Yield calculation:**

$$ \text{Gum yield (\%)} = \frac{W_{\text{gum}}}{W_{\text{seed}}} \times 100 $$

For 100 g of cleaned fenugreek seeds, the expected yield is:

$$ \text{Gum yield} = \frac{17.5}{100} \times 100 = 17.5\% $$

This 17.5% is well within the reported 15–20% range.

## 3.5 Distillation Yield for Fenugreek Essential Oil

The volatile oil (essential oil) yield via hydro-distillation or steam distillation is given by:

$$ \eta = \frac{V_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{seed}}} \times 100\% $$

Typical yield values:

$$ \eta_{\text{essential oil}} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.15\% $$

This is very low compared to other seed spices, which is why **oleoresin** (containing both volatile and non-volatile flavor compounds) is industrially preferred over pure essential oil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** For a 14-mark question on "value-added products of fenugreek," always present the derivation of **oleoresin yield** using the formula:
> $$\text{Oleoresin yield} = \frac{W_{\text{oleoresin}}}{W_{\text{seed}}} \times 100 \approx 4 \text{ to } 6\%$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
**Define fenugreek. Mention its botanical name, family, chromosome number, and the two primary economic parts of the plant.**

**Model Answer:**

Fenugreek is an annual herbaceous seed spice crop of the family **Fabaceae (Papilionoideae)**. Its botanical name is ***Trigonella foenum-graecum*** L. The diploid chromosome number is **2n = 16**. The two primary economic parts are:
1. **Fresh and dried leaves** (used as a leafy vegetable — *methi*)
2. **Seeds** (used as a condiment, spice, and for industrial gum extraction)

The plant is indigenous to the **Mediterranean region** and is widely cultivated in **Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Madhya Pradesh** in India. India is the world's largest producer, contributing ~70–80% of global output.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
**List the major biochemical constituents of fenugreek seeds and their industrial significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The major biochemical constituents of fenugreek seeds are:

| Constituent | Content (%) | Industrial Significance |
|-------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Galactomannan gum | 15–20% | Food stabilizer, thickener |
| Diosgenin (saponin) | 0.8–2.2% | Steroid drug synthesis |
| Trigonelline (alkaloid) | 0.35–1.0% | Antidiabetic, neuroprotective |
| Fixed oil | 6–10% | Cooking, cosmetic industry |
| Proteins | 20–30% | Nutritional supplement |
| Sotolone (lactone) | Trace | Maple flavoring, perfumery |

These constituents make fenugreek a **multi-utility industrial crop** with applications in the **food, pharmaceutical, nutraceutical, and cosmetic industries**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **agronomic package of practices** for cultivation of fenugreek for seed production. Cover land preparation, sowing, manures, irrigation, and plant protection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **post-harvest technology** of fenugreek — harvesting, threshing, drying, grading, and value-added product development (oleoresin and galactomannan gum). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**1. Climatic and Soil Requirements:**

Fenugreek is a **cool-season (rabi) crop** requiring a temperature of **10–25 °C** and well-drained **loam to sandy loam** soil with pH **6.0–7.5**.

**2. Land Preparation:**

The field is ploughed **2–3 times** to a fine tilth, followed by planking. FYM is applied at **10–15 t/ha** and incorporated.

**3. Seed and Sowing:**

- Seed rate: **15 kg/ha** (line sowing)
- Spacing: 30 cm × 10 cm
- Sowing depth: **2–3 cm**
- Sowing time: **November to December** (for seed crop)
- Seed treatment: **Thiram @ 2.5 g/kg seed**; **Rhizobium inoculation** for N-fixation

**4. Manures and Fertilizers:**

- N: **20 kg/ha** (starter dose)
- P₂O₅: **40 kg/ha** (basal)
- K₂O: **20 kg/ha** (basal)
- S: **20 kg/ha** (for sulfur-loving crop, optional)

**5. Irrigation:**

Total **3–5 irrigations** are given. Critical stages: branching, flowering, and pod development. **Total water requirement: 250–400 mm**.

**6. Interculture:**

- Weeding at 25–30 DAS and 45–50 DAS
- Pre-emergence **Pendimethalin @ 1.0 kg a.i./ha** within 2 DAS

**7. Plant Protection:**

- **Powdery mildew** (*Erysiphe polygoni*): controlled by wettable sulfur @ 0.2% or hexaconazole @ 0.1%
- **Aphid** (*Aphis craccivora*): controlled by imidacloprid @ 0.5 ml/L
- **Root rot** (*Rhizoctonia solani*): controlled by seed treatment with **Trichoderma @ 4 g/kg**

**8. Harvesting:**

When **75–80% pods turn yellow-brown**, plants are cut at ground level in the morning, dried for 3–5 days, and threshed.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**1. Harvesting Indices:**

- **Maturity indicator:** Pods turn yellow-brown; seeds become hard and golden
- **Moisture at harvest:** ~20–25%
- **Drying to safe moisture:** **8–10%** (for safe storage)

**2. Threshing:**

- Manual beating or mechanical thresher
- Winnowing to separate chaff
- Sieving through **2 mm mesh**

**3. Drying:**

- **Sun drying** on tarpaulin for 2–3 days, OR
- **Mechanical drying** at **40–45 °C** to maintain color and aroma
- Final moisture content: **8–10%**

**4. Grading:**

Seeds are graded by size and color. BIS specifications (IS 7051:1996):
- **Grade I:** 100% pure, moisture ≤ 10%, foreign matter ≤ 1%
- **Grade II:** 98% pure, moisture ≤ 10%, foreign matter ≤ 2%

**5. Storage:**

- In **gunny bags or jute bags** in cool, dry place
- Treated with **aluminum phosphide** (fumigant) for insect control
- Storage life: **1–2 years** at proper moisture

**6. Value-Added Product: Oleoresin Extraction**

**Process flow:**
- Cleaned seeds → **Crushing/Grinding** → **Solvent extraction with hexane/acetone (40–60 °C)** → **Filtration** → **Solvent recovery (vacuum distillation)** → **Concentrated oleoresin (4–6% yield)**

**7. Value-Added Product: Galactomannan Gum Extraction**

**Process flow:**
- Seeds → **Roasting (110–120 °C, 10 min)** → **Dehusking (acid/alkali treatment)** → **Endosperm grinding** → **Hot water extraction (80–90 °C, 1:20 ratio)** → **Filtration** → **Precipitation with 95% ethanol** → **Vacuum drying (40–50 °C)** → **Milling to 80–100 mesh** → **Gum powder (15–20% yield)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **botanical description** of fenugreek — root, stem, leaf, floral formula, and pod/seed morphology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Enumerate the **medicinal and pharmacological uses** of fenugreek, with emphasis on its bioactive compounds (trigonelline, diosgenin, galactomannan, 4-hydroxyisoleucine). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**1. Root System:**

Fenugreek has a **tap root system** with extensive lateral branching. The roots form **symbiotic nodules** with *Rhizobium meliloti*, enabling **biological nitrogen fixation** at 30–50 kg N/ha/year.

**2. Stem:**

- Erect, hollow, branched, **herbaceous**, slightly pubescent
- Height: **30–80 cm**
- Green to slightly reddish-green

**3. Leaf:**

- **Pinnately trifoliate compound** leaf
- 3 obovate leaflets with **toothed (serrate) margins**
- Petiole: short, pubescent
- Stipules: small, lanceolate

**4. Inflorescence and Flower:**

- Solitary or paired **axillary flowers**
- **Papilionaceous (zygomorphic)** corolla
- Calyx: 5 fused sepals (gamosepalous)
- Corolla: pale yellow to white, with **standard (1) + wings (2) + keel (2)**
- Androecium: **10 stamens, diadelphous (9) + 1**
- Gynoecium: **monocarpellary, superior ovary, marginal placentation**

**Floral Formula:**

$$ \text{Br} \, \% \, \oplus \, \text{K}_{(5)} \, \text{C}_{1+2+(2)} \, \text{A}_{(9)+1} \, \text{G}_{\underline{1}} $$

**5. Fruit (Pod):**

- A **linear, falcate (sickle-shaped) legume**
- Length: **5–10 cm**
- Contains **10–20 seeds**
- Dehisces along both sutures at maturity

**6. Seed:**

- **Rhomboidal** in shape, **3–5 mm** long
- **Hard testa**, smooth, **golden-yellow to brown**
- Deep groove dividing seed into two unequal lobes
- **Endosperm** rich in galactomannan
- **Test weight:** 10–16 g per 1000 seeds

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**1. Galactagogue (Milk-Production Enhancer):**

Fenugreek seeds contain **phytoestrogens** (e.g., diosgenin) and **galactomannan** that stimulate **mammary gland development** and prolactin secretion. Traditional use as a lactation aid is supported by clinical studies showing a **20–30% increase in milk yield** in lactating mothers consuming 5–10 g of seed powder daily.

**2. Antidiabetic Activity:**

- **Trigonelline** and **4-hydroxyisoleucine** (an unusual amino acid) improve insulin sensitivity and stimulate glucose-induced insulin secretion.
- **Galactomannan** reduces post-prandial blood glucose by **delaying gastric emptying** and slowing carbohydrate absorption.
- **Mechanism:** 4-Hydroxyisoleucine acts as an **insulin secretagogue** on pancreatic β-cells, but only in the presence of **glucose concentrations > 5.5 mM**, avoiding hypoglycemia risk.

**3. Anti-inflammatory and Analgesic:**

- **Diosgenin** inhibits **NF-κB** signaling pathway and reduces **COX-2** expression.
- Effective in reducing joint inflammation in **arthritis models**.

**4. Cardioprotective Effects:**

- **Fixed oil** (rich in **linoleic and oleic acids**) reduces **LDL cholesterol** and **triglycerides**.
- Galactomannan reduces **serum cholesterol** by binding bile acids in the intestine.

**5. Antioxidant Activity:**

- **Polyphenols and flavonoids** in fenugreek seeds scavenge **free radicals (DPPH, superoxide, hydroxyl radicals)**.
- **IC50 values:** ~25–50 μg/mL for ethanolic extract.

**6. Anticancer Potential:**

- **Diosgenin** induces **apoptosis** in **breast cancer (MCF-7)**, **colon cancer (HT-29)**, and **leukemia (HL-60)** cell lines via **mitochondrial pathway activation**.

**7. Other Therapeutic Uses:**

- **Digestive aid:** Fiber and mucilage relieve constipation
- **Appetite stimulant:** Used in convalescence and anorexia
- **Dyslipidemia management:** Lowers total cholesterol by 10–15%
- **Antiulcer activity:** Mucilage coats gastric mucosa, reducing ulcer indices
- **Hepatoprotective:** Reduces serum ALT, AST, and bilirubin in hepatotoxicity models

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Chromosome number confusion:** Students often write **2n = 14** (which is for *Trigonella corniculata*) or **2n = 18** (for *Medicago*). Always write **2n = 16** for *Trigonella foenum-graecum*.
> 2. **Family misattribution:** Fenugreek is **Fabaceae (Leguminosae)**, NOT Apiaceae (which includes cumin, fennel, coriander). This is the most common error.
> 3. **Galactomannan M:G ratio:** Fenugreek has **1:1**, guar has **1.6:1**, locust bean has **3.5:1**. Do not confuse them.
> 4. **Diosgenin content:** Often overestimated as 5–10%. The correct range is **0.8–2.2%**. Only *Dioscorea* species have higher diosgenin (>5%).
> 5. **Floral formula mistakes:** A(9)+1 is **diadelphous**, not monadelphous. Many students incorrectly write A(10).
> 6. **Spacing confusion:** Leaf-purpose fenugreek = 20 cm row spacing; seed-purpose = 30 cm row spacing. Examiners specifically test this.
> 7. **Yield units:** Always express seed yield in **kg/ha**, not quintals/ha. Conversion: 1 quintal = 100 kg.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Fire Revision Checklist

- **Botanical name:** *Trigonella foenum-graecum* L. (memorize the authority)
- **Family:** Fabaceae (Papilionoideae)
- **Chromosome number:** 2n = 16
- **Origin:** Mediterranean region
- **India's share in world production:** ~70–80%
- **Top state:** Rajasthan (~35–40%)
- **Sowing time:** October–November (leaf), November–December (seed)
- **Seed rate:** 25 kg/ha (broadcast), 15 kg/ha (line sowing)
- **Spacing:** 30 cm × 10 cm (seed crop), 20 cm rows (leaf crop)
- **Fertilizer:** N₂₀P₄₀K₂₀ kg/ha + Rhizobium inoculation
- **Irrigations:** 3–5 (250–400 mm total)
- **Critical weed control:** Pendimethalin @ 1.0 kg a.i./ha (pre-emergence)
- **Major disease:** Powdery mildew (*Erysiphe polygoni*)
- **Major pest:** Aphid (*Aphis craccivora*)
- **Harvesting (seed):** 100–120 DAS (75–80% pods yellow-brown)
- **Yield (seed):** 1500–2500 kg/ha (irrigated)
- **Yield (leaf):** 8000–12000 kg/ha
- **Galactomannan M:G ratio:** 1:1
- **Diosgenin content:** 0.8–2.2%
- **Trigonelline content:** 0.35–1.0%
- **Fixed oil:** 6–10%
- **Sotolone:** Maple aroma compound
- **Floral formula:** Br % ⊕ K(5) C1+2+(2) A(9)+1 G̲1
- **Pod type:** Falcate (sickle-shaped) legume
- **Test weight:** 10–16 g/1000 seeds
- **Safe storage moisture:** 8–10%
- **Value-added products:** Oleoresin (4–6% yield), Galactomannan gum (15–20% yield), Essential oil (0.02–0.15%)
- **Major medicinal use:** Galactagogue, antidiabetic, anti-inflammatory
- **BIS standard:** IS 7051:1996
- **Symbolic importance:** Nitrogen-fixing legume ideal in crop rotation

### Common Confusable Topics to Avoid

| Confusable Pair | Fenugreek (Correct Answer) | Similar Crop (Wrong Answer) |
|-----------------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Family | Fabaceae | Apiaceae (Cumin/Coriander) |
| Chromosome number | 16 | 14 (T. corniculata) |
| Pod shape | Falcate (sickle) | Schizocarp (Cumin) |
| Active compound | Diosgenin (saponin) | Cuminaldehyde (Cumin) |
| Gum M:G ratio | 1:1 | 1.6:1 (Guar) |
| Seed shape | Rhomboidal | Oval (Cumin) |
| Inflorescence | Solitary axillary | Umbel (Cumin) |

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For Module 2 questions on Fenugreek, structure your 14-mark answer as:
> 1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Botanical name + family + origin
> 2. **Botany (2–3 marks):** Morphology + floral formula
> 3. **Agronomy (4–5 marks):** Climate, soil, sowing, manures, irrigation, plant protection
> 4. **Post-harvest (3–4 marks):** Harvesting, processing, value addition
> 5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Economic importance / future scope
> 
> Always use **tables and bullet points** to score higher in KTU valuation. Avoid long paragraphs.

# 🧄 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Garlic** (*Allium sativum* L.) is a bulbous, herbaceous monocotyledonous spice and condiment crop belonging to the family **Amaryllidaceae** (formerly classified under *Alliaceae* and *Liliaceae*). It is a perennial plant commercially cultivated as an **annual** for its underground compound bulb composed of multiple edible cloves (bulblets) enclosed in a thin, papery tunic. Garlic is regarded as the **"Stinking Rose"** of the spice world due to its intensely pungent sulfurous aroma, which is biochemically generated upon cellular disruption of the clove tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Botanical Identity Snapshot**
> - **Botanical Name:** *Allium sativum* L.
> - **Family:** Amaryllidaceae (Liliaceae sensu lato)
> - **Chromosome Number:** 2n = 16
> - **Edible Part:** Bulb (compound bulb with 6–30 cloves)
> - **Propagule:** Vegetative (cloves) — rarely sets true seed
> - **Origin:** Central Asia (wild ancestor: *Allium longicuspis*)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **compressed, multi-segmented onion wearing a paper jacket** — that is essentially a garlic bulb! Each individual segment inside the bulb is a **clone** of the parent plant, packed with starch reserves and pungent sulfur oils. When a student crushes a garlic clove, the enzyme **alliinase** (stored separately) meets **alliin** (also stored separately in neighbouring cells), and their reaction generates **allicin** — the volatile compound that makes garlic smell strong, sting the eyes, and act as a potent antimicrobial agent.

Think of it as a **biological hand grenade**: the bulb is a defensive storage organ, and the "explosion" of flavour and chemistry only happens when the cellular compartmentalization is mechanically broken (chopping, crushing, chewing).

## 1.3 Global & National Significance

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Global Production** | ~28–30 million tonnes/year (FAO estimate) |
| **Largest Producer** | 🇨🇳 China (~75% of world output) |
| **India's Rank** | 2nd largest producer globally |
| **India's Production** | ~3.0 million tonnes (approx.) |
| **India's Major States** | Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, UP, Punjab, Maharashtra |
| **Productivity (India)** | ~7.5–9.0 tonnes/ha |

> [!NOTE]
> The pungent principle of garlic is **allicin** (diallyl thiosulfinate) — a thiosulfinate formed by the enzyme-mediated hydrolysis of *S*-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide (alliin) when the clove tissue is macerated.

## 1.4 Place in the KTU Curriculum

Garlic is taught under **Module 2 (Spice Crops – Part II: Bulbous, Rhizomatous & Tree Spices)** because it represents a fundamentally different propagation and post-harvest architecture from seed-spices covered in Module 1. It also bridges the disciplines of **agronomy, post-harvest technology, and nutraceutical chemistry**, making it a high-yield examination topic.

# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Botanical Morphology — The Living Architecture

The garlic plant is a textbook example of a **monocot geophyte** with a determinate growth habit. Below is the structured, exam-ready anatomy.

### 2.1.1 Root System
- **Type:** Fibrous, adventitious
- **Origin:** From the basal plate (modified stem) of the clove
- **Depth:** Shallow (20–30 cm)
- **Function:** Anchorage + water/mineral absorption

### 2.1.2 Stem
- The visible stem is a **highly compressed, flattened basal plate** (looks like a disc).
- An aerial **pseudostem** is formed by tightly sheathing leaf bases (NOT a true stem).
- A flowering stalk (**scape**) emerges from the basal plate, terminating in an **umbel** (in bolting types only).

### 2.1.3 Leaves
- **Shape:** Long, linear, strap-like, parallel-veined
- **Arrangement:** Distichous (two-ranked, alternately arranged in a flat plane)
- **Number:** 6–13 per plant (variety-dependent)
- **Sheath:** Tubular, encasing the pseudostem

### 2.1.4 Inflorescence (Scape & Umbel)
- Present only in **bolting varieties** (e.g., *Sweta, Agrifound Parvati*).
- Scape is solid, circular, and up to **60–90 cm** tall.
- The umbel is a **spathe-enclosed cluster** containing **sterile flowers + aerial bulbils (topsets)**.
- **True seed** is rarely set due to **sterility** — this is why garlic is propagated vegetatively.

### 2.1.5 Bulb Structure
- The **bulb** is a *compound* bulb (an aggregation of 6–30 bulblets called **cloves**).
- Each clove is a modified **leaf base + axillary bud** with a single terminal bud.
- Cloves are encased in **2–3 layers of papery tunics** (modified leaf sheaths) that are pinkish-white to purplish.
- Basal plate → tunics → cloves (inner storage leaves) → protective skins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why vegetative propagation?** Garlic is **sterile** in most commercial cultivars — flowers abort, pollen is non-viable, and ovules do not develop. Therefore, **cloves are the only commercially viable propagule.** This is the #1 reason for **clonal degeneration** and accumulation of viral diseases in garlic (e.g., Onion Yellow Dwarf Virus, Leek Yellow Stripe Virus).

## 2.2 The "Why & How" of Garlic Chemistry

When the clove is **intact**, two compounds are sequestered in separate cell compartments:
- **Alliin** (*S*-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide) — stored in the **cytoplasm** of storage cells.
- **Alliinase** (a C-S lyase enzyme) — stored in **vacuoles**.

**The Crushing Equation (conceptual):**

$$\text{Alliin} \xrightarrow{\text{Alliinase (mechanical disruption)}} \text{Pyruvate} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{Allicin (2-propenethiosulfinate)}$$

**Allicin** then spontaneously decomposes into:
- **Diallyl disulfide (DADS)** — garlic's dominant aroma compound
- **Diallyl trisulfide (DATS)**
- **Ajoene** (anti-thrombotic)
- **Vinyl dithiins**

## 2.3 Climate & Soil Requirements

| Parameter | Optimum Range |
|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 13–24 °C (vegetative); 25–30 °C (bulbing) |
| **Photoperiod** | Long day (>13 hours) for **bulb initiation** |
| **Rainfall** | 600–1000 mm (well-distributed) |
| **Soil Type** | Sandy loam to clay loam, well-drained |
| **Soil pH** | 6.0–7.0 |
| **Altitude** | Up to 1500 m MSL |

## 2.4 Cultural Operations & Critical Inputs

### 2.4.1 Propagation
- **Seed rate:** 200–300 kg cloves per hectare
- **Clove size:** 5–8 g (larger cloves → higher yield)
- **Pre-planting dip:** Carbendazim 0.1% for 15 minutes (fungal disease control)
- **Planting time:** **October–November** in plains; **March–April** in hills

### 2.4.2 Spacing & Method
- **Row-to-Row:** 15 cm
- **Plant-to-Plant:** 10 cm
- **Depth:** 2.5–5 cm, point upwards
- **Method:** Furrow or dibbling

### 2.4.3 Nutrient Schedule (per hectare)
- **FYM:** 25–30 tonnes
- **N:** 100–120 kg (split: 50% basal + 50% 30–45 DAS)
- **P₂O₅:** 50–60 kg (basal)
- **K₂O:** 50–75 kg (basal)
- **S:** 30–40 kg (critical for allicin synthesis)

### 2.4.4 Irrigation Schedule
- **Total:** 7–10 irrigations
- **Critical stages:** Vegetative growth + bulb bulking
- **Stop:** 15–20 days before harvest (helps curing)

### 2.4.5 Harvesting & Yield
- **Harvest indicator:** Neck softening, leaf yellowing, pseudostem lodging
- **Time:** 130–170 days after planting
- **Yield:** 8–15 t/ha (fresh bulbs)
- **Curing:** 7–10 days in shade, then neck-cutting

## 2.5 Pests, Diseases & Physiological Disorders

| Type | Common Name | Causal Agent | Symptoms |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disease | Purple blotch | *Alternaria porri* | Purple lesions on leaves |
| Disease | Stemphylium blight | *Stemphylium vesicarium* | Pale yellow→brown leaf spots |
| Disease | White rot | *Sclerotium cepivorum* | Decay + black sclerotia at base |
| Disease | Viral complex | OYDV, LYSV | Mosaic, stunting, yield loss |
| Pest | Thrips | *Thrips tabaci* | Silvery leaf streaks, rasping damage |
| Disorder | Waxy breakdown | Heat + handling | Cloves turn translucent, waxy |

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Field Layout & Plant Population

> **Sample KTU Numerical Style Problem:** A farmer plans to cultivate garlic variety *Yamuna Safed* in **1.2 hectares** of land. The recommended spacing is **15 cm × 10 cm**. Each clove weighs on average **6 g**. Calculate:
> 1. Total plant population in the field.
> 2. Seed (clove) requirement in kg.
> 3. Expected cured yield, if fresh yield is 14 t/ha and curing recovery is 60%.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Plant Population per Hectare

The standard population formula:

$$P_{ha} = \dfrac{\text{Area of 1 hectare}}{\text{Area occupied by one plant}}$$

**Substituting:**

$$P_{ha} = \dfrac{10{,}000 \, m^2}{(0.15 \, m \times 0.10 \, m)}$$

$$P_{ha} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.015}$$

$$\boxed{P_{ha} = 666{,}667 \, \text{plants / ha}}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Plant Population in 1.2 Hectares

$$P_{total} = P_{ha} \times 1.2$$

$$P_{total} = 666{,}667 \times 1.2$$

$$\boxed{P_{total} = 800{,}000 \, \text{plants (approximately)}}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Seed (Clove) Requirement

$$S_{kg} = P_{total} \times \text{avg. clove weight (kg)}$$

$$S_{kg} = 800{,}000 \times 0.006 \, kg$$

$$S_{kg} = 4{,}800 \, kg$$

$$\boxed{S_{kg} = 4{,}800 \, kg = 4.8 \, \text{tonnes (this is unusually high — practical seed rate is 200–300 kg/ha, but the calculation is mathematically valid for the given inputs)}}$$

> **Examiner's note:** Practical agronomy uses **weight-based seed rate (kg/ha)** because clove size varies. The *theoretical* count-based approach gives higher values due to assumed ideal germination; field practice assumes ~85–90% germination.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Expected Cured Yield

$$Y_{cured} = Y_{fresh} \times \dfrac{\text{Recovery}}{100} \times \text{Area (ha)}$$

$$Y_{cured} = 14 \, t/ha \times 0.60 \times 1.2$$

$$Y_{cured} = 14 \times 0.72$$

$$\boxed{Y_{cured} = 10.08 \, \text{tonnes cured bulbs from 1.2 ha}}$$

## 3.2 Stoichiometry of Allicin Formation

**Problem:** A garlic processing unit receives 1.5 tonnes of fresh garlic cloves. Fresh cloves contain 0.4% w/w alliin. Assuming 100% enzymatic conversion efficiency, calculate the theoretical allicin yield. (Molecular weights: alliin = 177.22 g/mol; allicin = 162.26 g/mol)

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Total Mass of Alliin in 1.5 t

$$M_{alliin} = 1500 \, kg \times \dfrac{0.4}{100}$$

$$M_{alliin} = 6.0 \, kg = 6000 \, g$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Moles of Alliin

$$n_{alliin} = \dfrac{M_{alliin}}{MW_{alliin}}$$

$$n_{alliin} = \dfrac{6000 \, g}{177.22 \, g/mol}$$

$$n_{alliin} = 33.86 \, \text{mol}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Stoichiometric Moles of Allicin

From the reaction $\text{Alliin} \rightarrow \text{Allicin}$ (1:1 stoichiometry):

$$n_{allicin} = n_{alliin} = 33.86 \, \text{mol}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Theoretical Mass of Allicin

$$M_{allicin} = n_{allicin} \times MW_{allicin}$$

$$M_{allicin} = 33.86 \, \text{mol} \times 162.26 \, g/mol$$

$$M_{allicin} = 5494 \, g$$

$$\boxed{M_{allicin} = 5.494 \, kg \approx 5.5 \, kg}$$

## 3.3 Cost-Economics of Garlic Dehydration (Conceptual)

> **Given:** 1 quintal (100 kg) fresh garlic → 22 kg garlic flakes/powder (industry average). 
> Market price: Fresh = ₹15/kg, Dehydrated = ₹180/kg.

**Step 1 — Cost of raw material:**

$$C_{raw} = 100 \times 15 = \text{₹} 1500$$

**Step 2 — Gross revenue from dehydrated product:**

$$R_{gross} = 22 \times 180 = \text{₹} 3960$$

**Step 3 — Value addition margin (excluding processing cost):**

$$M = R_{gross} - C_{raw} = 3960 - 1500 = \text{₹} 2460$$

**Step 4 — Value addition percentage:**

$$V_{\%} = \dfrac{M}{C_{raw}} \times 100$$

$$V_{\%} = \dfrac{2460}{1500} \times 100 = 164\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A farmer/entrepreneur can potentially increase the value of garlic by **~164%** through simple dehydration — a fact of huge importance in **post-harvest technology** modules.

## 4.1 Garlic Plant — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
    A9 --> A10[Bulb Bulking and Development - 90 to 150 DAS]
    A10 --> A11[Stop Irrigation 15 to 20 days before harvest]
    A11 --> A12[Harvesting - Pseudostem lodging indicator]
    A12 --> A13[Curing - Shade 7 to 10 days]
    A13 --> A14[Neck Cutting and Sorting]
    A14 --> A15{Post Harvest Route}
    A15 --> B1[Fresh Market Sale]
    A15 --> B2[Dehydrated Garlic Flakes]
    A15 --> B3[Garlic Powder - 0.5 to 1 percent MC]
    A15 --> B4[Garlic Paste and Oil]

    B1 --> C1[Short Term Storage 0 to 4 C at 60 to 70 percent RH]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2[Sealed Airtight Container - Shelf life 12 months]
    B4 --> C2

```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Garlic Bulb — Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Bulb[Compound Garlic Bulb] --> Tunics[Outer Papery Tunics - 2 to 3 layers]
    Bulb --> Cloves[Cloves or Bulblets - 6 to 30 per bulb]
    Bulb --> BasalPlate[Basal Plate - Modified Compressed Stem]
    Tunics --> TunCol[Tunic Color - White / Pink / Purple]
    Cloves --> ClStr[Each Clove is a Modified Leaf Base plus Axillary Bud]
    Cloves --> ClArr[Arrangement - Concentric around Basal Plate]
    BasalPlate --> Roots[Fibrous Adventitious Roots]
    BasalPlate --> Scape[Scape - Flowering Stalk in Bolting Varieties]
    Scape --> Umbel[Umbel with Sterile Flowers plus Aerial Bulbils]
    ClStr --> CloveComp[Composition - 62 percent Moisture, 30 percent CHO, 6 percent Protein, 1 percent Minerals]
    CloveComp --> Alliin[Alliin - Cysteine Sulfoxide in Vacuole]
    CloveComp --> Alliinase[Alliinase - Enzyme in Cytoplasm]
    Alliin --> AllicinRxn[Allicin Formation on Cellular Disruption]
    Alliinase --> AllicinRxn
    AllicinRxn --> DADS[Diallyl Disulfide - 60 percent of Aroma]
    AllicinRxn --> DATS[Diallyl Trisulfide]
    AllicinRxn --> Ajoene[Ajoene - Antithrombotic]
```
