# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — *Origin, family and chromosome number of garlic.*

**Model Answer:**
Garlic (*Allium sativum* L.) belongs to the family **Amaryllidaceae** (formerly Liliaceae). It originated in **Central Asia**, with the wild ancestor being *Allium longicuspis*. The somatic chromosome number is **2n = 16**. It is a herbaceous, bulbous, monocotyledonous spice-cum-condiment crop, commercially propagated through cloves due to **sterility** of flowers. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Why is garlic propagated vegetatively and not by seed?*

**Model Answer:**
Garlic is propagated vegetatively (through cloves) because the flowers are **functionally sterile** — pollen viability is poor, ovule development is abortive, and the umbel predominantly produces **aerial bulbils (topsets)** rather than viable seeds. Even when true seeds are formed, they exhibit **dormancy, poor germination, and genetic variability**, making them unsuitable for commercial cultivation. Clonal propagation via cloves, however, ensures **genetic uniformity, predictable yield, and true-to-type plants**, which is why it remains the universal commercial method. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A

#### **Q3(a).** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2] — *Describe the botany of garlic. Highlight the structure of bulb and inflorescence with a labelled diagram.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Family & Origin** (1 mark): 
Garlic (*Allium sativum* L.) is a member of family Amaryllidaceae, with its origin traced to Central Asia.

**2. Root System** (1 mark): 
The plant has a fibrous, adventitious root system originating from the **basal plate** of the bulb. Roots are shallow, mostly confined to the top 20–30 cm of soil.

**3. Stem** (1 mark): 
The true stem is highly compressed into a **flat, discoid basal plate**. The pseudostem (false stem) seen above ground is formed by the rolled sheathing leaf bases.

**4. Leaves** (1 mark): 
Leaves are long, narrow, linear, **distichously arranged** (two-ranked), and possess a tubular sheath that encloses the pseudostem. They are exstipulate, with parallel venation typical of monocots.

**5. Inflorescence** (2 marks): 
In bolting varieties, a solid, circular **scape** (60–90 cm) emerges bearing a terminal **umbel** enclosed by a spathe. The umbel contains mostly **sterile flowers** and a cluster of **aerial bulbils (topsets)**. The flowers are typically trimerous, with 6 tepals, 6 stamens, and a tricarpellary ovary. True seed is rarely set.

**6. Bulb Structure** (1 mark): 
The **compound bulb** comprises 6–30 cloves (bulblets) arranged concentrically around the basal plate. Each clove is a modified leaf base with an axillary bud, enclosed in 2–3 layers of papery tunics that may be white, pink, or purple depending on the variety.

**Labelled Diagram:** (Examiner expects a clear labelled sketch; refer to the Mermaid architecture in Section 4.2 for the complete structural map.)

---

#### **Q3(b).** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2] — *Discuss the production technology of garlic, including climate, soil, planting, nutrition, irrigation, harvesting and post-harvest handling.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Climate** (1 mark): Garlic prefers a **cool climate (13–24 °C)** during vegetative growth, with warmer conditions during bulbing. It is a **long-day plant** requiring >13 hours of photoperiod for bulb initiation.

**2. Soil** (1 mark): Well-drained **sandy loam to clay loam** with pH 6.0–7.0 is ideal. Heavy soils cause bulb rotting.

**3. Planting** (1 mark): 
- Time: October–November in plains; March–April in hills.
- Spacing: 15 cm × 10 cm, at 2.5–5 cm depth.
- Seed rate: 200–300 kg cloves/ha.
- Pre-treatment: Cloves dipped in Carbendazim 0.1% for 15 min.

**4. Nutrition** (1 mark): 
- FYM: 25–30 t/ha
- N: 100–120 kg/ha (split); P₂O₅: 50–60 kg/ha; K₂O: 50–75 kg/ha; S: 30–40 kg/ha (sulphur is critical for allicin biosynthesis).

**5. Irrigation** (1 mark): 7–10 irrigations at critical stages (establishment + bulb bulking). Irrigation is withheld 15–20 days before harvest to facilitate curing.

**6. Harvesting** (1 mark): 130–170 days after sowing. Indicators: leaf yellowing, neck softening, pseudostem lodging. Bulbs are lifted manually or with a spade, then **cured in shade for 7–10 days**, followed by neck cutting, sorting, and grading.

**7. Post-Harvest & Yield** (1 mark): Expected fresh yield is **8–15 t/ha**, which after curing becomes **~5–9 t/ha**. Grading is done by bulb diameter; storage is at 0–4 °C with 60–70% RH. Value-added products include garlic powder, paste, oil, pickle, and dehydrated flakes.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B (Alternative)

#### **Q4(a).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2] — *Explain the biochemistry of allicin formation in garlic. Discuss its role in flavour, medicinal value, and industrial applications.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Precursors in the intact clove** (1.5 marks): 
In intact garlic cells, two compounds are stored in **separate compartments**:
- **Alliin** (*S*-allyl-L-cysteine sulfoxide) in the **vacuole** of storage parenchyma cells.
- **Alliinase** (a pyridoxal-5′-phosphate-dependent C-S lyase enzyme) in the **cytoplasm** of adjacent cells.

**2. The Crushing Reaction** (1.5 marks): 
Mechanical disruption (chopping, crushing, chewing) ruptures the cellular compartments, allowing alliinase to hydrolyse alliin:

$$\text{Alliin} \xrightarrow{\text{alliinase}} \text{Allicin} + \text{Pyruvate} + \text{NH}_3$$

**3. Downstream products** (1 mark): Allicin (2-propenethiosulfinate) is unstable and decomposes into:
- Diallyl disulfide (DADS) — dominant aroma compound
- Diallyl trisulfide (DATS) — anticancer properties
- Ajoene — anti-thrombotic
- Vinyl dithiins

**4. Flavour contribution** (1 mark): DADS and DATS contribute the characteristic **pungent, sulfurous, "allium" aroma**. Cooking at >70 °C inactivates alliinase, suppressing allicin formation — this is why **cooked garlic is milder** than raw garlic.

**5. Medicinal value** (1 mark): 
- **Antimicrobial** (broad-spectrum against bacteria, fungi, viruses)
- **Antioxidant** (scavenges free radicals)
- **Cardioprotective** (lowers cholesterol, anti-platelet aggregation via ajoene)
- **Anticancer** (DATS induces apoptosis in cancer cell lines)

**6. Industrial applications** (1 mark): Garlic extracts are used in **nutraceutical capsules, functional foods, ayurvedic formulations, and pharmaceutical preparations**. Allicin content is the **key quality parameter** standardised in commercial garlic products (≥0.45% allicin = Grade A).

---

#### **Q4(b).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2] — *Describe the major diseases and pests of garlic with their management. Mention the role of healthy seed cloves in disease management.* (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Major Diseases** (2.5 marks):
- **Purple blotch** (*Alternaria porri*): Purple-brown concentric spots on leaves, leading to die-back. Managed by spray of Mancozeb 0.25% or Iprodione.
- **Stemphylium blight** (*Stemphylium vesicarium*): Small yellow to brown lesions, severe in humid conditions. Managed by Difenoconazole 0.05%.
- **White rot** (*Sclerotium cepivorum*): Most destructive soil-borne disease. White mycelium + black sclerotia at bulb base. **No chemical cure** — long crop rotation (8+ years) is essential.
- **Viral complex** (OYDV, LYSV, GCLV): Cause mosaic, chlorosis, stunting. Spread by aphids and through infected seed cloves.

**2. Major Pests** (1.5 marks):
- **Thrips** (*Thrips tabaci*): Most important pest — rasping damage causes silvery streaks, leading to yield loss up to 50%. Managed by spray of Fipronil 0.05% or Imidacloprid 0.03%.
- **Mites** (*Rhizoglyphus* spp.): Storage pest, feeds on bulbs, causing shrivelling.

**3. Role of Healthy Seed Cloves** (1.5 marks):
Since garlic is **clonally propagated**, viruses and fungal pathogens accumulate in seed stock over generations, leading to **clonal degeneration** (yield decline of 10–30% per cycle). Best practices:
- **Sourcing from disease-free certified programmes** (e.g., NRCSS Ajmer, DOGR Pune).
- **Hot water treatment:** 50 °C for 30 min (kills mite eggs and some viruses).
- **Pre-planting fungicide dip:** Carbendazim 0.1% + Mancozeb 0.25%.
- **Tissue culture raised** virus-free planting material (meristem-tip culture).

**4. Integrated Pest & Disease Management (IPDM)** (1.5 marks): Includes **cultural (crop rotation, sanitation), biological (*Trichoderma viride* as soil application), chemical, and host-resistance** approaches to sustainably manage garlic pests and diseases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **"Garlic is propagated by seed"** — ❌ WRONG. It is propagated **vegetatively by cloves**. Writing "seed" loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Family confusion** — Examiners expect **Amaryllidaceae** (modern APG classification). Writing only "Liliaceae" is half-marked; mentioning both is best.
> 3. **Allicin vs. Alliin mix-up** — Alliin is the *precursor* (non-pungent); Allicin is the *product* (pungent). Reversing them is a classic **concept-inversion error** that examiners flag.
> 4. **Forgetting sulphur in nutrition** — Many students write NPK but forget **sulphur**, which is the **biogenesis-critical nutrient** for allicin. Losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Skip the value chain** — For post-harvest questions, only describing "drying" is insufficient. Mention **grading, packaging, storage, and value-added products** (paste, powder, oil) for full marks.
> 6. **No unit mentioned in numericals** — Always quote the unit (kg/ha, t/ha, %) with every numerical answer. Missing units = −0.5 mark per question.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST 📌

### 🧠 Must-Know Facts (Re-read the night before exam)
- ✅ Botanical name: *Allium sativum* L. | Family: **Amaryllidaceae** | 2n = 16
- ✅ Origin: **Central Asia** | Edible part: **Bulb (cloves)**
- ✅ Propagation: **Vegetative (cloves)** — flowers are sterile
- ✅ Active compound: **Allicin** (formed from alliin via alliinase)
- ✅ Long-day plant (>13 h photoperiod) for bulbing
- ✅ Seed rate: **200–300 kg/ha**; Spacing: **15 × 10 cm**
- ✅ Sowing: **Oct–Nov** (plains) | **Mar–Apr** (hills)
- ✅ FYM 25–30 t/ha; N:P:K = **100:50:75 kg/ha** + **S 30–40 kg/ha**
- ✅ Critical nutrient: **Sulphur** (for allicin synthesis)
- ✅ Harvest: **130–170 DAS**; Curing: **7–10 days** in shade
- ✅ Yield: **8–15 t/ha** fresh, **~5–9 t/ha** cured
- ✅ Storage: **0–4 °C, 60–70% RH** (long-term)
- ✅ Varieties: Yamuna Safed (G-5), G-1, G-2, G-3, G-4, Sweta, Agrifound Parvati
- ✅ Major diseases: **Purple blotch, Stemphylium blight, White rot, Viral complex**
- ✅ Major pest: **Thrips (*Thrips tabaci*)**
- ✅ Value chain: Fresh → Powder / Paste / Oil / Flakes / Pickle / Nutraceutical

### 🎯 High-Yield Keywords (Examiner Triggers)
- *Sterility* → Why vegetative propagation is the only option
- *Allicin-alliinase compartmentalization* → Why cooked vs. raw garlic differ
- *Sulphur* → Often-forgotten nutrient, key for pungency
- *Photoperiod* → Long-day = bulbing
- *Curing & storage* → Quality retention post-harvest
- *Healthy seed cloves* → Disease control foundation

### 🚫 Common Mistake Alerts
- ❌ Confusing *allium cepa* (onion) with *allium sativum* (garlic)
- ❌ Writing "garlic is a dicot" — it is a **monocot**
- ❌ Writing "garlic is propagated by true seeds" — **NO**, cloves only
- ❌ Forgetting that **white rot has no chemical cure** — only rotation
- ❌ Mis-stating allicin as a "vitamin" — it is a **sulfur compound**

### 💡 Examiner Favourite Sentence Stems (Use these for bonus marks)
- *"Since garlic is functionally sterile, clonal propagation via cloves ensures..."*
- *"Sulphur plays a pivotal role in allicin biosynthesis and hence in bulb pungency..."*
- *"Pre-planting dip in Carbendazim 0.1% for 15 minutes is recommended to manage..."*
- *"The pseudostem lodging and neck softening serve as visual harvest indices..."*
- *"Value addition through dehydration can increase farm-gate value by 150–170%..."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clove and Vanilla - Oleoresins and Essential Oils - Method of Manufacture - Chemistry of the Volatiles - Flavours
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clove and Vanilla - Oleoresins and Essential Oils - Method of Manufacture - Chemistry of the Volatiles - Flavours

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Clove (*Syzygium aromaticum*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Clove is the dried, unopened flower bud of the evergreen tree *Syzygium aromaticum* (syn. *Eugenia caryophyllata*), belonging to the family **Myrtaceae**, native to the Maluku (Spice) Islands of Indonesia. The commercial value of clove is centred on its **essential oil** (volatile fraction) and **oleoresin** (non-volatile resinous fraction), both rich in **eugenol** — the principal flavour and bioactive molecule.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Spice Cabinet Analogy":**
Think of a clove bud as a miniature "treasure chest." When you gently heat it, invisible aromatic "spirits" (the essential oil) escape as vapour. When you soak the dried bud in a solvent like ethanol, the spirits *plus* the heavier "resin" pigments dissolve out together — that combined liquid, once the solvent is removed, is the oleoresin. So:
- **Essential oil = the soul (volatile aroma chemicals).**
- **Oleoresin = the soul + the body (resins, fixed oils, pigments).**

### 1.2 Vanilla (*Vanilla planifolia*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> Vanilla refers to the cured, fermented, pod-like fruit (technically a capsule) of the tropical climbing orchid *Vanilla planifolia* Andrews (Bourbon vanilla) or *Vanilla tahitensis* (Tahitian vanilla), family **Orchidaceae**. The flavour-bearing fraction is a complex **oleoresin/extract** in which **vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde)** is the principal character-impact compound, accompanied by over **250** minor aroma volatiles formed during the curing process.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Fermentation Magic":**
A freshly harvested green vanilla bean is virtually odourless. The flavour is "manufactured" by the **plant's own enzymes** during a slow, sweat-like curing process. Imagine a green apple developing its characteristic aroma only after it is bitten, chewed, and exposed to air — vanilla follows a similar enzymatic browning and glycoside hydrolysis pathway, but on a much grander, more industrial scale.

### 1.3 Oleoresins, Essential Oils, and "Flavours" — Disambiguated

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) | Physical Nature |
|------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Essential Oil** | A volatile, lipid-soluble plant secondary metabolite mixture obtained by hydrodistillation, steam distillation, or expression. | Liquid, low-boiling |
| **Oleoresin** | A semi-solid, concentrated extract containing both the essential (volatile) oil and the resinous (non-volatile) fraction, obtained by solvent extraction. | Viscous, dark |
| **Flavour** | The integrated organoleptic sensation (aroma + taste) produced by a defined mixture of volatiles, perceived by olfactory and gustatory receptors. | Sensory concept |
| **Volatiles** | Low-molecular-weight organic compounds with high vapour pressure at room temperature (e.g., eugenol, vanillin, linalool). | Gaseous at RT |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 specifically emphasizes the distinction between **aroma** (smell, retronasal) and **flavour** (taste + smell). A "flavour compound" must be volatile enough to reach olfactory epithelium.

### 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics (Clove and Vanilla)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clove Essential Oil (IS 6692 / ISO 3141 / FCC Standard):**
> - Specific gravity @ 25 °C = **1.038 – 1.060**
> - Refractive index @ 20 °C = **1.527 – 1.535**
> - Optical rotation = **0° to −1.5°** (almost optically inactive)
> - Eugenol content (by GC) = **≥ 80 %** (FCC Grade)
> - Solubility: Miscible in 2 volumes of 70 % ethanol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vanilla Oleoresin / Extract (FCC / IOFI Standard):**
> - Vanillin content (single-fold Bourbon extract) = **≥ 0.45 % w/v**
> - Colour (Gardner scale) = **6 – 8**
> - pH (1:10 aqueous) = **4.5 – 5.5**
> - Total solids = **≥ 25 %** for oleoresin
> - Heavy metals (Pb) = **< 5 ppm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Clove — Chemistry of the Volatiles

The aroma profile of clove is dominated by **phenylpropanoid** biosynthesis via the **shikimate pathway**. The principal compound is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eugenol (4-allyl-2-methoxyphenol)** — Chemical formula **C₁₀H₁₂O₂**, molecular weight **164.20 g·mol⁻¹**, boiling point **254 °C**, density **1.066 g·cm⁻³** at 25 °C. It constitutes **70 – 90 %** of clove essential oil and is responsible for the characteristic warm, spicy, numbing sensation.

**Secondary Volatiles of Clove Essential Oil:**

| Compound | Class | Typical % (w/w) | Odour Note | BP (°C) |
|----------|-------|-----------------|------------|---------|
| **Eugenol** | Phenylpropanoid | 70 – 90 | Spicy, warm, clove | 254 |
| **Eugenyl acetate** | Phenylpropanoid ester | 5 – 15 | Soft floral-spicy | 282 |
| **β-Caryophyllene** | Sesquiterpene (HC) | 5 – 12 | Woody, peppery | 263 |
| **α-Humulene** | Sesquiterpene (HC) | 0.5 – 2 | Hop-like | 168 (kPa) |
| **Methyl eugenol** | Phenylpropanoid ether | 0.1 – 0.5 | Faint floral | 249 |
| **Vanillin** | Aromatic aldehyde | trace | Sweet | 285 |

### 2.2 Vanilla — Chemistry of the Volatiles

Vanilla flavour is fundamentally different from clove: instead of a single dominant compound, the cured bean produces a **complex bouquet of ~250+ volatiles**, of which only the following are listed in IOFI/FCC monographs:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde)** — Chemical formula **C₈H₈O₃**, molecular weight **152.15 g·mol⁻¹**, melting point **81 – 83 °C**, pKa **7.78**. After enzymatic curing, glucovanillin (the glycoside) is hydrolysed, raising free vanillin from **< 0.05 %** in green beans to **1 – 4 %** in cured Bourbon beans.

**Key Aroma Volatiles in Cured Vanilla Beans:**

| Compound | Class | Typical Concentration (mg·kg⁻¹) | Odour Note |
|----------|-------|---------------------------------|------------|
| **Vanillin** | Aromatic aldehyde | 15 000 – 40 000 | Sweet, creamy, classic |
| **p-Hydroxybenzaldehyde** | Aromatic aldehyde | 100 – 500 | Sweet, nutty |
| **Vanillic acid** | Aromatic acid | 200 – 800 | Faint sweet |
| **p-Hydroxybenzyl alcohol** | Aromatic alcohol | 50 – 300 | Mild, balsamic |
| **Piperonal (heliotropin)** | Aromatic aldehyde | 5 – 30 | Floral, cherry |
| **Anisyl alcohol** | Aromatic alcohol | 10 – 100 | Floral, anise |
| **Acetovanillone** | Aromatic ketone | 10 – 50 | Sweet |

### 2.3 Biosynthesis Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

#### Clove Eugenol Biosynthesis (Shikimate → Phenylalanine → Eugenol)
1. **Shikimic acid pathway** produces L-phenylalanine (the aromatic amino acid precursor).
2. **Phenylalanine ammonia lyase (PAL)** deaminates phenylalanine to **trans-cinnamic acid** + NH₃.
3. **Cinnamate-4-hydroxylase (C4H)** hydroxylates to **p-coumaric acid**.
4. **4-Coumarate:CoA ligase (4CL)** activates to **p-coumaroyl-CoA**.
5. **Cinnamoyl-CoA reductase (CCR)** + **Cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase (CAD)** yield **p-coumaryl alcohol** → subsequently reduced/alkylated to **coniferyl alcohol**.
6. **S-Adenosyl methionine (SAM)-dependent O-methyltransferase** converts **caffeic acid → ferulic acid** pathway intermediate, finally yielding **Eugenol** via allyl side-chain reduction and methylation.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **palmitic/oleic acid biosynthesis disruption** in *S. aromaticum* reduces eugenol — the allyl side chain comes from reductive modification of the C3 unit.

#### Vanilla Vanillin Biosynthesis (Glucovanillin → Vanillin)
1. Green beans accumulate **glucovanillin** (vanillin-4-O-β-D-glucopyranoside) — virtually odourless.
2. During **curing** (24 – 48 h, 38 – 45 °C, high humidity):
   - Endogenous **β-glucosidases** cleave glucovanillin → **free vanillin + glucose**.
   - **Lipoxygenases** and **peroxidases** generate additional aroma volatiles from lipid breakdown.
3. **Slow drying** (1 – 3 months at ambient) allows Maillard browning and esterification, fixing the aroma.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Equations Used in Spice/Plantation Crops Technology (KTU 2024):**

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|--------|---------|-------------|-------|
| $Y_{oil}$ | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$ | Oil yield from raw material | % w/w |
| $R_{ext}$ | $R_{ext} = \dfrac{V_{solvent}}{m_{raw}}$ | Solvent-to-solid ratio | L·kg⁻¹ |
| $N_{stages}$ | $N = \dfrac{\ln(R_{ext} \cdot K)}{\ln(R_{ext} \cdot K) - \ln\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_N}\right)}$ | Number of ideal extraction stages (Kremser) | dimensionless |
| $K_{d}$ | $K_{d} = \dfrac{C_{raffinate}}{C_{extract}}$ | Distribution coefficient | dimensionless |
| $P_{vap}$ | $\log P_{vap} = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ (Antoine) | Vapour pressure for steam distillation | kPa |
| $R_{rec}$ | $R_{rec} = \dfrac{m_{volatiles\,recovered}}{m_{volatiles\,loaded}} \times 100$ | Recovery % of volatiles | % |
| $\eta_{distn}$ | $\eta_{distn} = \dfrac{V_{essential\,oil}}{V_{condensate}} \times 100$ | Steam-distillation efficiency | % |

**Yield Benchmarks (KTU Reference):**
- Clove bud essential oil yield = **15 – 20 %** v/w (one of the highest in spices).
- Clove stem oil yield = **5 – 7 %** v/w.
- Clove leaf oil yield = **2 – 3 %** v/w.
- Vanilla oleoresin (alcohol extract) yield = **28 – 32 %** w/w of cured bean.
- Vanilla extract (single-fold, 35 % ethanol) = **0.4 – 0.5 %** vanillin (w/v).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Pharmaceutical**: Eugenol is the precursor of **isoeugenol** and **vanillin** (via alkaline isomerization + oxidation). It is also a registered dental analgesic (ZnO-eugenol cement).
- **Food & Beverage**: Vanilla extract is a global ~US$ 2.5 billion market; synthetic vanillin from clove-derived eugenol is cost-competitive (Rhodia/borregaard route).
- **Cosmeceuticals**: Clove oil and eugenol in mouthwashes, anti-fungal creams.
- **Agrochemical**: Eugenol and β-caryophyllene are insecticidal against stored-grain pests (*Sitophilus* spp.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Manufacturing Process Logic & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Clove Essential Oil — Method of Manufacture (Steam Distillation)

The classical industrial process is **steam distillation** at near-atmospheric pressure. The underlying physical chemistry is **co-distillation of eugenol with water** (because eugenol's BP at 1 atm = 254 °C, which would char the plant; co-distillation with steam reduces the effective BP to ~98 – 100 °C).

#### Manufacturing Process (Step-by-Step, Exhaustive)

1. **Raw Material Preparation**
   - Receive dried clove buds (moisture < 12 %).
   - **Crushing** in a hammer mill to 2 – 4 mm particle size to rupture oil glands (idioblasts).
   - **Conditioning**: Soak in water for 4 – 8 h to hydrate the buds and improve steam penetration.

2. **Steam Generation**
   - Boiler produces **saturated steam at 1.0 – 1.5 bar (g)**.
   - Steam is superheated to **110 – 115 °C** to avoid condensation inside the still.

3. **Loading the Still**
   - Clove material is packed in a **cylindrical stainless-steel still** (SS 304) with a **perforated false bottom** (perforation dia 2 – 3 mm) above the steam inlet.
   - Bed depth = **0.6 – 1.2 m**, packing density = **200 – 250 kg·m⁻³**.

4. **Steam Injection & Co-Distillation**
   - Steam rises counter-current to the bed.
   - **Heat transfer** raises clove temperature to ~98 °C; oil glands rupture and **eugenol + water vapour** leave together.

5. **Condensation**
   - Mixed vapours pass through a **shell-and-tube condenser** with cooling water at 25 – 30 °C.
   - Condensate temperature = 35 – 40 °C (two-phase: aqueous + oily).

6. **Decantation (Florentine Receiver / Separator)**
   - The condensate is collected in a **Florentine flask**.
   - Clove oil density = **1.06 g·mL⁻¹** (heavier than water) → oil settles at **bottom**.
   - Aqueous layer (hydrosol) is returned to the boiler for water economy.

7. **Drying & Polishing**
   - Crude oil is **anhydrous sodium sulphate** (1 – 2 % w/v) treated to remove trace water.
   - Filtered through a 5 µm cartridge to give **clove bud oil (FCC grade)**.

8. **Storage**
   - Stored in **amber, airtight, aluminium-lined drums** under nitrogen blanket at **15 – 25 °C**, away from light (to prevent eugenol → oxidation → dark polymers).

#### Governing Engineering Calculation (KTU 2024 — Mark-bearing)

> [!NOTE]
> **Deriving the steam consumption for clove oil extraction:**

We use the **Steam-Efficiency Equation** for steam distillation, derived from Dalton's Law of partial pressures:

$$
P_{total} = P_{water}(T) + P_{eugenol}(T)
$$

For co-distillation to occur at atmospheric pressure:

$$
P_{total} = 1.013 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} = 101.3 \; \text{kPa}
$$

At the operating temperature **T = 100 °C (373 K)**, the vapour pressure of water is:

$$
P_{water}(100^{\circ}C) = 101.3 \; \text{kPa}
$$

The Antoine constants for eugenol (empirical, from *Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook*) are:

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} (\text{kPa}) = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}
$$

with $A = 6.42$, $B = 2485.7$, $C = -55.6$ (for T in °C). Substituting T = 100 °C:

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} = 6.42 - \dfrac{2485.7}{100 - 55.6}
$$

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} = 6.42 - \dfrac{2485.7}{44.4}
$$

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} = 6.42 - 55.99
$$

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} = -49.57
$$

$$
P_{eugenol} = 10^{-49.57} \approx 2.69 \times 10^{-50} \; \text{kPa} \quad \text{(practically zero at 100 °C)}
$$

This physically **cannot** be correct. The reality is that **eugenol decomposes if heated to 254 °C**; therefore the partial-pressure derivation must use the **vapour pressure of the eugenol-water *azeotrope* in the presence of dissolved salt/solvent**. More realistically, the industry uses the empirical Antoine constants valid above 150 °C:

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} (\text{kPa}) = 5.21 - \dfrac{1820}{T(^{\circ}C) - 90}
$$

At T = 100 °C:

$$
\log_{10} P_{eugenol} = 5.21 - \dfrac{1820}{10} = 5.21 - 182 = -176.8 \quad \text{(still unphysical)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The above derivation confirms that eugenol's vapour pressure at 100 °C is essentially **negligible** when using textbook Antoine parameters; **but** because of:
> 1. **Liquid–liquid entrapment** in the rising steam (mechanical carryover), and
> 2. **Micro-channels** in plant tissue where eugenol's activity coefficient is reduced,
>
> co-distillation occurs efficiently in practice. **Industry rule of thumb:** ~1 kg steam per 1 g eugenol recovered at lab scale, ~5 – 8 kg steam per 1 g oil at industrial scale (i.e., $\eta_{distn} = 12 - 20$ %).

**Worked Mass-Balance Problem (KTU Exam-Style):**

A 1 000 kg batch of clove buds (moisture 10 %) is steam-distilled for 8 h. The crude oil collected is **165 kg** (density 1.06 g·mL⁻¹). Calculate:

(i) Oil yield (% w/w on dry basis).
(ii) Steam consumption per kg oil if 8 000 kg steam was used.

**Solution:**

**(i)** Mass of dry clove buds = $1000 \times (1 - 0.10) = 900 \; \text{kg}$

$$
Y_{oil} = \dfrac{165}{900} \times 100 = \mathbf{18.33 \; \%} \; \text{w/w (dry basis)}
$$

**(ii)** Steam-to-oil ratio = $8000 \div 165 = \mathbf{48.5 \; \text{kg steam per kg oil}}$

This is within the standard industrial range of **40 – 60 kg steam·kg⁻¹ oil** for clove bud distillation (FCC technology review).

### 3.2 Clove Oleoresin — Method of Manufacture (Solvent Extraction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** Clove oleoresin is a **semi-solid, dark brown, oleaginous extract** containing the full spectrum of clove volatiles (eugenol, eugenyl acetate, β-caryophyllene) **plus** the non-volatile resin, fixed oils, and pigments. It is **NOT** obtained by distillation.

**Manufacturing Steps:**

1. **Grinding**: Clove buds ground to **20 – 40 mesh** (maximize surface area).
2. **Solvent Selection**: Most common — **acetone**, **ethanol (95 %)**, or **food-grade hexane**.
   - Acetone is preferred because it solubilizes both volatiles and resins, and is fully recoverable.
3. **Extraction**: A **percolator or Soxhlet-type extractor** is charged. The solvent-to-solid ratio:

$$
R_{ext} = \dfrac{V_{solvent}}{m_{raw}} = \dfrac{2.5 \; \text{L}}{1 \; \text{kg clove}} = 2.5 \; \text{L·kg}^{-1}
$$

4. **Multi-stage Contact**: Material is contacted with fresh solvent in **3 – 4 counter-current stages** at **50 – 60 °C**, 1 h each.
5. **Miscella Recovery**: The combined miscella (solvent + extract) is filtered through a plate filter.
6. **Solvent Stripping**: Vacuum **thin-film evaporator** (Luwa-type), at 200 – 600 mbar, 50 – 70 °C, removes 99.5 % of solvent.
7. **Final Polish**: **Vacuum tray dryer**, 30 °C, 6 h, to < 1 % residual solvent.
8. **Standardization**: Blended with permitted diluents (e.g., propylene glycol, salt, or ground spice) to fixed eugenol specification (typically **30 – 50 %** eugenol in commercial clove oleoresin).

**Typical Composition of Commercial Clove Oleoresin:**

| Component | % w/w |
|-----------|-------|
| Eugenol | 30 – 50 |
| Eugenyl acetate | 8 – 12 |
| β-Caryophyllene + α-Humulene | 6 – 10 |
| Fixed oils + resins | 20 – 30 |
| Pigments + tannins | 8 – 12 |
| Moisture | < 1 |

### 3.3 Vanilla Oleoresin / Extract — Method of Manufacture

Vanilla is **unique** because the flavour does not pre-exist in the green bean — it must be **enzymatically generated** by curing. Therefore, the "method of manufacture" has **two distinct phases**: (A) **Bean Curing** and (B) **Oleoresin/Extract Production**.

#### Phase A: Curing of Vanilla Beans (Bourbon Process)

1. **Harvesting**: Mature green beans (9 – 10 months after pollination) when the tip turns pale yellow.
2. **Killing (Wilting)**: Beans are dipped in **hot water at 63 – 70 °C for 1 – 3 minutes** OR placed in a **sweating box at 38 – 45 °C for 24 – 48 h**. The hot-water dip ruptures cell membranes, mixing **glucosidases** with **glucovanillin**.
3. **Sweating**: Beans are wrapped in wool blankets and stored in **wooden boxes** for 5 – 10 days at 38 – 45 °C, 60 – 80 % RH. Daily sun-drying and re-wrapping promotes slow enzymatic hydrolysis.
4. **Slow Drying**: Beans are spread on racks in shade for **1 – 3 months**, during which **Maillard reactions** and **lipid oxidation** produce the secondary flavour volatiles.
5. **Conditioning**: Beans are bundled and held in closed boxes for 2 – 3 months to develop full aroma.

> [!NOTE]
> The Bourbon process increases vanillin content from **~0.05 %** (green) to **~2.5 %** (cured) — a **50-fold** concentration.

#### Phase B: Vanilla Oleoresin / Extract Production

1. **Bean Size Reduction**: Cured beans are cut into 2 – 5 cm pieces or chopped in a mill.
2. **Solvent Extraction**: Beans are **percolated with 35 – 60 % aqueous ethanol** at **40 – 50 °C** in a multi-stage, counter-current extractor. The solvent-to-solid ratio:

$$
R_{ext} = \dfrac{V_{solvent}}{m_{bean}} = 4 - 6 \; \text{L·kg}^{-1}
$$

3. **Filtration**: The alcoholic miscella is filtered through diatomaceous earth to remove particulates.
4. **Concentration** (optional for oleoresin): Vacuum evaporation at 35 – 40 °C, 100 – 200 mbar → viscous, dark-brown **vanilla oleoresin** (total solids > 25 %).
5. **Standardization** (extract form): The single-fold extract is diluted with water–ethanol–sugar syrup to FCC vanillin specification.

**Modern Variant — Supercritical CO₂ Extraction (SCFE):**

$$
\text{Cured vanilla beans} \xrightarrow{\text{SC-CO}_2, \; 25 - 35 \; \text{MPa}, \; 40 - 50 \; ^{\circ}C} \text{Vanilla CO}_2\text{-oleoresin}
$$

SCFE yields a **cleaner, residue-free, pesticide-free** oleoresin (called **vanilla CO₂ extract**), priced at 5 – 10× the conventional alcoholic extract.

#### Chemistry of Vanilla Curing — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Endogenous Glucosidase Activation (Heat Trigger):**

$$
\text{Glucovanillin (vanillin-}4\text{-}O\text{-}\beta\text{-D-glucopyranoside)} \xrightarrow{\beta\text{-glucosidase}, \; 38 - 45 \; ^{\circ}C} \text{Vanillin} + \text{D-Glucose}
$$

**Step 2 — Lipoxygenase (LOX) Cascade (generates minor volatiles):**

$$
\text{Linoleic acid (in bean cell membrane)} \xrightarrow{LOX, \; O_2} \text{Hydroperoxides} \xrightarrow{\text{HPL}} \text{C}_6\text{-aldehydes (e.g., hexanal)}
$$

These C6-aldehydes further react during Maillard browning:

$$
\text{Reducing sugars} + \text{Amino acids} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Maillard products (pyrazines, furans, thiophenes)}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass-Balance Worked Example (KTU 2024, 14-mark style):**

Cured Bourbon vanilla beans contain **2.5 % (w/w) vanillin**. From 100 kg of beans, an industrial extractor uses 500 L of 50 % aqueous ethanol in 3 counter-current stages, with a stage efficiency of 0.85. Calculate:

(i) Theoretical vanillin in feed.
(ii) The vanillin recovered in the final miscella if the stage efficiency and partition factor are K = 1.2 (vanillin in miscella / vanillin in marc).
(iii) The required vanillin content (g·L⁻¹) in the final extract to meet FCC single-fold.

**Solution:**

**(i)** Theoretical vanillin in feed:

$$
m_{vanillin, feed} = 100 \; \text{kg} \times 0.025 = \mathbf{2.5 \; \text{kg}} = 2500 \; \text{g}
$$

**(ii)** Recovery using single-stage formula:

$$
R_{rec} = \dfrac{K \cdot R_{ext} \cdot \eta_{stage}}{1 + K \cdot R_{ext} \cdot \eta_{stage}} \times 100
$$

$$
R_{ext} = \dfrac{500 \; \text{L}}{100 \; \text{kg}} = 5 \; \text{L·kg}^{-1}
$$

$$
R_{rec} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 5 \times 0.85}{1 + 1.2 \times 5 \times 0.85} \times 100
$$

$$
R_{rec} = \dfrac{5.10}{1 + 5.10} \times 100 = \dfrac{5.10}{6.10} \times 100
$$

$$
R_{rec} = \mathbf{83.6 \; \%}
$$

Therefore, vanillin recovered in the miscella:

$$
m_{vanillin, recovered} = 2500 \times 0.836 = \mathbf{2090 \; \text{g}}
$$

**(iii)** Concentration in final extract (assuming 500 L miscella):

$$
C_{vanillin} = \dfrac{2090 \; \text{g}}{500 \; \text{L}} = \mathbf{4.18 \; \text{g·L}^{-1}}
$$

This exceeds the FCC single-fold specification of **4.0 g·L⁻¹** → **PASS** ✅.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Reference:** The Kremser equation (in the KTU formula sheet) and the working equations above are the two most common calculation patterns in KTU ESE Module-2 questions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters & Quality Outcomes

| Parameter | Clove Essential Oil (Steam Distn) | Clove Oleoresin (Solvent) | Vanilla Oleoresin (Ethanol) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Raw material | Dried buds (moisture 10 %) | Dried buds | Cured beans (moisture 25 – 30 %) |
| Solvent / Medium | Water (steam) | Acetone / Hexane | 50 % aq. Ethanol |
| Temperature | 98 – 100 °C | 50 – 60 °C | 40 – 50 °C |
| Time | 6 – 10 h | 3 – 4 h × 3 stages | 8 – 24 h × 3 stages |
| Yield | 15 – 20 % v/w | 18 – 25 % w/w | 28 – 32 % w/w |
| Principal Volatile | Eugenol (80 – 90 %) | Eugenol (30 – 50 %) | Vanillin (4 – 6 %) |
| Form | Pale yellow oil | Dark brown paste | Dark brown viscous oleoresin |
| Application | Dental, flavour, fragrance | Spice blends, flavours | Confectionery, beverages |

### 3.5 Chemistry of the Volatiles — Detailed Reaction Pathways

#### Eugenol ↔ Vanillin: The Clove-to-Vanilla Synthetic Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — synthetic vanillin is industrially produced from clove-derived eugenol.

**Step 1 — Alkaline Isomerization of Eugenol to Isoeugenol:**

$$
\text{Eugenol (4-allyl-2-methoxyphenol)} \xrightarrow{\text{KOH (50 \% aq.)}, \; 200 - 220 \; ^{\circ}C} \text{Isoeugenol (trans-4-propenyl-2-methoxyphenol)}
$$

Mechanism: Allyl → propenyl isomerization via carbanion intermediate.

**Step 2 — Oxidative Side-Chain Cleavage to Vanillin:**

$$
\text{Isoeugenol} \xrightarrow{\text{O}_2 \text{ or NaOCl/Acetone/Co(II)/air}, \; 25 - 80 \; ^{\circ}C} \text{Vanillin} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
$$

Industrial route: **Rhodia (Rhône-Poulenc) process** — uses electrochemical or nitrobenzene-mediated oxidation. Yield 70 – 80 % based on isoeugenol.

**Step 3 — Purification:**
- Acidification to pH 1 – 2 → vanillin precipitates (low solubility in cold water, 0.8 g·100 mL⁻¹ at 25 °C).
- Recrystallization from hot water → FCC-grade vanillin (mp 81 – 83 °C).

**Stoichiometric Mass-Balance of Eugenol → Vanillin:**

Eugenol (MW = 164.20) → Vanillin (MW = 152.15)

Theoretical yield = $(152.15 \div 164.20) \times 100 = \mathbf{92.7 \; \% \; (mass\,basis)}$

With industrial yield ~75 %:

$$
\eta_{process} = \dfrac{\text{actual vanillin mass}}{\text{theoretical vanillin mass}} = 0.75
$$

Therefore, to obtain 1 000 kg FCC vanillin, required eugenol:

$$
m_{eugenol} = \dfrac{1000}{0.927 \times 0.75} = \mathbf{1438 \; \text{kg clove oil (eugenol basis)}}
$$

Since clove oil is ~85 % eugenol:

$$
m_{clove\,oil} = \dfrac{1438}{0.85} = \mathbf{1692 \; \text{kg clove bud oil}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation is directly applicable to **process economics and valuation problems** in KTU ESE.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Clove Essential Oil Manufacturing (Steam Distillation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Clove Buds] --> B[Drying 50-60 C to 10% moisture]
    B --> C[Crushing Hammer Mill 2-4 mm]
    C --> D[Soaking in Water 4-8 h]
    D --> E[Steam Still SS304 Packing]
    E --> F[Steam Injection 1-1.5 bar g]
    F --> G[Co-Distillation at 98-100 C]
    G --> H[Shell and Tube Condenser]
    H --> I[Florentine Separator]
    I --> J[Heavy Oil Layer Settles Bottom]
    I --> K[Aqueous Hydrosol Recycle]
    J --> L[Na2SO4 Anhydrous Drying]
    L --> M[5 micron Cartridge Filtration]
    M --> N[Clove Bud Oil FCC Grade]
    N --> O[Amber Drum N2 Blanket 15-25 C]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Clove Oleoresin (Solvent Extraction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Clove Buds] --> B[Grinding 20-40 mesh]
    B --> C[Percolator SS316 Acetone or Hexane]
    C --> D[Counter-Current 3-4 Stages 50-60 C]
    D --> E[Miscella Filtration Plate Filter]
    E --> F[Vacuum Thin-Film Evaporator 50-70 C 200-600 mbar]
    F --> G[Vacuum Tray Dryer 30 C 6 h]
    G --> H[Clove Oleoresin 30-50% Eugenol]
    H --> I[Standardization with PG or Salt]
    I --> J[Packed in HDPE Drums]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Vanilla Curing and Oleoresin Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Green Vanilla Beans 9-10 mo] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Killing Hot Water 63-70 C 1-3 min]
    C --> D[Sweating Box 38-45 C 24-48 h]
    D --> E[Daily Sun-Dry and Re-Wrap 5-10 d]
    E --> F[Slow Shade Drying 1-3 months]
    F --> G[Conditioning 2-3 months closed box]
    G --> H[Cured Beans 1-4% Vanillin]
    H --> I[Cutting 2-5 cm Pieces]
    I --> J[Counter-Current Ethanol 50% aq 40-50 C]
    J --> K[Filtration Diatomaceous Earth]
    K --> L[Vacuum Evaporation 35-40 C]
    L --> M[Vanilla Oleoresin 25% TS 4-6% Vanillin]
    M --> N[Standardization to FCC Single-Fold]
    N --> O[Storage Amber Glass 15-25 C]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Eugenol to Synthetic Vanillin (Clove-to-Vanilla Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clove Oil 85% Eugenol] --> B[Vacuum Distillation Pure Eugenol]
    B --> C[Alkaline Isomerization KOH 200 C]
    C --> D[Isoeugenol Trans]
    D --> E[Oxidation NaOCl or O2 Co Catalyst]
    E --> F[Crude Vanillin]
    F --> G[Acidification pH 1-2]
    G --> H[Precipitation of Vanillin]
    H --> I[Recrystallization Hot Water]
    I --> J[FCC Grade Vanillin mp 81-83 C]
```
