## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define clove essential oil. List the principal volatile constituent and two important physicochemical properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Clove essential oil is the volatile, lipid-soluble fraction obtained by **steam or hydrodistillation** of dried, unopened flower buds of *Syzygium aromaticum*. The principal volatile constituent is **eugenol (4-allyl-2-methoxyphenol)**, present at **80 – 90 %** of the oil.

Important physicochemical properties:
1. **Specific gravity @ 25 °C = 1.038 – 1.060** (heavier than water).
2. **Refractive index @ 20 °C = 1.527 – 1.535**.
3. **Miscible in 2 volumes of 70 % ethanol** (FCC solubility test).
4. **Boiling point of eugenol = 254 °C** (or **co-distils with steam at ~98 °C**).
5. **Practically optically inactive** ($[\alpha]_D = 0$ to $-1.5°$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Briefly explain the role of the curing process in developing vanilla flavour.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Green vanilla beans contain flavour precursors, principally **glucovanillin**, which is virtually odourless. The curing process — a controlled sequence of **killing (hot-water dip at 63 – 70 °C), sweating (38 – 45 °C, 24 – 48 h), and slow drying (1 – 3 months)** — activates endogenous **β-glucosidases** that hydrolyse glucovanillin to **free vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde)** and D-glucose. Concurrently, **lipoxygenase-mediated lipid oxidation** and **Maillard browning** generate ~250 minor aroma volatiles (e.g., p-hydroxybenzaldehyde, vanillic acid, piperonal). Curing increases vanillin content from **< 0.05 % to 1 – 4 %**, creating the characteristic complex aroma.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the industrial process for clove oil (steam distillation) with that for clove oleoresin (solvent extraction).** Many students write "essential oil is obtained by solvent extraction" — this is a 4-mark loss.
> 2. **Vanilla flavour is NOT present in the green bean.** Always mention the **curing step first** before discussing oleoresin manufacture.
> 3. **Always specify the solvent** in any extraction process (acetone, hexane, or ethanol). "Solvent" alone loses a mark.
> 4. **Units are mandatory in numericals** (kg, °C, %, w/w). Missing unit = −1 mark per answer.
> 5. **Always state the analytical end-test** (GC for eugenol, HPLC for vanillin). Just naming "purity" is too vague for KTU valuation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model question, internal choice Option A]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **method of manufacture of clove essential oil by steam distillation**, with a neat flow diagram. List the major volatile constituents and the chemistry of the principal component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **method of manufacture and applications of clove oleoresin**. How does the oleoresin differ from the essential oil in composition, method of extraction, and end use? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Raw material preparation** (1 mark): Dried, unopened flower buds of *Syzygium aromaticum* (moisture < 12 %), crushed to 2 – 4 mm to rupture oil-bearing idioblasts.

2. **Still loading and steam injection** (2 marks): SS 304 cylindrical still with perforated false bottom; saturated steam at 1.0 – 1.5 bar (110 – 115 °C) injected from below. Co-distillation principle: eugenol (BP 254 °C) co-distils with water at ~98 °C, because total pressure equals sum of partial pressures:

$$
P_{total} = P_{water} + P_{eugenol}
$$

3. **Condensation and Florentine separation** (1 mark): Mixed vapours condensed in shell-and-tube condenser at 35 – 40 °C. Clove oil (density 1.06 g·mL⁻¹) is **heavier than water** → settles at the bottom in a Florentine flask; aqueous hydrosol is recycled to the boiler.

4. **Drying, polishing, and storage** (1 mark): Anhydrous Na₂SO₄ (1 – 2 % w/v) treatment, 5 µm filtration, storage in amber, N₂-blanketed aluminium drums at 15 – 25 °C.

5. **Major volatile constituents** (1 mark):
   - **Eugenol (4-allyl-2-methoxyphenol): 80 – 90 %**
   - **Eugenyl acetate: 5 – 15 %**
   - **β-Caryophyllene: 5 – 12 %**
   - α-Humulene, methyl eugenol (trace).

6. **Chemistry of eugenol** (1 mark): Molecular formula **C₁₀H₁₂O₂** (MW 164.20). It is a **phenylpropanoid** biosynthesized from L-phenylalanine via the **shikimate pathway**: PAL → C4H → 4CL → CCR → CAD → methylation (O-methyltransferase) → allyl side-chain formation.

7. **Yield and quality** (½ mark): Typical yield **15 – 20 %** v/w (FCC). Quality assessed by GC and standard physicochemical tests.

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Definition** (1 mark): Clove oleoresin is a **semi-solid, dark-brown extract** containing the full volatile profile **plus** non-volatile resins, fixed oils, and pigments. It is **NOT** an essential oil.

2. **Method of manufacture** (3 marks):
   - **Grinding** to 20 – 40 mesh.
   - **Solvent extraction** in counter-current percolators using **acetone, ethanol, or food-grade hexane** at 50 – 60 °C, 3 – 4 stages, with solvent-to-solid ratio $R_{ext} = 2.5$ L·kg⁻¹.
   - **Filtration** of miscella through plate filter.
   - **Solvent stripping** in a **vacuum thin-film (Luwa) evaporator** at 50 – 70 °C, 200 – 600 mbar to < 1 % residual solvent.
   - **Final drying** in a vacuum tray dryer at 30 °C for 6 h.
   - **Standardization** with permitted diluents (propylene glycol, salt, or ground spice) to a fixed eugenol specification (30 – 50 % eugenol).

3. **Composition** (1 mark): Eugenol (30 – 50 %), eugenyl acetate (8 – 12 %), β-caryophyllene (6 – 10 %), fixed oils + resins (20 – 30 %), pigments + tannins (8 – 12 %).

4. **Applications** (1 mark): Standardized spice blends (curry, garam masala, meat seasonings), encapsulated flavour powders, dental formulations, perfumery.

5. **Differences between oil and oleoresin** (1 mark):

| Feature | Essential Oil | Oleoresin |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| Extraction | Steam distillation | Solvent extraction |
| Form | Pale yellow liquid | Dark brown paste |
| Volatiles only | Yes | Volatiles + non-volatiles |
| Eugenol content | 80 – 90 % | 30 – 50 % |
| Solvent residue | None | < 1 ppm after stripping |
| Application | Fragrance, dental | Spice blends, seasonings |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model question, internal choice Option B]**

**(a)** Describe the **Bourbon method of curing vanilla beans**. What is the biochemical basis of flavour development during curing? List the major volatile aroma compounds of cured vanilla. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a flow diagram, describe the **industrial method of manufacture of vanilla oleoresin/extract**. How is supercritical CO₂ (SC-CO₂) extraction advantageous over conventional alcoholic extraction? Calculate the % recovery of vanillin in a 3-stage counter-current extractor for 100 kg of cured beans (2.5 % vanillin) using 500 L of 50 % ethanol, given $K = 1.2$ and stage efficiency = 0.85. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Bourbon curing method** (3 marks): Step-by-step:
   - **Harvest** mature green beans (9 – 10 months, tip pale yellow).
   - **Killing**: Hot water dip at 63 – 70 °C for 1 – 3 min, OR sweating box at 38 – 45 °C for 24 – 48 h.
   - **Sweating**: Wrapped in wool blankets in wooden boxes, 38 – 45 °C, 60 – 80 % RH, 5 – 10 days, with daily sun-drying and re-wrapping.
   - **Slow drying**: Spread on shade racks for 1 – 3 months.
   - **Conditioning**: Bundled in closed boxes 2 – 3 months to mature the aroma.

2. **Biochemical basis of flavour development** (2 marks):
   - **β-Glucosidase hydrolysis** of glucovanillin → free vanillin + D-glucose (rises from 0.05 % to 2 – 4 %).
   - **Lipoxygenase cascade** on membrane linoleic acid → hydroperoxides → C6-aldehydes (hexanal, etc.) that participate in Maillard reactions.
   - **Maillard browning** between amino acids and reducing sugars → pyrazines, furans, thiophenes → minor but character-impact volatiles.

3. **Major volatile aroma compounds of cured vanilla** (2 marks):
   - **Vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde)** — 15 000 – 40 000 mg·kg⁻¹
   - **p-Hydroxybenzaldehyde** — 100 – 500 mg·kg⁻¹
   - **Vanillic acid** — 200 – 800 mg·kg⁻¹
   - **p-Hydroxybenzyl alcohol** — 50 – 300 mg·kg⁻¹
   - **Piperonal (heliotropin)**, **anisyl alcohol**, **acetovanillone** (trace).

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Industrial method of vanilla oleoresin/extract** (3 marks):
   - **Bean cutting**: Cured beans cut into 2 – 5 cm pieces.
   - **Counter-current extraction** with 35 – 60 % aqueous ethanol, $R_{ext} = 4 - 6$ L·kg⁻¹, 40 – 50 °C, 3 – 5 stages.
   - **Filtration** through diatomaceous earth.
   - **Vacuum evaporation** at 35 – 40 °C, 100 – 200 mbar → viscous oleoresin (TS > 25 %).
   - **Standardization** to FCC vanillin (≥ 4 g·L⁻¹ for single-fold).

2. **SC-CO₂ vs. conventional alcoholic extraction** (1 mark):
   - **No solvent residue** (CO₂ is gaseous at ambient).
   - **Pesticide-free, residue-free**, food-grade.
   - **Lower temperature** (40 – 50 °C) → preserves thermolabile volatiles.
   - **Tunable selectivity** by varying pressure (10 – 35 MPa).
   - **Green technology** with recyclable solvent.

3. **Numerical — % recovery of vanillin** (3 marks):
   - Mass of vanillin in feed = $100 \times 0.025 = 2.5 \; \text{kg} = 2500 \; \text{g}$.
   - $R_{ext} = 500 \div 100 = 5 \; \text{L·kg}^{-1}$.
   - Using single-stage equivalent recovery formula:

$$
R_{rec} = \dfrac{K \cdot R_{ext} \cdot \eta_{stage}}{1 + K \cdot R_{ext} \cdot \eta_{stage}} \times 100
$$

$$
R_{rec} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 5 \times 0.85}{1 + 1.2 \times 5 \times 0.85} \times 100
$$

$$
R_{rec} = \dfrac{5.10}{6.10} \times 100 = \mathbf{83.6 \; \%}
$$

- Vanillin recovered = $2500 \times 0.836 = \mathbf{2090 \; \text{g}}$.
- Concentration in miscella = $2090 \div 500 = \mathbf{4.18 \; \text{g·L}^{-1}}$ → meets FCC single-fold specification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Module 2, Clove & Vanilla:**

- **Clove** is the dried unopened bud of *Syzygium aromaticum* (Myrtaceae); **eugenol = 80 – 90 %** of essential oil.
- **Clove essential oil** is manufactured by **steam distillation** (co-distillation principle: $P_{total} = P_{water} + P_{eugenol}$).
- **Clove oleoresin** is manufactured by **solvent extraction** (acetone, hexane, or ethanol), followed by **vacuum thin-film evaporation**.
- **Yield benchmarks**: Clove bud oil 15 – 20 %; clove oleoresin 18 – 25 % w/w.
- **Vanilla** is the cured pod of *Vanilla planifolia* (Orchidaceae); **vanillin is the principal flavour**, formed by **β-glucosidase hydrolysis of glucovanillin** during curing.
- **Curing steps**: Killing (63 – 70 °C, 1 – 3 min) → Sweating (38 – 45 °C, 5 – 10 d) → Slow drying (1 – 3 mo) → Conditioning (2 – 3 mo).
- **Vanilla oleoresin/extract** is made by **counter-current aqueous ethanol extraction** (35 – 60 %), followed by vacuum evaporation; **SC-CO₂** is a modern residue-free alternative.
- **Clove oil is the industrial precursor of synthetic vanillin**: eugenol → isoeugenol (KOH, 200 °C) → vanillin (oxidation). **Theoretical yield = 92.7 %** on mass basis.
- **Eugenol** is C₁₀H₁₂O₂ (MW 164.20, BP 254 °C); **Vanillin** is C₈H₈O₃ (MW 152.15, MP 81 – 83 °C).
- **Distribution coefficient K and stage efficiency η** govern extraction recovery; use **Kremser equation** for multi-stage design.
- **Quality control**: GC for eugenol; HPLC for vanillin; FCC / ISO 3141 / IS 6692 are the main standards.
- **Difference table (oil vs. oleoresin)**: Distillation vs. Solvent; Liquid vs. Paste; Volatiles only vs. Volatiles + Resins.
- **Numerical must-knows**: Antoine vapour-pressure equation; steam-to-oil ratio (40 – 60 kg·kg⁻¹); Kremser stage equation; mass-balance of eugenol → vanillin (92.7 %).
- **Common pitfall**: Do **NOT** say "vanilla flavour is present in the green bean" — it must be enzymatically generated by curing.
- **Common pitfall**: Do **NOT** confuse eugenol (phenylpropanoid) with capsaicin or piperine (alkaloid/amide class).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Control (QC)** in the context of spices and plantation crops technology refers to the systematic, planned set of activities and standardized procedures implemented at every stage — from pre-harvest to post-harvest, processing, packaging, and distribution — to ensure that the produce conforms to predefined physical, chemical, sensory, microbiological, and safety parameters prescribed by regulatory bodies such as the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, **Spices Board of India**, **American Spice Trade Association (ASTA)**, and the **European Spice Association (ESA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Focus):**
> Quality control is defined as the operational technique and regulatory framework used to measure, evaluate, and maintain the inherent and extrinsic characteristics of spices (e.g., black pepper, cardamom, turmeric, ginger, chilli) and plantation crops (e.g., tea, coffee, cocoa, rubber) within specified tolerance limits of national and international standards.

The principal quality attributes monitored under QC frameworks include:

- **Physical parameters**: appearance, size, color, uniformity, density, and the count of defective units per unit mass.
- **Chemical parameters**: moisture content, volatile oil content, oleoresin content, piperine, capsaicin, curcumin, total ash, acid-insoluble ash, and crude fiber.
- **Sensory parameters**: aroma, flavor, pungency, and color intensity assessed through trained panel evaluations.
- **Safety parameters**: pesticide residues, heavy metal contamination, aflatoxin levels, microbiological load, and absence of foreign matter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen Inspector"**
> Imagine a famous restaurant chain that promises its customers a uniform taste across 500 outlets worldwide. To deliver this promise, the chain employs quality control inspectors who visit every kitchen, take samples of the signature dish, and measure it against a *master recipe standard* — checking saltiness (chemical), portion size (physical), aroma (sensory), and the absence of harmful bacteria (safety). If a kitchen's sample deviates beyond the acceptable limit, that kitchen is corrected immediately.
>
> **Spices and plantation crops work exactly the same way.** A single batch of black pepper exported from Kerala must match a globally accepted "master recipe standard" (e.g., ASTA cleanliness specifications) so that the buyer in Germany or the USA receives a product indistinguishable from the previous batch. QC is the "kitchen inspector" that ensures this consistency and safety — protecting the consumer, the trader's reputation, and the nation's export revenue.

## 1.3 Governing Bodies and Their Standards

| Regulatory Body | Jurisdiction | Primary Standard Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) | India | Domestic grading, terminology, and test methods (IS codes) |
| **FSSAI** (Food Safety and Standards Authority) | India | Safety, contaminants, labelling (FSSAI License) |
| **Spices Board of India** | India | Export promotion, registration, quality certification |
| **AGMARK** (Agricultural Marketing) | India | Grading of agricultural commodities including spices |
| **ASTA** (American Spice Trade Association) | USA / Global | Export cleanliness, pesticide residue limits |
| **ESA** (European Spice Association) | Europe | European market specifications, contaminant limits |
| **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) | Global | Harmonized international standards (e.g., ISO 676 for spices nomenclature) |
| **Codex Alimentarius Commission** | Global | International food safety and quality benchmarks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard reference metric for moisture in most cured spices under **IS 1797 (Spices and Condiments)** and **AGMARK** is **≤ 11%** for whole spices and **≤ 10%** for ground spices, with the **volatile oil content** acting as the chief indicator of aromatic potency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar-Wise Breakdown of Quality Control

### Pillar 1 — Physical Quality Parameters
These are the *first* attributes visually assessed in trade, often determining the grade and price of the lot.

- **Size & Shape**: Sieve analysis — passing through standard test sieves (e.g., for black pepper: 6 mm, 5 mm, 4 mm grades).
- **Bulk Density (BD)**: Mass per unit volume, expressed in **g/L** or **kg/m³**. Indicates maturity and drying efficiency.
- **Color**: Measured by visual grading (e.g., "Extra Special", "Special", "Standard" for cardamom green) or instrumentally by a **colorimeter** (L*, a*, b* Hunter scale).
- **Defect Count**: Number of mouldy, insect-damaged, immature, or broken units per **100 g** sample.
- **Foreign Matter (FM)**: Defined as any material other than the named spice — soil, stones, stalks, or other plant parts. BIS limits FM to **≤ 2%** for most whole spices.

### Pillar 2 — Chemical Quality Parameters
The *scientific core* of QC, requiring laboratory instrumentation.

- **Moisture Content (MC)**: Determined gravimetrically by oven-drying at **105 ± 2 °C** to constant weight, or by **Dean-Stark distillation** for oleoresins.
- **Volatile Oil (VO)**: Determined by **hydrodistillation** in a **Clevenger apparatus**; reported as **mL per 100 g** of dry spice. E.g., cardamom requires ≥ **3.0%** VO.
- **Oleoresin**: Extracted by organic solvent (acetone/ethanol) in a **Soxhlet extractor**; reported as **% w/w**.
- **Total Ash**: Residue after incineration at **550 ± 20 °C**; indicates mineral/inorganic content.
- **Acid-Insoluble Ash (AIA)**: The fraction of ash insoluble in dilute HCl — a marker of soil/dust contamination.
- **Crude Fiber**: Residue after acid-alkali digestion; high fiber in spices like ginger or turmeric suggests lower extractable value.

### Pillar 3 — Sensory Quality Parameters
- Conducted by a **trained sensory panel** under controlled conditions (ISO 8589).
- Evaluated attributes: aroma intensity, flavor profile, pungency, bitterness, and aftertaste.
- For pungency in chilli and black pepper, the **Scoville Heat Unit (SHU)** and the more accurate **High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)** quantification of **capsaicin** or **piperine** are used.

### Pillar 4 — Safety & Contaminant Parameters
- **Pesticide Residues**: Quantified by **Gas Chromatography (GC)** and **GC-MS**; compared against **MRLs (Maximum Residue Limits)**.
- **Heavy Metals**: Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Mercury (Hg) quantified by **Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS)** or **ICP-MS**.
- **Aflatoxins (B1, B2, G1, G2)**: Mycotoxin contamination from *Aspergillus* species, quantified by **HPLC-FLD** or **ELISA**; FSSAI limit for total aflatoxins in spices is **≤ 30 µg/kg**.
- **Microbiological Load**: Total plate count (TPC), yeast & mould count, coliform count, *Salmonella* and *E. coli* detection per **ICMSF** standards.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Units | Instrument / Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture Content | $\text{MC \%} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Hot-air oven at **105 °C** |
| Volatile Oil Recovery | $\text{VO \%} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % (v/w) | Clevenger apparatus |
| Oleoresin Yield | $\text{Oleoresin \%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{extract}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Soxhlet extractor |
| Total Ash | $\text{Total Ash \%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ash}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Muffle furnace at **550 °C** |
| Acid-Insoluble Ash | $\text{AIA \%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{AIA}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Dilute HCl digestion |
| Pungency (Scoville) | $\text{SHU} = \text{Capsaicin (ppm)} \times 16{,}000$ | SHU | HPLC + conversion factor |
| Bulk Density | $\text{BD} = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | g/mL or kg/m³ | Standard container |
| Defect Percentage | $\text{Defect \%} = \dfrac{N_{\text{defective}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Visual / sieve analysis |
| Foreign Matter | $\text{FM \%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{FM}}}{W_{\text{total sample}}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Manual sorting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every formula above, $W_0$ is the empty dish weight, $W_1$ is dish + sample weight before drying, and $W_2$ is dish + sample weight after drying to constant mass.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The QC system directly impacts **trade acceptance, consumer safety, and export revenue**. India — the world's largest producer, consumer, and exporter of spices — earns over **USD 4 billion** in annual spice exports. Without a robust QC framework, rejection at the importing port (e.g., the EU's Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed, **RASFF**) would cause massive economic loss. Hence, QC is the *engineering backbone* that converts raw agricultural produce into a globally tradable, standardized commodity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Methods of Quality Evaluation

| Quality Parameter | Standard Reference | Acceptable Limit (Example: Black Pepper) | Analytical Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture | IS 1797, ASTA | ≤ **11.0%** | Oven drying, **105 °C**, 4 h |
| Volatile Oil | IS 1797, ASTA | ≥ **2.0%** (v/w) | Clevenger hydrodistillation |
| Piperine | ASTA | ≥ **5.0%** (w/w) | HPLC-UV at **343 nm** |
| Total Ash | IS 1797 | ≤ **6.0%** | Muffle furnace, **550 °C** |
| Acid-Insoluble Ash | IS 1797 | ≤ **1.2%** | HCl digestion of ash |
| Foreign Matter | IS 1797 | ≤ **1.0%** | Manual sorting |
| Bulk Density | IS 4952 | ≥ **500 g/L** | Standard container |
| Aflatoxin (Total) | FSSAI | ≤ **30 µg/kg** | HPLC-FLD / ELISA |
| Lead (Pb) | FSSAI | ≤ **10 mg/kg** | AAS / ICP-MS |
| Salmonella | FSSAI | Absent in 25 g | Pre-enrichment + selective media |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Derivation — Moisture Content Determination

**Problem Statement:**
A **5.000 g** sample of ground turmeric was weighed in a pre-dried, empty petri dish with a tare weight of **40.000 g**. After drying in a hot-air oven at **105 ± 2 °C** for 4 hours and cooling in a desiccator, the final weight was found to be **44.625 g**. Determine the moisture content of the sample and decide whether it conforms to the **BIS specification (IS 3576)** of ≤ **10%** for ground turmeric.

### Step 1 — Identify the Known Variables

We identify the following measurements from the laboratory record:

$$W_0 = 40.000 \text{ g (empty petri dish weight)}$$

$$W_1 = 45.000 \text{ g (petri dish + sample weight before drying)}$$

$$W_2 = 44.625 \text{ g (petri dish + sample weight after drying)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Moisture Formula

The standard gravimetric moisture formula is:

$$\text{MC \%} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 - W_0} \times 100$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

We substitute the measured values into the formula:

$$\text{MC \%} = \frac{45.000 - 44.625}{45.000 - 40.000} \times 100$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Numerator

The numerator represents the mass of water lost during drying:

$$45.000 - 44.625 = 0.375 \text{ g}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Denominator

The denominator represents the initial mass of the spice sample:

$$45.000 - 40.000 = 5.000 \text{ g}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Final Percentage

We perform the division and multiply by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$\text{MC \%} = \frac{0.375}{5.000} \times 100 = 7.500 \text{ \%}$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the Result Against the Standard

The calculated moisture content is **7.500 %**, which is **less than** the BIS upper limit of **10%** for ground turmeric. The sample **conforms** to the standard. This also implies the sample is microbiologically safer (lower water activity, $a_w$), since the moisture content is sufficiently low to prevent mould proliferation during storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Water Activity ($a_w$):**
> The moisture content alone is not a full predictor of spoilage. The more rigorous metric is **water activity** ($a_w$), defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in the product to the saturation vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature.
>
> $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$
>
> Most spoilage bacteria require $a_w \geq 0.91$, most yeasts $\geq 0.88$, and most moulds $\geq 0.70$. Drying spices to ≤ 11% moisture typically brings $a_w$ below 0.60, making them shelf-stable for 12–24 months.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Derivation — Volatile Oil Content

**Problem Statement:**
A **20.00 g** sample of cardamom was hydrodistilled in a Clevenger apparatus for 4 hours. The volume of volatile oil collected was **0.85 mL**. Compute the volatile oil content and check against the **AGMARK specification** of ≥ **3.0% v/w** for premium-grade large cardamom.

### Step 1 — State the Formula

$$\text{VO \%} = \frac{V_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$\text{VO \%} = \frac{0.85 \text{ mL}}{20.00 \text{ g}} \times 100$$

### Step 3 — Calculate

$$\text{VO \%} = 0.0425 \times 100 = 4.25 \text{ \% (v/w)}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

The volatile oil content is **4.25%**, which **exceeds** the AGMARK minimum of **3.0%**. The sample qualifies for the premium grade — a strong indicator of rich aroma, freshness, and high consumer acceptability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Spices & Plantation Crops Quality Control Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Intake] --> B[Pre-Processing Inspection]
    B --> C{Visual and Physical QC}
    C --> D[Sieve Analysis]
    C --> E[Color and Size Grading]
    C --> F[Foreign Matter Detection]
    D --> G[Chemical QC Lab]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Moisture Content]
    G --> I[Volatile Oil Assay]
    G --> J[Oleoresin Extraction]
    G --> K[Ash and Crude Fiber]
    H --> L{Specification Check}
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -->|Pass| M[Safety QC Lab]
    L -->|Fail| N[Reject or Rework Batch]
    M --> O[Pesticide Residue GC-MS]
    M --> P[Heavy Metal AAS]
    M --> Q[Aflatoxin HPLC]
    M --> R[Microbiological ICMSF]
    O --> S{Final Regulatory Compliance}
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S -->|Pass| T[Certification BIS FSSAI AGMARK ASTA]
    S -->|Fail| N
    T --> U[Packaging and Storage]
    U --> V[Domestic and Export Dispatch]
    N --> W[Documentation and CAPA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> The flow illustrates a sequential, multi-tier QC pipeline. Each block represents a quality checkpoint, and every arrow indicates the flow of the product through successive tests. The two decision diamonds **{Specification Check}** and **{Final Regulatory Compliance}** are the gatekeepers that either approve the batch for certification or divert it to **CAPA — Corrective and Preventive Action**. The two side branches — *Reject or Rework Batch* and *Documentation and CAPA* — form the closed-loop feedback system that drives continuous improvement, a principle aligned with **ISO 22000:2018 (Food Safety Management Systems)** and **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)**.

## 4.2 Textual Schematic — Quality Pyramid for Spices

The hierarchical importance of QC parameters can be visualized as a **pyramid of priority**:

- **Top (Safety Apex)**: Aflatoxins, pesticide residues, heavy metals, *Salmonella* — **non-negotiable**.
- **Upper Middle (Chemical Quality)**: Volatile oil, oleoresin, piperine, capsaicin, curcumin — **defines value**.
- **Middle (Physical Quality)**: Size, color, density, defect count — **defines grade**.
- **Lower Base (Sensory Quality)**: Aroma, flavor, pungency, aftertaste — **defines consumer preference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pyramid model is the gold standard mental map for answering KTU exam questions on QC prioritization: when in doubt, **safety always outranks quality**, which in turn outranks sensory appeal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Control. List any four quality parameters evaluated in spices. **[3 Marks]**
**Model Answer:**
Quality control is the operational framework of planned activities and standardized procedures used to measure, evaluate, and maintain the physical, chemical, sensory, and safety characteristics of spices within the tolerance limits prescribed by national and international standards (BIS, FSSAI, AGMARK, ASTA).

Four quality parameters evaluated in spices are:
- **Physical** — size, color, foreign matter, density.
- **Chemical** — moisture content, volatile oil, oleoresin, piperine.
- **Sensory** — aroma, flavor, pungency.
- **Safety** — aflatoxins, pesticide residues, heavy metals, *Salmonella*.

---

### Question 2: What is the BIS specification for moisture content in whole black pepper and ground spices? Why is moisture control critical? **[3 Marks]**
**Model Answer:**
As per **IS 1797**, the permissible moisture content is:
- **≤ 11%** for whole black pepper.
- **≤ 10%** for ground black pepper and most other ground spices.

Moisture control is critical because excess moisture:
- Promotes **mould growth** and **aflatoxin** production.
- Reduces **volatile oil** retention through hydrolysis.
- Causes **caking, discolouration**, and **microbial spoilage**.
- Lowers **bulk density**, making the product commercially unappealing.
- Shortens the **shelf life** from 24 months to a few weeks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Discuss the various physical and chemical quality parameters used in the evaluation of spices. Add a note on BIS and AGMARK standards. **[14 Marks]**

#### (a) Physical Quality Parameters [7 Marks]

Physical parameters are the first tier of QC evaluation and are assessed by visual and instrumental methods. They include:

- **Size and Shape**: Determined by sieve analysis using standard test sieves. For black pepper, grades are defined by retention on **6 mm, 5 mm, 4 mm** sieves (e.g., *Tellicherry Garbled Special Extra Bold* retains on 6.5 mm).
- **Color**: Evaluated visually under standardized lighting (D65) or instrumentally using a **colorimeter** measuring L*, a*, b* values. Cardamom "Extra Special" grade must show a uniform green color.
- **Bulk Density**: Reported in **g/L**; indicates maturity, drying efficiency, and freedom from hollow kernels.
- **Foreign Matter**: Includes soil, stones, stalks, and extraneous plant material. BIS limit is **≤ 1%** for whole black pepper and **≤ 2%** for most other whole spices.
- **Defect Count**: Categories include immature, shriveled, mouldy, insect-damaged, and broken units; expressed as a percentage of the total lot.

#### (b) Chemical Quality Parameters and Standards [7 Marks]

Chemical parameters are evaluated in a QC laboratory and form the scientific basis for grading and export clearance.

- **Moisture Content**: Gravimetric oven method at **105 ± 2 °C**; BIS limit ≤ 11% (whole) and ≤ 10% (ground).
- **Volatile Oil**: Clevenger hydrodistillation; ASTA requires ≥ 2.0% for black pepper, AGMARK requires ≥ 3.0% for large cardamom.
- **Oleoresin**: Soxhlet extraction with acetone; reported as % w/w. Indicates the total extractable flavor and color compounds.
- **Total Ash and Acid-Insoluble Ash**: Furnace incineration at **550 °C**; AIA indicates soil contamination.
- **Crude Fiber**: Acid-alkali digestion; high values indicate over-mature or stem-rich material.
- **Piperine, Capsaicin, Curcumin**: Quantified by **HPLC** with UV detection at specific wavelengths (piperine at **343 nm**, capsaicin at **280 nm**, curcumin at **425 nm**).

**BIS and AGMARK Standards:**
- **BIS** prescribes the **IS codes** (e.g., IS 1797 for black pepper, IS 3576 for turmeric, IS 1907 for cardamom) that define test methods, grading terminology, and tolerance limits.
- **AGMARK** is a voluntary **certification mark** under the Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, **1937**, used for domestic trade. Grades are denoted by the AGMARK label — *Special*, *Standard*, *General*.
- Together, BIS and AGMARK ensure **uniformity**, **consumer protection**, and **fair trade practices** in the Indian spice market.

---

### Question B: Explain in detail the safety parameters tested in spices and plantation crops. Add a note on aflatoxin contamination, its causes, and permissible limits. **[14 Marks]**

#### (a) Safety Parameters in Spices and Plantation Crops [7 Marks]

Safety testing is **mandatory** for both domestic sale (FSSAI) and export (ASTA, ESA, EU regulations). The key parameters are:

- **Pesticide Residues**: Quantified by **GC-MS/MS** and **LC-MS/MS**. Compared against **MRLs** (Maximum Residue Limits) set by FSSAI and the importing country. Common offenders in spices: chlorpyrifos, quinalphos, and monocrotophos.
- **Heavy Metals**: Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Mercury (Hg) — measured by **AAS (Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy)** or **ICP-MS (Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry)**. FSSAI limits: Pb ≤ 10 mg/kg, Cd ≤ 1.5 mg/kg in spices.
- **Microbiological Load**: Total Plate Count (TPC), yeast & mould count, coliform count, *Salmonella* (must be absent in **25 g**), and *E. coli* — per **ICMSF** sampling plans.
- **Mycotoxins**: Particularly aflatoxins and ochratoxin A, the most economically damaging safety hazards for spice exports.
- **Adulterants**: Detection of Sudan dye in chilli, lead chromate in turmeric, mineral oil in black pepper, and artificial colours — identified by **HPTLC** or **LC-MS**.

#### (b) Aflatoxin Contamination — Causes and Permissible Limits [7 Marks]

**Aflatoxins** are toxic secondary metabolites produced by *Aspergillus flavus* and *Aspergillus parasiticus*. They are the most regulated contaminants in global spice trade.

**Causes of Aflatoxin Contamination:**
- **Pre-harvest**: Drought stress, insect damage, and late-harvest maturity.
- **Post-harvest**: Improper drying (moisture > 12%), poor storage humidity (RH > 70%), and insect infestation.
- **Processing**: Lack of sorting to remove visibly mouldy kernels, where aflatoxin concentration can be 1000× higher than in healthy kernels.

**Types and Toxicity:**
- **Aflatoxin B1** — most potent natural hepatocarcinogen (Group 1 IARC carcinogen).
- **Aflatoxin B2, G1, G2** — co-occurring congeners with lesser potency.
- **Aflatoxin M1** — hydroxylated metabolite appearing in milk of livestock fed contaminated feed.

**Permissible Limits (FSSAI, 2011 / EU 1881/2006):**

| Commodity | Total Aflatoxin Limit | Aflatoxin B1 Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Spices (Chilli, Pepper, Turmeric, Nutmeg, etc.) | **≤ 30 µg/kg** (FSSAI) / **≤ 10 µg/kg** (EU) | **≤ 5 µg/kg** (EU) |
| Arecanut | **≤ 15 µg/kg** | — |
| Dried figs, groundnuts | **≤ 10 µg/kg** (EU) | **≤ 5 µg/kg** (EU) |

**Control Measures:**
- **GAP** (Good Agricultural Practices) — timely harvest, proper drying.
- **GHP** (Good Handling Practices) — moisture control below 10%, use of hermetic storage.
- **HACCP** — critical control points at drying, sorting, and storage.
- **Decontamination** — UV irradiation, ozone treatment, and ammoniation for high-value lots.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing BIS with AGMARK** in answers. Remember: BIS = standards body, AGMARK = certification mark for grading.
> - **Forgetting units** in chemical formulas — always write **% v/w for volatile oil** and **% w/w for moisture, ash, and oleoresin**.
> - **Ignoring safety parameters** when asked about QC. Safety (aflatoxins, pesticides) is the most mark-yielding section in KTU answers.
> - **Not mentioning specific Indian standards** (IS 1797, IS 3576, etc.) — citing standard codes shows depth and scores full marks.
> - **Writing vague, generic answers** for sensory evaluation. Always name the **method (Hedonic scale, ISO 8589)** and the **attribute (aroma, pungency)**.
> - **Skipping real-world numbers** (e.g., 11% moisture, 30 µg/kg aflatoxin) — KTU examiners reward students who quote actual specification values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality Control = Physical + Chemical + Sensory + Safety** — remember the four-pillar framework.
- **BIS** sets standards via **IS codes**; **AGMARK** is a grading mark; **FSSAI** enforces safety.
- **Moisture** is the **master variable** — control it first, and you control mould, aflatoxin, color, and shelf life.
- **Volatile oil (Clevenger)** and **oleoresin (Soxhlet)** are the *value-defining* parameters.
- **Aflatoxin B1** is the **most toxic** congener; FSSAI total limit is **30 µg/kg** in spices.
- **HPLC** is the gold standard for quantifying pungency (capsaicin, piperine) and curcumin.
- **GC-MS / LC-MS** are the gold standards for pesticide residue and mycotoxin quantification.
- **ASTA and ESA** standards govern **export** quality; FSSAI governs **domestic** safety.
- **ISO 22000 + HACCP** is the **management framework** that integrates all QC parameters into a closed-loop system.
- **Water activity ($a_w$)** is a better predictor of microbial spoilage than moisture content alone.
- **Quality pyramid rule**: **Safety > Chemical > Physical > Sensory** — in that order of priority.
- **Export rejection** in spices is most commonly due to **aflatoxins, pesticide residues, and *Salmonella*** — memorize these three.
- The standard drying temperature for moisture determination is **105 ± 2 °C**; ash incineration is at **550 ± 20 °C**.
- For **cardamom**, AGMARK requires **≥ 3%** volatile oil; for **black pepper**, ASTA requires **≥ 2%** volatile oil and **≥ 5%** piperine.
- The single most important formula: **MC % = (W₁ − W₂) / (W₁ − W₀) × 100** — practice it until it is second nature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tea: Occurrence - Chemistry of Constituents - Harvesting - Types of Tea - Green
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tea: Occurrence - Chemistry of Constituents - Harvesting - Types of Tea - Green

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Botanical and Industrial Definition of Tea

**Tea** is a manufactured aromatic beverage prepared by the controlled infusion of the cured young leaves, buds, and tender stems of the evergreen plant *Camellia sinensis* (L.) O. Kuntze, belonging to the family **Theaceae** (or Camelliaceae). From a KTU 2024 Scheme standpoint, *tea* is classified as a **plantation beverage crop**, distinct from spices, and the term legally refers to the product derived exclusively from *Camellia sinensis* and its recognized botanical varieties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The word "Tea" used commercially refers only to products derived from *Camellia sinensis*. Beverages made from other plants (e.g., mint, chamomile) are called "tisanes" or "herbal infusions" and are NOT tea.

### 1.2 Three Principal Botanical Varieties

| Variety | Common Name | Morphology | Natural Habitat |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Camellia sinensis* var. *sinensis* | Chinese tea | Small, hardy, slow-growing, small leaves | Cool, high-altitude regions of China |
| *Camellia sinensis* var. *assamica* | Assam tea | Tall tree, large drooping leaves | Hot, humid lowlands of Assam (India) |
| *Camellia sinensis* var. *cambodiensis* | Cambodian/Indian hybrid | Intermediate morphology | Southeast Asia, used in hybrid breeding |

Most commercial cultivars are **clonal hybrids** of these three, e.g., *TV1, TV9, TRI2025, BSS1, S.3A/3* (popular Indian clones).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Tea as a Chemical Time Capsule"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a freshly plucked tea leaf as a **locked chemistry box**. Inside the box are colorless catechins, amino acids like L-theanine, and aromatic precursors. Once the leaf is crushed, an enzyme called **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** acts like a switch — it unlocks the box and turns the contents into the brown pigments (theaflavins) and brisk flavor molecules (thearubigins) we associate with black tea. If we "freeze the box" by heating (as in **green tea**), the enzyme is destroyed and the contents remain as they were — fresh, grassy, and green. So, *all true teas come from the same leaf — the difference is whether we let the enzyme act or not.*

### 1.4 Global Occurrence and Production

Tea is cultivated between **45°N and 34°S latitudes** in regions where climate is suitable.

**Top 5 Tea-Producing Countries (2023 data, FAO):**

| Rank | Country | Approx. Annual Production |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **China** | ~3.1 million tonnes |
| 2 | **India** | ~1.4 million tonnes |
| 3 | **Kenya** | ~0.57 million tonnes |
| 4 | **Sri Lanka** | ~0.28 million tonnes |
| 5 | **Türkiye** | ~0.25 million tonnes |

**Major Tea-Producing Regions of India:**

- **Assam** (Brahmaputra Valley) — CTC and orthodox black tea
- **Darjeeling** (West Bengal) — High-quality "Champagne of teas"
- **Nilgiris** (Tamil Nadu) — Unique flavor profile
- **Munnar, Wayanad, Idukki** (Kerala) — Predominantly CTC teas
- **Himachal Pradesh & Uttarakhand** — Small-scale cultivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Kerala Context:** Kerala contributes ~6% of India's tea production. Tea estates in Idukki, Wayanad, and Munnar (Kannan Devan Hills Plantations - KDHP) are well-known, and the state produces a significant quantity of **CTC tea** for the export market.

### 1.5 Agro-Climatic Requirements

- **Altitude:** Sea level up to 2,400 m (high altitude = superior flavor)
- **Temperature:** **10–30 °C** (optimum 20–25 °C)
- **Rainfall:** **1,500–3,000 mm** well distributed across the year
- **Relative Humidity:** 70–90%
- **Soil pH:** **4.5–5.5** (acidic, well-drained loamy soils rich in organic matter)
- **Slope:** Gentle to moderate slopes for drainage

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemistry of Tea Constituents — The Biochemical Profile

A fresh tea leaf contains approximately **75% water** and **25% solids**. The dry matter composition is highly complex and is the biochemical foundation of tea quality.

### 2.2 Comprehensive Constituent Table (Dry Weight Basis)

| Constituent Class | Sub-component | % Dry Weight | Functional/Quality Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyphenols** (Catechins) | Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), Epicatechin gallate (ECG), Epigallocatechin (EGC), Epicatechin (EC) | **20–30%** | Astringency, antioxidant, color precursors |
| **Flavonols** | Quercetin, Kaempferol, Myricetin glycosides | 3–4% | Co-pigments, antioxidant |
| **Methylxanthines (Alkaloids)** | **Caffeine, Theobromine, Theophylline** | **3–4%** | Stimulant, briskness, bitterness |
| **Amino Acids** | L-Theanine, Glutamic acid, Aspartic acid | 1–5% | Umami, sweetness, calming effect |
| **Carbohydrates** | Cellulose, Pectins, Starch, Mono-sugars | 10–25% | Structure, energy |
| **Proteins** | Enzymes (PPO, Peroxidase), Storage proteins | 15–20% | Enzyme catalysts, building blocks |
| **Lipids** | Linoleic, Linolenic acids | 2–5% | Membrane, aroma precursors |
| **Pigments** | Chlorophyll, Carotenoids, Xanthophylls | 0.5–1% | Color, volatile precursors |
| **Minerals & Trace Elements** | Mn, K, Ca, F, Al, Zn | 5–6% | Growth, fluoride content |
| **Vitamins** | Vitamin C, B-complex, E, K | Trace | Nutritional value |
| **Volatile Compounds** | Linalool, Geraniol, Hexenal, Aldehydes | 0.01–0.1% | Aroma (essential oils) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Polyphenols are the heart of tea chemistry.** The ratio of **Total Catechins to Total Methylxanthines (TC/TM ratio)** is a key biochemical quality index. A ratio >8 is considered excellent for quality green tea.

### 2.3 Key Chemical Reactions in Tea Processing

#### 2.3.1 Catechins — The Master Molecules

The four major catechins in descending order of abundance in green tea:

1. **Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)** — Most abundant (~50% of total catechins)
2. **Epigallocatechin (EGC)**
3. **Epicatechin gallate (ECG)**
4. **Epicatechin (EC)**

#### 2.3.2 The Oxidation Reaction (Black Tea Pathway)

$$\text{Catechin} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \xrightarrow[\text{(in presence of O}_2\text{)}]{\text{Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)}} \text{Quinone} \rightarrow \text{Theaflavins} \rightarrow \text{Thearubigins}$$

- **Theaflavins (TF)** — Orange-red, ~3% — contribute briskness and brightness
- **Thearubigins (TR)** — Brown, ~12% — contribute body, color, and depth

#### 2.3.3 Theanine — Tea's Signature Amino Acid

**L-Theanine (γ-glutamylethylamide)** is a non-protein amino acid found almost exclusively in tea (and in the mushroom *Xerocomus badius*). It constitutes ~50% of the total free amino acids in green tea and is responsible for the "umami" (savory) taste and the calming, focused mental state attributed to tea.

$$\text{L-Theanine} \xrightarrow{\text{Stress/Heat}} \text{Glutamic Acid} + \text{Ethylamine}$$

### 2.4 Harvesting of Tea — "Two Leaves and a Bud"

#### 2.4.1 Plucking Standards

| Plucking Standard | Composition | Quality Grade |
|---|---|---|
| **Fine Plucking** | Two leaves + an unopened bud | Highest (Darjeeling, Orthodox) |
| Medium Plucking | Two leaves + a bud + one partially open leaf | Standard commercial |
| **Coarse Plucking** | Three leaves + a bud | Lower grade (mass-market CTC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "Two leaves and a bud" is the universal standard for premium quality. The bud (pekoe) is covered in fine silvery hairs (called "tip") which contain high concentrations of amino acids, particularly L-theanine, and contribute a delicate flavor.

#### 2.4.2 Plucking Rounds and Frequency

- **Plucking cycle (round):** Every **7–10 days** during peak growth (May–September in India)
- **Slow growth months:** Plucking rounds extend to 14–21 days
- **Annual yield:** ~2,500–3,500 kg of made tea per hectare
- **Time of day:** Early morning (8–11 AM) is preferred because leaves are turgid, cool, and have optimal moisture content.

#### 2.4.3 Methods of Harvesting

**A. Hand Plucking (Manual)**
- Traditional, labor-intensive, but provides selective plucking
- Pluckers carry baskets on their backs; one plucker can harvest **30–40 kg of green leaf per day**
- Used for fine-quality orthodox and Darjeeling teas

**B. Mechanical Harvesting**
- Tractor-mounted or self-propelled machines with cutting blades
- Used for CTC and mass-market teas
- Cannot selectively pick "two leaves and a bud" — often results in **coarse plucking**
- Reduces labor cost by 60–70%

**C. Shear Harvesting**
- Hand-held shears for small estates

#### 2.4.4 Harvesting Season in India

| Season | Months | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **First Flush** | March–May | Light, fragrant, premium (Darjeeling) |
| **Second Flush** | June–July | Stronger, fuller-bodied |
| **Monsoon/Rains** | Aug–Sept | Bulk quantity, lower quality |
| **Autumn Flush** | Oct–Nov | Mellow, smooth |

### 2.5 Types of Tea — The Six Categories (KTU 2024 Classification)

The global classification of tea is based on the **degree of enzymatic oxidation** during processing.

| Type | Oxidation Level | Color of Liquor | Processing Style |
|---|---|---|---|
| **White Tea** | ~5–10% (minimal) | Pale yellow | Wither + sun-dry |
| **Yellow Tea** | ~10–15% | Yellow | Wither + fix + men-hui (sweating) |
| **Green Tea** | **0–5% (non-fermented)** | **Green-yellow** | Wither + heat-fix + roll + dry |
| **Oolong Tea** | ~30–70% (semi-fermented) | Golden-orange | Wither + partial ferment + fix + roll + dry |
| **Black Tea** | ~95–100% (fully fermented) | Deep red-brown | Wither + roll + ferment + dry |
| **Dark/Pu-erh Tea** | Microbial post-fermentation | Dark brown | Wither + fix + pile-ferment + dry |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Fermentation" in tea terminology is a misnomer.** It refers to **enzymatic oxidation by PPO**, not microbial fermentation. The only tea truly involving microbes is **Pu-erh (Dark) tea**, where *Aspergillus niger* and other fungi are involved.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Process Flow, and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Green Tea Manufacturing Process

The following block-flow traces the **green tea production process** from the field to the finished product. This is the most examinable flow in this module.

---

**Step 1 — Plucking (Harvesting)**
Green tea is made exclusively from the **immature, tender apical growth** — the unopened bud and the first two leaves. The leaf must be intact, undamaged, and harvested before 9 AM to preserve maximum polyphenols and moisture.

> **Why tender leaves?** Tender leaves have **higher catechin and L-theanine content** and **lower fiber content** → smoother, sweeter liquor.

**Step 2 — Withering (Partial, not full)**

- Fresh leaf is spread on **wire mesh "tats" (racks)** in a shaded, ventilated area.
- Duration: **4–8 hours**.
- Moisture reduction: from ~78% to ~70% (partial wilting only — not the deep withering used for black tea).
- The leaf becomes flaccid (rubbery) so it does not break during rolling.

> **Why partial wither only?** Excessive withering would activate PPO and lead to color change. We want to keep the leaf *green*.

**Step 3 — Fixation (Heat Killing of Enzymes) — THE CRITICAL STEP**

This is the step that distinguishes green tea from all other teas. The goal is to **denature Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** and inactivate all enzymes.

Two main methods exist:

| Method | Region | Process | Temperature/Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pan-firing** (Chinese — *cha qing*) | China, India | Leaves are tossed in hot iron pan | 200–250 °C for 2–3 minutes |
| **Steaming** (Japanese — *seiryu cha*) | Japan, some Kerala units | Leaves pass through steam conveyor | 95–100 °C for 30–90 seconds |

**Chemical event in Step 3:**

$$\text{Active PPO} \;(Cu^{2+}-enzyme) \xrightarrow{\Delta T \approx 85\text{–}90°C} \text{Denatured (Inactive PPO)}$$

Once PPO is denatured, **enzymatic oxidation is halted** and the catechins remain intact — preserving the green color and the antioxidant profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "**Fixation = Frying/Steaming to kill PPO**" is the most important single sentence in this module. Master this, and the chemistry of all tea types becomes a logical extension.

**Step 4 — Rolling and Shaping**

- After fixation, the cooled leaf is passed through **roller machines** (a series of cylindrical pressure rollers).
- The leaf is twisted into characteristic **needle, gunpowder, or curly shapes**.
- Rolling ruptures cells slightly, allowing controlled release of cell sap, but without oxidation (since PPO is dead).
- Duration: 30–45 minutes.

**Step 5 — Drying (Final Moisture Reduction)**

- Rolling is followed by drying in a **chain-belt or tray dryer**.
- Temperature: **90–110 °C** for 20–30 minutes.
- Final moisture content: **< 3%** (to enable long shelf life and prevent microbial spoilage).
- The product is now a stable, finished "made tea."

**Step 6 — Grading and Sorting**

Leaves are sorted by size through vibrating sieves into grades such as:
- **Pekoe (P), Orange Pekoe (OP), Flowery Orange Pekoe (FOP), Broken grades (BOP, BP), Fannings (F), Dust (D)**.

**Step 7 — Packing and Storage**

- Sealed airtight (vacuum or nitrogen-flushed) to prevent moisture absorption, oxidation, and aroma loss.
- Stored at **cool, dark, dry** conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Catechin → Theaflavin Conversion Stoichiometry

Although green tea stops oxidation, understanding the chemistry of catechin oxidation (which happens in black tea) is important for KTU questions that ask to **compare green and black tea chemistry**.

**Starting reaction:**

$$2\,\text{EC} + 2\,\text{EGC} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \rightarrow \text{Theaflavin (TF)} + 2\,H_2O$$

**General form of Theaflavin synthesis:**

$$\text{Catechin}_1 + \text{Catechin}_2 \xrightarrow[O_2]{PPO} \text{Theaflavin} + H_2O$$

When both catechins carry a **gallate ester (galloyl) group**, the product is a **Theaflavin Digallate**, which is the most pungent and briskly astringent compound in black tea.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why green tea retains its green color:** Without the enzymatic reaction, catechins (which are colorless or pale yellow) remain intact. The chlorophylls and carotenoids in the leaf are also preserved by rapid heat-fixation, giving the dried leaf its characteristic **dull olive-green** to **silvery-green** appearance.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Green vs. Black Tea

| Parameter | Green Tea | Black Tea |
|---|---|---|
| **Catechin content** | Very high (80–90% of polyphenols intact) | Low (converted to TF/TR) |
| **Theaflavin content** | Trace | 2–3% |
| **Thearubigin content** | Trace | 10–15% |
| **Caffeine** | 3–4% (slightly higher) | 2.5–3.5% |
| **L-Theanine** | High (preserved by heat) | Lower (some degraded) |
| **Liquor color** | Pale yellow → green-yellow | Deep red-brown |
| **Astringency** | Soft, vegetable-like | Strong, brisk, malty |
| **Antioxidant capacity** | **Higher** (ORAC value higher) | Lower |
| **pH of infusion** | 6.0–7.0 | 4.5–5.5 |
| **Primary producing countries** | China, Japan, Vietnam, India (limited) | India, Kenya, Sri Lanka |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Health Note (for general knowledge — KTU often asks):** Green tea's high EGCG content is associated with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic, and cardioprotective effects (L-Theanine promotes α-wave brain activity, enhancing relaxation and focus).

### 3.4 Yield Calculation (Optional but Examinable)

**Manufactured tea yield from green leaf:**

$$\text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of made tea}}{\text{Mass of fresh green leaf}} \times 100$$

**Typical values:**
- Green tea yield: ~22–25% (4.0–4.5 kg of fresh leaf to make 1 kg of finished tea)
- Black tea yield: ~22–23% (similar)
- The water loss during withering, fixation, and drying is the main mass loss.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *With a neat schematic, explain the standard of "two leaves and a bud" in tea harvesting. Why is this standard important for quality?* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **"Two leaves and a bud"** standard in tea harvesting refers to plucking the **unopened apical bud (pekoe)** along with the **two youngest fully expanded leaves** below it. This is the **fine plucking standard**, and the bud is typically covered in fine silvery "tips" rich in amino acids (especially L-theanine) and catechins (especially EGCG). The standard is important because (i) tender apical leaves have the highest concentration of flavor and aroma precursors; (ii) lower fiber content gives a smoother, less astringent liquor; (iii) it ensures uniformity of quality and the typical "tip" appearance in finished orthodox and green teas; and (iv) plucking immature leaves encourages further bud break and maintains the **flat (plucked) table** of the tea bush, which boosts yield.

*(Diagrammatic representation may be added: a small diagram of a tea shoot showing the bud and the two leaves below it.)*

---

> **Q2.** *List the major chemical constituents of fresh tea leaves and indicate the role of the most abundant polyphenol.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major chemical constituents of fresh tea leaves (on dry weight basis) are:

1. **Polyphenols (Catechins)** — 20–30%
2. **Methylxanthines** (Caffeine, Theobromine, Theophylline) — 3–4%
3. **Amino acids** (mainly L-theanine) — 1–5%
4. **Carbohydrates** (cellulose, pectin, sugars) — 10–25%
5. **Proteins and enzymes** (Polyphenol Oxidase, Peroxidase) — 15–20%
6. **Minerals and trace elements** (Mn, K, Ca, F) — 5–6%
7. **Pigments and lipids** (Chlorophyll, Carotenoids) — 3–6%

The most abundant polyphenol is **Epigallocatechin Gallate (EGCG)**, which constitutes ~50% of total catechins. Its role is multi-fold: it is a **potent natural antioxidant** (free radical scavenger), it contributes the characteristic **astringent-vegetal taste** of green tea, and it serves as the **precursor** for theaflavins and thearubigins during black tea fermentation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A).** *Discuss in detail the chemistry of tea constituents with reference to polyphenols, methylxanthines, and amino acids. How does green tea processing differ from black tea processing with respect to these constituents?* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Polyphenols (7 marks)**

Polyphenols are the most abundant and chemically significant class of tea constituents, constituting **20–30% of the dry weight** of fresh tea leaves. The principal polyphenols belong to the **flavan-3-ol (catechin) group**. The four major catechins, in decreasing order of abundance, are:

1. **Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)** — ~50% of total catechins — the most bioactive
2. **Epigallocatechin (EGC)**
3. **Epicatechin gallate (ECG)**
4. **Epicatechin (EC)**

Catechins carry **hydroxyl (-OH) groups** that are responsible for their **antioxidant activity** by donating hydrogen atoms to free radicals. They are **colorless or pale yellow** in the fresh leaf, but in the presence of **oxygen and the enzyme Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, they undergo oxidation to form **quinones**, which then polymerize to form **theaflavins (orange-red)** and **thearubigins (dark brown)** — the pigments of black tea. In green tea, heat fixation **denatures PPO** and preserves catechins in their original reduced form, retaining the green color and the antioxidant capacity.

**(b) Methylxanthines and Amino Acids (7 marks)**

**Methylxanthines (3–4%)** are purine alkaloids comprising **caffeine (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine)** as the major component, along with smaller amounts of **theobromine** and **theophylline**. Caffeine is responsible for the **stimulant, alertness-promoting, and mild diuretic** effects of tea. It also contributes to the **briskness and bitterness** of black tea. The caffeine content of green tea (~3–4%) is slightly higher than that of black tea because of negligible loss during the short heat-fixation step.

**Amino acids (1–5%)** in tea are dominated by **L-theanine (γ-glutamylethylamide)**, a unique non-protein amino acid found almost exclusively in tea. L-theanine constitutes ~50% of the total free amino acids and is responsible for the **umami (savory) taste**, the **sweetness of the bud tip**, and the **calming, focused mental state** (alpha-wave promotion in the brain). Other amino acids include **glutamic acid, aspartic acid, and serine**. In green tea processing, the rapid heat-fixation **preserves amino acids**, giving green tea its characteristic **sweet-vegetal umami profile**. In contrast, the long fermentation step in black tea partially degrades amino acids, leading to lower L-theanine content and a more astringent, less sweet liquor.

**Comparative summary:**

| Constituent | Green Tea | Black Tea | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catechins | High (preserved) | Low (oxidized) | PPO denatured in green tea by heat |
| Caffeine | 3–4% | 2.5–3.5% | No loss in green tea; minor loss in black |
| L-Theanine | High | Moderate | Heat-fixation preserves it |

---

> **Q3(B).** *Describe the step-by-step manufacturing process of green tea, highlighting the biochemical changes at each stage. Mention the characteristic quality parameters of finished green tea.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Process of Green Tea Manufacturing (7 marks)**

Green tea manufacturing is designed to **prevent enzymatic oxidation** of catechins. The steps are:

**1. Plucking (Day 1, morning):** Only the **unopened bud and the first two leaves** are hand-plucked. This "fine pluck" is harvested when the leaf is tender and turgid, typically between 6 AM and 11 AM to minimize moisture loss and field heat.

**2. Partial Withering (4–8 hours):** Fresh leaf is spread thinly on wire-mesh racks (tats) in a well-ventilated shade. Moisture is reduced from ~78% to ~70%. The leaf becomes flaccid (rubbery) to prevent breakage in subsequent rolling. **Biochemically:** slight concentration of cell sap, but PPO remains inactive due to low moisture and lack of cell disruption.

**3. Fixation (Heat-Killing of PPO) — THE CRITICAL STEP (2–3 min in pan; 30–90 sec in steam):** The wilted leaf is subjected to high heat either by **pan-firing** in a hot iron wok (~200–250 °C) or by **steaming** on a conveyor (~95–100 °C). **Biochemically:** Polyphenol Oxidase, Peroxidase, and all other enzymes are **denatured**. This **locks in the green color**, the catechin profile, and the chlorophyll. This is the step that fundamentally distinguishes green tea from black tea.

**4. Rolling and Shaping (30–45 min):** The cooled, fixed leaf is passed through a rolling machine. Cells are slightly ruptured to release cell sap, and the leaf is shaped into needle, gunpowder, or curly forms. **Biochemically:** Limited cell disruption is permitted, but oxidation does not occur because PPO is already denatured.

**5. Drying (90–110 °C for 20–30 min):** Final moisture is reduced to **< 3%** for shelf-life stability.

**6. Grading and Sorting:** The dried tea is sieved into grades (P, OP, FOP, BOP, F, D).

**7. Packing:** Sealed in airtight, often nitrogen-flushed or vacuum packets to prevent moisture, oxygen, and aroma loss.

**(b) Characteristic Quality Parameters of Finished Green Tea (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Standard/Specification |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Dull olive-green to silvery-green; needle/curly shape |
| **Aroma** | Fresh, grassy, slightly sweet, with hints of chestnut |
| **Liquor color** | Pale yellow to green-yellow (NOT brown) |
| **Taste** | Mildly astringent, vegetal, sweet aftertaste (umami) |
| **Moisture** | < 3% |
| **Total ash** | < 8% (acid-soluble ash > 1%) |
| **Catechin content** | > 12% (in high-quality grade) |
| **Caffeine** | 2.5–4% |
| **L-Theanine** | > 1% |
| **Microbial load** | < 10⁴ CFU/g (Tea Board's FSSAI limits) |
| **Iron filings / extraneous matter** | Absent (per Tea Act, 1953) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Green Tea:**
> - ❌ **Do NOT confuse "fixation" with "fermentation."** In tea, "fermentation" means *enzymatic oxidation by PPO*, not microbial action. Fixation is its opposite.
> - ❌ **Do NOT claim green tea contains "theaflavins"** as a major component. It contains negligible theaflavins because PPO is denatured.
> - ❌ **Do NOT say "green tea is made from a different plant."** All true teas (green, black, oolong, white, yellow, pu-erh) come from the **same plant** — *Camellia sinensis*. The difference is **processing**.
> - ❌ **Do NOT skip the biochemical reason** for each step. KTU examiners award marks to the **"why"** — heat denatures PPO, catechins stay intact, green color is preserved.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Tea: One-Page Rapid Recap**

- ☑ **Botanical name:** *Camellia sinensis* (Family: Theaceae).
- ☑ **Three varieties:** *sinensis* (Chinese, small leaves, cold-hardy), *assamica* (Assamese, large leaves, tropical), *cambodiensis* (Cambodian, hybrid).
- ☑ **Climate:** 1,500–3,000 mm rainfall; 10–30 °C; pH 4.5–5.5; up to 2,400 m altitude.
- ☑ **Major producers (world):** China > India > Kenya > Sri Lanka > Türkiye.
- ☑ **Major Indian regions:** Assam, Darjeeling, Nilgiris, **Kerala (Munnar, Wayanad, Idukki)**, Himachal.
- ☑ **Dominant polyphenol:** EGCG (~50% of catechins); 20–30% polyphenols dry weight.
- ☑ **Methylxanthines:** Caffeine (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine) at 3–4%.
- ☑ **Signature amino acid:** L-Theanine — umami, calming effect, alpha-wave promotion.
- ☑ **The critical enzyme:** Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO) — its activity/denaturation determines the tea type.
- ☑ **Plucking standard:** "Two leaves and a bud" = fine plucking; cycle every 7–10 days.
- ☑ **Six tea types by oxidation level:** White < Yellow < Green < Oolong < Black < Dark (Pu-erh).
- ☑ **Green tea process:** Pluck → Wither (partial) → **Fix (kill PPO)** → Roll → Dry (< 3% moisture) → Grade → Pack.
- ☑ **Fixation methods:** Pan-firing (Chinese) at 200–250 °C, OR Steaming (Japanese) at 95–100 °C.
- ☑ **Green tea retains 80–90% catechins**; black tea retains ~10% catechins (rest converted to theaflavins/thearubigins).
- ☑ **"Tea fermentation"** = enzymatic oxidation (NOT microbial). Exception: Pu-erh uses microbes.
- ☑ **Catechin → Quinone → Theaflavin → Thearubigin** is the black-tea oxidation pathway.
- ☑ **Health relevance:** Green tea (EGCG, L-theanine) → antioxidant, anti-carcinogenic, cardioprotective, calming.
- ☑ **Yield:** ~4.0–4.5 kg fresh leaf → 1 kg finished green tea.
