> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oolong Tea CTC - Chemistry and Technology of CTC Tea - Manufacturing Process and Equipment Involved - Green Tea Manufacture - Instant Tea Manufacture - Grading of Tea

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Oolong Tea — Definition and Concept

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Oolong tea is a **semi-fermented (semi-oxidized) traditional Chinese tea** belonging to the *Camellia sinensis* species, undergoing a controlled oxidation level of **30%–70%** (typically **40%–60%**) before heat fixation. It is positioned chemically and organoleptically **between unoxidized green tea and fully oxidized black tea**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Oolong tea is the only major commercial tea whose polyphenol oxidase (PPO) activity is deliberately **partially arrested**. The product is graded on the degree of fermentation: **Light Oolong (10–25% oxidation)**, **Medium Oolong (30–50%)**, and **Heavy Oolong (60–70%)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a banana ripening on a counter. If you freeze it green, you get "green tea chemistry." If you let it fully brown, you get "black tea chemistry." Oolong is the **exact moment of "partially browned banana"** — enzymes have done some work, but heat was applied before the job was finished. The brew thus carries a complex aroma, floral-fruity, sitting between fresh grass (green) and malt (black).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard:** The oxidation in oolong is **enzymatic (not microbial)** and occurs under controlled humidity (**60–80% RH**) and temperature (**22–28 °C**) for **2–6 hours**.

---

## 1.2 CTC Tea — Definition and Concept

**Formal Definition:**
**CTC** stands for **Crush, Tear, and Curl** — a mechanical method of tea leaf processing that ruptures cell walls to produce a uniform, granular, small-particle black tea. It was invented by **Sir William McKercher** in **1930** in Assam, India, and revolutionized black tea manufacturing by accelerating enzymatic oxidation.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine wringing out a wet towel with your hands — the twisting motion crushes cells and releases juices. The CTC machine does the same to tea leaves using **counter-rotating rollers with sharp teeth**. Because cells are violently torn open, oxidation happens **extremely fast** (sometimes within **60–90 minutes**), producing a strong, full-bodied, highly colored liquor — the kind you find in most commercial tea bags.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** CTC accounts for **over 60% of global black tea production**, especially dominant in **India, Kenya, Sri Lanka, and Argentina**.

---

## 1.3 Green Tea — Definition and Concept

**Formal Definition:**
Green tea is the **non-fermented (unoxidized) tea** produced by immediately inactivating the **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** and **peroxidase** enzymes through heat treatment (**pan-firing or steaming**) right after plucking, thereby preserving the natural catechins and chlorophyll content of the fresh leaf.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If black tea is "cooked apple sauce" (enzymatic browning fully completed) and oolong is "half-browned apple slices," then green tea is the **freshly cut apple dipped in lemon juice** — the enzymes that cause browning are instantly neutralized by heat, locking in the green color, fresh vegetal flavor, and high antioxidant content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Major Green Tea Methods (KTU Must-Know):**
> 1. **Chinese Method (Pan-fired / "Kill-green"):** Dry heat in iron pans (chao qing) at **120–150 °C**.
> 2. **Japanese Method (Steamed / "Mushisei"):** Steam at **95–100 °C** for **30–90 seconds**.

---

## 1.4 Instant Tea — Definition and Concept

**Formal Definition:**
Instant tea is a **water-soluble, powdered or granulated extract of black or green tea**, produced by extracting brewed tea liquor, concentrating it, and drying it via **spray drying, freeze drying, or vacuum belt drying** to a moisture content of **≤ 4%**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of how "instant coffee" works — boil tea leaves in water, evaporate the water, and you're left with a powder that dissolves back into water with a stir. **Instant tea is the tea-bag version on steroids** — used in RTD (ready-to-drink) beverages, vending machines, and iced tea mixes.

---

## 1.5 Grading of Tea — Definition and Concept

**Formal Definition:**
Tea grading is the **mechanical sorting of manufactured tea** into uniform size categories using vibrating/rotating sieves called **sieves** (maze), with the intent of ensuring **brewing consistency, cup quality, and market price uniformity**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Just as rice is sold as Basmati, Sona Masoori, and broken rice at different prices, tea is sorted by **leaf size and style** — from whole leaves (TGFOP — Tippy Golden Flowery Orange Pekoe) to dust (the smallest particles used in tea bags).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** Grading nomenclature differs between **orthodox** (long-leaf rolling) and **CTC** (granular) processing. Students must know both.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chemistry of Tea Fermentation (Universal Foundation)

The transformation of green leaf to finished tea is governed by **enzymatic oxidation of polyphenols** (catechins) by the enzyme **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)**, in the presence of **molecular oxygen (O₂)**.

### 2.1.1 Major Chemical Constituents of Fresh Tea Leaf

| Constituent | % in Fresh Leaf | Functional Role |
|-------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Catechins (Polyphenols)** | 20–30% | Substrate for oxidation → theaflavins, thearubigins |
| Caffeine | 3–4% | Stimulant, bitterness |
| Amino Acids (L-Theanine) | 1–5% | Umami, "sweetness," calming effect |
| Chlorophyll | 0.5–1.0% | Green color (degraded to pheophytin in black tea) |
| PPO Enzyme | Active in young leaves | Catalyst for oxidation |
| Cellulose/Pectins | 20–30% | Structural matrix |
| Moisture | 75–82% | Critical for withering |

### 2.1.2 The Oxidation Chemistry (Catechin Pathway)

Step-by-step transformation:

- **Step 1 — Withering:** Moisture reduces from **75–82% → 55–65%**. Cell membranes become permeable, PPO and substrates come into contact.
- **Step 2 — Rolling/CTC:** Cell rupture exposes catechins and PPO to **O₂** from air.
- **Step 3 — Oxidation (Fermentation):**
  - **Catechins → Quinones** (catalyzed by PPO).
  - **Quinones → Theaflavins (TFs)** — yellow-orange pigments, **brightness, briskness**.
  - **Theaflavins → Thearubigins (TRs)** — brown-red pigments, **color, body, strength**.
- **Step 4 — Termination:** Heat (drying) destroys PPO, locking in the chemistry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Ratio to Remember for KTU Board:**
> The **TR/TF ratio** in black tea liquor determines **quality**:
> - Optimum: **TR/TF ≈ 10:1 to 15:1**
> - TF contributes **briskness and brightness**; TR contributes **body and color**.

---

## 2.2 Chemistry and Technology of CTC Tea (Deep Dive)

### 2.2.1 Why CTC Was Invented

Orthodox rolling was **slow, labor-intensive, and inconsistent**. McKercher's CTC machine:

- Crushes leaf through **differential roller speeds** (typically **1:10 ratio** between fast and slow roller).
- Each leaf passes through **3 sets of rollers** in series: **CTC → Rotorvane → CTC → CTC** (or similar).
- Produces **uniform granules of 1–3 mm** diameter.
- **Oxidation time reduces from 90–180 min (orthodox) → 45–90 min (CTC)**.

### 2.2.2 Cell Damage Index

The percentage of ruptured cells in CTC is **80–95%**, compared to only **20–30%** in orthodox rolling. This explains:

- **Faster fermentation** (more enzyme-substrate contact).
- **Stronger, darker liquor** (more TF/TR formation).
- **Lower grade in the eye** (granular vs. long-leaf appearance) but **higher cup quality per gram**.

### 2.2.3 CTC Process Flow (Detailed)

| Stage | Process | Parameters | Equipment |
|-------|---------|------------|-----------|
| 1 | Withering | Moisture 75% → 55–60% | Trough withering, tunnel withering |
| 2 | Pre-conditioning (optional) | Moisture raise to 62–65% | Rotorvane (Type N), CFM (Continuous Feed Machine) |
| 3 | CTC cutting | Cell rupture 80–95% | CTC tri-roll machine (3 stages) |
| 4 | Fermentation | 22–28 °C, 90% RH, 45–90 min | Fermenting machine (concrete floor or aluminum trough) |
| 5 | Drying (Firing) | 120–130 °C inlet, moisture ≤ 3% | Fluidized bed dryer (FBD) or Vibro Fluidized Bed Dryer (VFBD) |
| 6 | Sorting/Grading | Size separation | Vibro-sorter, Myddleton separator, electrostatic stalk separator |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Must-Know Term:** **VFBD (Vibro Fluidized Bed Dryer)** — the modern replacement for the older chain-dryer. It is energy-efficient, gives uniform drying, and is the standard equipment in **Dooars, Assam, and South Indian CTC factories** today.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Numerical Parameters

The following table summarizes **all key formulas, ratios, and process parameters** required for KTU Board numericals and short-answer questions.

| Parameter / Formula | Symbol / Equation | Standard Value / Unit | Application |
|---------------------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Withering moisture loss | $M_{wither} = M_{initial} - M_{final}$ | 75% → 55% (ΔM ≈ 20%) | Mass balance in factory |
| Final tea moisture (mandatory) | $M_{final}$ | **≤ 3.0% (w/w)** | Tea Quality Control Order, FSSAI |
| Cell rupture efficiency (CTC) | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{N_{ruptured}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | 80–95% | Process quality |
| TR/TF quality ratio | $R_{TR/TF}$ | **10:1 to 15:1** | Cup quality index |
| Extraction yield (instant tea) | $Y_{extract} = \dfrac{W_{solubles}}{W_{leaf}} \times 100\%$ | 25–35% | Instant tea process |
| Spray-dryer outlet moisture | $M_{spray}$ | **≤ 4%** | Instant tea powder spec |
| Energy for evaporation | $E = m \times L_{v}$ | $L_{v}$ = 2260 kJ/kg | Concentration stage |
| Withering time | $t_{wither}$ | **12–18 hours** | Trough method |
| Fermentation temperature | $T_{ferm}$ | **22–28 °C** (optimum 25 °C) | Critical for TF/TR balance |
| Fermentation RH | $RH_{ferm}$ | **> 90%** | Prevents drying of leaf surface |
| CTC roller speed ratio | $N_{fast} : N_{slow}$ | **10:1** | Differential cutting action |
| Roller gap | $g$ | **0.05–0.15 mm** | Cell rupture control |
| Theaflavin content (black tea) | $TF$ | **0.5–2.0% of dry weight** | Briskness |
| Thearubigin content | $TR$ | **10–20% of dry weight** | Body & color |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Break Rule Applied:** All absolute values and ratios above are written using **text descriptions** (e.g., "10:1") rather than the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.4 Green Tea — Detailed Chemistry

### 2.4.1 The "Kill-Green" (杀青, *shā qīng*) Principle

In green tea, the goal is to **destroy PPO activity within 2–4 hours of plucking** to prevent oxidation. The inactivation temperature for PPO is approximately **70–80 °C**, so heating above this threshold is mandatory.

**Mechanism:**

- Heat denatures PPO (loss of tertiary structure → active site collapse).
- Catechins remain **unoxidized** → retained in finished tea.
- Chlorophyll is partially converted to **pheophytin** (olive-green color).
- Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is preserved at higher levels than black tea.

### 2.4.2 Catechins in Green Tea (KTU Must-Know)

The major catechins are:

- **Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)** — 50–60% of total catechins; the most potent antioxidant.
- **Epicatechin gallate (ECG)** — 10–15%.
- **Epigallocatechin (EGC)** — 10–15%.
- **Epicatechin (EC)** — 5–10%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Green tea extracts are used in **nutraceuticals, cosmetics, and functional foods** precisely because of their **high EGCG content** (50–100 mg per cup).

### 2.4.3 Green Tea Manufacturing Process

| Step | Process | Parameters | Equipment |
|------|---------|------------|-----------|
| 1 | Plucking (1 bud + 2 leaves) | Hand or shear plucking | Plucking basket |
| 2 | Spreading / Indoor Withering | 2–4 h, ambient | Bamboo trays / withering troughs |
| 3 | **Fixation (Kill-Green)** | **120–150 °C (Chinese) OR steam 95–100 °C (Japanese)** | Pan / Steaming drum |
| 4 | Rolling & Shaping | 10–30 min | Rolling machine |
| 5 | First Drying | 100–110 °C, 8–12 min | Primary dryer |
| 6 | Second Drying / Refiring | 80–90 °C to ≤ 3% moisture | Final dryer |
| 7 | Sorting & Grading | Size separation | Sieve sorter |

---

## 2.5 Instant Tea — Engineering Perspective

### 2.5.1 Process Stages

1. **Hot-water extraction** of black/green tea leaf in counter-current extractors at **70–90 °C** for **20–45 min**.
2. **Filtration/Centrifugation** to remove insolubles.
3. **Concentration** by **vacuum evaporation** (multi-effect evaporator) to **30–50% solids**.
4. **Aromatization recovery** — volatile flavor compounds (aldehydes, alcohols) are stripped and condensed separately, then re-added.
5. **Drying** by **spray drying** (inlet 180–220 °C, outlet 80–100 °C) or **freeze drying** (−40 °C, vacuum).
6. **Agglomeration** (optional) to improve dispersibility in cold water.

### 2.5.2 Engineering Utility

Instant tea is the **backbone of the RTD (ready-to-drink) tea industry**, vending machine tea, and military rations due to:
- Long shelf life (≥ 2 years at < 4% moisture).
- Easy reconstitution in cold/hot water.
- Standardized taste and color.

---

## 2.6 Grading of Tea — Standard Nomenclature

### 2.6.1 Orthodox Tea Grades (Long-Leaf)

| Grade | Abbreviation | Particle Size | Typical Use |
|-------|--------------|---------------|-------------|
| Tippy Golden Flowery Orange Pekoe | TGFOP | Whole leaf + golden tips | Premium loose tea |
| Golden Flowery Orange Pekoe | GFOP | Whole leaf + tips | Premium loose tea |
| Flowery Orange Pekoe | FOP | Whole leaf | Loose tea |
| Orange Pekoe | OP | Whole leaf, shorter | Loose tea |
| Pekoe | P | Smaller whole leaf | Loose tea |
| Broken Orange Pekoe | BOP | Broken leaf | Tea bags |
| Broken Pekoe | BP | Smaller broken | Tea bags |
| Fannings | F | Very small | Tea bags, blends |
| Dust | D | Powder | Tea bags (low grade) |

### 2.6.2 CTC Tea Grades

| Grade | Abbreviation | Mesh Size | Application |
|-------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| Pekoe | P | 8–10 | Loose tea |
| Broken Pekoe | BP | 10–14 | Tea bags |
| Broken Pekoe Souchong | BPS | 12–16 | Tea bags |
| Broken Orange Pekoe | BOP | 14–18 | Tea bags |
| Broken Orange Pekoe Fannings | BOPF | 18–24 | Tea bags |
| Pekoe Dust | PD | 24–30 | Tea bags |
| Red Dust | RD | 30–40 | Tea bags |
| Dust | D | Below 40 | Tea bags (cheapest) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In CTC grades, the term **"Souchong"** is purely a size indicator (medium-broken) — it does **NOT** mean "smoked" (which is the orthodox Lapsang Souchong).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Mass Balance Derivation for Tea Withering

### Problem Setup

A tea factory receives **1000 kg of fresh green leaf** at **78% moisture (wet basis)**. After withering, the moisture content is reduced to **55% (wet basis)**. Calculate:
1. The mass of withered leaf.
2. The mass of water evaporated.
3. The mass of non-volatile solids (NVS) preserved.

### Step 1: Define Moisture Content (Wet Basis)

$$ M_{wb} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} $$

**Explanation:** Wet-basis moisture is the standard in tea factories because it directly relates to the fresh weight being processed.

### Step 2: Calculate Non-Volatile Solids (NVS) in Fresh Leaf

$$ m_{NVS} = m_{total, fresh} \times (1 - M_{wb, fresh}) $$

**Substitute** $m_{total, fresh} = 1000$ kg and $M_{wb, fresh} = 0.78$:

$$ m_{NVS} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.78) $$

$$ m_{NVS} = 1000 \times 0.22 $$

$$ m_{NVS} = 220 \text{ kg} $$

**Explanation:** The NVS (polyphenols, caffeine, cellulose, proteins) are **conserved** during withering because they are non-volatile. This is the **key invariant** in all tea mass balance problems.

### Step 3: Express Withered Leaf Mass

After withering, the leaf still contains **220 kg of NVS**, but the moisture fraction is now 55%. So:

$$ m_{NVS} = m_{total, withered} \times (1 - M_{wb, withered}) $$

$$ 220 = m_{total, withered} \times (1 - 0.55) $$

$$ 220 = m_{total, withered} \times 0.45 $$

**Solve for withered leaf mass:**

$$ m_{total, withered} = \frac{220}{0.45} $$

$$ m_{total, withered} = 488.89 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 4: Calculate Water Evaporated

$$ m_{water, evaporated} = m_{total, fresh} - m_{total, withered} $$

$$ m_{water, evaporated} = 1000 - 488.89 $$

$$ m_{water, evaporated} = 511.11 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 5: Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Fresh leaf input | 1000.00 kg |
| Withered leaf output | 488.89 kg |
| Water evaporated | 511.11 kg |
| NVS conserved | 220.00 kg |
| Withering loss | 51.11% of fresh weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A **withering loss of ~50%** is the **standard industry benchmark**. If loss is much lower, withering is insufficient; if higher, over-withering causes leaf breakage in CTC.

---

## 3.2 Process Yield Derivation: Green Leaf → Black Tea (CTC)

### Problem Setup

A factory processes **10,000 kg of withered leaf (55% moisture)** into finished CTC black tea. Factory records show:

- **Drying loss** (moisture removal): from 55% → 3%.
- **Volatiles/sorting loss**: **3% of dry solids**.
- **Fines (dust) rejected** during grading: **5% of dry solids**.

Calculate the final **finished tea yield (%)** and **kg of saleable tea** from this batch.

### Step 1: Calculate Dry Solids in Withered Leaf

$$ m_{dry, withered} = 10000 \times (1 - 0.55) = 10000 \times 0.45 = 4500 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 2: Apply Drying Loss (Sortable Dry Solids)

After drying, moisture = 3%, so total dry mass is still **4500 kg** (only water removed):

$$ m_{dry, post-dry} = 4500 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 3: Apply Volatiles & Sorting Loss (3%)

$$ m_{dry, post-sort} = 4500 \times (1 - 0.03) = 4500 \times 0.97 = 4365 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 4: Apply Fines/Dust Rejection (5% of dry solids)

**Method A — Apply on entire 4365 kg:**

$$ m_{saleable} = 4365 \times (1 - 0.05) = 4365 \times 0.95 $$

$$ m_{saleable} = 4146.75 \text{ kg} $$

**Method B — Combined-loss shortcut** (multiplicative):

$$ m_{saleable} = 4500 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 = 4500 \times 0.9215 $$

$$ m_{saleable} = 4146.75 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 5: Calculate Final Yield (with respect to WITHERED leaf)

$$ Y = \frac{4146.75}{10000} \times 100\% = 41.47\% $$

### Step 6: Final Answer Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Withered leaf input | 10,000 kg |
| Dry solids entering process | 4,500 kg |
| After sorting (3% loss) | 4,365 kg |
| Saleable finished tea (5% fines) | 4,146.75 kg |
| **Yield (w.r.t. withered leaf)** | **41.47%** |
| **Yield (w.r.t. fresh leaf @ 78% mc)** | **~20.7%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Rule of Thumb:** The **fresh-leaf to made-tea ratio** is approximately **4.5:1 to 5:1** in commercial CTC production. A KTU numerical answer of ~20% yield (from fresh leaf) is the standard expected answer.

---

## 3.3 Extraction Yield Derivation for Instant Tea

### Problem Setup

An instant tea plant uses **1000 kg of black tea leaf** (3% moisture) for hot-water extraction at **80 °C** for **30 minutes**. The extract contains **32% dissolved solids**. The concentrate after evaporation is **45% solids** before spray drying. Spray-dried powder has **3.5% moisture**. Calculate:

1. The **extraction yield (%)** of soluble solids.
2. The **mass of extract liquor** at 32% solids.
3. The **mass of spray-dried instant tea powder** (saleable product).
4. The **overall process yield** from leaf → instant tea.

### Step 1: Extract Yield (Soluble Solids Recovery)

$$ Y_{extract} = \frac{W_{solubles}}{W_{dry, leaf}} \times 100\% $$

$$ W_{dry, leaf} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.03) = 970 \text{ kg} $$

$$ W_{solubles} = 970 \times 0.32 = 310.4 \text{ kg} $$

**So:**

$$ Y_{extract} = \frac{310.4}{970} \times 100\% = 32.0\% $$

### Step 2: Mass of Extract Liquor (at 32% solids)

$$ m_{liquor} = \frac{W_{solubles}}{0.32} = \frac{310.4}{0.32} = 970.0 \text{ kg} $$

**Sanity check:** This is the **total mass of extract** containing **310.4 kg solids + 659.6 kg water**.

### Step 3: Mass of Concentrate (at 45% solids)

Solids are conserved in the evaporator:

$$ m_{concentrate} = \frac{310.4}{0.45} = 689.78 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 4: Mass of Spray-Dried Instant Tea (3.5% moisture → 96.5% solids)

$$ m_{instant} = \frac{310.4}{0.965} = 321.66 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 5: Overall Yield (Leaf → Instant Tea, dry basis)

$$ Y_{overall} = \frac{321.66}{970} \times 100\% = 33.16\% $$

### Step 6: Final Answer Summary Table

| Stage | Mass (kg) | Solids % | Moisture % |
|-------|-----------|----------|------------|
| Black tea leaf input | 1000.00 | 97.0 | 3.0 |
| Extract liquor (32% sol.) | 970.00 | 32.0 | 68.0 |
| Concentrate (45% sol.) | 689.78 | 45.0 | 55.0 |
| **Instant tea powder** | **321.66** | **96.5** | **3.5** |
| **Overall yield** | — | — | **~33.2%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The instant tea industry typically reports **extraction efficiency of 30–40%** of dry leaf, which is why instant tea is **3–4× more expensive per kg than leaf tea** — only a fraction of the leaf becomes the final powder.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Tea Processing Methods

This is the **master comparison table** KTU examiners expect students to reproduce for full marks on "Compare" or "Distinguish between" type questions.

| Parameter | Green Tea | Oolong Tea | Orthodox Black | CTC Black | Instant Tea |
|-----------|-----------|------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Oxidation (%)** | 0% (enzymes killed) | 30–70% | 85–95% | 90–95% | Depends on base |
| **PPO activity** | Destroyed at start | Partially destroyed | Destroyed at drying | Destroyed at drying | Destroyed during extraction |
| **Withering** | Light / short | Long, sun + indoor | Full wither | Full wither | Not applicable (uses black/green leaf) |
| **Cell rupture** | 10–20% (light rolling) | 30–50% (light rolling) | 20–30% (rolling) | 80–95% (CTC rollers) | Full extraction |
| **Fixation** | YES — pan/steam (120–150 °C) | Partial sun-fixation + pan | No fixation | No fixation | N/A |
| **Fermentation time** | None | 2–6 h | 1.5–3 h | 45–90 min | N/A |
| **Major pigments** | Chlorophyll, catechins | Theaflavin + chlorophyll | TF + TR | TF + TR (higher) | TF + TR (soluble) |
| **Caffeine** | 3–4% | 3–4% | 3–4% | 3–4% | 3–4% (concentrated) |
| **Color of liquor** | Pale yellow-green | Yellow-amber | Bright reddish-brown | Deep reddish-brown | Dark brown (powder dissolves) |
| **Flavor** | Grassy, vegetal, umami | Floral, fruity, complex | Malty, brisk | Strong, full-bodied | Astringent, concentrated |
| **Drying final mc** | ≤ 3% | ≤ 3% | ≤ 3% | ≤ 3% | ≤ 4% |
| **Final form** | Whole / curled leaf | Whole / rolled leaf | Long twisted leaf | Granular 1–3 mm | Powder / granules |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CTC Tea Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Green Leaf 75 to 82 percent mc] --> B[Trough Withering 12 to 18 h]
    B --> C[Leaf moisture 55 to 60 percent]
    C --> D[Rotorvane Preconditioning]
    D --> E1[CTC Machine Stage 1 Roller gap 0.15 mm]
    E1 --> E2[CTC Machine Stage 2 Roller gap 0.10 mm]
    E2 --> E3[CTC Machine Stage 3 Roller gap 0.05 mm]
    E3 --> F[Fermentation Trough 22 to 28 C RH 90 percent 45 to 90 min]
    F --> G[VFBD Vibro Fluidized Bed Dryer Inlet 130 C Outlet 3 percent mc]
    G --> H[Sorting and Grading Vibro Sorter]
    H --> I1[BP Broken Pekoe]
    H --> I2[BOP Broken Orange Pekoe]
    H --> I3[BOPF Fannings]
    H --> I4[PD Pekoe Dust]
    H --> I5[RD Red Dust]
    H --> I6[D Dust Reject]

    style A fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
    style G fill:#FFD700
    style H fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Exams:** The three CTC stages are **non-negotiable** in Board diagrams. Skipping a stage will lose **2 marks** in the KTU 2024 scheme, as per the examiner pattern. Always show **three CTC rollers** in any CTC process flow diagram.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Green Tea Manufacturing (Chinese Pan-Fired Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pluck 1 bud 2 leaves] --> B[Spreading 2 to 4 h ambient]
    B --> C[Pan Firing Kill Green 120 to 150 C 5 to 10 min]
    C --> D[Initial Rolling 10 to 15 min]
    D --> E[First Drying 100 to 110 C 8 to 12 min]
    E --> F[Second Rolling Shape]
    F --> G[Final Drying 80 to 90 C to 3 percent mc]
    G --> H[Sorting by Sieve]
    H --> I1[Gunpowder Pearl Style]
    H --> I2[Longjing Dragon Well Style]
    H --> I3[Flat and Needle Style]

    style A fill:#90EE90
    style C fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Orthodox and CTC methods of tea processing. Give any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Orthodox | CTC |
|---------|-----------|----------|-----|
| 1 | **Cell rupture** | 20–30% (gentle rolling) | 80–95% (mechanical crushing) |
| 2 | **Fermentation time** | 1.5–3 hours | 45–90 minutes |
| 3 | **Leaf appearance** | Long, twisted, wiry leaf | Small, granular 1–3 mm |
| 4 | **Cup quality** | Aromatic, light, brisk | Strong, full-bodied, dark |
| 5 | **Equipment** | Rolling table, orthodox roller | CTC tri-roll machine, Rotorvane |
| 6 | **Market** | Premium loose tea | Tea bags, mass market |

*(Any three points = 3 marks. Tabulated answer preferred by KTU examiners.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is "kill-green" (shā qīng) in green tea manufacture? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** "Kill-green" is the **heat-fixation step** in green tea processing, in which fresh leaves are subjected to **dry heat (Chinese method, 120–150 °C in iron pans)** or **steam (Japanese method, 95–100 °C for 30–90 seconds)** immediately after plucking.

- **Purpose (1 mark):** The step **inactivates polyphenol oxidase (PPO) and peroxidase enzymes**, which would otherwise catalyze the oxidation of catechins to theaflavins/thearubigins.

- **Consequence of skipping (1 mark):** Without kill-green, the green leaf would undergo **enzymatic browning**, producing a low-quality black-tea-like product and losing **chlorophyll, vitamin C, and the characteristic grassy flavor** of green tea.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each question carries **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.** Either **Question A** *or* **Question B** must be answered. We provide both options.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Describe in detail the Chemistry and Technology of CTC tea manufacture. Include the equipment used and the biochemical changes occurring during fermentation.**

#### Part (a) — Chemistry of CTC Tea (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The chemistry of CTC tea centers on the **enzymatic oxidation of catechins** (a class of polyphenols) by the enzyme **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)**, in the presence of atmospheric oxygen.

1. **Major substrates in fresh leaf:** The four key catechins are **epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG, 50–60% of total catechins), epigallocatechin (EGC), epicatechin gallate (ECG), and epicatechin (EC).** These account for 20–30% of fresh leaf dry weight.

2. **Activation by withering:** During withering, moisture drops from **75% to 55%**, cells lose turgor, membranes become permeable, and PPO encounters its substrates.

3. **Cell rupture in CTC:** The three-stage CTC rollers rupture **80–95% of cells**, releasing large amounts of catechins and PPO into contact with **O₂**.

4. **Oxidation pathway (during fermentation):**
   - PPO + O₂ + Catechins → **Quinones** (intermediate)
   - Quinones couple to form **Theaflavins (TF)** — yellow-orange, gives briskness.
   - TF further oxidizes/polymerizes to **Thearubigins (TR)** — red-brown, gives body and color.

5. **Quality indicator:** The **TR/TF ratio** of **10:1 to 15:1** indicates optimum fermentation. Over-fermentation makes TR dominate (>20:1) producing dull, heavy liquor.

6. **Termination:** Drying at **120–130 °C** denatures PPO, freezing the chemistry.

7. **Pigment profile of made tea:** TF = 0.5–2%, TR = 10–20%, **caffeine = 3–4%**, theanine = 1–2%, all on dry weight basis.

*(7 marks distributed: 1 mark each point + 1 mark for concluding line)*

#### Part (b) — Technology and Equipment (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The technology of CTC manufacture follows a defined process chain:

1. **Withering (2 marks):** Trough withering for **12–18 h**, reducing moisture to 55–60%. Modern factories use **tunnel withering** with controlled airflow (15–25 m³/min per m² of trough area).

2. **Pre-conditioning (1 mark):** A **Rotorvane** (Type N) re-moisturizes and partially crushes leaf, ensuring uniform feed to CTC rollers.

3. **CTC Cutting (2 marks):** The **CTC machine** has **3 stages** of counter-rotating rollers, each with differential speed ratio of **10:1** (slow roller 5 rpm, fast roller 50 rpm). Roller gap reduces from **0.15 mm → 0.10 mm → 0.05 mm** across stages. Cell rupture increases progressively to 80–95%.

4. **Fermentation (1 mark):** Cut leaf spreads on **concrete/aluminum floors** or **continuous fermenting machines (CFM)** for **45–90 min** at **22–28 °C, >90% RH**, with periodic turning.

5. **Drying (1 mark):** **Vibro Fluidized Bed Dryer (VFBD)** at inlet **130 °C**, outlet moisture **≤ 3%**. Modern factories use **two-stage drying** (primary + secondary VFBD) for energy efficiency.

6. **Sorting and Grading:** **Vibro-sorter** with mesh screens separates BP, BOP, BOPF, PD, RD, and D fractions, which are then packed.

*(Total = 7 marks. Each stage carries defined weightage.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Write in detail the manufacturing process of Green Tea and Instant Tea. Compare the biochemical principles underlying both.**

#### Part (a) — Green Tea Manufacture (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Green tea manufacture focuses on **preventing oxidation** to preserve catechins, chlorophyll, and amino acids.

1. **Plucking (0.5 mark):** Standard plucking standard is **"1 bud + 2 leaves"** (pekoe flush) for finest quality, plucked in cool morning hours.

2. **Spreading / Indoor Withering (1 mark):** Leaf is spread on **bamboo trays or withering troughs** for **2–4 hours** at ambient temperature to reduce surface moisture and soften leaves.

3. **Fixation / "Kill-Green" (2.5 marks):**
   - **Chinese method:** Pan-firing in cast-iron woks at **120–150 °C** for **5–10 minutes**, with constant tossing. PPO is denatured.
   - **Japanese method:** Steaming on a moving belt at **95–100 °C** for **30–90 seconds** in a steaming chamber.

4. **Rolling and Shaping (1 mark):** After fixation, leaf is rolled under pressure for **10–30 min** in rolling machines to give shape (twisted, curled, or needle-like depending on variety).

5. **First Drying (1 mark):** Hot air at **100–110 °C** for **8–12 min**, reducing moisture to **15–20%**.

6. **Second Drying / Refiring (0.5 mark):** Final drying at **80–90 °C** to **≤ 3% moisture** for shelf stability.

7. **Sorting (0.5 mark):** Sieve sorting into grades like **Gunpowder, Young Hyson, Hyson, Imperial, Fannings, Dust**.

#### Part (b) — Instant Tea Manufacture (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Raw material selection (0.5 mark):** Black tea (or green tea) of low-grade leaf is preferred (cost-effective); high-extraction-grade cultivars give better yield.

2. **Hot-water extraction (2 marks):** Leaf is extracted in **counter-current extractors** with water at **70–90 °C** for **20–45 min**, giving a liquor with **3–6% dissolved solids** initially. Counter-current design maximizes extraction efficiency (typical yield 30–35% of leaf dry weight).

3. **Clarification (1 mark):** Extract is **centrifuged and filtered** to remove suspended leaf particles, proteins, and cold-water-insoluble compounds (using **chill tanks at 4–8 °C** to precipitate tannins).

4. **Volatile recovery (1 mark):** The aroma compounds (aldehydes, alcohols, linalool, geraniol) are **stripped from the extract** in a steam-stripping column, concentrated, and stored for re-addition.

5. **Concentration (1 mark):** Multi-effect **vacuum evaporator** concentrates the clarified extract to **30–50% solids**, preserving heat-labile compounds.

6. **Drying (1 mark):** **Spray drying** at **inlet 180–220 °C, outlet 80–100 °C** to a fine powder with **≤ 4% moisture**. Alternatively, **freeze drying** at **−40 °C under vacuum** produces higher-quality granules.

7. **Aromatization and packaging (0.5 mark):** Recovered volatile fraction is sprayed back onto the powder, then packed in **nitrogen-flushed, foil-laminated pouches** for 2-year shelf life.

**Comparative biochemical note (embedded in answer):**
- Green tea retains **unoxidized catechins (EGCG)**, giving antioxidant and anticancer properties.
- Instant tea is a **soluble extract** of already-oxidized (black) tea, retaining **theaflavins and thearubigins** but with a slightly reduced polyphenol profile due to heat exposure during evaporation.
- Both share the common base of *Camellia sinensis* but differ in **oxidation state, processing temperature profile, and final moisture content**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU 2024 BOARD — MOST COMMON MARK-LOSS MISTAKES ON THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "fermentation" with microbial fermentation.** In tea, "fermentation" is purely **enzymatic oxidation** — no microbes are involved. Writing "bacterial fermentation" will cost **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Mis-stating the CTC roller speeds.** Always write the **differential ratio (10:1)** or specify slow (5 rpm) and fast (50 rpm). Writing only "rotating rollers" loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to mention VFBD.** The **Vibro Fluidized Bed Dryer** is the modern standard equipment. Examiners specifically look for this term in CTC process questions.
>
> 4. **Wrong final moisture.** Tea MUST end at **≤ 3% moisture** (black/green) and **≤ 4% moisture** (instant tea). Writing "5%" or "10%" indicates poor knowledge of **FSSAI Tea Regulations 2023** and will lose marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing theaflavin (TF) and thearubigin (TR).** Students often swap their roles. **TF = briskness + brightness (yellow-orange); TR = body + color (red-brown).** Memorize this color-attribute association.
>
> 6. **Skipping the third CTC stage in diagrams.** Always draw **3 CTC rollers** in any process flowchart. The examiner's checklist explicitly includes "three CTC stages."
>
> 7. **Writing "Orthodox = Chinese, CTC = Indian"** — incorrect generalization. Both methods are used in India, China, Sri Lanka, and Kenya. Avoid oversimplified national attributions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Ready)

- ✅ **Oolong tea** is a **semi-fermented** tea (30–70% oxidation), traditional Chinese product, requires controlled **2–6 h** of partial oxidation.
- ✅ **CTC** stands for **Crush, Tear, Curl**; invented by **W. McKercher (1930)** in **Assam**; produces **1–3 mm granules**; cell rupture **80–95%**.
- ✅ CTC process chain: **Withering → Rotorvane → 3-stage CTC rollers → Fermentation → VFBD drying → Sorting.**
- ✅ **Polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** is the master enzyme; **destroyed at 70–80 °C**.
- ✅ **Catechins → Quinones → Theaflavins (TF, yellow-orange) → Thearubigins (TR, red-brown).**
- ✅ **TR/TF ratio of 10:1 to 15:1** is the quality benchmark.
- ✅ **Green tea** requires **immediate kill-green** (Chinese pan 120–150 °C OR Japanese steam 95–100 °C) to preserve catechins and chlorophyll.
- ✅ **Instant tea** is made by **hot-water extraction → filtration → vacuum concentration → spray drying**, with **aroma recovery** as a critical step.
- ✅ **Final moisture standards:** Black/green tea ≤ **3%**; Instant tea ≤ **4%**.
- ✅ **CTC grades (descending size):** P → BP → BPS → BOP → BOPF → PD → RD → D.
- ✅ **Orthodox grades (descending size):** TGFOP → GFOP → FOP → OP → P → BOP → BP → F → D.
- ✅ **VFBD (Vibro Fluidized Bed Dryer)** — modern energy-efficient drying equipment; standard in CTC factories.
- ✅ **EGCG** is the dominant catechin (50–60% of total catechins) and the principal antioxidant of green tea.
- ✅ **Instant tea yield** from leaf is typically **30–40%** of dry leaf mass — hence its premium pricing.

### Memory Aid: The "**OCC-GI**" Acronym

| Letter | Tea Type | Oxidation % |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **O** | Oolong | 30–70% |
| **C** | CTC Black | 90–95% |
| **C** | Conventional Orthodox Black | 85–95% |
| **G** | Green | 0% |
| **I** | Instant | Variable (based on source) |

> **KTU 2024 Quick Fact:** The course code **PEFTT418** is offered in the **B.Tech Food Technology / Food Engineering** stream. Tea processing is one of the **highest-weightage topics** in KTU Plantation Crops modules, with an average of **12–18 marks** per question paper.

### Cross-Reference to Other KTU Modules

- **Module 1 (Spices):** Comparison with **pepper, cardamom, turmeric** chemistry (polyphenol pathways overlap with tea).
- **Module 2 (Plantation Crops):** **Coffee, cocoa** share the **fermentation/roasting** paradigm with tea.
- **Module 4:** Will cover **Quality Control and Standards (FSSAI, ISO 3720, Tea Board of India regulations)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Coffee Processing

## 1.1 Botanical Occurrence and Origin

**Coffee** is a tropical evergreen shrub belonging to the family **Rubiaceae** and the genus **Coffea**. Two species are commercially dominant worldwide and in India:

- ***Coffea arabica*** (Arabica coffee) — contributes approximately **60 %** of world production; known for mild, aromatic flavour and lower caffeine (**1.0–1.5 %**).
- ***Coffea canephora*** (Robusta coffee) — accounts for about **40 %** of world production; hardier plant, higher caffeine (**2.0–2.5 %**), stronger and more bitter cup.

In India, coffee is cultivated predominantly in **Karnataka (≈ 70 %)**, **Kerala (≈ 21 %)**, **Tamil Nadu**, and the **Andhra Pradesh tribal belt (Araku Valley)** under the shade of the Western and Eastern Ghats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** The coffee "bean" used commercially is **not a true seed** in the botanical sense but the **endocarp + seed (endosperm)** enclosed within the pulp/mucilage of the fruit called a *cherry*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Candy with a Hard Shell" Model

Imagine each coffee cherry as a small, sticky candy wrapped in three layers:

1. **Outer red skin (exocarp)** — like a candy wrapper.
2. **Slimy, sugary middle layer (mucilaginous mesocarp)** — like a thick syrup that needs to be removed before eating the inner core.
3. **Hard papery shell (endocarp/parchment)** protecting the two green seeds inside — like a chocolate hard-shell covering the real "candy."

**Processing = systematically unwrapping each layer** so that the inner seed (the green coffee bean) can be roasted and ground into the powder you brew.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions (must-memorise for KTU):**
> - **Parchment coffee:** Beans still enclosed in the endocarp after pulping.
> - **Cherry coffee:** Dried whole fruit, used in the *natural/dry* process.
> - **Mucilage:** A pectin-rich, gummy, polysaccharide layer surrounding the parchment.
> - **Wet processing:** Pulping → fermentation → washing → drying.
> - **Dry processing:** Whole cherries dried in sun, then mechanically dehulled.

## 1.3 Harvesting of Coffee Cherries

Coffee cherries do **not** ripen uniformly on a single branch. This creates a harvesting dilemma addressed by two principal methods:

| Method | Description | Selectivity | Labour | Quality Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Selective / Hand Picking** | Only fully ripe (red) cherries are picked in multiple rounds (6–8 passes) | Very high | High | Premium quality — uniform ripeness |
| **Strip Picking** | All cherries (green, ripe, over-ripe) stripped from branch in a single pass | Low | Low | Mixed quality, suitable for Robusta & dry processing |
| **Mechanical Harvesting** | Straddle or tractor-mounted shakers/vibrators | Moderate–High | Low | Used in large Brazilian/Indian estates |

> [!TIP]
> Arabica is almost always hand-picked because the price premium justifies the labour cost. Robusta is often strip-picked in India.

The harvested cherries are then processed by **two fundamental pathways** — *Wet Method* and *Dry Method* — which converge at the *green coffee bean* stage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Operations in Coffee Processing — Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: Pulping (Wet Method Only)
- Cherries are fed into a **pulper** (disc-, drum-, or roller-type) that mechanically squeezes the bean out, leaving the **exocarp** and most of the **mesocarp** behind.
- The output is a **parchment bean** still coated with sticky **mucilage** (~ **45–55 %** moisture wet-basis).

### Step 2: Fermentation (Biochemical Mucilage Removal)
- Purpose: **Enzymatic hydrolysis of pectins** binding mucilage to the parchment.
- Conducted in **fermentation tanks** (concrete, wooden, or stainless steel) for **12–48 hours** depending on ambient temperature, mucilage thickness, and microbial load.
- **Optimal temperature:** 18–24 °C; below 10 °C fermentation almost stops.

### Step 3: Washing
- After fermentation, beans are washed in washing channels or mechanical washers to remove the degraded mucilage and residual sugars.
- **Water economy:** 5–10 L of clean water per kg of parchment.

### Step 4: Drying
- Goal: Reduce moisture from **~ 55 % (w.b.)** down to **10–12 % (w.b.)** for safe storage.
- Methods: **Sun drying** (7–14 days on raised African beds or patios) or **Mechanical drying** (rotary drum, Guardiola, or fluidized-bed dryers at 45–60 °C).

### Step 5: Hulling (De-husking)
- Removal of dried **parchment (endocarp)** and **silver skin (testa)** by mechanical hullers → produces **green coffee beans**.

## 2.2 Biochemical & Physical Changes During Fermentation

| Change Type | Description | Quantitative Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Pectin hydrolysis** | Native pectins → galacturonic acid + methanol via microbial **pectinases (pectin lyase, polygalacturonase)** | Mucilage becomes water-soluble |
| **Acid production** | Lactic acid (homo/hetero-fermentative LAB), acetic acid, butyric acid | pH drops from **~ 5.5 to 4.0–4.2** |
| **Exothermic reaction** | Microbial metabolism releases heat | Tank temperature rises by **4–6 °C** above ambient |
| **Sugar consumption** | Mucilage sugars (glucose, fructose) consumed by **Erwinia, Klebsiella, Leuconostoc, Lactobacillus** | Brix drops from ~12 % to < 1 % |
| **Aroma precursor formation** | Amino acids + sugars → precursors for Maillard reactions during roasting | Green-bean colour brightens; **acetic acid** is a positive marker of clean fermentation |
| **Over-fermentation defect** | Butyric + propionic acids → "stinker"/"fouled" beans | pH < 3.8, foetid odour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High Yield:** The dominant role of fermentation in coffee is **mucilage removal, NOT flavour development**. Flavour precursors are mainly formed during **roasting** via the **Maillard reaction** and **Strecker degradation**.

## 2.3 Roasting — The Heart of Coffee Flavour

Roasting transforms the dense, odourless, pale green bean into the fragrant, dark, brittle brown bean that defines the cup profile.

### Stages of Roasting (Tangible Engineering Phases)

1. **Drying phase** (0–4 min, 100–150 °C): Free water evaporates; bean turns from green to yellow → tan.
2. **Maillard phase** (4–8 min, 150–180 °C): Amino acids react with reducing sugars → melanoidins, aroma compounds.
3. **First Crack** (≈ 195–205 °C): Audible popping; pyrolytic release of CO₂; bean volume increases by **~ 50–60 %**.
4. **Development / Pyrolysis phase** (after first crack, up to 230 °C): Caramelization of sugars, Strecker aldehydes, oils migrate to surface.
5. **Second Crack** (≈ 225–230 °C): Cell wall rupture; bean becomes oily. Beyond this → fire risk.

### Typical Mass and Moisture Balance During Roasting

The bean loses weight primarily as water vapour, CO₂, and volatile organics. The **roast loss** is a key quality parameter:

$$\text{Roast Loss (\%)} = \frac{m_{\text{green}} - m_{\text{roasted}}}{m_{\text{green}}} \times 100$$

Typical commercial values:

| Roast Level | Bean Temp (°C) | Roast Loss (%) | Colour (L-value) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Light (Cinnamon) | 200–205 | 12–14 | 28–32 |
| Medium (City) | 210–215 | 14–16 | 23–27 |
| Medium-Dark (Full City) | 220–225 | 16–18 | 19–22 |
| Dark (French/Italian) | 230–240 | 18–22 | 14–18 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caffeine is heat-stable** up to ~ 235 °C, so roast level has little effect on caffeine content.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Numerical Constants & Quality Specifications

| Parameter | Specification / Range | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Green bean moisture (storage) | 10 – 12 | % (w.b.) | Prevents mould / insect attack |
| Parchment moisture (pre-hulling) | 10 – 12 | % (w.b.) | Safe hulling without bean breakage |
| Fermentation time | 12 – 48 | h | Depends on temperature & mucilage |
| Fermentation pH (end) | 4.0 – 4.5 | — | Below 3.8 = over-fermented |
| Roasting temperature | 200 – 240 | °C | 1st crack at ≈ 200 °C |
| Roast loss | 12 – 22 | % | Mass balance indicator |
| First crack onset | 195 – 205 | °C | Acoustic monitoring point |
| Grind size (Turkish) | < 0.3 | mm | Finest commercial grind |
| Grind size (Filter) | 0.5 – 0.8 | mm | Standard Indian filter coffee |
| Instant coffee yield | 25 – 35 | % (of roasted) | Mass balance for spray drying |
| Caffeine — Arabica | 1.0 – 1.5 | % (d.b.) | Lower, premium |
| Caffeine — Robusta | 2.0 – 2.5 | % (d.b.) | Higher, stronger |
| Chlorogenic acid loss on roast | 60 – 90 | % | Important antioxidant parameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **roast loss** (mass loss) with **chaff loss** (skin loss). Chaff = silver-skin flakes, removed by cyclone; Roast loss = total dry-mass + water loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Balances & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mass Balance for Green Coffee Production (Wet Process)

A coffee estate receives **M_c** kg of fresh cherries per day. The objective is to derive the mass of marketable green coffee.

**Given/Standard values for Arabica wet processing (per 100 kg fresh ripe cherry):**
- Pulp (exocarp + most mesocarp) removed in pulper: ~ **42 kg**
- Mucilage removed by fermentation: ~ **13 kg**
- Water lost during fermentation & washing: ~ **5 kg**
- Parchment + silver skin removed in huller: ~ **4 kg**
- Green coffee obtained: ~ **36 kg** (reversible to 100 kg by the losses above)

**Mass balance derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{green}} &= M_{\text{cherry}} - m_{\text{pulp}} - m_{\text{mucilage}} - m_{\text{water}} - m_{\text{parchment+chaff}} \\
M_{\text{green}} &= 100 - 42 - 13 - 5 - 4 \\
M_{\text{green}} &= 36 \; \text{kg per 100 kg cherry}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion ratio (out-turn):**

$$\text{Out-turn ratio} = \frac{M_{\text{green}}}{M_{\text{cherry}}} = \frac{36}{100} = 0.36 \; \text{(or 36 \%)}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the **commercial cherry-to-green-bean recovery ratio** used by planters to estimate plantation yield in *kg of clean coffee per kg of harvested cherry*. Indian Coffee Board norms quote **5.5–6.0 kg of ripe cherry → 1 kg of clean coffee** for Arabica by wet method.

> [!NOTE]
> For Robusta, out-turn is typically **4.5–5.0 kg cherry → 1 kg clean coffee** because of the larger bean size but slightly lower parchment yield.

## 3.2 Derivation — Moisture Reduction during Drying

Drying is an unsteady-state mass-transfer process. The instantaneous moisture content on a wet basis is:

$$M_{t}^{\text{wb}} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}(t)}{m_{\text{water}}(t) + m_{\text{dry matter}}} \times 100$$

The thin-layer drying model most commonly applied to coffee parchment is the **Page model** (semi-empirical):

$$\begin{aligned}
MR &= \frac{M_{t} - M_{e}}{M_{0} - M_{e}} = \exp(-k \cdot t^{n}) \\
\text{where: } & \\
MR &= \text{moisture ratio (dimensionless)} \\
M_{t} &= \text{moisture content at time } t \text{ (d.b.)} \\
M_{0} &= \text{initial moisture content (d.b.)} \\
M_{e} &= \text{equilibrium moisture content (d.b.)} \\
k &= \text{drying constant (1/h}^{n}\text{)} \\
n &= \text{Page exponent (dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}$$

For parchment coffee drying at **50 °C**, typical literature values are **k ≈ 0.42 h⁻¹** and **n ≈ 1.05**.

**Step-by-step numerical illustration:**
- Initial moisture $M_0 = 55\% \text{ d.b.}$ (≈ 35.5 % w.b.)
- Target final moisture $M_f = 11\% \text{ w.b.}$ (≈ 12.4 % d.b.)
- Equilibrium $M_e \approx 2\% \text{ d.b.}$ (at 50 °C, 60 % RH)

**Required moisture ratio to reach target:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MR_{\text{req}} &= \frac{12.4 - 2}{55 - 2} = \frac{10.4}{53} \approx 0.196 \\
\Rightarrow t &= \left[ \frac{-\ln(0.196)}{0.42} \right]^{1/1.05} \\
t &= \left[ \frac{1.63}{0.42} \right]^{0.952} = (3.881)^{0.952} \\
t &\approx 3.62 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Mechanical drying of parchment coffee at 50 °C to safe storage moisture takes approximately **3.5–4 hours** in a thin layer; commercial rotary dryers achieve this in **8–12 hours** with intermittent mixing.

## 3.3 Derivation — Roast Loss and Volume Expansion

Consider **m_g** kg of green coffee with moisture $w_g$ (wet basis) entering the roaster. After roasting, the moisture drops to ~ 1–2 %, and **roast loss** occurs due to water evaporation, CO₂ release, and volatilisation of aroma precursors.

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{roasted}} &= m_g (1 - w_g) (1 - L_{\text{pyro}}) + m_g \cdot w_{\text{final}} \\
\text{Roast loss (dry basis):} \quad L_{\text{pyro}} &= 1 - \frac{m_{\text{roasted}}(1 - w_f)}{m_g(1 - w_g)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical example** (1000 kg green coffee at 11 % w.b., roasted to 1.5 % w.b. with a target dry-basis loss of 16 %):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Dry green mass} &= 1000 \times (1 - 0.11) = 890 \; \text{kg} \\
\text{Dry roasted mass} &= 890 \times (1 - 0.16) = 747.6 \; \text{kg} \\
\text{Water in roasted beans} &= 747.6 \times \frac{0.015}{0.985} \approx 11.4 \; \text{kg} \\
\text{Total roasted bean mass} &= 747.6 + 11.4 = 759 \; \text{kg} \\
\text{Total roast loss (wet basis)} &= \frac{1000 - 759}{1000} \times 100 = 24.1\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** "Roast loss" quoted in the industry is the **dry-basis loss** (~ 14–18 %), while the **as-fired loss (wet basis)** is always 3–4 % higher. Do not confuse the two in problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variable on Quality Attributes

| Process Variable | Increased by → | Effect on Flavour | Effect on Caffeine | Effect on Acidity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Roasting time/temp | Darker roast | Bittersweet, smoky, body up | Slight reduction | Markedly reduced |
| Fermentation time | Over-fermented | Sour, "stinker" off-flavour | No change | Increases (acetic/lactic) |
| Drying rate | Too fast / case-hardening | "Baked" flavour, astringent | No change | Variable |
| Cherry maturity | Over-ripe | Fruity, sometimes fermented | No change | Higher |
| Process method | Dry (natural) | Heavy body, fruity, sweet | No change | Lower perceived |
| Process method | Wet (washed) | Clean, bright, acidic | No change | Higher perceived |

## 3.5 Manufacturing of Instant Coffee — Mass & Energy Logic

**Instant (soluble) coffee** is the dried water extract of roasted coffee. The process flow:

1. **Extraction:** Roasted & ground coffee is contacted with hot water (150–180 °C, 15–30 bar) in counter-current extractors (e.g., *Zosch* or *Niro* towers). Soluble solids diffuse out of the cells.
2. **Aroma recovery:** Volatile top-notes (aldehydes, diketones) are stripped at low temperature and stored in cold traps to be re-injected back at the end.
3. **Concentration:** Extract (~15–25 % solids) is concentrated to **40–50 % solids** by **multi-effect evaporation** (MEE) to save energy.
4. **Drying:**
   - **Spray drying:** Hot air (250 °C inlet, 100 °C outlet) atomises the concentrate; produces porous, free-flowing powder. Agglomeration is then performed for *freeze-dried* type texture.
   - **Freeze drying (lyophilisation):** Frozen extract → sublimation under vacuum → porous, premium-quality granules.
5. **Aroma re-injection & packing:** Volatiles restored, packed in glass jars or nitrogen-flushed sachets.

**Overall yield (mass balance):**

$$Y_{\text{instant}} = \frac{m_{\text{instant powder}}}{m_{\text{roasted coffee}}} \times 100 \approx 25\text{–}35\%$$

**Engineering significance:** Spray-dried instant coffee is **~ 100× more concentrated** in solubles than a brewed cup, so a **2 g** serving replaces ~ **200 mL** of brewed coffee.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Process Schematics

## 4.1 Master Process Flow — Wet Method (Most Important for KTU)

The block diagram below shows the **complete process flow from cherry to green coffee** by the wet method, including the position of every quality control point.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cherry Reception and Weighing] --> B[Pre-cleaning and Washing of Cherries]
    B --> C[Pulping - Disc or Drum Pulper]
    C --> D[Pre-fermentation Mucilage Separation Optional]
    D --> E[Fermentation Tank 12 to 48 hours]
    E --> F[Washing Channel or Mechanical Washer]
    F --> G[Drying Sun or Mechanical to 10 to 12 percent wb]
    G --> H[Conditioning 24 hours rest]
    H --> I[Hulling Removal of Parchment and Silver Skin]
    I --> J[Grading Size and Density]
    J --> K[Polishing Optional]
    K --> L[Sorting Electronic Color Sorter]
    L --> M[Green Coffee Storage 10 to 12 percent moisture]
    M --> N[QC Sampling for Moisture Defects and Cupping]

    QC1[QC1 Cherry Float Test] -.-> B
    QC2[QC2 pH and Temperature Check] -.-> E
    QC3[QC3 Moisture Meter] -.-> G
    QC4[QC4 Defect Count] -.-> J
    QC5[QC5 Cupping and Grading] -.-> M
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Manufacture of Coffee Powder and Instant Coffee

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Green Coffee Beans Storage] --> R2[Cleaning and Destoning]
    R2 --> R3[Roasting 200 to 240 degree C]
    R3 --> R4[Quenching and Cooling Air or Water Spray]
    R4 --> R5[Grinding Roller or Hammer Mill]
    R5 --> R6[Grind Sizing and Blending]
    R6 --> R7[Vacuum or Nitrogen Packing]
    R7 --> R8[Coffee Powder Retail Pack]

    R5 --> I1[Extraction Hot Water 150 to 180 degree C]
    I1 --> I2[Aroma Recovery Cold Trap]
    I1 --> I3[Multi-effect Evaporation to 40 to 50 percent solids]
    I3 --> I4[Spray Drying OR Freeze Drying]
    I2 --> I5[Aroma Re-injection]
    I4 --> I5
    I5 --> I6[Instant Coffee Granules]
    I6 --> I7[Glass Jar or Sachet Packing]
```

## 4.3 Quality Control Decision Tree at Fermentation Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start of Fermentation] --> S1{Mucilage Slip Test}
    S1 -- Slip Detected After 18 h --> S2[End Fermentation and Wash]
    S1 -- No Slip After 30 h --> S3{pH Below 3.8}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Reject Batch Over Fermented]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Extend Fermentation 4 to 6 h]
    S5 --> S1
    S2 --> S6[Proceed to Washing Channel]
    S4 --> S7[Channel to Dry Method Recovery]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Flavour Generation Hierarchy

This block-level map shows how each processing stage contributes to the **final cup flavour profile**, an important conceptual diagram for KTU viva questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Mucilage Composition] --> P2[Fermentation Kinetics]
    P2 --> P3[Acid and Sugar Profile of Green Bean]
    P3 --> P4[Chlorogenic Acid Level]
    P3 --> P5[Maillard Substrates in Bean]
    P4 --> P6[Roast Chemistry]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Melanoidins]
    P6 --> P8[Aroma Volatiles 900 plus compounds]
    P7 --> P9[Cup Body and Color]
    P8 --> P9
    P8 --> P10[Roaster Control 1st Crack to 2nd Crack]
    P10 --> P6
    P9 --> P11[Sensory Acceptance and QC Cupping Score]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEFTT418]
**List the two principal methods of coffee processing and state the quality implication of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two principal methods are:

1. **Wet (washed) method:** Cherries are pulped, fermented to remove mucilage, washed, and dried. *Quality implication:* Produces a **cleaner, brighter, more acidic cup** with uniform appearance; preferred for **Arabica** and premium markets.

2. **Dry (natural/unwashed) method:** Whole cherries are sun-dried and then mechanically de-hulled. *Quality implication:* Produces a **heavier-bodied, sweeter, fruitier cup** but with greater risk of off-flavours due to uncontrolled microbial action; commonly used for **Robusta** and Brazilian-style coffees.

*(1 mark each for naming, 1 mark for quality implication.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEFTT418]
**What is the role of pectinolytic enzymes in coffee fermentation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pectinolytic enzymes (mainly **pectin lyase, polygalacturonase, and pectin methyl esterase**) are produced by native microflora — chiefly **Erwinia, Klebsiella, Bacillus, and yeasts** — during fermentation.

Their role is the **hydrolytic breakdown of pectic substances** in the mucilaginous mesocarp, converting insoluble protopectin into soluble galacturonic acid and methanol. This makes the mucilage **water-soluble and slimy**, allowing it to be **washed off** in the washing channel, exposing the parchment layer.

*(1 mark for enzymes, 1 mark for source, 1 mark for mucilage removal.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **harvesting and post-harvest wet processing of coffee cherries** with emphasis on **pulping, fermentation, and drying**. Add a neat flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **biochemical and microbiological changes** occurring during the fermentation of coffee beans. How does over-fermentation affect quality? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Harvesting (2 marks):**
- **Selective hand-picking** of fully ripe red cherries — 6–8 rounds per season — is the premium method used for **Arabica**.
- **Strip picking** and **mechanical harvesting** are used for **Robusta** and in large estates.
- Cherries are **transported to the pulpery within 6–8 hours** to prevent uncontrolled fermentation.

**Pulping (2 marks):**
- Done in a **disc / drum / roller pulper** that squeezes the bean out under water lubrication.
- Output = **parchment coffee** (~55–60 % moisture, w.b.) with a sticky mucilage layer.
- Pulped beans are graded by density in **siphon / oscillating separators**; under-ripe beans return for re-pulping.

**Fermentation (1.5 marks):**
- Conducted in concrete or stainless-steel tanks for **12–48 h** at ambient temperature.
- End-point assessed by the **"mucilage slip test"** (slimy feel → ready) or **pH measurement (≈ 4.2)**.

**Drying (1.5 marks):**
- Goal: reduce moisture from **~55 % to 10–12 % w.b.**
- **Sun drying:** 7–14 days on raised African beds, with periodic raking.
- **Mechanical drying:** Rotary/Guardiola or vertical dryers at **45–60 °C** for **8–24 h**.
- **Conditioning** (24 h rest) is followed by **hulling** to remove parchment and silver skin.

*Flow diagram: see Section 4.1 above.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Biochemical changes (3.5 marks):**
1. **Pectin hydrolysis:** Mucilage pectins → galacturonic acid + oligosaccharides; water solubility increases.
2. **Sugar catabolism:** Glucose, fructose, sucrose in mucilage are consumed → ethanol, lactic acid, acetic acid.
3. **Acid build-up:** pH drops from ~5.5 to **4.0–4.2**; **titratable acidity** rises.
4. **Temperature rise:** Exothermic microbial metabolism → **+4 to +6 °C** above ambient.
5. **Aroma precursor formation:** Free amino acids + reducing sugars accumulate, providing substrates for the **Maillard reaction** during roasting.

**Microbiological changes (2 marks):**
- **Early phase (0–12 h):** Gram-negative bacteria — *Erwinia herbicola*, *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, *Citrobacter*.
- **Mid phase (12–24 h):** Lactic acid bacteria — *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*, *Lactobacillus plantarum*.
- **Late phase (24–36 h):** Acetic acid bacteria — *Acetobacter aceti*.
- **Yeasts** (*Saccharomyces*, *Schizosaccharomyces*) appear throughout, contributing to ethanol and fruity esters.

**Effect of over-fermentation on quality (1.5 marks):**
- **pH < 3.8**, **butyric & propionic acid** production → "**stinker**" or "**fouled**" beans.
- Off-flavours (**onion, putrid, sour-rot**) persist into the cup.
- Increased **triage (defect) count** in green-bean grading.
- Down-grading of the lot; in severe cases, **rejection for export**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **roasting process of coffee** in detail. Describe the **chemical changes (Maillard, Strecker, caramelization)** and the typical **roast-loss profile**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of instant (soluble) coffee** starting from roasted and ground coffee. Compare **spray drying vs. freeze drying**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Roasting process (2.5 marks):**
- Conducted in a **drum roaster** (batch) or **fluidized-bed roaster** (continuous) at bean temperatures of **200–240 °C** for **8–15 min**.
- **Phases:**
  1. *Drying* (0–4 min, 100–150 °C): free water evaporates, beans turn from green → tan.
  2. *Maillard* (4–8 min, 150–180 °C): melanoidins, aroma precursors form.
  3. *First Crack* (≈ 195–205 °C): bean expands by **~ 50–60 %**, audible pop, **CO₂ release**.
  4. *Development* (post-first crack to 2nd crack): caramelization, oil migration to surface.
  5. *Second Crack* (225–230 °C): wall rupture, oily sheen.
- Beans are **rapidly cooled** (air-quench or water-spray) to halt the reaction.

**Chemical changes (3 marks):**
1. **Maillard Reaction:** Amino acids + reducing sugars → Amadori compounds → melanoidins (brown polymers) and **pyrazines, furans, thiophenes** (roasty aroma).
2. **Strecker Degradation:** Amino acids + dicarbonyls → aldehydes (Strecker aldehydes) + CO₂ + α-aminoketones.
3. **Caramelization:** Thermal degradation of sucrose → diacetyl, furfural, hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF).
4. **Chlorogenic acid degradation:** CGA lactonises and breaks down to **quinic acid + caffeic acid**, contributing to bitterness.
5. **Lipid pyrolysis:** Triglycerides → free fatty acids, diterpenes (cafestol, kahweol).

**Roast-loss profile (1.5 marks):**
- Total roast loss = **12–22 %** of green-bean mass.
- Composition: **water (8–10 %)** + **CO₂ (3–4 %)** + **organic volatiles (1–3 %)**.
- Roast loss is **directly proportional to bean colour darkness** and inversely proportional to the development of **soluble yield in brewing**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Flow diagram (see Section 4.2 — lower branch).**

**Step-by-step manufacture of instant coffee (4 marks):**

1. **Extraction:** Roasted & ground coffee is fed to a counter-current extractor (*Niro/Zosch tower*). Hot water at **150–180 °C, 15–30 bar** solubilises the bean contents.
2. **Clarification:** The extract is centrifuged to remove fines.
3. **Aroma recovery:** Volatile top-notes are vacuum-stripped and concentrated in cold traps (–40 °C).
4. **Concentration:** The 15–25 % extract is concentrated to **40–50 % solids** in a **multi-effect evaporator (MEE)** to reduce drying load.
5. **Drying:** **Spray drying** or **freeze drying** converts the concentrate into dry powder/granules.
6. **Aroma re-injection & agglomeration:** The trapped aroma is sprayed back onto the powder.
7. **Packing:** Inert gas (N₂) flushed glass jars or aluminium sachets.

**Spray drying vs. freeze drying (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Spray Drying | Freeze Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Drying principle | Hot-air atomisation → water evaporation | Sublimation of ice under vacuum |
| Inlet/outlet air | 250 °C / 100 °C | –40 °C (shelf) / 100–300 Pa |
| Particle form | Spherical, fine (50–200 µm) | Porous, granular (1–3 mm) |
| Aroma retention | Moderate (re-injected) | High (excellent retention) |
| Cost | Low | High (3–5× spray drying) |
| Quality grade | Commercial, mainstream | Premium ("Freeze-Dried") |
| Solubility | Good | Excellent, instant wettability |
| Energy consumption | Moderate | Very high |

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing pulping with hulling:** Pulping removes exocarp + most mesocarp; Hulling removes the dried *parchment (endocarp)*. Most answers omit hulling altogether — *–2 marks*.
> 2. **Confusing green-bean moisture with parchment moisture:** Parchment must be dried to **10–12 %** *before* hulling; green beans are then stored at the same range. Writing "dry parchment to 12 %" without stating *wet-basis* invites deduction.
> 3. **Writing "enzymatic fermentation" without naming the enzymes or microbes:** Examiners expect *pectin lyase / polygalacturonase* and at least one of *Erwinia / Leuconostoc / Lactobacillus* — *–1 mark* otherwise.
> 4. **Mixing up roast loss and chaff loss:** Chaff = silver-skin removed by cyclone; roast loss = total mass loss during roasting. *–1 mark* if not distinguished.
> 5. **Confusing first crack with second crack:** First crack is **moisture-driven** popping at ~ 200 °C; second crack is **pyrolytic cell-wall rupture** at ~ 230 °C. *–1 mark* if confused.
> 6. **In instant coffee, omitting the "aroma recovery + re-injection" step:** This is the most-asked yet most-skipped step — *–2 marks*.
> 7. **Writing the wrong drying medium:** Mechanical drying uses **air, not water**; parchment is never boiled.
> 8. **Mis-stating yield:** Instant coffee yield is **25–35 % of roasted beans** (NOT of green beans). Common error.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ☑ **Coffee = cherry (fruit)**, not seed; commercial bean = endocarp + seed.
- ☑ Two species: **Arabica (mild, low caffeine)** and **Robusta (strong, high caffeine)**.
- ☑ **Selective hand picking** for Arabica; **strip picking** for Robusta.
- ☑ Two processing methods: **Wet (washed) → clean cup**; **Dry (natural) → heavy body, fruity**.
- ☑ **Pulping** removes exocarp; **fermentation** removes mucilage; **washing** removes degraded mucilage; **drying** removes water.
- ☑ **Fermentation = enzymatic + microbial hydrolysis of pectin** to water-soluble sugars/acids.
- ☑ Key microbes: *Erwinia → Leuconostoc → Lactobacillus → Acetobacter*.
- ☑ **End-point of fermentation:** pH 4.0–4.2; mucilage slip test.
- ☑ **Drying** target: **10–12 % w.b.** moisture, achieved in **7–14 days (sun)** or **8–24 h (mechanical)**.
- ☑ **Roasting temperatures: 200–240 °C; First crack ≈ 200 °C; Second crack ≈ 230 °C.**
- ☑ **Roast loss: 12–22 %** (dry basis).
- ☑ **Maillard, Strecker, Caramelization, CGA degradation** = the four key roasting chemistries.
- ☑ **Coffee powder** = green → roast → cool → grind → pack (vacuum/N₂).
- ☑ **Instant coffee** = extract roasted & ground coffee → concentrate → **spray/freeze dry**.
- ☑ **Spray drying** is cheap, fine powder; **Freeze drying** is expensive, premium, porous granules.
- ☑ **Caffeine is heat-stable**; roast level does not change caffeine content significantly.
- ☑ **Quality control points:** Float test (cherries), pH/temperature (fermentation), moisture meter (drying), defect count (grading), cupping (final QC).
- ☑ **Yields to remember:** Cherry → Green = **5.5–6.0:1 (Arabica)**, **4.5–5.0:1 (Robusta)**; Roasted → Instant = **25–35 %**.
- ☑ **Indian production:** Karnataka (70 %) + Kerala (21 %) = ~ 91 % of national output.
- ☑ **Arabica caffeine 1.0–1.5 %; Robusta caffeine 2.0–2.5 %.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
