### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Describe in detail the post-harvest processing of black pepper with a labeled flow diagram. Mention the critical quality parameters at each stage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Black pepper (*Piper nigrum* L.), the "King of Spices," is a major export commodity of Kerala. The post-harvest processing converts fresh green berries (MC ≈ 65–70% w.b.) into dried black pepper (MC ≤ 11% w.b.) suitable for storage and trade.

**Processing Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Harvesting:** Mature green berries (one or two fully mature berries per spike) are hand-plucked. Improper harvest (over-ripe/under-ripe) reduces piperine and oil content.

2. **Threshing / Decorning:** Berries are separated from spikes mechanically or by trampling. This step determines whether the pepper is graded as **MG-1, MG-2, or TG (Tellicherry Garbled)**.

3. **Blanching:** Optional but recommended. Berries are dipped in **hot water at 80–90 °C for 1 minute** to:
   - Inactivate polyphenol oxidase (prevents browning).
   - Soften the pericarp for uniform drying.
   - Sterilize surface microflora.

4. **Drying (Critical Step):**
   - **Sun drying:** 3–5 days on clean, raised platforms. Limitation: weather-dependent, contamination risk.
   - **Mechanical drying:** Cabinet/tray dryers at **50–55 °C for 10–12 hours** until MC ≤ 11% w.b.
   - **Fluidized bed drying:** Best for bulk — drying time reduces to **4–6 hours**.

5. **Cleaning and Polishing:** Removal of pinheads, immature berries, dust, and stalks. Mechanical polishers (rubber rollers) give a uniform black luster.

6. **Grading:** By size using rotary screens — grades include **4 mm (special), 5 mm (MG-1), and bulk grades**.

7. **Packaging and Storage:** Packed in **double-lined gunny bags** (jute + PP liner), stored at **< 25 °C, RH < 70%** to prevent mold and aflatoxin.

**Flow Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 diagram; reproduce with hand-drawn or ASCII arrows in the exam.)*

**Quality Parameters (1 mark):**
- Moisture: **≤ 11% w.b.**
- Piperine content: **≥ 5% (for MG-1 grade)**
- Essential oil: **≥ 2% v/w**
- Bulk density: **500–600 g/L**
- Aflatoxin: **< 30 μg/kg** (as per FSSAI)

---

**(b) Explain the principle of supercritical fluid extraction (SCFE) of spices. List two advantages over conventional solvent extraction.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (4 marks):**

Supercritical fluid extraction uses a solvent **above its critical temperature ($T_c$) and critical pressure ($P_c$)**, where it possesses properties of **both a liquid (high density, good solvating power) and a gas (low viscosity, high diffusivity)**.

For **carbon dioxide** ($\text{CO}_2$):
- $T_c = 31.1°\text{C}$, $P_c = 73.8$ bar.

The supercritical $\text{CO}_2$ is contacted with the ground spice in an **extractor vessel** at **40–60 °C and 100–300 bar**. The supercritical fluid dissolves the non-polar flavor compounds (terpenes, oleoresins). The extract-laden $\text{CO}_2$ is then passed to a **separator** where pressure is reduced, causing $\text{CO}_2$ to revert to gas, **precipitating the extract** as a solvent-free product. The $\text{CO}_2$ is recycled.

**Process Block:**

$$
\text{Spice (solid)} \xrightarrow{\text{SC-CO}_2 \text{ at 100-300 bar, 40-60 °C}} \text{Extract + SC-CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{↓ Pressure}} \text{Extract (pure)} + \text{CO}_2 \text{ (gas, recycled)}
$$

**Advantages over conventional solvent extraction (2 marks):**

1. **Solvent-Free Product:** $\text{CO}_2$ is a GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) solvent; it leaves **zero residue**, unlike hexane/acetone which require strict removal (< 5 ppm as per FSSAI).

2. **Selectivity & Mild Operation:** Low temperature (40–60 °C) preserves **heat-labile aroma compounds**; pressure tunability allows **selective extraction** (e.g., separate essential oil from oleoresin by staged pressure drop).

3. **Environmentally Benign:** $\text{CO}_2$ is non-toxic, non-flammable, recyclable — **green process**.

4. **Higher Quality Product:** Color, flavor, and biological activity (e.g., curcuminoids in turmeric, piperine in pepper) are better retained.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
SCFE is the *gold standard* for producing high-purity, food-grade spice extracts used in the flavor, fragrance, and nutraceutical industries. Companies like **Synthite Industries (Kerala) and Flavourtech (Australia)** use industrial SCFE for spice extracts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) With a neat flow diagram, describe the processing of tea (Camellia sinensis). Explain the biochemical changes during fermentation.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Tea is the **most consumed beverage worldwide after water**. Its characteristic flavor, color, and aroma are developed through a series of controlled biochemical and physical operations. The two principal manufacturing methods are:
- **CTC (Crush, Tear, Curl)** — used for granulated black tea.
- **Orthodox** — used for whole-leaf tea.

**Processing Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Plucking:** Only the **"two leaves and a bud"** are hand-plucked — they contain the highest concentration of polyphenols and enzymes.

2. **Withering:** Spread thinly on **withering troughs** with airflow at **20–25 °C, RH 70–80%** for **12–18 hours**. Moisture reduces from **75–80% w.b. to 55–65% w.b.**. The leaves become flaccid and pliable, suitable for rolling.

3. **Rolling / Cell Disruption:** Leaves are passed through **rolls or CTC machines** that rupture cell walls, bringing **polyphenols and polyphenol oxidase (PPO) enzyme** into contact, initiating oxidation. In orthodox processing, leaves retain their shape; in CTC, they are curled into small granules.

4. **Fermentation (Oxidation):** The rolled leaves are spread on a clean floor in a **humid fermentation room (25–30 °C, RH > 95%) for 1–3 hours.** The enzyme **PPO catalyzes** the oxidation of catechins → theaflavins (bright, brisk) → thearubigins (depth, color, body).

5. **Drying (Firing):** Fermented leaves are passed through a **drier at 90–95 °C for 20–30 minutes** to:
   - Inactivate enzymes (stop oxidation at desired level).
   - Reduce moisture to **< 3% w.b.** for shelf stability.
   - Develop roasted aroma.

6. **Sorting and Grading:** By size using **vibratory sieves** — grades: **Pekoe, Orange Pekoe (OP), Broken Orange Pekoe (BOP), Fannings, Dust**.

7. **Blending and Tasting:** Master blenders combine different lots for **consistent flavor profile** across batches.

8. **Packaging:** Vacuum-sealed, nitrogen-flushed **aluminum-laminated pouches** (MVTR < 1 g/m²/day).

**Biochemical Changes During Fermentation (2 marks):**

The key reaction sequence is:

$$
\text{Catechins (flavan-3-ols)} \xrightarrow{\text{Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO) + O}_2} \text{Quinones} \rightarrow \text{Theaflavins} \rightarrow \text{Thearubigins}
$$

- **Theaflavins** (1–2% of dry weight) — give **brightness, briskness, and golden color** of black tea.
- **Thearubigins** (10–20% of dry weight) — give **depth, body, and reddish-brown color**.
- Substrate to product ratio determines tea *quality* — assessed by **"Liquor color, briskness, and strength"** in professional cupping.

**Flow Diagram (refer Section 4.3).**

---

**(b) Differentiate between wet processing and dry processing of coffee. Which method is preferred in India and why?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Coffee (*Coffea arabica* and *Coffea canephora* / Robusta) is a major plantation crop of Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu. The two principal post-harvest processing methods are **Wet (Washed) Processing** and **Dry (Natural) Processing**, which differ in how the fruit pulp is removed.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Wet (Washed) Processing** | **Dry (Natural) Processing** |
|---|---|---|
| Pulp removal | Mechanical depulper after harvesting | Sun-dried with pulp intact |
| Mucilage removal | **Fermentation** in water tanks for 12–48 h | Mucilage dries naturally with the bean |
| Water usage | **High** (50–100 L/kg cherry) | **Negligible** |
| Time required | 5–7 days | 10–25 days |
| Bean quality | Uniform color, **mild/acidic flavor, premium grade** | Variable, **fruity/sweet/lively flavor** |
| Capital cost | High (pulper, fermentation tanks, washing channels) | Very low |
| Suitable for | **Arabica** (premium) | **Robusta** + Arabica (commercial) |
| Risk of defect | Low (controlled) | High (over-fermentation, fungal defects) |
| Geographical fit | Hill regions with abundant water | Drought-prone, water-scarce regions |

**Steps in Wet Processing (1 mark):**

1. Pulping (mechanical depulper).
2. Mucilage fermentation (12–48 h, controlled, pH drops from ~ 6.0 to ~ 4.0).
3. Washing (mucilage-free beans in clean water channels).
4. **Drying** to MC ≤ 12% w.b. (sun-dried on raised beds or mechanical dryers).
5. **Hulling** to remove parchment (endocarp).

**Method Preferred in India and Why (2 marks):**

In India, **wet processing is preferred for Arabica** (grown in Chikmagalur, Coorg, Wayanad, Bababudangiri) and is increasingly adopted for premium Robusta as well. The reasons are:

1. **Higher Cup Quality:** Washed coffee commands **premium export prices (20–40% higher)** in global markets (Germany, Japan, USA).
2. **Uniformity:** Standardized processing yields beans of uniform color, size, and defect count.
3. **Lower Mold/OTA Risk:** Wet processing followed by controlled drying minimizes **Ochratoxin A (OTA)** contamination, a major concern in dry-processed Robusta.
4. **Indian Specialty Coffee Brand:** Indian washed Arabica is positioned as **specialty coffee** (e.g., "Monsooned Malabar AA," "Coorg Arabica") — premium segment.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
While **dry processing is cheaper and suited to small tribal farmers**, the **Indian coffee industry has increasingly shifted to wet processing** to capture export value, ensure food safety, and align with the **Coffee Board of India's** quality assurance scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (KTU Board 2024):**

- **Process = Unit Operations Chain**, not a single step.
- **Standard Moisture Target:** **≤ 11–13% w.b.** for spices; **< 3% w.b.** for tea; **< 12% w.b.** for coffee/parchment.
- **Black Pepper Pipeline:** *Pluck → Thresh → Blanch (80 °C, 1 min) → Dry (50 °C, 10–12 h) → Polish → Grade → Pack*.
- **Tea Pipeline:** *Pluck (2L+1B) → Wither (12–18 h) → Roll (CTC/Orthodox) → Ferment (1–3 h, PPO + O₂) → Fire (90–95 °C, 20–30 min) → Sort → Blend → Pack*.
- **Coffee:** Wet processing preferred for Arabica → premium cup quality.
- **Essential Oil vs Oleoresin:** Volatile-only vs. volatile + non-volatile. SCFE is the cleanest industrial method.
- **Drying Formula Mastery:** $W_d = W_w (1 - M_{wb})$; remember to convert w.b. ↔ d.b.
- **Grinding Laws:** *Kick's* (coarse, D > 500 μm) vs. *Bond's* (fine). Cryogenic grinding preserves volatiles.
- **Critical Temperatures:** SCFE $\text{CO}_2$ at **> 31.1 °C, > 73.8 bar**; supercritical state.
- **Aflatoxin & OTA:** Always mention **food safety regulations** (FSSAI limits: aflatoxin < 30 μg/kg for spices).
- **Diagram:** Always draw a **labeled block flow diagram** in 14-mark process questions — 1 mark reserved.
- **Numerical Examples:** Expect a moisture-content or yield-calculation problem (3–7 marks).
- **Crop-Specific Data:** *Standard yields* — pepper oil 2–4%, turmeric oleoresin 5–8%, clove oil 14–18%, cardamom oleoresin 6–8%.
- **Reference Standards:** **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)**, **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **Codex Alimentarius**, **AGMARK** grades.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Equipment Involved - Chicory Chemistry - Quality Grading of Coffee
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** and Equipment Involved - Chicory Chemistry - Quality Grading of Coffee

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Equipment Involved in Coffee and Chicory Processing

#### Formal Definition
The equipment used in coffee and chicory processing encompasses a comprehensive range of mechanical, thermal, and chemical processing machinery designed to transform the harvested raw agricultural produce (cherries for coffee; roots for chicory) into a marketable, stable, consumable product. These equipment categories are broadly classified into **primary processing equipment** (post-harvest handling, drying, grading) and **secondary processing equipment** (roasting, grinding, extraction, and packaging).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Equipment Involved" in Module 3 typically refers to the processing machinery specific to coffee and chicory value addition — since both are roasted beverages, their equipment lines share significant overlap (roasters, grinders, extractors, agglomerators).

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine making instant coffee at home. You start with a green bean, heat it in a pan (roasting), crush it with a stone (grinding), and boil it in water (extraction). Now, multiply this by 10,000 and make it hygienic, consistent, and export-quality. That is essentially what **industrial processing equipment** does. The cherry (fruit) must be stripped of its skin, the bean dried, the bean roasted uniformly, the flavor compounds developed, and the water-soluble solids extracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Processing Metrics:**
> - **Moisture content of green coffee bean:** 10–12 %
> - **Roasting temperature:** 180–240 °C
> - **Roasting time:** 8–15 minutes
> - **Grind size (espresso):** 200–300 μm
> - **Extraction yield:** 18–24 % of bean mass

---

### 1.2 Chemistry of Chicory

#### Formal Definition
**Chicory (Cichorium intybus L.)** is a perennial herbaceous plant of the family *Asteraceae*, whose roasted roots are widely used as a coffee substitute, adulterant, and functional food ingredient. The chemistry of chicory is dominated by **inulin** (a fructan polysaccharide), **sesquiterpene lactones** (lactucin, lactucopicrin), **phenolic acids**, and **coumarins**, which together define its prebiotic, hepatoprotective, and bitter-flavor properties.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of chicory root as a **"natural sugar battery"** — it stores energy not as starch (like rice or potato) but as **inulin**, a chain of fructose molecules. When roasted, this long fructose chain breaks down, caramelizes, and produces a deep brown color and a coffee-like aroma. This is *why* roasted chicory looks, smells, and brews similarly to coffee, even though botanically it is unrelated to the coffee plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Chemistry at a Glance:**
> - **Inulin content (fresh root):** 15–20 % of fresh weight
> - **Inulin content (dry root):** 40–50 %
> - **Fructose-to-glucose ratio in inulin:** ≈ 25:1
> - **Roasting temperature:** 170–200 °C
> - **Degree of Polymerization (DP):** 2–60 fructose units

---

### 1.3 Quality Grading of Coffee

#### Formal Definition
**Quality Grading of Coffee** is a multi-tiered evaluation framework that classifies green and roasted coffee based on **physical parameters** (bean size, defects, color), **sensory parameters** (aroma, acidity, body, flavor, aftertaste — commonly abbreviated **AAFB + Aftertaste** or aligned with **SCA cupping protocols**), and **chemical parameters** (moisture, caffeine, chlorogenic acid content). The grading standards are issued by bodies such as the **Coffee Board of India (CBI)**, **Specialty Coffee Association (SCA)**, and **International Coffee Organization (ICO)**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of coffee grading like **diamond grading**. A diamond is judged on the "4 Cs" (Cut, Clarity, Color, Carat). A coffee bean is judged on the **"4 Cs" of its own**: **Color, Cleanliness, Consistency, and Cup quality**. A premium coffee bean is large, uniform in size, free from defects (broken, black, sour beans), and produces a balanced, aromatic, and clean cup. Just as a low-grade diamond still has value but commands a fraction of the price, lower-grade coffee is functional but lacks the signature flavor profile of premium beans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Quality Benchmarks (SCA Cupping Score):**
> - **Specialty Grade:** ≥ 80 points (out of 100)
> - **Premium Grade:** 70–79 points
> - **Below Premium / Commercial:** 60–69 points
> - **Off-grade:** < 60 points
> - **Moisture content (green bean):** 10–12 % (max permissible 12.5 %)
> - **Defect tolerance (specialty):** 0 primary defects per 350 g sample

---

### 1.4 Pre-Reading Snapshot for the Module

| Sub-Topic | Key Focus | Industrial Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment in Coffee Processing | Pulpers, washers, dryers, roasters, grinders, extractors | Determines bean quality and end-product form |
| Equipment in Chicory Processing | Root slicers, dryers, roasters, grinders, extractors | Critical for instant chicory production |
| Chicory Chemistry | Inulin, sesquiterpenes, phenolics, Maillard reaction products | Defines prebiotic value, flavor, adulteration detection |
| Coffee Quality Grading | Bean size, defect count, moisture, cup quality, SCA score | Drives export price, market access, premium certification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Equipment Involved — Coffee Processing (Detailed Walkthrough)

Coffee processing involves a multi-stage pipeline from **cherry to green bean to roasted bean to soluble powder**. Each stage has dedicated equipment, listed below in process order.

#### Stage 1: Harvesting and Primary Processing
- **Coffee Pulper:** Mechanically removes the outer skin (exocarp) of the ripe cherry using rotating disks or drums fitted with rubber/steel beaters. The pulped bean (with mucilage intact) is then passed through a **demucilager** or **fermentation tank**.
- **Fermentation Tank:** A controlled vessel (stainless steel or concrete) where naturally occurring pectinolytic microorganisms degrade the mucilage layer in 12–36 hours at ambient temperature.
- **Washing Channel / Mechanical Washer:** Removes the degraded mucilage by water flow or agitation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet processing** (washed coffee) typically yields a cleaner, more acidic, and higher-grade cup compared to **dry processing** (natural/sun-dried), which yields a heavier-bodied, sweeter cup.

#### Stage 2: Drying
- **Sun Drying Yard / Mechanical Dryer:**
  - **Sun drying:** Coffee spread on raised African beds or patios, dried to 10–12 % moisture over 7–14 days.
  - **Mechanical dryers:** Guardiolas (rotary drum dryers), Guardiola-type column dryers, or silo dryers using hot air at 40–50 °C to prevent case-hardening and ensure uniform moisture reduction.

#### Stage 3: Hulling and Polishing
- **Huller:** Removes the **parchment** (endocarp) from dried washed coffee.
- **Polisher:** Removes the **silverskin** (perisperm) attached to the green bean, improving appearance and reducing chaff during roasting.

#### Stage 4: Grading and Sorting
- **Vibratory Sieves / Size graders:** Separate beans by screen size (commonly Screens 12 to 20, where the number denotes the diameter in 1/64 inch).
  - **AA grade:** Screens 17–18 (≈ 6.75–7.14 mm)
  - **A grade:** Screens 15–16 (≈ 5.95–6.35 mm)
  - **B grade:** Screens 12–14 (≈ 4.76–5.56 mm)
- **Pneumatic Separator / Catador:** Uses air-stream to separate light/chaffy beans.
- **Color Sorter (Electronic):** Optical sensors eject defective beans (black, sour, insect-damaged) based on chromatic deviation.

#### Stage 5: Roasting
- **Drum Roaster:** A horizontal rotating drum heated externally by gas/electricity. Residence time: 8–20 minutes. Suitable for medium to large batches.
- **Hot-Air (Fluidized Bed) Roaster:** Hot air suspends beans in a fluidized state for uniform heat transfer; faster (3–5 min) and brighter in cup profile.
- **Spent Heat Recovery Unit:** Captures exhaust heat for energy efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roasting Reactions (Pyrolysis Stage):**
> 1. **Drying phase** (0–150 °C): Moisture loss
> 2. **Maillard Reaction** (140–165 °C): Amino acids + reducing sugars → melanoidins
> 3. **First Crack** (≈ 196 °C): Audible bean fracture, release of CO₂ and aromatic volatiles
> 4. **Carbohydrate Caramelization** (170–200 °C)
> 5. **Second Crack** (≈ 224 °C): Dark roast, smoke, oils on bean surface
> 6. **Quenching:** Rapid cooling to halt reaction at target roast level

#### Stage 6: Grinding
- **Blade Grinder (Domestic):** High-speed rotating blade; produces non-uniform particle size.
- **Burr Grinder (Industrial):** Conical or flat burrs set at a precise gap; produces uniform particles critical for extraction consistency.
- **Roller Mill:** Used for industrial-scale grinding; multiple rollers with progressively decreasing gap.

#### Stage 7: Extraction and Concentration
- **Extraction Column / Percolator:** Green or roasted coffee is contacted with hot water (175–195 °C) under pressure in a battery of columns. Soluble solids are extracted into a "brew" of 15–30 % total solids.
- **Evaporator (Multiple Effect):** Concentrates the extract to 40–60 % solids before drying. May be a **falling film** or **rising film** type.
- **Spray Dryer:** Atomizes the concentrate into a hot air stream (150–250 °C inlet), producing free-flowing instant coffee granules.
- **Agglomerator (Instant Coffee):** Re-wets fine spray-dried powder with steam to form larger, more soluble granules.
- **Freeze Dryer (Lyophilizer):** Most premium method — frozen extract is sublimed under vacuum, preserving volatile aromatics.

---

### 2.2 Equipment Involved — Chicory Processing

| Stage | Equipment | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Root harvesting | Root harvester / plow | Uproots chicory roots |
| Washing | Rotary drum washer | Removes soil and debris |
| Slicing | Disc slicer / cuber | Reduces root to uniform 3–5 mm cubes for efficient drying |
| Drying | Belt dryer / rotary dryer | Reduces moisture from ~75 % to ~12 % |
| Roasting | Drum roaster (150–200 °C) | Develops color, aroma; converts inulin → fructose + oligofructose |
| Grinding | Hammer mill / disc mill | Produces chicory powder |
| Extraction | Diffusion battery | Hot water extracts soluble solids |
| Concentration | Multi-effect evaporator | Concentrates chicory extract to 50–60 % solids |
| Drying | Spray dryer / drum dryer | Produces chicory powder for blending |

---

### 2.3 Chemistry of Chicory — Deep Theoretical Analysis

#### A. Inulin — The Signature Carbohydrate
**Inulin** is a polydisperse fructan with the general formula:

$$
G_{F_n} = G_{1} F_{n}
$$

where $G$ = terminal glucose unit, $F_n$ = chain of $n$ fructose units linked by $\beta(2 \rightarrow 1)$ glycosidic bonds.

- **Molecular weight range:** 350–5,500 Da (chicory inulin)
- **Degree of polymerization (DP):** 2–60 (average DP ≈ 12 for native chicory inulin)
- **Solubility in water:** High (forms colloidal dispersions)
- **Hydrolysis products:** Fructose + glucose (mild acid or inulinase enzyme)

> [!NOTE]
> Inulin is a **prebiotic** — it is not digested by human enzymes but is fermented by beneficial colonic bacteria (*Bifidobacterium*, *Lactobacillus*), promoting gut health.

#### B. Sesquiterpene Lactones
Chicory contains bitter principles, primarily:
- **Lactucin**
- **Lactucopicrin** (also called intybin)
- **8-deoxylactucin**

These compounds are responsible for the characteristic bitter taste and reported sedative, anti-inflammatory, and hepatoprotective biological activities. The bitter threshold concentration is extremely low (µg/L range).

#### C. Phenolic Acids
- **Chicoric acid** (dicaffeoyl tartaric acid) — dominant phenolic
- **Chlorogenic acid**
- **Caffeic acid**
- **Ferulic acid**

These contribute to antioxidant activity and also undergo Maillard-type reactions during roasting.

#### D. Maillard Reaction During Chicory Roasting
When chicory root is roasted (170–200 °C), the following cascade occurs:

$$
\text{Inulin} \xrightarrow{\Delta, H^+} \text{Fructose} \xrightarrow{\text{Maillard}} \text{Melanoidins} + \text{Aromatic Volatiles}
$$

Key aroma compounds generated:
- **Furfural** (caramel-like)
- **Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF)** (sweet, caramel)
- **5-HMF** (used as an indicator of overheating)
- **Pyrazines** (roasty, nutty)
- **Diacetyl** (buttery)
- **Acetol** (caramel)

#### E. Protein and Lipid Content
- **Protein:** 5–7 % of dry root
- **Lipid:** 0.5–1.5 % (much lower than coffee's 10–15 %)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **low lipid** and **high inulin** content of chicory, compared to coffee's high lipid and low free-carbohydrate content, is the key reason chicory can be stored, roasted, and brewed similarly to coffee — yet chemically it is a fundamentally different beverage base.

---

### 2.4 Coffee Quality Grading — Theoretical Framework

Coffee grading operates at **three levels**:

#### Level 1: Green Bean Grading (Physical)
| Parameter | Specialty Grade | Premium | Below Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary defects per 350 g | 0 | ≤ 5 | 6–15 |
| Secondary defects per 350 g | ≤ 5 | ≤ 15 | 16–30 |
| Moisture (%) | 10–12 | 10–12 | 10–13 |
| Screen size | ≥ Screen 15 | ≥ Screen 14 | Mixed |
| Color | Uniform bluish-green | Slight discoloration | Pale, bleached |

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary defects (fatal):** Black bean, sour bean, pod/cherry, large stone, medium stone, large stick.
> **Secondary defects:** Partial black, partial sour, parchment, broken/chipped, immature, withered, small stick, shell, water damage, insect damage.

#### Level 2: Roast Grading
- **Bean color uniformity** (Agtron scale: 95 = light, 25 = very dark)
- **Surface defects** (quakers, scorched beans)
- **Quaker count:** 0 for specialty, ≤ 3 for premium
- **Moisture loss during roast:** 12–20 %

#### Level 3: Cup Quality (SCA Protocol)
Ten attributes scored on a 1–10 scale:
1. Fragrance/Aroma
2. Flavor
3. Aftertaste
4. Acidity
5. Body
6. Balance
7. Uniformity
8. Clean Cup
9. Sweetness
10. Overall Impression

**Total SCA Cupping Score = sum of 10 attributes**

$$
\text{Cup Quality Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i
$$

where $s_i$ ∈ {6, 7, 8, 9, 10} (below 6 = "not rated").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification by Final Score:**
> - **Outstanding:** 90–100
> - **Excellent:** 85–89.99
> - **Very Good:** 80–84.99 (Specialty threshold)
> - **Good:** 75–79.99
> - **Commercial:** 60–74.99
> - **Off-grade:** < 60

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture content (wet basis) | $MC_w = \frac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | Drying control | % |
| Moisture content (dry basis) | $MC_d = \frac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | Storage stability | % |
| Inulin content | $\%I = \frac{m_{inulin}}{m_{dry \, root}} \times 100$ | Chicory quality | % |
| Extraction yield | $Y_e = \frac{m_{soluble}}{m_{bean}} \times 100$ | Soluble coffee yield | % |
| Screen size conversion | $D_{mm} = \frac{S}{64} \times 25.4$ | Bean size grading | mm |
| Roast weight loss | $L_r = \frac{m_{green} - m_{roast}}{m_{green}} \times 100$ | Roast efficiency | % |
| Cupping score | $S_{cup} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i$ | Final quality grade | points |
| Defect score | $D_{score} = \sum p_j \cdot 1 + \sum q_k \cdot \frac{1}{2}$ | Green bean grading | units |

> [!NOTE]
> In the defect score formula, $p_j$ = number of primary defects (each counts as 1 full defect), and $q_k$ = number of secondary defects (each counts as half a defect). A specialty-grade coffee must have a defect score of **0**.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Domain |
|---|---|
| Spray drying and agglomeration | Food engineering, instant beverage production |
| Optical sorting | Machine vision, AI-driven quality control |
| Cupping score digitization | Sensory science, chemometrics, machine learning |
| Maillard reaction control | Flavor chemistry, kinetics modeling |
| Prebiotic inulin extraction | Nutraceuticals, functional food industry |
| Adulteration detection (HPLC of inulin markers) | Food safety and regulatory compliance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Screen Size to Diameter Conversion

In international coffee trade, screen size is reported in **1/64 inch increments** (legacy US grading unit). To convert to millimeter diameter for engineering design purposes (e.g., sieve aperture selection), the following derivation applies.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship.**
A "Screen 17" bean passes through a round hole of $17/64$ inch diameter.

$$
D_{in} = \frac{S}{64}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert inches to millimeters.**
$1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$

$$
D_{mm} = D_{in} \times 25.4
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
D_{mm} = \frac{S}{64} \times 25.4 = S \times \frac{25.4}{64} = S \times 0.396875
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final compact form.**

$$
\boxed{D_{mm} = 0.396875 \cdot S}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical verification.**
For Screen 17:

$$
D_{mm} = 0.396875 \times 17 = 6.747 \text{ mm}
$$

For Screen 15 (A grade lower limit):

$$
D_{mm} = 0.396875 \times 15 = 5.953 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering implication:** A vibratory sieve design for separating AA-grade coffee (S = 17) from B-grade (S = 12) must accommodate apertures of approximately 6.75 mm and 4.76 mm respectively, with a tolerance band of $\pm 0.1$ mm to account for bean shrinkage during drying.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Defect Score for Green Coffee

The Coffee Board of India (CBI) and ICO use a weighted defect count.

**Step 1 — Classify defects.**
- **Primary defects** (fatal): 1 primary = 1 full defect
- **Secondary defects** (non-fatal): 1 secondary = 0.5 defect

**Step 2 — Define the count variables.**
- $n_p$ = number of primary defects in 350 g sample
- $n_s$ = number of secondary defects in 350 g sample

**Step 3 — Write the weighted score.**

$$
D_{score} = n_p \times 1 + n_s \times 0.5
$$

$$
D_{score} = n_p + 0.5 \, n_s
$$

**Step 4 — Example computation.**
Suppose a 350 g sample contains $n_p = 1$ black bean and $n_s = 4$ broken beans.

$$
D_{score} = 1 + 0.5 \times 4 = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

Since 3 > 0, this sample is **not specialty grade** (which requires $D_{score} = 0$).

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation for specialty threshold.**

$$
D_{score} = 0 \implies n_p = 0 \text{ and } n_s = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** A specialty-grade processing line must achieve **zero primary defects** at the optical sorter — this drives the design specification for high-resolution chromatic sorters (≥ 2 MP cameras, multi-spectral LED illumination, ejection accuracy ≥ 99.5 %).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Roast Weight Loss Calculation

**Step 1 — Define variables.**
- $m_g$ = mass of green coffee before roast (g)
- $m_r$ = mass of roasted coffee after quench (g)
- $L_r$ = roast weight loss (decimal fraction)

**Step 2 — Mass balance.**

$$
m_g = m_r + m_{water} + m_{volatiles}
$$

**Step 3 — Define weight loss as mass fraction lost.**

$$
L_r = \frac{m_g - m_r}{m_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for roasted mass.**

$$
m_r = m_g (1 - L_r)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
A 1 kg (1000 g) green coffee batch is roasted to a medium roast (18 % weight loss).

$$
m_r = 1000 \times (1 - 0.18) = 1000 \times 0.82 = 820 \text{ g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical roast weight loss spans:
> - **Light roast:** 12–14 %
> - **Medium roast:** 15–18 %
> - **Dark roast:** 19–22 %
> - **Very dark / Italian:** 23–25 %

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Inulin Conversion to Fructose Upon Roasting

Inulin is a fructo-oligosaccharide. During roasting with heat and residual moisture, partial acid-hydrolysis occurs.

**Step 1 — General hydrolysis.**

$$
\text{Inulin} + (n-1) H_2O \xrightarrow{\Delta, H^+} n \, Fructose + 1 \, Glucose
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric mass balance.**
If $M_{inulin}$ is the molecular weight of an inulin chain with $n$ fructose units and 1 terminal glucose, the theoretical maximum fructose yield is:

$$
m_{fructose} = M_{inulin} \times \frac{n \times 180}{M_{inulin} + 18(n-1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate for high DP.**
For $n \geq 10$, the bound water is negligible:

$$
m_{fructose} \approx \frac{n \times 180}{n \times 162 + 180} \times m_{inulin}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
100 g of chicory inulin (DP = 12, M = 12 × 162 + 180 = 2124 g/mol) yields:

$$
m_{fructose} = \frac{12 \times 180}{2124} \times 100 = \frac{2160}{2124} \times 100 \approx 101.7 \text{ g}
$$

(Theoretical yield > 100 % reflects the addition of water during hydrolysis — a small overestimate, as the 18(n−1) g of bound water added in hydrolysis cannot exceed the system's moisture content. In practice, yield is 90–95 %.)

**Step 5 — Practical engineering note.**
Only 40–60 % of inulin is converted to free fructose under typical chicory roasting conditions (170–200 °C, 15–20 min). The remainder is partially hydrolyzed oligofructose (DP 2–10), which contributes to body and mouthfeel of the brew.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Coffee vs Chicory at a Glance

| Attribute | Coffee (Coffea arabica/canephora) | Chicory (Cichorium intybus) |
|---|---|---|
| Family | Rubiaceae | Asteraceae |
| Plant part used | Seed (bean) | Root |
| Major carbohydrate | Polysaccharides (hemicellulose, galactomannans) | Inulin (fructan) |
| Major lipid | Coffee oil (10–15 %), diterpenes (kahweol, cafestol) | < 1.5 % |
| Major protein | 11–13 % | 5–7 % |
| Caffeine | 1.0–1.5 % (arabica); 2.0–3.0 % (robusta) | 0 % (absent) |
| Major bitter principle | Caffeine + chlorogenic acid | Lactucin, lactucopicrin |
| Major phenolic | Chlorogenic acid (5–12 %) | Chicoric acid, chlorogenic acid |
| Roast color | Brown (light to dark) | Deep brown (inulin → caramel) |
| Acidity in cup | High (pH 4.8–5.2) | Low (pH 5.5–6.2) |
| Body | Medium to high | Medium |
| Prebiotic effect | None | Strong (inulin) |
| Typical use | Pure coffee | Coffee substitute, extender, functional food |

---

### 3.6 Process Flow: From Raw Material to Soluble Powder (Analytical Walkthrough)

The following analytical walkthrough describes the end-to-end processing pipeline shared (with modifications) by both coffee and chicory for instant powder production.

**Step 1 — Raw material intake.**  
Green coffee (12 % moisture) or dry chicory slices (12 % moisture) arrive in silos with moisture verification.

**Step 2 — Cleaning.**  
Magnetic separator removes ferrous metal; destoner removes stones; air-aspiration removes light chaff.

**Step 3 — Roasting (Batch / Continuous).**  
Drum roaster or fluidized bed roaster brings material to 180–220 °C internal bean temperature. Time-temperature profile is logged for batch traceability.

**Step 4 — Quenching and Cooling.**  
Water spray or air quench drops temperature to < 50 °C within 90 seconds.

**Step 5 — Grinding.**  
Roller mill or hammer mill reduces particle size to 200–500 μm, ensuring uniform extraction kinetics.

**Step 6 — Extraction.**  
Hot water (175–195 °C, 15–25 bar in some systems) percolates through a battery of 6–8 columns. Soluble yield is 18–24 % for coffee; 55–65 % for chicory (due to high inulin solubility).

**Step 7 — Concentration.**  
Multi-effect evaporator raises solids from 15–25 % to 40–60 % using steam economizers (3-effect, 4-effect systems common).

**Step 8 — Drying.**  
Spray dryer (inlet 200–250 °C, outlet 80–110 °C) yields free-flowing powder. Agglomeration or freeze-drying may follow for premium products.

**Step 9 — Quality Check.**  
Moisture (< 4 %), bulk density, particle size, color (L*a*b*), and sensory evaluation are recorded.

**Step 10 — Packaging.**  
Nitrogen-flushed, vacuum-sealed, or glass-jar packaging to prevent oxidation and moisture pickup.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 (CO3, Understand) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Explain the chemistry of chicory root and justify why it is used as a coffee substitute.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Chicory root (*Cichorium intybus* L., family Asteraceae) contains **inulin (40–50 % of dry root)**, a polydisperse fructo-polysaccharide with degree of polymerization 2–60 and $\beta(2 \rightarrow 1)$ glycosidic linkages. Upon roasting at 170–200 °C, inulin undergoes **partial hydrolysis** to yield **fructose and oligofructose**, which participate in **Maillard reactions** with amino acids to produce **melanoidins, furfural, hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), and pyrazines** — compounds that impart a coffee-like brown color, caramel aroma, and roasted body.

Additionally, chicory contains **sesquiterpene lactones (lactucin, lactucopicrin)** that contribute bitterness, and **phenolic acids (chicoric acid, chlorogenic acid)** that provide antioxidant activity. Since it is **caffeine-free**, **prebiotic** (inulin promotes *Bifidobacterium* growth), and produces a **dark, aromatic, full-bodied brew** upon roasting and extraction, chicory is widely blended with coffee (typical ratios 30:70, 50:50, 70:30 chicory:coffee) as a **caffeine-free coffee substitute and extender** in products such as Indian "café chicory" blends and European post-meal beverages. (3 marks)

---

**Question 2 (CO3, Remember) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> List any six primary defects considered in green coffee grading.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six primary defects recognized by the Coffee Board of India (CBI) and ICO for green coffee grading are:

1. **Black bean** — bean turned black due to over-fermentation or fungal infection.
2. **Sour bean** — bean with fermented, vinegar-like odor from improper processing.
3. **Pod/Cherry** — dried, unbroken coffee fruit with bean still inside.
4. **Large stone** — stone > 1 cm that may damage milling equipment.
5. **Medium stone** — stone 0.5–1 cm.
6. **Large stick** — woody fragment > 1 cm. (3 marks — 0.5 marks each)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Question A (Module 3, CO3):**

> **(a)** Describe in detail the major equipment involved in primary and secondary processing of coffee, with a focus on the roasting and extraction stages. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the chemistry of chicory with emphasis on inulin, sesquiterpene lactones, and Maillard reaction products formed during roasting. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution A(a) — Equipment in Coffee Processing (7 marks)

Coffee processing equipment is divided into **primary (cherry-to-green-bean)** and **secondary (green-bean-to-instant-powder)** categories.

**Primary Processing (4 marks):**

1. **Pulper (1 mark):** Mechanical device using rotating discs/drums to remove the outer skin (exocarp) of ripe coffee cherries. Throughput: 1–5 tonnes/hour for industrial pulpers. Critical to avoid bean damage (latent defect risk).

2. **Fermentation tank (1 mark):** Stainless-steel or concrete tanks where mucilage is degraded by pectinolytic microorganisms in 12–36 hours. Temperature must be controlled (18–25 °C) to prevent over-fermentation (sour beans).

3. **Washer / mucilage remover (0.5 mark):** Mechanical washers use water jets to remove degraded mucilage; mechanical demucilagers use friction.

4. **Mechanical dryer / sun-drying yard (0.5 mark):** Reduces moisture from ~55 % to 10–12 %. Guardiolas (rotary drum dryers) use hot air at 40–50 °C; sun drying takes 7–14 days on raised African beds.

5. **Huller and polisher (0.5 mark):** Removes parchment (endocarp) and silverskin (perisperm) from dried beans. Improves appearance, reduces chaff during roasting.

6. **Size and color sorter (0.5 mark):** Vibratory sieves classify by screen size (AA = 17–18, A = 15–16, B = 12–14); electronic optical color sorters eject black, sour, and insect-damaged beans using multi-spectral cameras.

**Secondary Processing (3 marks):**

7. **Roaster (1.5 marks):**
   - **Drum roaster:** Horizontal rotating drum, externally heated. Residence time 8–20 min. Profile control via time-temperature curves.
   - **Fluidized-bed (hot-air) roaster:** Faster (3–5 min), uniform heat transfer, brighter cup profile.
   - **Stages:** Drying → Maillard (140–165 °C) → First Crack (≈196 °C) → Caramelization (170–200 °C) → Second Crack (≈224 °C) → Quench.

8. **Grinder (0.5 mark):** Burr grinder (conical/flat) for uniform particle size (200–500 μm for filter coffee; 200–300 μm for espresso).

9. **Extraction column (0.5 mark):** Percolation battery of 6–8 columns, hot water at 175–195 °C, pressure up to 25 bar. Soluble yield 18–24 %.

10. **Evaporator and spray dryer (0.5 mark):** Multi-effect evaporator concentrates brew to 40–60 % solids; spray dryer atomizes into hot air (200–250 °C inlet) yielding free-flowing instant coffee powder. Freeze-drying used for premium products.

---

#### Model Solution A(b) — Chemistry of Chicory (7 marks)

**1. Inulin (2.5 marks):**
Inulin is the **signature carbohydrate** of chicory root, comprising 40–50 % of the dry root mass. It is a **polydisperse fructan** with the general formula $G_{1}F_n$ — a terminal glucose unit followed by a chain of $n$ fructose units joined by $\beta(2 \rightarrow 1)$ glycosidic bonds. The degree of polymerization (DP) ranges from 2 to 60, with an average DP of ~12. Inulin is **not digested by human enzymes** but is fermented by colonic *Bifidobacterium* and *Lactobacillus* species, conferring a strong **prebiotic effect**. During roasting, inulin undergoes **partial acid hydrolysis** to yield **fructose and oligofructose (DP 2–10)**, which serve as Maillard substrates.

**2. Sesquiterpene Lactones (2 marks):**
Chicory contains bitter **sesquiterpene lactones**, primarily **lactucin**, **lactucopicrin (intybin)**, and **8-deoxylactucin**, at concentrations of 0.1–0.5 % of fresh weight. These compounds exhibit a low bitterness threshold (μg/L range) and contribute to the characteristic bitter note. They also possess reported **anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, and sedative** pharmacological activities. Roasting partially decomposes them, reducing bitterness by 30–50 %.

**3. Maillard Reaction and Caramelization (2.5 marks):**
During roasting at 170–200 °C, fructose (from inulin hydrolysis) and amino acids (from root protein) undergo **non-enzymatic browning via the Maillard reaction**, producing:
- **Melanoidins** (high-molecular-weight brown polymers) — responsible for deep brown color and antioxidant activity.
- **Furfural and Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF)** — sweet, caramel-like aroma; HMF is an indicator of overheating.
- **Pyrazines** (alkyl pyrazines) — nutty, roasty, earthy aroma.
- **Diacetyl, acetol, furaneol** — buttery, sweet notes.

Simultaneously, **caramelization** of fructose (without amino acid) at 170–200 °C yields additional aroma compounds. The combined Maillard-caramelization cascade is responsible for chicory's coffee-like **color, aroma, and body**, making it a functional and economic coffee substitute. Total color formation (L*a*b* darkening) increases with roasting time, plateauing after 15–20 minutes at 190 °C.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Question B (Module 3, CO3):**

> **(a)** Discuss the physical and sensory quality grading of coffee, including the SCA cupping protocol and green bean classification by the Coffee Board of India. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the end-to-end processing of chicory from root to instant powder, highlighting the chemistry at each thermal stage. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution B(a) — Coffee Quality Grading (7 marks)

**1. Green Bean Physical Grading (3.5 marks):**
The Coffee Board of India (CBI) and International Coffee Organization (ICO) classify green coffee by:

- **Defect count (per 350 g sample):**
  - **Primary defects** (fatal): black bean, sour bean, pod/cherry, large stone, medium stone, large stick — each = 1 full defect.
  - **Secondary defects** (non-fatal): partial black, partial sour, parchment, broken/chipped, immature, withered, shell, water damage, insect damage — each = 0.5 defect.
  - **Specialty grade:** $D_{score} = 0$ (zero primary, ≤ 5 secondary).
  - **Premium grade:** $D_{score} \leq 5$ full defects.
  - **Below premium / Commercial:** $D_{score} = 6$–$15$.
- **Moisture content:** 10–12 % (maximum permissible 12.5 %).
- **Screen size:** AA (17–18), A (15–16), B (12–14), PB (peaberry).
- **Color:** Uniform bluish-green for premium; pale/bleached indicates over-drying or age.

**2. Roast Grading (1 mark):**
- **Quaker count** (unripe beans that fail to roast) ≤ 3 for premium.
- **Color uniformity** measured on Agtron scale (95 = very light, 25 = very dark).
- **Surface defects:** scorched, tipped, or unevenly roasted beans.

**3. SCA Cupping Protocol — Sensory Grading (2.5 marks):**
The Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) protocol scores 10 attributes on a 1–10 scale (increments of 0.25):

1. Fragrance/Aroma
2. Flavor
3. Aftertaste
4. Acidity
5. Body
6. Balance
7. Uniformity (across 5 cups)
8. Clean Cup
9. Sweetness
10. Overall Impression

$$
S_{cup} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i
$$

**Classification (CBI/SCA aligned):**
- **Specialty:** $\geq 80$ points
- **Premium:** 70–79.99
- **Commercial:** 60–69.99
- **Off-grade:** < 60

A coffee must pass **all three** tests (physical + roast + cupping) to be certified specialty grade.

---

#### Model Solution B(b) — Chicory Processing Block Diagram and Chemistry (7 marks)

**Block Diagram of Chicory Processing:**

```
Fresh Root (75% moisture)
   → Washing (rotary drum)
   → Slicing (disc slicer, 3–5 mm cubes)
   → Belt Drying (45–60 °C, down to 12% moisture)
   → Roasting (drum roaster, 170–200 °C, 15–20 min)
       │
       ├── Inulin Hydrolysis → Fructose + Oligofructose
       ├── Maillard Reaction → Melanoidins + Pyrazines
       ├── Caramelization → Furfural + HMF
       └── Sesquiterpene Decomposition → Reduced bitterness
   → Quenching (air cool to < 50 °C)
   → Grinding (hammer mill, < 500 μm)
   → Diffusion Extraction (hot water, 80–95 °C)
   → Multi-Effect Evaporation (concentrate to 50–60 % solids)
   → Spray Drying (inlet 200 °C, outlet 90 °C)
   → Agglomeration (optional, for instant chicory)
   → Quality Grading (color, moisture, sensory)
   → Nitrogen-flushed Packaging
```

**Stage-wise Chemistry (5 marks):**

1. **Drying stage (1 mark):** Free water is removed; cell wall pectin partially degrades; concentration of inulin rises in the dry matrix.

2. **Roasting — Inulin hydrolysis (1.5 marks):** At 170–200 °C, residual moisture and acidic cell contents (e.g., citric, malic acid) catalyze partial hydrolysis of inulin:

$$
\text{Inulin (DP 12)} \xrightarrow{\Delta, H^+} \text{Fructose} + \text{Oligofructose (DP 2-10)}
$$

About 40–60 % of inulin is converted to free fructose and shorter oligofructoses; these become reactive Maillard substrates.

3. **Maillard reaction (1.5 marks):** Fructose reacts with amino acids (from 5–7 % root protein) to form **glycosylamines → Amadori compounds → melanoidins**, with concurrent release of **pyrazines, furans, and oxazoles** responsible for roasted aroma.

4. **Caramelization (1 mark):** Fructose itself undergoes thermal degradation at 170–200 °C to yield **HMF, furfural, levulinic acid, and formic acid** — adding caramel and sweet notes.

5. **Sesquiterpene degradation (1 mark):** Lactucin and lactucopicrin partially decompose, reducing bitterness by 30–50 % and balancing the cup profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The drying-to-roasting temperature ramp must be carefully controlled. Inulin begins to caramelize at 170 °C, and over-roasting (> 200 °C for > 25 min) generates excessive HMF (> 100 mg/kg), which is both a quality defect and a regulatory concern in the EU.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing inulin with starch:** Students often describe inulin as a "starch-like polysaccharide." Inulin is a **fructan**, not a starch. This costs full 1-mark credit in 3-mark questions.
>
> 2. **Mixing up "primary" and "secondary" defects:** Always state both the defect name AND its category (primary = fatal, secondary = non-fatal). Vague listing without classification costs 0.5 mark per item.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the SCA numerical threshold:** A common error is to write "specialty grade = high quality" without citing the **≥ 80 score** and **0 primary defects** criteria. KTU examiners explicitly look for numerical benchmarks.
>
> 4. **Omitting chemistry equations:** For chicory, students often narrate the roasting process but fail to write the hydrolysis reaction:

$$
\text{Inulin} + H_2O \rightarrow \text{Fructose} + \text{Glucose}
$$

> Always include balanced chemical equations in 7-mark questions.
>
> 5. **Forgetting equipment specifications:** "Roaster" alone is insufficient. State **type** (drum / fluidized bed), **temperature range** (180–240 °C), and **residence time** (8–20 min). Generic answers lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Confusing cupping attributes:** The 10 SCA attributes are **not** the same as the "4 Cs" of diamond grading. Memorize them in correct order: Fragrance, Flavor, Aftertaste, Acidity, Body, Balance, Uniformity, Clean Cup, Sweetness, Overall.
>
> 7. **Not drawing a block diagram in 7-mark equipment questions:** KTU valuation key allocates **0.5–1 mark** for a labeled block diagram. A textual-only answer forfeits this even if the content is correct.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

#### Equipment Quick-Recall
- **Coffee cherry → green bean:** Pulper → Fermenter → Washer → Dryer (solar/Guardiola) → Huller → Polisher → Grader (size + color).
- **Green bean → instant powder:** Roaster (drum / fluidized bed) → Quencher → Grinder (burr / roller) → Extractor (percolation column) → Evaporator (multi-effect) → Spray dryer / Freeze dryer → Agglomerator → Packager.
- **Chicory root → instant powder:** Washer → Slicer → Belt dryer → Roaster (170–200 °C) → Grinder → Diffusion extractor → Evaporator → Spray dryer → Packager.

#### Chicory Chemistry — Key Facts
- Inulin: **40–50 % of dry root**, **fructan**, **DP 2–60**, **prebiotic**.
- Sesquiterpene lactones: **lactucin, lactucopicrin** — bitter, anti-inflammatory.
- Phenolics: **chicoric acid** (dominant), chlorogenic, caffeic.
- Roasting markers: **HMF, furfural, pyrazines, melanoidins**.
- **Caffeine-free**; low lipid; high soluble carbohydrate.

#### Coffee Quality Grading — Key Facts
- **Screen size:** 1/64 inch increments; AA ≥ 17, A = 15–16, B = 12–14.
- **Defect weight:** Primary × 1 + Secondary × 0.5 (per 350 g).
- **Specialty:** 0 primary defects, ≤ 5 secondary, ≥ 80 cupping score, 10–12 % moisture.
- **SCA 10 attributes:** Fragrance, Flavor, Aftertaste, Acidity, Body, Balance, Uniformity, Clean Cup, Sweetness, Overall.
- **Moisture:** 10–12 % green, < 5 % instant powder, < 4 % agglomerated premium.
- **Roast stages:** Drying → Maillard → First Crack (≈ 196 °C) → Caramelization → Second Crack (≈ 224 °C).

#### Critical Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize for KTU)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Specialty cupping threshold | ≥ 80 / 100 |
| Specialty defect score | 0 |
| Green bean moisture | 10–12 % |
| Inulin in dry chicory root | 40–50 % |
| First crack temperature | ≈ 196 °C |
| Second crack temperature | ≈ 224 °C |
| Roast weight loss (medium) | 15–18 % |
| Soluble yield of coffee | 18–24 % |
| Soluble yield of chicory | 55–65 % |
| Drying temperature (green coffee) | 40–50 °C |
| Roasting temperature range | 180–240 °C |

#### High-Yield One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision
1. Inulin is a **$\beta(2 \rightarrow 1)$ fructan**, not a starch.
2. Chicory has **zero caffeine**; coffee's caffeine is 1–1.5 % (arabica) or 2–3 % (robusta).
3. **Primary defects = 1 full defect each; secondary = 0.5 defect each.**
4. **SCA cupping = 10 attributes, each 1–10, sum ≥ 80 for specialty.**
5. **Maillard + caramelization = roasted chicory's coffee-like character.**
6. **Screen 17 ≈ 6.75 mm; Screen 15 ≈ 5.95 mm.**
7. **Quaker bean = unripe bean that fails to roast; ≤ 3 for premium.**
8. **Spray drying 200 °C inlet, 90 °C outlet**; freeze-drying preserves aromatics.
9. **Roast weight loss:** light 12–14 %, medium 15–18 %, dark 19–22 %.
10. **Chicoric acid is the dominant phenolic in chicory; chlorogenic acid dominates in coffee.**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Cocoa Bean — Botanical and Compositional Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** The cocoa bean is the fermented, dried seed of *Theobroma cacao* L. (family Malvaceae), which serves as the fundamental raw material for the production of cocoa liquor, cocoa butter, cocoa powder, and chocolate. The term "cocoa bean" technically refers to the seed *after* fermentation, drying, and roasting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin Note:** The name *Theobroma* literally translates from Greek as "food of the gods" (*theos* = god, *broma* = food). Cocoa beans were so revered in pre-Columbian Mesoamerican civilizations (Olmec, Maya, Aztec) that they were used as currency.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a cocoa pod as a small football containing 30–50 almond-shaped beans, each embedded in a sweet, white, mucilaginous pulp (like the flesh of a lychee). This pulp is rich in sugars (10–14% w/w), which is what allows natural fermentation to occur. Without this sugary "incubator pulp," the critical flavor development of chocolate would never happen. The bean itself is essentially a **storage organ** packed with fats, proteins, and polyphenols — but it remains bitter, astringent, and inedible until fermentation transforms it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Compositional Metrics of the Fermented, Dried Cocoa Bean (per 100 g dry weight):**
> - **Cocoa Butter (Lipids):** 50–55% — the prized fat fraction
> - **Proteins:** 10–15% — including storage albumins and globulins
> - **Starch:** 6–9% — polysaccharide energy reserve
> - **Polyphenols (Tannins):** 5–10% — flavan-3-ols, anthocyanins, catechins
> - **Cellulose & Hemicellulose:** 4–6% — structural fiber
> - **Theobromine:** 1–2% — methylxanthine alkaloid
> - **Caffeine:** 0.2–0.5% — secondary alkaloid
> - **Minerals (K, Mg, Ca, P, Fe):** 2–4% — total ash
> - **Water (post-drying):** ≤ 7% (mandatory for safe storage)
> - **pH:** 4.5–5.5 (acidified after fermentation)

### 1.2 Plantation Crops — Industrial Scope

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Plantation crops are high-value, perennial commercial crops cultivated extensively on large estates, primarily for export and industrial processing rather than direct fresh consumption. Coconut, tea, coffee, rubber, oil palm, cashew, and cocoa are the principal plantation crops relevant to food technology in the Indian and global context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coconut (Cocos nucifera L., family Arecaceae)** is the single most important plantation crop in the KTU food technology syllabus. Often called the "Tree of Life" (Kalpavriksha), every part of the coconut palm is industrially exploitable: water, kernel (copra), oil, shell, husk (coir), and leaves.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A coconut is essentially a **natural multi-product packaging unit**. The outer green/brown husk is the protective fiber layer (used for coir), the hard endocarp (shell) is the "container," the clear liquid (coconut water) is a sterile, isotonic beverage, and the white kernel is a high-fat raw material for oil extraction and desiccated products. Each layer is processed using a different technology, making coconut a model "waste-zero" industrial crop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Composition of Mature Coconut Kernel (per 100 g):**
> - **Moisture:** 45–50%
> - **Fat (Coconut Oil):** 35–40%
> - **Protein:** 3.5–4.0%
> - **Carbohydrates:** 10–12%
> - **Minerals:** 1.0–1.2%
> - **Crude Fiber:** 3.0–3.5%
