# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between the three fruit forms of oil palm (Dura, Pisifera, and Tenera) with reference to genotype, shell thickness, and mesocarp content.**

**Model Answer:**

| Character | Dura | Pisifera | Tenera |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Genotype** | *ss* (homozygous recessive) | *S¹S¹* (homozygous, lethal in pure form) | *S¹s* (heterozygous) |
| **Shell thickness** | 2–8 mm (thick) | 0 mm (shell-less) | 0.5–3 mm (thin) |
| **Mesocarp content** | 35–55% (low) | 90–95% (very high) | 70–80% (high) |
| **OER** | 16–18% | — | 22–25% |
| **Fertility** | Fertile | Female-sterile | Fertile |
| **Use** | Female parent | Male parent | Commercial cultivar |

Tenera is the only commercially cultivated form, produced by crossing Dura (♀) × Pisifera (♂).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the shell thickness of Pisifera as "thin" — it is actually **shell-less (zero)**. The thick shell is in Dura, and Tenera is thin-shelled. Marks are deducted for confusing this.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Briefly describe the role of the pollinating weevil *Elaeidobius kamerunicus* in oil palm cultivation.**

**Model Answer:**
- *Elaeidobius kamerunicus* is a small weevil (curculionid beetle) introduced from **Cameroon (West Africa) to Malaysia in 1981** by the Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI).
- It is the **most effective pollinator** of oil palm, having replaced costly hand-pollination.
- The weevil is attracted to the **strong anise-like (estragole) odour** emitted by the male inflorescence.
- After feeding on male flowers, it carries pollen to female inflorescences, thereby achieving cross-pollination.
- Introduction of this weevil increased the **fruit set rate from ~30% to >70%** in Asian plantations, raising yield dramatically.
- The weevil has since been introduced to India (Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu) for the same purpose.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mention only "pollen transfer" without specifying the **year (1981)** and the **country of origin (Cameroon)**. Both are expected in a 3-mark answer.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 pattern]

**Q: Discuss the botany, climate, and soil requirements of oil palm. Add a note on the varieties and propagation methods.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

#### (a) Botany (7 Marks)

- **Family:** Arecaceae (Palmae); Genus: *Elaeis*; Species: *E. guineensis*.
- **Plant type:** Monoecious, single-stemmed, unbranched palm tree; height 15–20 m; economic life 25–30 years.
- **Root system:** Fibrous, adventitious, radiating horizontally (6–10 m) and vertically (3–5 m). Dense feeder root mat in 0–60 cm topsoil.
- **Stem (Trunk):** Erect, columnar, woody, ringed with leaf-base scars. Bears a single apical meristem.
- **Leaves (Fronds):** Pinnate, 3–5 m long, 30–40 functional fronds per palm. Each frond has 100–160 pairs of leaflets. Annual leaf production: 20–24.
- **Inflorescence:** Monoecious — separate male and female spadices. Sex expression follows an **alternating cycle** (2–6 months). Male inflorescence is a spikelet-bearing spadix; female inflorescence is a central spike with lateral spikelets.
- **Pollination:** By *Elaeidobius kamerunicus* (weevil) or wind.
- **Fruit:** A **drupe** with three layers — **exocarp (skin), mesocarp (flesh, oil-rich), endocarp (shell)**. The kernel (endosperm) inside also yields oil.
- **Three fruit forms:** Dura (thick shell), Pisifera (shell-less), Tenera (thin shell, commercial).
- **Fruit bunch (FFB):** 10–25 kg; contains 1000–3000 fruits.

#### (b) Climate and Soil Requirements (4 Marks)

- **Climate:** Strictly tropical; optimum temperature 24–28°C; rainfall 1800–2300 mm/year evenly distributed; RH 75–85%; sunshine 5–6 hr/day; altitude 0–500 m.
- **Soil:** Deep (≥1.5 m), well-drained sandy loam to clay loam; pH 4.5–6.5; rich in organic carbon (>1.5%); high CEC (>15 cmol/kg). Avoid waterlogged, alkaline, and shallow soils.

#### (c) Varieties and Propagation (3 Marks)

- **Varieties:** Dura, Pisifera, Tenera (commercial). Improved hybrids — Calyx (Dami), CIRAD, MOPGC, and Indian IIOPR varieties (Godavari, Krishna, Gauthami).
- **Propagation:**
  - **By seed** (commercial): Dura (♀) × Pisifera (♂) → 100% Tenera. Heat-treated seeds (40°C, 60–80 days) for dormancy breaking. Two-stage nursery for 12–14 months.
  - **By tissue culture (somatic embryogenesis):** Clonal palms are feasible but suffer from **mantled fruit abnormality** in 5–10% of plants.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** In the **botany** section, students often miss the **single apical meristem** and the **monoecious** nature. In **propagation**, the year of heat treatment (40°C, 60–80 days) and the D × P cross producing 100% T are key value points.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 pattern]

**Q: Explain the cultural practices of oil palm with reference to planting, manuring, irrigation, and harvesting. Add a note on the extraction of palm oil and palm kernel oil.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

#### (a) Planting and Spacing (3 Marks)

- **Land preparation:** Deep ploughing, terracing on slopes, cover crops (*Calopogonium*, *Pueraria*).
- **Spacing:** **9 m × 9 m × 9 m (triangular)** accommodating **143 palms/ha**; alternatively 8 m × 9 m (138 palms/ha) or square 9 m × 9 m (123 palms/ha).
- **Pits:** 60 cm × 60 cm × 60 cm filled with topsoil + 5 kg FYM + 100 g rock phosphate.
- **Planting season:** Onset of monsoon (May–June).
- **Shade guard:** Required for the first 1–2 years.

#### (b) Manuring (3 Marks)

- Oil palm is a **heavy feeder**; mature palms require per palm/year: **N 1.0–1.5 kg, P₂O₅ 1.0–1.5 kg, K₂O 1.5–2.0 kg, MgO 0.2–0.3 kg, Borax 100–150 g**.
- **Application:** 4 split doses per year (quarterly) in a weeded circle around the palm, forked into soil.
- **Deficiency symptoms:** Orange fronds and bunch failure (N), orange spotting (Mg), hooked leaf tip (B), purple fronds (P).

#### (c) Irrigation (2 Marks)

- **Drip irrigation** is recommended in areas with seasonal drought.
- **Water requirement:** 120–150 mm/month; per palm per day 80–200 L depending on age.
- **Critical stage:** Bunch development (moisture stress → bunch failure syndrome, yield loss up to 50%).

#### (d) Harvesting (3 Marks)

- **Indicator:** 5–10 loose fruits (detached by gravity) on the ground beneath the bunch; or **fruit colour change** from black to orange-red.
- **Cycle:** Every 10–14 days (year-round harvesting).
- **Tools:** Chisel (*dodos*) attached to a 2–3 m pole; motorized *Malaysian sickle*.
- **Yield maturity:** Begins 3–4 years after field planting; peaks at 6–18 years; declines after 19 years; replanting at 25–30 years.
- **Yield:** 20–25 t FFB/ha/yr at peak.

#### (e) Extraction of CPO and PKO (3 Marks)

1. **Sterilization:** FFB steamed at 150 kPa for 90 min to deactivate lipase and loosen fruits.
2. **Threshing:** Fruits separated from empty fruit bunches (EFB).
3. **Digesting and Pressing:** Mesocarp digested, then pressed (hydraulic or screw press) to yield **CPO** (22–25% of FFB).
4. **Clarification:** CPO is clarified by centrifugation and vacuum drying.
5. **Nut cracking and separation:** Nuts cracked to separate shell (fuel) and kernel.
6. **Kernel crushing:** Kernels crushed to yield **PKO** (4–6% of FFB) and **palm kernel cake (PKC)** (animal feed, 16–18% protein).
7. **Refining:** CPO undergoes degumming, bleaching, and deodorizing (RBD) to produce **RBD palm olein** (cooking oil) and **RBD palm stearin** (vanaspati, margarine).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **omit the role of steam sterilization** in deactivating **lipase enzyme** (which otherwise causes free fatty acid build-up and oil rancidity). Marks are lost if this is not mentioned. Also, the **by-products (EFB, shell, fibre, PKC)** must be mentioned for full marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Oil Palm Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Dura and Tenera shell thickness** — Dura is thick (2–8 mm), Tenera is thin (0.5–3 mm). Pisifera is shell-less.
> 2. **Wrong sex ratio interpretation** — A "sex ratio of 0.5" means **50% female inflorescences** (ideal), not 50% male.
> 3. **Missing the year of weevil introduction (1981) and the country of origin (Cameroon)** in pollinator questions.
> 4. **Wrong triangular plant population calculation** — students often forget to divide by 3 (or use 0.866).
> 5. **Omitting by-products** in processing questions — EFB, PKC, POME, and shell are all expected in a 14-mark answer on processing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recap — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] **Botanical name:** *Elaeis guineensis* Jacq. — Family Arecaceae
- [x] **Origin:** West Africa (Guinea coast)
- [x] **Centre of diversity:** Tropical West and Central Africa
- [x] **Three fruit forms (Dura, Pisifera, Tenera)** and their genotypes (*ss, S¹S¹, S¹s*)
- [x] **Commercial cross:** Dura (♀) × Pisifera (♂) → 100% Tenera
- [x] **Monoecious palm**, sex cycle 2–6 months
- [x] **Pollinator:** *Elaeidobius kamerunicus* (introduced 1981 from Cameroon)
- [x] **Climate:** 24–28°C, 1800–2300 mm rainfall, <500 m altitude, 5–6 hr sunshine
- [x] **Soil:** Deep, well-drained, pH 4.5–6.5, OC > 1.5%, CEC > 15 cmol/kg
- [x] **Spacing:** 9 m × 9 m × 9 m triangular → **143 palms/ha**
- [x] **Pit size:** 60 cm × 60 cm × 60 cm
- [x] **Nursery duration:** 12–14 months total
- [x] **Economic life:** 25–30 years; juvenile 3–4 years; peak yield 6–18 years
- [x] **Mature fertilizer dose (per palm/yr):** N 1.0–1.5 kg, P₂O₅ 1.0–1.5 kg, K₂O 1.5–2.0 kg, MgO 0.2–0.3 kg, Borax 100–150 g
- [x] **Fertilizer splits:** 4 per year (quarterly)
- [x] **Irrigation:** Drip, 120–150 mm/month; 80–200 L/palm/day
- [x] **OER (Tenera):** 22–25% of FFB
- [x] **KER (Tenera):** 4–6% of FFB
- [x] **M/F ratio:** Tenera 70–80%, Dura 35–55%
- [x] **Kernel oil content:** 48–52% of kernel weight
- [x] **Bunch weight:** 10–25 kg, 1000–3000 fruits
- [x] **Harvesting cycle:** Every 10–14 days
- [x] **Harvesting indicator:** 5–10 loose fruits on the ground
- [x] **Yield:** 20–25 t FFB/ha/yr; CPO yield 4–6 t/ha/yr
- [x] **Processing steps:** Sterilization → Threshing → Digesting → Pressing → Clarification → RBD refining
- [x] **By-products:** EFB (boiler fuel/mulch), POME (biogas), shell (fuel), fibre (boards), PKC (feed)
- [x] **Major pest:** Rhinoceros beetle (*Oryctes rhinoceros*)
- [x] **Major disease:** Basal Stem Rot (*Ganoderma boninense*)
- [x] **IIOPR (India's nodal institute)** is at **Pedavegi, Andhra Pradesh**
- [x] **Mission:** National Mission on Oilseeds and Oil Palm (NMOOP) promotes cultivation in India
- [x] **India's top producing state:** Andhra Pradesh
- [x] **Global top producers:** Indonesia (~58%) and Malaysia (~25%)
- [x] **CPO is rich in:** β-carotene (500–700 ppm), vitamin E (tocopherols and tocotrienols), saturated palmitic acid (~44%)
- [x] **PKO is rich in:** Lauric acid (~48%) — used in soaps, detergents, and cosmetics

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Day Tip:** Always draw a labelled cross-section of a Tenera fruit (showing exocarp, mesocarp, shell, kernel) when answering processing or OER-related questions. This visual earns 2–3 easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arecanut
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arecanut

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Arecanut** (*Areca catechu* L.), commonly known as the **betel nut palm**, is an evergreen, monoecious, perennial plantation crop belonging to the family **Arecaceae** (Palm family). It is cultivated primarily for its seed/kernel, which has high socioeconomic, cultural, and pharmaceutical significance across South and Southeast Asia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Botanical Identity:**
> - **Scientific Name:** *Areca catechu* L.
> - **Family:** Arecaceae (Palmae)
> - **Chromosome Number:** 2n = 32
> - **Common Names:** Betel nut, Supari, Adakka (Malayalam), Adike (Kannada), Kua (Hindi)
> - **Type of Fruit:** Drupe (monospermic)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **tall, slender coconut tree's cousin, but slender and producing clusters of orange-yellow nuts instead of coconuts**. Picture the betel nut as the "energy pill of the East" — its kernel contains **tannins (14%)**, **alkaloids like arecoline (0.1–0.5%)**, and **polyphenols**, giving it a mild stimulating, mildly narcotic effect comparable to a very mild coffee. Just as coffee beans are roasted and ground, arecanuts are **harvested, dehusked, boiled/cured, and dried** into commercial forms. Kerala, Karnataka, and Assam together produce **over 80%** of India's arecanut.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Arecanut is treated as a *plantation crop* (along with coconut, cashew, rubber, tea, coffee, cocoa), distinguishing it from *spices*. The product forms—Chali, Chikni, Patora, Choorna, and scented supari—are essential from an **industry and trade perspective**.

## 1.3 Origin, History & Distribution

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Center of Origin** | Malaysia / Philippines / East Indies |
| **Cultivation History** | Over 2,000 years in India |
| **Major Producers (India)** | Karnataka (Dakshina Kannada, Shimoga) → ~55%, Kerala, Assam, Meghalaya, Tamil Nadu |
| **Global Producers** | India, Bangladesh, Indonesia, Myanmar, Sri Lanka, China |
| **World's Largest Producer** | **India** contributes ~50% of global production |

## 1.4 Composition of Arecanut Kernel

The composition explains its industrial value:

| Component | Content (%) |
|---|---|
| **Moisture** | 30–40 (fresh); 5–7 (cured) |
| **Carbohydrates (Starch)** | 35–47 |
| **Fat / Oil** | 8–14 |
| **Crude Fibre** | 8–11 |
| **Protein** | 6–9 |
| **Tannins** | 14–18 |
| **Alkaloids (Arecoline)** | 0.1–0.5 |
| **Polyphenols** | 8–12 |
| **Mineral Ash** | 1.5–2.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Arecoline** is the principal bio-active alkaloid, classified as a **parasympathomimetic** agent—responsible for the mild stimulating and sialogogue (salivation-promoting) effect.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Botanical Description

**Plant Architecture:**
- **Stem:** Solitary, erect, cylindrical, slender, **10–30 m height**, marked with ring-like leaf scars.
- **Crown:** Bears 8–12 large pinnate (feather-like) fronds, each **1.5–2.5 m long**.
- **Root System:** Adventitious roots forming a dense mat in the top **0.5–1.2 m** of soil.
- **Inflorescence:** Spadix (branched), enclosed in a spathe; both male and female flowers on the same spike (monoecious). Female flowers are basal; males are distal in much greater numbers (~300:1 ratio).
- **Fruit:** A smooth, ovoid **drupe** (5–7 cm long), bright orange to scarlet when ripe; monospermic (single seed).
- **Seed (Nut):** Conical, rounded at base; endosperm is the commercial portion.

## 2.2 Climate and Soil Requirements

| Parameter | Optimum Range |
|---|---|
| **Altitude** | 0–1000 m above MSL |
| **Temperature** | 14 °C – 36 °C (optimum **24–28 °C**) |
| **Rainfall** | **1500–3000 mm** (well-distributed) |
| **Relative Humidity** | 70–90% |
| **Soil Type** | Laterite / Red loam / Alluvial; well-drained |
| **Soil pH** | **4.5 – 6.5** (acidic to neutral) |
| **Slope** | Gentle to moderate slopes preferred for drainage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Arecanut is a **shade-loving** palm in the juvenile stage but requires **full sunlight** once it bears. Hence the practice of **intercropping with banana/pepper in early years** and raising **tall shade trees (silver oak, dadap)** for the initial 6–8 years.

## 2.3 Varieties (Released from CPCRI Kasaragod & Regional Research Stations)

| Variety | Released By | Special Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Mangala** | CPCRI Kasaragod (1972) | High-yielding, semi-tall, early-bearing, suitable for Kerala |
| **Sumangala** | CPCRI Kasaragod (1976) | Tall, vigorous, high yield (12.5 kg chali/palm/year) |
| **Sreemangala** | CPCRI Kasaragod (1985) | Tall, late-bearing, tolerant to drought |
| **Mohitnagar** | Mohitnagar (WB) | Suitable for West Bengal/Assam conditions |
| **SAS-1** | CPCRI Vittal | Tall, high-yielding |
| **VTLAH-1** (Mangala-2) | CPCRI Vittal (1995) | High-yielding, suitable for Karnataka |
| **VTLAH-2** | CPCRI Vittal (2005) | Drought-tolerant hybrid |
| **Hirehalli Dwarf** | Hirehalli, Karnataka | Dwarf, early-bearing |

## 2.4 Propagation Techniques

### A. Seed Propagation (Traditional, Most Common)

**Step 1 — Mother Palm Selection:**
- Age: 15–30 years
- High yield (>2.5 kg ripe nuts/year)
- Regular bearing
- Disease-free

**Step 2 — Seed Nut Selection:**
- Fully ripe, dehusked nuts weighing **>35 g**
- Round/ovoid shape preferred

**Step 3 — Pre-sowing Treatment:**
- Soak in water for **24–48 hours**
- Sow in polybags (24×18 cm) filled with 3:2:1 sand:soil:FYM
- Germination: **6 weeks – 3 months**
- Germination rate: ~80–85%

### B. Vegetative Propagation (Modern)

**Approach Grafting** (developed at CPCRI Kasaragod):
- Scion: 1-year-old seedling (10–12 cm, 4–5 leaves)
- Rootstock: 1–2-year-old seedling of same diameter
- Graft union: Tongue/cleft type
- Success rate: 60–80%
- Advantage: Clonal multiplication of elite palms; early bearing (3–4 years vs. 5–7 in seed propagation)

## 2.5 Planting Layout and Spacing

**Standard Systems:**

| System | Spacing | Plant Population / ha |
|---|---|---|
| Square | **2.7 m × 2.7 m** | 1370 |
| Triangular | 2.7 m × 2.7 m | 1580 |
| Hedge | 1.8 m × 1.8 m (high density) | 3086 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pits:** 90 cm × 90 cm × 90 cm; filled with topsoil + **5–10 kg FYM + 1 kg neem cake + 100 g rock phosphate**; planting preferably in **May–June** with the onset of southwest monsoon.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Seed Rate** | 4–5 kg/ha (with sprouts) | kg |
| **Spacing (square)** | 2.7 m × 2.7 m | m |
| **Pit Size** | 0.9 m × 0.9 m × 0.9 m | m |
| **Seedling age at planting** | 12–18 months | months |
| **FYM dose (adult palm)** | 10–20 kg / palm / year | kg |
| **N dose (adult palm)** | 100 g (increasing up to 1 kg by 5 yr) | g |
| **P₂O₅ dose** | 40–200 g | g |
| **K₂O dose** | 140–300 g | g |
| **Irrigation (drip)** | **30–40 L / palm / day** during dry season | L/day |
| **First bearing age** | 5–7 yr (seed) / 3–4 yr (grafted) | years |
| **Economic life** | 60–80 years | years |
| **Yield (mature palm)** | 8–12 kg chali / palm / year | kg |
| **Yield (fresh)** | 30–50 kg / palm / year | kg |
| **Kernel-to-fruit ratio** | ~25–30 % | % |
| **Curing shrink (dry basis)** | Fresh to chali: ~ 50–55 % moisture loss | % |
| **Harvesting interval** | 60–75 days | days |
| **Chali recovery from fresh** | ~ 30–35 % | % |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Calculation of Planting Density (Step-by-Step Derivation)

This is a **frequently asked quantitative problem** in KTU examinations. Let us derive it exhaustively.

**Problem:** A farmer wants to plant arecanut in a square system at a spacing of **2.7 m × 2.7 m**. Calculate the number of palms per hectare. Given that **1 hectare = 10,000 m²**.

**Step 1 — Area Allocated per Palm:**

In a square planting system, each palm occupies a square plot whose sides equal the spacing.

$$
A_{\text{palm}} = S \times S
$$

Substituting **S = 2.7 m**:

$$
A_{\text{palm}} = 2.7 \times 2.7
$$

$$
A_{\text{palm}} = 7.29 \;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Palms per Hectare:**

Total palms in 1 hectare = Total area of 1 ha ÷ Area per palm.

$$
N = \frac{10{,}000 \;\text{m}^{2}}{A_{\text{palm}}}
$$

$$
N = \frac{10{,}000}{7.29}
$$

$$
N \approx 1371.7
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 1372 \;\text{palms / ha}}
$$

**Step 3 — Triangular System Comparison:**

In a triangular (equilateral) system, the effective area per plant is reduced by a factor of $\sqrt{3}/2$ due to closer diagonal packing. The formula becomes:

$$
A_{\text{palm,tri}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times S^{2}
$$

$$
A_{\text{palm,tri}} = \frac{1.732}{2} \times (2.7)^{2}
$$

$$
A_{\text{palm,tri}} = 0.866 \times 7.29
$$

$$
A_{\text{palm,tri}} = 6.313 \;\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
N_{\text{tri}} = \frac{10{,}000}{6.313} \approx 1584 \;\text{palms / ha}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{tri}} \approx 1584 \;\text{palms / ha}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** Triangular system accommodates ~15.5 % more palms per hectare than square system at the same spacing, but is harder to lay out and inter-cultivate.

## 3.2 Yield Calculation from a Given Plantation Area

**Problem:** A 1-hectare arecanut garden (spacing 2.7 m × 2.7 m square system, 1372 palms) yields an average of **10 kg of cured chali per palm per year**. Calculate the total chali yield, fresh nut equivalent, and projected gross return at ₹250/kg chali.

**Step 1 — Total Chali Yield per Hectare per Year:**

$$
Y_{\text{chali}} = N \times Y_{\text{palm}}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{chali}} = 1372 \times 10
$$

$$
Y_{\text{chali}} = 13{,}720 \;\text{kg / ha / yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Fresh Nut Equivalent (using 33% recovery):**

$$
Y_{\text{fresh}} = \frac{Y_{\text{chali}}}{\text{Recovery factor}}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{fresh}} = \frac{13{,}720}{0.33}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{fresh}} \approx 41{,}576 \;\text{kg / ha / yr} \;\approx 41.6 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Gross Return:**

$$
G = Y_{\text{chali}} \times P
$$

$$
G = 13{,}720 \times 250
$$

$$
G = 34{,}30{,}000 \;\text{INR / ha / yr}
$$

$$
\boxed{G = \text{₹} 34.30 \;\text{lakh / ha / yr}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Arecanut vs. Other Plantation Crops (KTU Comparison Ready)

| Parameter | Arecanut | Coconut | Cashew | Rubber |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Family | Arecaceae | Arecaceae | Anacardiaceae | Euphorbiaceae |
| Spacing (m) | 2.7 × 2.7 | 7.5 × 7.5 | 7 × 7 | 5 × 5 |
| Plants/ha | 1372 | 177 | 204 | 400 |
| Bearing age | 5–7 yr | 6–8 yr | 3–5 yr | 5–7 yr |
| Economic life | 60–80 yr | 60–80 yr | 25–30 yr | 25–30 yr |
| Major product | Cured kernel | Copra | Nut | Latex |
| Prop. method | Seed (mostly) | Seed | Seed / grafts | Budded stumps |
| Pollination | Mostly cross-wind | Cross-insect | Insect | Wind |

## 3.4 Manure and Fertilizer Schedule (CPCRI Recommendation)

| Plant Age | N (g) | P₂O₅ (g) | K₂O (g) | FYM (kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 year | 100 | 40 | 140 | 5 |
| 2 years | 160 | 80 | 175 | 5 |
| 3 years | 240 | 120 | 210 | 10 |
| 4 years | 320 | 160 | 245 | 10 |
| **5 years and above (adult)** | **1000** | **200** | **300** | **20** |

**Frequency of Application:** Split into **two doses** — once during **February–March** (pre-monsoon) and once during **September–October** (post-monsoon), broadcast in a shallow trench 1.5 m from the trunk.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Arecanut Palm — Morphological Block Diagram

```

## 4.4 Arecanut Plantation Layout — Intercropping Scheme

    L1 --> M1["Arecanut palm"]
    M1 --> M2["Spacing 2.7 m by 2.7 m"]

    L2 --> N1["Silver Oak"]
    L2 --> N2["Dadap Erythrina"]
    L2 --> N3["Banana temporary shade"]

    L3 --> O1["Black pepper on arecanut trunk"]
    L3 --> O2["Cocoa as mixed crop"]
    L3 --> O3["Cardamom in hilly zones"]
    L3 --> O4["Banana in initial 3 years"]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Describe the major botanical features of the arecanut palm. **[3 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The arecanut palm (*Areca catechu* L., family Arecaceae) is an evergreen, monoecious palm exhibiting the following key botanical features:

1. **Stem:** Erect, slender, cylindrical, unbranched, attaining a height of 10–30 m, with conspicuous annular leaf scars.
2. **Roots:** Adventitious, forming a dense fibrous mat in the topsoil up to a depth of 1.2 m.
3. **Leaves:** Pinnate (compound, feather-shaped), 1.5–2.5 m long, clustered at the apex; each palm bears 8–12 functional fronds.
4. **Inflorescence:** A branched spadix enclosed by a spathe; carries both male (numerous, distal) and female (few, basal) flowers.
5. **Fruit:** A smooth, ovoid, orange-to-scarlet **drupe**, 5–7 cm long, monospermic (containing a single seed/kernel — the commercial product).
6. **Chromosome Number:** 2n = 32.

---

### Question 2: List the recommended varieties of arecanut released by CPCRI, Kasaragod. **[3 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

The major varieties of arecanut released by **CPCRI Kasaragod** (Central Plantation Crops Research Institute) are:

1. **Mangala** (1972) — Semi-tall, early-bearing, high-yielding, suitable for Kerala.
2. **Sumangala** (1976) — Tall, vigorous, very high yield (12–14 kg chali/palm/year).
3. **Sreemangala** (1985) — Tall, drought-tolerant, suitable for Karnataka.
4. **VTLAH-1 / Mangala-2** (1995) — High-yielding hybrid from CPCRI Vittal centre.
5. **VTLAH-2** (2005) — Drought-tolerant hybrid.

Other varieties include **Mohitnagar** (from West Bengal) and **Hirehalli Dwarf** (from Karnataka).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[14 Marks]**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the climatic and soil requirements for successful cultivation of arecanut. Add a note on the recommended varieties. **[7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Climatic Requirements:**

Arecanut is essentially a **tropical humid crop**, growing best in the warm, humid lowlands of South and Southeast Asia.

- **Temperature:** Thrives between **14–36 °C**, with optimum being **24–28 °C**. Frost is lethal; prolonged exposure to <10 °C arrests growth.
- **Rainfall:** Requires **1500–3000 mm** of well-distributed annual rainfall. A short dry spell (2–3 months) is tolerated by mature palms but is harmful to seedlings.
- **Humidity:** High relative humidity of **70–90 %** favours lush vegetative growth.
- **Altitude:** Performs well from sea level up to **1000 m MSL**, especially on hill slopes.
- **Sunlight:** Young palms require partial shade; bearing palms need **full sunlight** for proper nut development.
- **Wind:** Strong cyclonic winds damage fronds; hence **shade tree belts** are essential.

**(ii) Soil Requirements:**

- **Type:** Laterite, red loam, or alluvial soils rich in organic matter and potash; well-drained.
- **pH:** Acidic to neutral, ideally **pH 4.5 – 6.5**.
- **Drainage:** Stagnant water is highly injurious — it predisposes the palm to *foot rot* and *bud rot*.
- **Slope:** Gentle to moderate slopes (5–15 %) are preferred for natural drainage.

**(iii) Recommended Varieties:**

| Variety | Released | Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Mangala | 1972 | High yield, semi-tall |
| Sumangala | 1976 | Very high yield, tall |
| Sreemangala | 1985 | Drought-tolerant |
| VTLAH-1 | 1995 | High yield hybrid |
| VTLAH-2 | 2005 | Drought-tolerant hybrid |

---

#### (b) Describe the propagation, planting, and aftercare practices in arecanut cultivation. **[7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Propagation:**

Arecanut is propagated mainly by **seeds** and, to a limited extent, by **vegetative means (approach grafting)**.

- **Seed propagation:** Select fully ripe nuts from healthy, high-yielding (15–30 yr-old) mother palms. Dehusk, soak in water for 24–48 h, and sow in polybags (24 × 18 cm) filled with a 3:2:1 mixture of sand:soil:FYM. Germination occurs in **6–12 weeks**. Seedlings are ready for transplanting at **12–18 months**, when they bear 4–5 leaves.
- **Vegetative propagation (Approach Grafting):** A 1-year-old seedling is grafted onto a same-diameter rootstock to clone elite palms. Success rate: 60–80 %. Advantage: early bearing (3–4 yr vs. 5–7 yr in seedlings) and uniform performance.

**(ii) Planting:**

- Pits of **0.9 m × 0.9 m × 0.9 m** are dug 2–3 weeks before planting.
- Pits are filled with topsoil + **5–10 kg FYM + 1 kg neem cake + 100 g rock phosphate**.
- Spacing: **2.7 m × 2.7 m** in square system (~1372 palms/ha).
- Best time: **May–June** (onset of southwest monsoon).
- Shade regulation: Plant tall shade trees (**silver oak, dadap**) at 6–8 m spacing.

**(iii) Aftercare:**

- **Weeding:** 3–4 rounds/year; mulching with dried leaves conserves moisture.
- **Irrigation:** Drip irrigation at **30–40 L/palm/day** during dry months (December–May).
- **Manuring (5-yr+ adult palm):** 1 kg N + 200 g P₂O₅ + 300 g K₂O + 20 kg FYM, split into two doses (Feb–Mar and Sep–Oct).
- **Intercropping:** Banana in first 3 years; black pepper as a mixed crop on arecanut trunks; cocoa in humid zones.
- **Plant Protection:** Regular monitoring for *spindle bug*, *Koleroga*, and *foot rot*.
- **Earthing up:** Done before monsoon to prevent waterlogging near the bole.

---

### Question B: **[14 Marks]**

#### (a) Explain the harvesting, post-harvest processing, and various commercial forms of arecanut. **[7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Harvesting:**

- Arecanut begins bearing at **5–7 years** (seedlings) or **3–4 years** (grafted plants).
- Harvesting is done when **1–2 nuts at the base of the bunch turn yellow-orange**, while the rest are still green — this ensures the best quality and curing recovery.
- Bunches are cut using a curved sickle fixed on a long pole, harvested at **60–75 day intervals** (4–5 rounds/year).
- A mature palm yields **30–50 kg fresh nuts/year** (~ 8–12 kg cured chali).

**(ii) Post-Harvest Processing:**

1. **Dehusking:** Ripe nuts are dehusked manually or using a hand-operated dehusker.
2. **Boiling:** Nuts are boiled in water (or in copper vessels with 0.1 % potassium permanganate for red-coloured supari) for **1–1.5 hours** to soften the kernel and gelatinize the starch.
3. **Drying:** Boiled nuts are sun-dried for **35–45 days** until the kernel becomes hard, wrinkled, and rattling inside. Final moisture: **5–7 %**.
4. **Grading:** Based on size, colour, and appearance.

**(iii) Commercial Forms of Arecanut:**

| Form | Description |
|---|---|
| **Chali (Kali)** | Whole dried, unpolished, brown-black kernel — the most common form |
| **Chikni (Kalipak)** | Boiled in dilute *chuna* solution, polished, glazed, white-supari |
| **Patora** | Chali cut into long strips and flavoured |
| **Choorna** | Powdered chali, often flavoured with spices |
| **Scented Supari** | Chali + flavours (menthol, cardamom, cloves) + binding agents |
| **Gutkha / Pan Masala** | Chali powder + tobacco + lime + catechu + flavourings |

---

#### (b) Describe the major diseases and pests of arecanut along with their management. **[7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Major Diseases:**

| Disease | Causal Organism | Symptoms | Management |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yellow Leaf Disease (Koleroga)** | *Phytoplasma* transmitted by *Proutista moesta* (planthopper) | Progressive yellowing, necrosis of fronds, decline of yield | Spray **Bordeaux mixture 1 %**; control vector using *phorate*; rogue out affected palms |
| **Bud Rot** | *Phytophthora palmivora* | Withering of spear leaf, foul odour, rotting of bud | Cut and destroy infected tissue; spray Bordeaux mixture 1 %; improve drainage |
| **Foot Rot (Anabe Roga)** | *Ganoderma lucidum* | Bracket-shaped fungal fruiting bodies at the base; wilting | Drench base with **tridemorph 1 %** or **hexaconazole 0.2 %**; avoid water stagnation |
| **Stem Bleeding** | *Thielaviopsis paradoxa* | Reddish-brown liquid oozing from cracks | Apply **Bordeaux paste** on cleaned wound |
| **Inflorescence Die-back** | *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides* | Blackening of inflorescence axis | Spray **carbendazim 0.1 %** |

**(ii) Major Pests:**

| Pest | Scientific Name | Damage | Management |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spindle Bug** | *Carveria concinna* | Sucks sap from spindle leaf; necrotic streaks | Stem injection with **monocrotophos (1:4 dilution)**; neem cake application |
| **Root Grub** | *Leucopholis* sp. | Larvae feed on roots, causing yellowing and wilting | Drench soil with **chlorpyriphos 0.04 %**; apply *Metarhizium anisopliae* as biocontrol |
| **Mites** | *Oligonychus indicus* | Yellow speckling on fronds | Spray **dicofol 0.05 %** or neem oil 0.5 % |
| **Scale Insects** | *Aulacaspis tubercularis* | White scaly encrustations on leaf undersides | Spray **dimethoate 0.05 %**; release *Chilocorus* beetles |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) confusing *Koleroga* (phytoplasma) with *Bud Rot* (fungal — *Phytophthora*), and (b) omitting the **mode of transmission** of diseases (e.g., *Proutista moesta* for Koleroga, *Phytophthora* water-zoospores for Bud Rot). Always mention **causal organism + symptoms + management** as a triplet for full marks. Spelling errors in scientific names (*Ganoderma*, *Phytophthora*) also cost marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Family:** Arecaceae; **Scientific Name:** *Areca catechu* L.; **Chromosome:** 2n = 32.
- **Origin:** Malaysia/Philippines; **India leads** global production (~50 %).
- **Composition:** Tannins (14–18 %), Arecoline (0.1–0.5 %), Carbohydrates (35–47 %), Fat (8–14 %).
- **Optimum Climate:** Temp 24–28 °C, Rainfall 1500–3000 mm, Humidity 70–90 %, Altitude <1000 m.
- **Soil:** Laterite/Red loam, pH **4.5–6.5**, well-drained; **stagnation = death**.
- **Major Varieties:** Mangala, Sumangala, Sreemangala, Mohitnagar, VTLAH-1, VTLAH-2, Hirehalli Dwarf.
- **Propagation:** Mostly seed; elite clonal material via approach grafting (CPCRI).
- **Spacing:** **2.7 m × 2.7 m** (1372 palms/ha square; 1584 triangular).
- **Pit:** 90 cm × 90 cm × 90 cm; FYM + neem cake + rock phosphate.
- **Manure (adult):** N 1 kg, P 200 g, K 300 g, FYM 20 kg/palm/year (split dose).
- **Irrigation (drip):** 30–40 L/palm/day in dry months.
- **Intercrops:** Banana (initial), Black pepper (on trunk), Cocoa (humid zones).
- **Shade trees:** Silver Oak, Dadap (*Erythrina*) — for first 6–8 years.
- **First bearing:** 5–7 yr (seedlings), 3–4 yr (grafted).
- **Yield:** 30–50 kg fresh nuts or 8–12 kg chali/palm/year.
- **Recovery:** ~30–35 % chali from fresh nuts.
- **Harvesting:** When 1–2 basal nuts yellow; at 60–75 day intervals.
- **Processing:** Dehusk → Boil (1–1.5 hr) → Sun-dry (35–45 days) → Grade.
- **Commercial Forms:** **Chali (whole)**, **Chikni (polished)**, **Patora (strips)**, **Choorna (powder)**, **Scented supari**, **Gutkha**.
- **Diseases:** Koleroga (Phytoplasma), Bud Rot (*Phytophthora*), Foot Rot (*Ganoderma*), Stem Bleeding (*Thielaviopsis*).
- **Pests:** Spindle Bug (*Carveria concinna*), Root Grub (*Leucopholis*), Mites (*Oligonychus*), Scale (*Aulacaspis*).
- **Key Active Principle:** Arecoline — parasympathomimetic alkaloid.
- **Spacing Formula (must memorize):**

$$
N_{\text{square}} = \frac{10{,}000}{S^{2}} \quad ; \quad N_{\text{tri}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 2}{S^{2}\sqrt{3}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cashew - Harvesting and Stages of Harvest - Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT418 - SPICES AND PLANTATION CROPS TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cashew - Harvesting and Stages of Harvest - Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cashew Harvesting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Cashew harvesting* is the controlled detachment of physiologically mature cashew apples and attached cashew nuts (raw seeds) from the cashew tree (*Anacardium occidentale* L., family Anacardiaceae) at the correct stage of maturity, followed by their separation, primary processing, and preservation treatments to obtain a commercially viable raw cashew nut (RCN) of acceptable moisture content (typically **< 8%** wet basis) for safe storage and downstream kernel extraction.

Cashew is unique among plantation crops because the **economic produce is the nut (the cashew seed)**, while the **cashew apple** (the swollen, fleshy peduncle) is a secondary, highly perishable by-product. Harvesting must therefore manage two distinct biological materials simultaneously.

### 1.2 Why Harvesting is Critical in Cashew

- **Maturity timing** directly determines kernel quality, percentage of fully-developed kernels, and the share of **"premature" (immature, shriveled) nuts** — which are rejected or heavily discounted.
- Improper harvesting causes **CNSL (Cashew Nut Shell Liquid) contamination** of the kernel if the shell is broken.
- Over-mature nuts fall to the ground and get contaminated with soil/fungi, increasing **aflatoxin risk**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy 🍎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Mango and its Seed":**
> Think of a cashew apple as a mango fruit, and the cashew nut (attached to its bottom) as the seed inside. Just as you wouldn't pick a green mango for eating but also wouldn't let it fall to the ground to rot, cashew harvesters must pick the **ripe apple + mature nut pair** at the precise moment when the apple turns yellow/red AND the nut shell has hardened. Picking too early → empty kernels. Picking too late → apples rot and nuts become fungal hosts. **Drying**, in turn, is like sun-drying soaked clothes — water must be removed *gradually* so the inside dries without case-hardening the outside.

### 1.4 Botanical Context — What You Are Actually Harvesting

| Component | Botanical Identity | Typical Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **Cashew Apple** | Swollen pedicel/peduncle (false fruit) | 80–85% moisture, rich in Vitamin C (5× orange), sugars, tannins |
| **Cashew Nut (in shell)** | True fruit — a kidney-shaped drupe | 11–15% moisture in raw nut; kernel ~ 25% of nut weight |
| **Shell** | Mesocarp + endocarp | Contains CNSL (Caustic, allergenic phenolic resin) — ~25% of nut weight |
| **Kernel** | Embryo (cotyledons) | ~ 47% fat, ~ 21% protein, ~ 5% moisture (final) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The cashew "nut" is botanically a **kidney-shaped drupe** that hangs *below* the cashew apple. This unusual orientation is why harvesting and detachment methods differ from other plantation crops like coffee or cocoa.

---

## 1.5 Maturity Indices for Cashew

The **stage of harvest** is decided using the following physiological and visual maturity indices:

1. **Colour Change of Apple** — Apple turns from green → yellow → red/pinkish (variety dependent).
2. **Apple Firmness** — Becomes slightly soft, juicy, and turgid.
3. **Nut Shell Hardening** — The grey-green shell becomes ash-grey/straw-coloured and **fully lignified**.
4. **Nut Size** — Reaches full varietal size; kernel is fully developed.
5. **Detachment Ease** — Mature nut + apple separates with a slight twist.
6. **Days After Fruit Set (DAFS):** Generally **45–70 days** depending on cultivar and climate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake by Students:** Mistaking apple colour change *alone* as maturity index. **Apple and nut mature on the same pedicel but can ripen at different rates.** A fully red apple may still have an under-developed kernel. Always cross-check shell hardness.

---

## 1.6 Stages of Cashew Harvest

The harvest operation is divided into **three operational stages**:

### Stage 1 — Pre-Harvest Preparation
- Field cleaning, weed removal, and clearing of fallen nuts.
- Mobilization of pickers, baskets, and collection crates.
- Scheduling based on flowering pattern (often staggered).

### Stage 2 — Detachment (Picking)
- Hand-picking of mature apple+nut pairs.
- Use of bamboo sticks with hooked ends for higher nuts (in tall trees).
- Fallen nuts and apples are collected separately (these are often infected).

### Stage 3 — Separation and Primary Handling
- **Twisting or cutting** the nut away from the apple.
- Apples are sent for juice/pulp/feni processing (Goa, Maharashtra) or discarded.
- Raw Cashew Nuts (RCN) are collected for drying.

---

## 1.7 Why Drying is Non-Negotiable in Cashew

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Freshly harvested RCN has **moisture content between 11% and 15% (wet basis)**. If dried, it stores for **> 1 year** at < 5% moisture. If stored wet, it suffers from:
> - **Mould and aflatoxin contamination** (Aspergillus flavus, A. parasiticus)
> - **Kernel shrinkage and breakage during roasting**
> - **Fermentation and heating** in heaps
> - **Insect infestation**
> 
> Hence, **drying is the most critical post-harvest unit operation in cashew**, more so than in any other plantation nut.

---

## 1.8 Standard Quality Benchmarks (KTU 2024 Module Highlights)

| Parameter | Target Value |
|---|---|
| Initial moisture of fresh RCN | **11–15% (wb)** |
| Safe storage moisture (kernel-grade) | **< 5% (wb)** |
| Safe storage moisture (in-shell) | **< 8% (wb)** |
| Drying temperature (mechanical) | **70–80 °C** |
| Sun drying duration | **2–3 bright days** |
| Mechanical drying duration | **6–10 hours** |
| Kernel recovery from dried RCN | **22–25%** (whole kernels basis) |
| Maximum permissible mouldy nuts | **< 1%** |

---

## 1.9 Section 1 Recap (Key Take-aways)

- Cashew harvest deals with **two products** simultaneously: apple (perishable) and nut (durable after drying).
- Maturity is judged by **apple colour + nut shell hardness + DAFS**.
- Drying is essential to bring RCN moisture to **< 8%** for safe storage.
- Improper drying = **aflatoxin risk = export rejection**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Cashew Harvesting — Step-by-Step

The harvest workflow follows a strict sequence. Each step is explained below with the underlying **"Why"** and **"How"**.

### Step 1: Maturity Survey (Day 0)
- **Why:** Avoid harvesting immature nuts (low kernel yield, shriveled kernels, high oil loss).
- **How:** Visual inspection of apple colour + tapping the nut to check shell hardness + cut-test on sample nuts (3–5 nuts per tree across 10% of trees).

### Step 2: Field Preparation
- **Why:** Reduce contamination, facilitate collection.
- **How:** Weed slashing, removal of fallen/rotten fruits, leveling of soil under the canopy.

### Step 3: Detachment of Apple+Nut Pair
- **Why:** Only mature pairs must be picked; fallen nuts are not preferred.
- **How:**
  - **Manual hand-picking** (most common in India — Kerala, Goa, Maharashtra, Karnataka).
  - **Bamboo pole with hook** for tall trees (> 4 m).
  - **Mechanical shakers** (experimental in large Brazilian and African plantations).
  - **Twist-and-pull method** — hold apple, twist 90°, pull downward.

### Step 4: Separation of Nut from Apple
- **Why:** Apples are perishable; nuts need drying. Different downstream uses.
- **How:**
  - **Manual twisting** (small holdings).
  - **Decorticator-separators** (large estates).
  - **Tying the apple to a rope and letting the nut drop by gravity** (traditional in some parts of Africa — *gravity separation*).

### Step 5: Collection of Raw Cashew Nuts (RCN)
- **Why:** Bulking and transport to drying yard.
- **How:** Woven baskets, plastic crates, or jute bags. Avoid polythene bags (trap moisture → heating).

### Step 6: Pre-Drying Inspection
- **Why:** Remove already-damaged, mouldy, or insect-attacked nuts before drying.
- **How:** Hand-sorting over a wire mesh table. Mouldy nuts are destroyed.

### Step 7: Drying
- **Why:** Reduce moisture to safe level (kernel 4–5%, in-shell < 8%).
- **How:** Sun drying / mechanical drying (see Section 2.3).

### Step 8: Bulk Storage
- **Why:** Wait for processing season.
- **How:** Store in dry godowns on wooden pallets; moisture-proof packaging; fumigation with aluminium phosphide if needed.

### Step 9: Bagging and Transport to Processing Units
- **Why:** Export/domestic sale.
- **How:** Jute bags, moisture < 8% verified by **moisture meter** (capacitance type).

---

## 2.2 Stages of Harvest — A Comparative View

| Stage | Activity | Tools Used | Time Span | Critical Control Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Harvest** | Maturity survey, field cleaning | Knife, gloves, sample bags | 1–3 days | Sample cut-test |
| **Detachment** | Picking / shaking | Hand, bamboo hook, ladder | 4–6 weeks (staggered) | Only mature nuts |
| **Separation** | Nut from apple | Hands, decorticator | Same day as picking | Avoid apple juice staining |
| **Pre-Drying** | Sorting, washing (optional) | Mesh, water tank | Same day | Remove mouldy nuts |
| **Drying** | Moisture removal | Sun / mechanical dryer | 2–3 days (sun) / 6–10 h (mech.) | Uniform drying, no overheating |
| **Storage** | Bulk storage | Jute bags, godown | Months | Moisture < 8%, pest-free |
| **Dispatch** | Bagging, transport | Trucks, moisture meter | Days | Final QC check |

---

## 2.3 Drying — Theoretical Framework

Drying is a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer** operation. Hot air transfers sensible heat to the nut → water evaporates from inside → water vapour diffuses out through the shell pores and kernel.

### 2.3.1 Two Major Drying Methods for Cashew

#### A. **Sun Drying (Traditional)**
- Spread RCN in a **single layer** on raised wire-mesh platforms, cemented drying yards, or tarpaulins.
- **Thickness:** 5–7 cm maximum; turn every 2–3 hours.
- **Duration:** 2–3 bright sunny days.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, no fuel, no equipment.
- **Disadvantages:** Weather-dependent, slow, contamination risk (dust, birds, animals), uneven drying.

#### B. **Mechanical (Hot-Air) Drying**
- Uses **batch tray dryers, rotary dryers, or continuous belt dryers**.
- Air temperature: **70–80 °C**.
- Air velocity: 1.0–2.0 m/s.
- Drying time: **6–10 hours**.
- **Advantages:** Fast, uniform, hygienic, year-round operation.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher capital and energy cost.

#### C. **Solar Tunnel / Solar Cabinet Drying** (Emerging)
- Hybrid method combining solar radiation with controlled airflow.
- Suitable for smallholder farmer clusters (especially in Africa).
- Reduces drying time to **1–2 days**.

### 2.3.2 Two-Stage Sun Drying Protocol (KTU-recommended)

1. **Stage 1 — Surface moisture removal** (Day 1, morning to noon).
2. **Stage 2 — Internal moisture diffusion** (Day 1 afternoon → Day 2, with overnight heaping and re-spreading in the morning).
3. **Stage 3 — Conditioning** (Day 3, brief drying + bulk equilibration in shaded area).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two-Stage?** If you try to dry 15% moisture RCN to < 5% in one continuous pass, the outer shell dries and hardens first (**case-hardening**), trapping moisture inside. Overnight heaping allows **moisture equalization** — internal moisture migrates outward, then resumes drying next day.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cashew Drying & Moisture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are tested in KTU ESE/IA questions.** Master these equations and the units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Moisture content on **wet basis** (most commonly used in cashew trade) | % |
| 2 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content on **dry basis** (used in drying engineering) | % |
| 3 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ | Wet → Dry basis conversion | % |
| 4 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}/100}$ | Dry → Wet basis conversion | % |
| 5 | $R = -\dfrac{dW}{A \cdot dt}$ | **Drying rate** (mass of water removed per unit area per unit time) | kg/m²·h |
| 6 | $MR = \dfrac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ | **Moisture Ratio** (dimensionless) used in thin-layer drying models | — |
| 7 | $MR = e^{-kt}$ | **Newton (Lewis) thin-layer drying model** (most used for cashew) | — |
| 8 | $MR = a \cdot e^{-kt}$ | **Henderson-Pabis model** (more accurate for cashew) | — |
| 9 | $MR = e^{-kt^n}$ | **Page model** (handles falling-rate period best) | — |
| 10 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | **Sensible heat** required to raise nut temperature | kJ |
| 11 | $Q_{lat} = m_w \cdot h_{fg}$ | **Latent heat** required to vaporize water | kJ |
| 12 | $h_{fg} = 2501 - 2.381 \cdot T$ | Latent heat of vaporization (Sonntag equation) | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $t_d = \dfrac{M_0 - M_f}{R_{avg}}$ | **Drying time** estimate | h |
| 14 | $\eta_{dryer} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | Dryer thermal efficiency | % |
| 15 | $W_{kernel} = W_{RCN} \times Y_{cut} \times Y_k$ | Kernel weight from RCN | kg |
| 16 | $K_{recovery} = \dfrac{W_{whole\,kernels}}{W_{RCN}} \times 100$ | Kernel recovery percentage | % |
| 17 | $L_m = \dfrac{h_{fg} \cdot \dot{m}_w}{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T}$ | Log mean temperature difference (used in heat-mass transfer balance) | — |
| 18 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_c \cdot d_p}{D_v}$ | **Sherwood number** — relates mass transfer coefficient to diffusivity | — |
| 19 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_p}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds number** for drying air over nut | — |
| 20 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | **Nusselt number** (Dittus-Boelter for turbulent internal flow) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Symbols used:**
> - $W_w$ = wet mass of nut (kg), $W_d$ = dry mass (kg)
> - $M_t$ = moisture at time *t*, $M_0$ = initial moisture, $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture, $M_f$ = final moisture
> - $k, a, n$ = drying constants, $t$ = time (h)
> - $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), $h_{fg}$ = latent heat (kJ/kg), $T$ = temperature (°C)
> - $k_c$ = mass transfer coefficient, $D_v$ = vapour diffusivity, $d_p$ = particle diameter
> - $Y_{cut}$ = cutting yield, $Y_k$ = kernel fraction

---

## 2.5 Physical Significance of Each Formula in Cashew Drying

- **Formulas 1–4** → Used to express and convert moisture content. KTU may give moisture in wb and ask to convert to db.
- **Formulas 5–9** → Mathematical models of drying curves. The **Page model** fits cashew drying the best; **Newton model** is the simplest exam choice.
- **Formulas 10–12** → Energy balance for designing mechanical dryers.
- **Formula 13** → Quick exam calculation of drying time.
- **Formula 14** → Used in dryer selection (efficiency > 50% is acceptable for cashew).
- **Formulas 15–16** → Industry economics — how much kernel you get from 1 tonne of RCN.
- **Formulas 17–20** → Used in design-oriented numerical questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Application

| Sector | Application of Cashew Drying Theory |
|---|---|
| **Cashew Processing Factories (Goa, Kerala, Africa)** | Sizing mechanical dryers based on daily RCN throughput, energy calculations |
| **Solar Drying SMEs** | Designing solar cabinet dryers using Page model to predict drying time |
| **Export Houses** | Verifying safe storage moisture to comply with **Codex Alimentarius** and **FSSAI** |
| **Quality Control Labs** | Calibration of moisture meters using oven-dry reference method ($M_{wb}$ formula) |
| **Storage Engineering** | Predicting shelf-life using equilibrium moisture content ($M_e$) |

---

## 2.7 Section 2 Recap

- Harvest = 9 logical steps from maturity survey to dispatch.
- Drying = simultaneous heat and mass transfer; moisture is the critical control parameter.
- **Sun drying** = cheap, slow, weather-dependent.
- **Mechanical drying** = fast, controllable, energy-intensive.
- KTU's high-yield formulas centre on **moisture content (wb, db), drying rate, drying time, and thin-layer drying models**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Moisture Content (Wet Basis) for Cashew RCN

### Statement
A sample of fresh cashew nuts has a wet mass $W_w = 120$ g. After oven drying at **105 °C for 24 h**, the dry mass is $W_d = 105$ g. Determine (a) moisture content on wet basis, (b) moisture content on dry basis, (c) percentage of water removed to reach a safe storage moisture of 5% (wb).

### Given
- $W_w = 120$ g (initial wet mass)
- $W_d = 105$ g (bone-dry mass)
- $M_f = 5\%$ (target wet-basis moisture)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute initial moisture content (wet basis).**

$$
M_{wb,0} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

Substitute values:

$$
M_{wb,0} = \frac{120 - 105}{120} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{wb,0} = \frac{15}{120} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{wb,0} = 12.5\%
$$

**Step 2: Compute initial moisture content (dry basis).**

$$
M_{db,0} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{db,0} = \frac{15}{105} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{db,0} = 14.286\%
$$

**Step 3: Convert target 5% (wb) to dry basis.**

$$
M_{db,f} = \frac{M_{wb,f}}{1 - M_{wb,f}/100}
$$

$$
M_{db,f} = \frac{5}{1 - 5/100} = \frac{5}{0.95} = 5.263\%
$$

**Step 4: Total water in 1 kg of fresh nut (initial).**

$$
m_{w,0} = \frac{M_{wb,0}}{100} \times W_w = 0.125 \times 1 = 0.125 \text{ kg water/kg wet nut}
$$

**Step 5: Dry matter content (constant).**

$$
m_d = \frac{W_d}{W_w} = \frac{105}{120} = 0.875 \text{ kg dry matter/kg wet nut}
$$

**Step 6: Final wet mass of nut when dried to 5% (wb).**

Dry matter is conserved. So:

$$
W_f = \frac{m_d}{1 - M_{wb,f}/100} = \frac{0.875}{0.95} = 0.9211 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 7: Water removed per kg of fresh nut.**

$$
m_{w,removed} = W_w - W_f = 1 - 0.9211 = 0.0789 \text{ kg water}
$$

**Step 8: Percentage of original water removed.**

$$
\%\,w_{removed} = \frac{m_{w,removed}}{m_{w,0}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0789}{0.125} \times 100 = 63.16\%
$$

### Final Answer
- (a) $M_{wb,0} = 12.5\%$
- (b) $M_{db,0} = 14.29\%$
- (c) $63.16\%$ of original water must be removed to reach safe storage.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Cashew trade in India uses **wet basis**. Always quote moisture in $M_{wb}$ unless specified.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Drying Time using Newton (Lewis) Model

### Statement
A cashew dryer is loaded with **500 kg of RCN** at initial moisture **12.5% (wb)**. Final moisture required is **5% (wb)**. The drying constant for cashew under the given conditions is $k = 0.42 \text{ h}^{-1}$. The equilibrium moisture content is $M_e = 1.5\%$ (db). Estimate the **drying time** using the Lewis thin-layer model.

### Given
- $W_w = 500$ kg
- $M_{wb,0} = 12.5\%$
- $M_{wb,f} = 5\%$
- $k = 0.42 \text{ h}^{-1}$
- $M_e = 1.5\%$ (db)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert initial and final moistures to dry basis.**

$$
M_{db,0} = \frac{12.5}{1 - 0.125} = \frac{12.5}{0.875} = 14.286\%
$$

$$
M_{db,f} = \frac{5}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{5}{0.95} = 5.263\%
$$

**Step 2: Apply Lewis thin-layer drying model.**

$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = e^{-kt}
$$

At final time $t_d$, $M_t = M_{db,f}$:

$$
MR_f = \frac{5.263 - 1.5}{14.286 - 1.5} = \frac{3.763}{12.786} = 0.2943
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $t_d$.**

$$
0.2943 = e^{-0.42 \cdot t_d}
$$

Take natural log on both sides:

$$
\ln(0.2943) = -0.42 \cdot t_d
$$

$$
-1.2232 = -0.42 \cdot t_d
$$

$$
t_d = \frac{1.2232}{0.42} = 2.912 \text{ h}
$$

### Final Answer
- **Drying time ≈ 2.91 hours** (≈ 2 h 55 min).

> [!NOTE]
> **Realism Check:** The Lewis model often *under-predicts* drying time in cashew because it ignores the falling-rate period. Actual time in a mechanical dryer at 70 °C is closer to **6–8 h**. For a more accurate KTU answer, use the **Page model** with two constants $k$ and $n$ if given in the question.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Drying Time using Page Model

### Statement
For the same cashew batch, the **Page model** parameters are $k = 0.31 \text{ h}^{-1}$ and $n = 1.25$. Estimate the drying time.

### Given
- $MR_f = 0.2943$ (from above)
- $k = 0.31 \text{ h}^{-1}$
- $n = 1.25$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply Page model.**

$$
MR = e^{-k t^n}
$$

**Step 2: Take natural log twice.**

$$
\ln(MR) = -k t^n
$$

$$
-\ln(MR) = k t^n
$$

$$
t^n = \frac{-\ln(MR)}{k}
$$

$$
t = \left( \frac{-\ln(MR)}{k} \right)^{1/n}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
-\ln(0.2943) = 1.2232
$$

$$
t^{1.25} = \frac{1.2232}{0.31} = 3.9458
$$

$$
t = (3.9458)^{1/1.25} = (3.9458)^{0.8}
$$

**Step 4: Compute power.**

Using $a^b = e^{b \ln a}$:

$$
t = e^{0.8 \times \ln(3.9458)} = e^{0.8 \times 1.3727} = e^{1.0982} = 2.999 \text{ h}
$$

### Final Answer
- **Drying time using Page model ≈ 3.0 hours**.

> [!TIP]
> Compare both models: Lewis (2.91 h) vs. Page (3.00 h). The Page model gives a slightly more conservative (realistic) estimate. **In KTU exams, always state which model you are using and why.**

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Heat Required to Dry Cashew Batch

### Statement
Determine the total **heat input** required to dry 500 kg of cashew RCN from 12.5% (wb) to 5% (wb) in a mechanical dryer. Inlet air: 80 °C; outlet air: 55 °C. Specific heat of cashew nut $c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K. Initial nut temperature = 28 °C; final nut temperature = 70 °C. Specific heat of dry air $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

### Given
- $W_w = 500$ kg
- Water to remove $= 0.0789 \times 500 = 39.47$ kg (from Section 3.1, Step 7 — but recompute for this batch)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Total water to be removed.**

Initial water in 500 kg:

$$
m_{w,0} = 0.125 \times 500 = 62.5 \text{ kg}
$$

Final water at 5% (wb):

$$
m_{w,f} = 0.05 \times W_f
$$

But $W_f$ depends on dry matter:

$$
W_f = \frac{W_d}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{500 \times 0.875}{0.95} = 460.53 \text{ kg}
$$

So:

$$
m_{w,f} = 0.05 \times 460.53 = 23.03 \text{ kg}
$$

Water removed:

$$
m_{w,rem} = 62.5 - 23.03 = 39.47 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Latent heat of vaporization at nut surface (~60 °C average).**

$$
h_{fg} = 2501 - 2.381 \times T = 2501 - 2.381 \times 60 = 2501 - 142.86 = 2358.14 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3: Latent heat required.**

$$
Q_{lat} = m_{w,rem} \times h_{fg} = 39.47 \times 2358.14 = 93{,}065 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4: Sensible heat to raise nut temperature.**

$$
Q_{sens} = W_w \times c_p \times \Delta T = 500 \times 2.1 \times (70 - 28) = 500 \times 2.1 \times 42 = 44{,}100 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 5: Total heat input (theoretical).**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{lat} + Q_{sens} = 93{,}065 + 44{,}100 = 137{,}165 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 6: Account for dryer efficiency (assume 60%).**

$$
Q_{input} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\eta_{dryer}} = \frac{137{,}165}{0.60} = 228{,}608 \text{ kJ}
$$

### Final Answer
- Total useful heat: **137,165 kJ**
- Actual heat input at 60% efficiency: **228,608 kJ** (≈ 63.5 kWh).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Drying Method Comparison for Cashew

| Parameter | Sun Drying | Mechanical Drying (Hot Air) | Solar Tunnel Drying |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital Cost** | Very Low | High | Medium |
| **Operating Cost** | Lowest | High (electricity/diesel) | Low–Medium |
| **Drying Time** | 2–3 days (16–24 h sunshine) | 6–10 h | 1–2 days |
| **Drying Temperature** | Ambient (30–40 °C) | 70–80 °C | 50–70 °C (with peaks) |
| **Uniformity** | Poor (manual turning) | High | Moderate–High |
| **Hygiene** | Low (open to dust, animals) | High (enclosed) | Moderate |
| **Weather Dependence** | Total | None | Partial |
| **Aflatoxin Risk** | Higher | Lower | Lower |
| **Energy Source** | Solar (free) | Fossil / electric | Solar (free) + fan |
| **Capacity** | Small batch (< 1 t/day) | Large (5–50 t/day) | Small–Medium |
| **Skill Required** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Best for** | Smallholders in dry season | Commercial processors | Farmer cooperatives |

---

## 3.6 Exhaustive Derivation 5: Drying Rate and Constant-Rate Period for Cashew

### Statement
A thin-layer drying experiment on cashew nuts gives the following moisture vs. time data (dry basis):

| Time (h) | Moisture (% db) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 14.29 |
| 1 | 11.80 |
| 2 | 9.70 |
| 3 | 8.00 |
| 4 | 6.60 |
| 5 | 5.50 |
| 6 | 4.65 |
| 7 | 3.95 |
| 8 | 3.40 |

Plot the drying curve, identify the **constant-rate** and **falling-rate** periods, and determine the drying constant $k$ for the falling-rate period using the Lewis model.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Plot $M_{db}$ vs. $t$.**

(Visualisation in a Mermaid block will be done in Section 4.)

**Step 2: Identify periods.**

A drying curve for cashew typically shows:
- **Constant-rate period** = the initial linear region where surface evaporation dominates. For cashew (low initial moisture), this period is **very short or absent** because the nut has high internal resistance.
- **Falling-rate period** = the bulk of drying, where internal diffusion dominates.

From the table, the moisture drops non-linearly throughout, so cashew exhibits **only the falling-rate period** for the bulk of drying.

**Step 3: Compute moisture ratio for each point.**

Using $M_e = 1.5\%$ (db):

$$
MR_t = \frac{M_t - 1.5}{14.29 - 1.5} = \frac{M_t - 1.5}{12.79}
$$

| t (h) | M (db) | MR | ln(MR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 14.29 | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 11.80 | 0.805 | −0.217 |
| 2 | 9.70 | 0.641 | −0.445 |
| 3 | 8.00 | 0.508 | −0.677 |
| 4 | 6.60 | 0.399 | −0.919 |
| 5 | 5.50 | 0.313 | −1.162 |
| 6 | 4.65 | 0.246 | −1.402 |
| 7 | 3.95 | 0.192 | −1.651 |
| 8 | 3.40 | 0.149 | −1.904 |

**Step 4: Apply Lewis model and compute k.**

From $MR = e^{-kt}$:

$$
\ln(MR) = -k t
$$

Plotting $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ should give a straight line. Slope = $-k$.

**Step 5: Linear regression.**

Using least squares on 9 points:

$$
\bar{t} = 4.0, \quad \overline{\ln(MR)} = -0.9537
$$

$$
\sum (t_i - \bar{t})(\ln MR_i - \overline{\ln MR}) = ?
$$

Compute:

| t | ln(MR) | (t-4) | ln(MR) - mean | product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | −4 | 0.954 | −3.815 |
| 1 | −0.217 | −3 | 0.737 | −2.210 |
| 2 | −0.445 | −2 | 0.509 | −1.018 |
| 3 | −0.677 | −1 | 0.277 | −0.277 |
| 4 | −0.919 | 0 | 0.035 | 0.000 |
| 5 | −1.162 | 1 | −0.208 | −0.208 |
| 6 | −1.402 | 2 | −0.448 | −0.897 |
| 7 | −1.651 | 3 | −0.697 | −2.092 |
| 8 | −1.904 | 4 | −0.950 | −3.800 |

Sum of products ≈ −14.317
Sum of $(t - \bar{t})^2$ = 16+9+4+1+0+1+4+9+16 = 60

Slope:

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{-14.317}{60} = -0.2386
$$

So:

$$
k = 0.2386 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6: Predict drying time to reach MR = 0.05 (≈ 2.14% db).**

$$
t_d = -\frac{\ln(0.05)}{0.2386} = \frac{2.996}{0.2386} = 12.56 \text{ h}
$$

### Final Answer
- Drying constant $k = 0.239 \text{ h}^{-1}$
- Estimated drying time to reach 2.14% db = **12.56 h** (this is the falling-rate portion only).

---

## 3.7 Section 3 Recap

- Cashew drying is governed by **falling-rate kinetics** — internal diffusion is the bottleneck.
- The **Page model** is the most accurate; **Lewis** is the simplest exam choice.
- Total drying energy = **latent heat** (for water removal) + **sensible heat** (to warm the nut).
- Moisture conversion between **wb** and **db** is essential for trade vs. engineering calculations.
- The drying constant $k$ is obtained by plotting $\ln(MR)$ vs. $t$ — slope magnitude gives $k$.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cashew Harvesting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Inspection and Maturity Survey] --> B[Cut Test on 10 percent of Trees]
    B --> C{Mature Apple + Nut Pair}
    C -- Yes --> D[Manual Picking with Bamboo Hook or Hand]
    C -- No --> E[Leave for 7 to 10 Days]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Collection in Baskets or Crates]
    F --> G[Separation: Twist Nut from Apple]
    G --> H[Cashew Apple: Feni / Juice / Pulp]
    G --> I[Raw Cashew Nut: RCN]
    I --> J[Sorting and Grading]
    J --> K{Mouldy or Damaged}
    K -- Yes --> L[Destroy or Downgrade]
    K -- No --> M[Pre-Drying Stage 1: Sun Drying Day 1]
    M --> N[Overnight Heaping for Moisture Equalisation]
    N --> O[Stage 2: Sun Drying Day 2]
    O --> P[Stage 3: Conditioning Day 3]
    P --> Q{Quality Check with Moisture Meter}
    Q -- Moisture less than 8 percent --> R[Bulk Storage in Jute Bags]
    Q -- Moisture greater than 8 percent --> O
    R --> S[Dispatch to Processing Unit]
    S --> T[Roasting and Kernel Extraction]
```

## 4.2 Cashew Drying Method Selection — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh RCN: 11 to 15 percent wb moisture] --> B{Quantity and Scale}
    B -- Smallholder less than 1 ton per day --> C[Sun Drying on Raised Platform]
    B -- Cooperative 1 to 5 ton per day --> D[Solar Tunnel Dryer]
    B -- Commercial greater than 5 ton per day --> E[Mechanical Hot Air Dryer]
    C --> F[Turn every 2 to 3 hours]
    D --> G[Internal fan and chimney effect]
    E --> H[70 to 80 degree celsius for 6 to 10 hours]
    F --> I[Final Moisture less than 8 percent wb]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Packaging in Jute Bags]
    J --> K[Cold Storage or Dry Godown]
```

## 4.3 Drying Process — Heat and Mass Transfer Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Air Inlet: 70 to 80 degree celsius] --> B[Sensible Heat Transfer to Nut Surface]
    B --> C[Nut Surface Temperature Rises]
    C --> D[Water Evaporates from Surface]
    D --> E[Internal Moisture Diffuses to Surface]
    E --> F[Water Vapour Carried Away by Air Stream]
    F --> G[Moist Air Exits: 50 to 60 degree celsius]
    G --> H[Latent Heat Removed: Qlat]
    C --> I[Sensible Heat Absorbed: Qsens]
    H --> J[Total Heat: Qtotal = Qlat + Qsens]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Dryer Efficiency Calculation]
    K --> L{Energy Input Source}
    L -- Electricity --> M[10 to 15 kWh per ton]
    L -- Diesel --> N[3 to 5 litres per ton]
    L -- Biomass --> O[40 to 60 kg wood per ton]
```

## 4.4 Cashew Maturity Index — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cashew Fruit on Tree] --> B{Apple Colour}
    B -- Green --> C[Immature: Wait]
    B -- Yellow or Red --> D{Nut Shell Hardness}
    D -- Soft and Green --> E[Wait 10 to 15 Days]
    D -- Hard and Grey or Straw --> F{DAFS greater than 45}
    F -- No --> G[Wait]
    F -- Yes --> H[READY TO HARVEST]
    H --> I[Pick Apple plus Nut Pair]
    I --> J[Twist and Separate]
```

## 4.5 Thin-Layer Drying Curve (Schematic) for Cashew

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseA[Constant Rate Period - Very Short]
        A1[Surface Water Evaporates] --> A2[Linear Moisture Drop]
    end
    subgraph PhaseB[Falling Rate Period - Dominant]
        B1[Internal Diffusion Begins] --> B2[Moisture Front Recedes Inside] --> B3[Exponential Decay of Moisture]
    end
    subgraph PhaseC[Equilibrium]
        C1[Moisture Approaches Me] --> C2[Drying Stops]
    end
    PhaseA --> PhaseB
    PhaseB --> PhaseC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The drying curve for cashew is **overwhelmingly falling-rate** because the nut has low porosity and a high internal resistance to moisture migration. This is why **drying time is long** compared to other plantation crops like coffee or cardamom.

---

## 4.6 Section 4 Recap

- Cashew harvest is a **9-step linear process** with quality gates at sorting and moisture check.
- Drying method selection depends on **scale, climate, and capital**.
- The drying process involves **two parallel transfer processes** — heat (in) and mass (out).
- The **decision tree for maturity** uses 3 cross-checked indices — colour, shell hardness, DAFS.
- Cashew drying is dominated by the **falling-rate period** — internal diffusion controls.
